# Module 1 — Types of Ships

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **ship** is a waterborne vessel of significant size (generally above **100 GT** — Gross Tonnage) designed for ocean or deep-water navigation, possessing means of steering, propulsion, and the structural integrity to operate safely in open seas as defined under the **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) Convention** and the **IMO (International Maritime Organization)** classification framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> **CO1** — *Understand the fundamental taxonomy, hull forms, and operational roles of different ship types used in global maritime trade and naval operations.*

### Conceptual Analogy — Why "Types of Ships" Matters

Imagine a **transportation network** on land: you have trucks for cargo, buses for passengers, tankers for fuel, and ambulances for emergencies. Each vehicle is engineered for a *very specific job*. If you tried to ferry people in a fuel tanker or carry oil in a passenger bus, both would be inefficient and unsafe.

The same logic applies to ships. A naval submarine and a cruise liner share the same ocean, but they are as different as a fighter jet and a holiday bus. **Ships are classified by their mission profile, hull form, propulsion, and operational environment** — and this classification governs their structural design, stability criteria, power-plant selection, and even the regulatory code (e.g., **MARPOL**, **SOLAS**, **Classification Society Rules** from **IRS**, **DNV**, **ABS**, **Lloyd's Register**).

### The Three Primary Classification Axes

A KTU 2024 examiner expects you to know that ships are classified along **three fundamental axes**:

| Axis | Question it Answers | KTU High-Yield Examples |
|------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| **By Purpose / Service** | *What job does the ship do?* | Cargo, Tanker, Passenger, Naval, Fishing, Tugs, Special Purpose |
| **By Hull Form / Hydrodynamics** | *How does the hull move through water?* | Displacement, Planing, Semi-Displacement, Hydrofoil, SWATH, Catamaran |
| **By Propulsion / Machinery** | *What drives the ship?* | Diesel, Steam Turbine, Gas Turbine, Nuclear, Hybrid, Sail-Assisted |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric (IMO):** Every commercial vessel is measured in **Gross Tonnage (GT)** — a volumetric unit (1 GT = 100 ft³ ≈ 2.83 m³ of enclosed space) and **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** — the carrying capacity in metric tonnes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification by Purpose / Service

This is the **most frequently tested axis** in KTU examinations. Let us break it down systematically.

### 2.1.1 Merchant / Commercial Ships

These form the **backbone of global trade** — over **80% of world trade by volume** moves by sea, as per **UNCTAD 2023** statistics.

**(a) General Cargo Ships**
- Multi-deck vessels with cargo holds fitted with **hatch covers**.
- Equipped with shipboard **derricks** or **deck cranes** (modern ones use **provision cranes**).
- Typical block coefficient: $C_B \approx 0.65 - 0.75$.
- Modern general cargo ships are being phased out in favour of **container ships**.

**(b) Bulk Carriers**
- Single-deck vessels with **large hatches** and **tweendecks**.
- Carry dry bulk: iron ore, coal, grain, bauxite.
- **Size classes** (must memorize for KTU):
  - **Handysize** — 15,000–35,000 DWT
  - **Handymax / Supramax** — 35,000–60,000 DWT
  - **Panamax** — 60,000–80,000 DWT (fits the original Panama Canal locks)
  - **Capesize** — 100,000–200,000 DWT (cannot pass Suez or Panama — must go around Cape of Good Hope)
  - **VLOC (Very Large Ore Carrier)** — 200,000+ DWT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why bulk carriers capsize:** The IMO's *SOLAS Chapter XII* and *BC Code* address the infamous "**bulk carrier capsize problem**" because of cargo liquefaction and uneven loading.

**(c) Container Ships**
- Carry **TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Units)** — the global standard.
- Cellular guide structure holds containers vertically.
- **Post-Panamax / Neo-Panamax** classes exceed 24,000 TEU.
- Hull form is highly optimized: **fine entry**, **parallel middle body**, and **stern with bulbous bow**.

**(d) Tankers**
- **Crude Oil Carriers (CRUDES)**:
  - **Product Tanker** — 10,000–60,000 DWT (refined products)
  - **Aframax** — 80,000–120,000 DWT
  - **Suezmax** — 120,000–200,000 DWT (fits the Suez Canal)
  - **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** — 200,000–320,000 DWT
  - **ULCC (Ultra Large Crude Carrier)** — 320,000+ DWT (rare today)
- **Chemical Tankers** — have **stainless steel tanks**, double-hull, and IMO Type 1/2/3 classification.
- **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) Carriers** — operate at **$-163^\circ C$**; membrane-type or Moss-type (spherical) tanks.
- **LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) Carriers** — pressurized or refrigerated.

**(e) Ro-Ro Ships (Roll-on/Roll-off)**
- Vehicles drive on and off via **stern ramps** or **side ramps**.
- High freeboard and **internal ramp systems** for multi-deck parking.

**(f) Reefers (Refrigerated Cargo Ships)**
- Insulated holds with **refrigeration plants** for perishable goods.

### 2.1.2 Passenger Ships

- **Cruise Liners** — modern giants like *Symphony of the Seas* (228,081 GT) carry 6,000+ passengers.
- **Ferries** — short-sea passenger/vehicle transport.
- **Ocean Liners** — historic long-distance passenger service (now virtually extinct as commercial category).

### 2.1.3 Naval / Military Ships

| Ship Type | Primary Role | Distinctive Feature |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Aircraft Carrier** | Mobile airbase | Flat flight deck, angled deck, catapults/arrester gear |
| **Destroyer (DDG)** | Anti-air/anti-sub | Gas turbine propulsion, AEGIS combat system |
| **Frigate (FFG)** | Escort role | Multi-mission, helicopter capable |
| **Corvette** | Coastal patrol | Small, fast, lightly armed |
| **Submarine (SSN/SSK/SSBN)** | Underwater warfare | Diesel-electric or nuclear |
| **Cruiser** | Flagship role | Large gun armament (mostly phased out) |

### 2.1.4 Specialized Vessels

- **Tugs** — high bollard pull (typically **50–100 tonnes**), compact, powerful.
- **Fishing Vessels** — trawlers, purse seiners, long-liners.
- **Dredgers** — trailing suction hopper, cutter suction, grab dredger.
- **Cable Layers** — lay submarine telecommunications cables.
- **Icebreakers** — reinforced bow, high propulsion power.
- **Research / Survey Ships** — oceanographic, hydrographic, seismic.

## 2.2 Classification by Hull Form / Hydrodynamic Type

This is the **second most-tested KTU topic**. The hull form determines the **resistance characteristics** and **speed regime** the ship operates in.

### 2.2.1 The Speed-Length Ratio — The Master Discriminator

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$$

where $F_n$ is the **Froude Number** (dimensionless), $V$ is speed in m/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s², and $L$ is the waterline length in metres.

> [!NOTE]
> The **speed-length ratio** $\frac{V}{\sqrt{L}}$ (with V in knots and L in feet, or both in SI) is the classical naval-architecture parameter that determines the dominant resistance regime.

### 2.2.2 The Six Hull-Form Regimes

| Hull Form | Typical V/√L | Operating Froude | Examples | Key Feature |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|----------|-------------|
| **Displacement** | 0.0 – 0.9 | $F_n < 0.4$ | Cargo ships, tankers, bulkers | Hull fully displaces water at rest |
| **Semi-Displacement** | 0.9 – 1.2 | $0.4 < F_n < 0.6$ | Patrol boats, fast ferries | Lift begins to assist buoyancy |
| **Planing** | 1.2 – 3.0+ | $F_n > 0.6$ | Speedboats, racing craft | Hull rises out of water at speed |
| **Hydrofoil** | 2.0 – 4.0+ | $F_n > 1.0$ | Passenger ferries, military | Foils lift hull clear of water |
| **Air-Cushion Vehicle (ACV/Hovercraft)** | Any | — | Military, civilian ferries | Air cushion supports weight |
| **Multi-Hull (Catamaran / SWATH / Trimaran)** | 1.0 – 2.0 | $F_n \approx 0.4 - 0.8$ | Fast ferries, naval | Two/three slender hulls |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SWATH (Small Waterplane Area Twin Hull):** Used for stability at low speed. The submerged volume is minimal — only thin struts pierce the water — so the ship has excellent seakeeping but limited payload.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semi-displacement hulls** are the *transitional* regime where both buoyancy and dynamic lift act. This is where most modern fast patrol vessels and high-speed ferries operate.

## 2.3 Classification by Propulsion / Power Plant

| Type | Power Output Range | Common Applications |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Diesel (Slow-speed 2-stroke)** | 5,000 – 100,000+ kW | Tankers, bulkers, container ships |
| **Diesel (Medium-speed 4-stroke)** | 1,000 – 20,000 kW | Naval, ferries, offshore |
| **Steam Turbine** | 20,000+ kW | Older LNG carriers, some naval |
| **Gas Turbine (COGAG / CODOG)** | 20,000 – 100,000 kW | Destroyers, frigates |
| **Nuclear (PWR)** | 100,000+ kW | Aircraft carriers, submarines |
| **Hybrid (Diesel-Electric + Batteries)** | Variable | Modern ferries, offshore, future fleet |
| **Sail-Assisted / Wind** | Auxiliary | Bulk carriers (re-emerging for emissions) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CODOG** = Combined Diesel or Gas; **COGAG** = Combined Gas and Gas; **CODAG** = Combined Diesel and Gas — these are **combined propulsion (COD)** arrangements standard in modern naval combatants.

## 2.4 Other Important Classification Axes

- **By Route / Operating Area:** Inland, Coastal (≤ 20 nm from shore), Short Sea, Deep-Sea.
- **By Structural Material:** Steel (95%+ of fleet), Aluminium (fast craft, superstructures), FRP/Composite (small craft, mine hunters), Wood (traditional).
- **By Ice-Class Notation:** Finnish-Swedish Ice Class (FSIC) 1A Super to E; **Polar Class (PC 1–7)** per IMO Polar Code.
- **By Classification Society:** IRS (Indian Register of Shipping), Lloyd's Register (UK), DNV (Norway-Germany), ABS (American Bureau of Shipping), BV (Bureau Veritas, France).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Equation Block** — must be memorized for ESE.

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Used For |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ | $\nabla$ = displaced volume (m³); $C_B$ = block coefficient; $L,B,T$ = length, beam, draft (m) | Estimating displacement of any ship |
| 2 | $DWT = \nabla_{loaded} - \nabla_{light}$ | Deadweight equals loaded minus lightship displacement (tonnes) | Cargo capacity calculation |
| 3 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$ | $V$ in m/s, $L$ in m, $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Hull-form regime identification |
| 4 | $\dfrac{V}{\sqrt{L}}$ | $V$ in knots, $L$ in feet (or both in SI) | Classical speed-length ratio |
| 5 | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ | Effective Horse Power; $R_T$ = total resistance (kgf); $V$ in m/s | Powering estimate |
| 6 | $BHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_S \cdot \eta_G}$ | Shaft + Gear efficiencies | Total installed power |
| 7 | $R_T = R_F + R_R + R_W$ | Frictional + Residuary + Wave resistance (Newton) | Resistance breakdown |
| 8 | $\eta_O = \dfrac{EHP}{BHP}$ | Overall propulsive efficiency | Performance analysis |
| 9 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Transverse metacentric height (m) | Stability check (not strictly "types" but KTU loves this) |
| 10 | $Trim = \dfrac{\Delta \cdot GM_L \cdot \sin\theta}{MCT}$ | Longitudinal trim equation (m) | Draft distribution |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Froude Number and Speed-Length Ratio

The **Froude Number** is derived from the dimensionless analysis of gravitational-inertial forces in free-surface flow.

### Step 1 — Identify the dominant forces

When a ship moves at speed $V$ through a fluid of density $\rho$ under gravity $g$, the two dominant forces for wave generation are:

- **Inertial force** $\sim \rho V^2 L^2$
- **Gravitational force** $\sim \rho g L^3$

### Step 2 — Form the dimensionless ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_n &= \sqrt{\frac{\text{Inertial}}{\text{Gravitational}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{\rho V^2 L^2}{\rho g L^3}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{V^2}{g L}} \\
&= \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to practical units

In naval architecture, $V$ is often in **knots** and $L$ in **feet** (or both in SI). For a typical tanker with $L = 300$ m, $V = 15$ knots:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 15 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s} \\
F_n &= \frac{7.716}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 300}} \\
&= \frac{7.716}{\sqrt{2943}} \\
&= \frac{7.716}{54.25} \\
&\approx 0.142
\end{aligned}
$$

This value of $F_n \ll 0.4$ confirms the ship operates firmly in the **displacement regime** — consistent with a tanker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Note:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s exactly (defined by the international nautical mile of **1,852 m** per hour).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Displacement from Principal Dimensions

### Step 1 — Definition of Block Coefficient

By definition, the block coefficient is the ratio of the **displaced volume** of the hull to the volume of the **rectangular block** defined by $L \times B \times T$:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\nabla$

Rearranging:

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$$

### Step 3 — Convert volume to mass (displacement)

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$$

where $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ tonnes/m³ (seawater) or **1.025 × 10³ kg/m³**.

### Step 4 — Worked Example

A bulk carrier has $L = 200$ m, $B = 32$ m, $T = 12$ m, and $C_B = 0.78$. Find the displacement in tonnes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= 0.78 \times 200 \times 32 \times 12 \\
&= 0.78 \times 76800 \\
&= 59904 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 1.025 \times 59904 \\
&= 61401.6 \text{ tonnes} \\
&\approx 61{,}400 \text{ DWT (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This places the vessel in the **Panamax** size class — a typical KTU textbook problem.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hull-Form Regime from Speed-Length Ratio

The **speed-length ratio** $\frac{V}{\sqrt{L}}$ is the traditional naval-architecture discriminant. Let us derive the threshold for **planing regime**.

### Step 1 — Definition of planing threshold

Planing begins when the dynamic lift force equals the weight of the vessel. Empirically (from Savitsky's planing theory, 1964), this occurs when:

$$\frac{V}{\sqrt{L}} \geq 1.2 \text{ (in SI units)}$$

### Step 2 — Cross-check via Froude number

For the same regime:

$$F_n \geq \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{g}} \approx \frac{1.2}{3.132} \approx 0.383$$

A more conservative threshold is $F_n \approx 0.4$, which is the standard KTU demarcation.

### Step 3 — Worked classification

For a **fast ferry** with $L = 40$ m, $V = 25$ knots:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 25 \times 0.5144 = 12.86 \text{ m/s} \\
F_n &= \frac{12.86}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 40}} \\
&= \frac{12.86}{\sqrt{392.4}} \\
&= \frac{12.86}{19.81} \\
&\approx 0.649
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $F_n > 0.6$, the vessel is in the **planing regime** — confirming it must use a planing or semi-displacement hull form.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Question Pattern Recognition

| If the Question Mentions… | Expected Answer Category | KTU Mark Weight |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| "VLCC, Suezmax, Panamax" | Tanker / Bulker size classes | 3–7 marks |
| "Bulk carrier, hatches, tweendecks" | Dry-bulk cargo | 3–7 marks |
| "Aircraft carrier, CODOG, AEGIS" | Naval warship | 3–7 marks |
| "Membrane-type, Moss-type, $-163^\circ C$" | LNG carrier | 3–7 marks |
| "Two hulls, slender, high stability" | Catamaran / SWATH | 3–7 marks |
| "$F_n > 0.6$" | Planing hull | 3–7 marks |
| "Foils lift hull clear of water" | Hydrofoil | 3 marks |
| "DWT = $\nabla_{loaded} - \nabla_{light}$" | Tonnage definition | 7 marks |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Ship Types (Block Architecture)

The following **Mermaid Block Diagram** captures the complete KTU 2024 taxonomy of ship types, organized as a hierarchical functional architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["SHIP TAXONOMY"]
    rootB["BY PURPOSE"]
    rootC["BY HULL FORM"]
    rootD["BY PROPULSION"]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC
    rootA --> rootD

    rootB --> p1["MERCHANT"]
    rootB --> p2["PASSENGER"]
    rootB --> p3["NAVAL"]
    rootB --> p4["SPECIAL"]

    p1 --> m1["General Cargo"]
    p1 --> m2["Bulk Carrier"]
    p1 --> m3["Container"]
    p1 --> m4["Tanker"]
    p1 --> m5["Ro-Ro"]
    p1 --> m6["Reefer"]

    m4 --> t1["Crude VLCC ULCC"]
    m4 --> t2["Product Aframax"]
    m4 --> t3["LNG LPG"]
    m4 --> t4["Chemical"]

    p2 --> pa1["Cruise Liner"]
    p2 --> pa2["Ferry"]
    p2 --> pa3["Ocean Liner"]

    p3 --> n1["Carrier"]
    p3 --> n2["Destroyer DDG"]
    p3 --> n3["Frigate FFG"]
    p3 --> n4["Submarine SSN SSK"]
    p3 --> n5["Corvette"]

    p4 --> s1["Tug"]
    p4 --> s2["Fishing"]
    p4 --> s3["Dredger"]
    p4 --> s4["Research"]
    p4 --> s5["Icebreaker"]
    p4 --> s6["Cable Layer"]

    rootC --> h1["Displacement"]
    rootC --> h2["Semi-Displacement"]
    rootC --> h3["Planing"]
    rootC --> h4["Hydrofoil"]
    rootC --> h5["Catamaran"]
    rootC --> h6["SWATH"]
    rootC --> h7["Trimaran"]
    rootC --> h8["Air Cushion"]

    rootD --> pr1["Diesel 2-Stroke Slow"]
    rootD --> pr2["Diesel Medium Speed"]
    rootD --> pr3["Steam Turbine"]
    rootD --> pr4["Gas Turbine COGAG CODOG"]
    rootD --> pr5["Nuclear PWR"]
    rootD --> pr6["Hybrid Diesel-Electric"]
    rootD --> pr7["Sail-Assisted"]
```

## 4.2 Hull-Form Regime Decision Flowchart

This Mermaid flowchart shows the **decision logic** for selecting a hull form based on the Froude Number — a direct application of KTU Section 2.2:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["OPERATING SPEED V AND LENGTH L KNOWN"]
    calcA["COMPUTE FR = V OVER SQRT G L"]
    decA{"FR LESS THAN 0.4"}

    decA -- YES --> typeA1["DISPLACEMENT HULL"]
    decA -- NO --> decB{"FR BETWEEN 0.4 AND 0.6"}

    decB -- YES --> typeB1["SEMI-DISPLACEMENT HULL"]
    decB -- NO --> decC{"FR BETWEEN 0.6 AND 1.0"}

    decC -- YES --> typeC1["PLANING HULL"]
    decC -- NO --> decD{"MULTI HULL DESIGN"}

    decD -- YES --> typeD1["CATAMARAN OR SWATH OR TRIMARAN"]
    decD -- NO --> decE{"FOIL LIFT BEARING"}

    decE -- YES --> typeE1["HYDROFOIL"]
    decE -- NO --> typeF1["AIR CUSHION VEHICLE HOVERCRAFT"]

    typeA1 --> exA["TANKERS BULKERS CONTAINERS CARGO SHIPS"]
    typeB1 --> exB["PATROL BOATS FAST FERRIES"]
    typeC1 --> exC["SPEEDBOATS RACING CRAFT"]
    typeD1 --> exD["FAST FERRIES NAVAL"]
    typeE1 --> exE["HIGH SPEED PASSENGER FERRIES"]
    typeF1 --> exF["MILITARY HOVERCRAFT"]
```

## 4.3 Tanker Size Class Hierarchy (Schematic)

This diagram shows the relative size classification of oil tankers, which is a **high-frequency KTU 2024 question**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    smallest["PRODUCT TANKER 10K TO 60K DWT"]
    aframax["AFRAMAX 80K TO 120K DWT"]
    suezmax["SUEZMAX 120K TO 200K DWT"]
    vlcc["VLCC 200K TO 320K DWT"]
    ulcc["ULCC 320K PLUS DWT RARE TODAY"]

    smallest --> aframax
    aframax --> suezmax
    suezmax --> vlcc
    vlcc --> ulcc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "**PAUL**" — **P**roduct → **A**framax → **U**LCC is wrong. Correct order small to large: **P-A-S-U-V-U** (Product → Aframax → Suezmax → (ULCC) → VLCC) — easiest to recall as: *Small to Large = Product < Aframax < Suezmax < VLCC < ULCC*.

## 4.4 Bulk Carrier Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    handy["HANDYSIZE 15K TO 35K DWT"]
    max["HANDYMAX SUPRAMAX 35K TO 60K DWT"]
    panamax["PANAMAX 60K TO 80K DWT"]
    cape["CAPESIZE 100K TO 200K DWT"]
    vloc["VLOC 200K PLUS DWT"]

    handy --> max
    max --> panamax
    panamax --> cape
    cape --> vloc
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canal Constraints:** Panamax vessels fit the **original Panama Canal locks (294 m × 32.3 m)**. Capesize vessels **cannot transit Suez or Panama** and must go around the Cape of Good Hope or Cape Horn.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Model answers are provided with the precise depth expected by a KTU board examiner for 3-mark questions.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Classify ships by hull form. Explain the displacement and planing regimes with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ships are classified by hull form based on the **Froude Number ($F_n$)** and the dominant hydrodynamic mechanism supporting the vessel.

1. **Displacement Hull:** The hull is fully supported by buoyancy at all speeds. Operates at $F_n < 0.4$. Examples: oil tankers, bulk carriers, container ships, general cargo ships. The hull moves water aside as it advances and does not rise out of the water.

2. **Planing Hull:** At higher speeds ($F_n > 0.6$), hydrodynamic lift generated by the planing surface becomes the dominant support mechanism. The hull rises partially out of the water, dramatically reducing wetted area and frictional resistance. Examples: speedboats, racing craft, military patrol boats.

3. Other regimes include **semi-displacement** ($0.4 < F_n < 0.6$), **hydrofoil**, **catamaran**, and **air-cushion vehicle**, which are intermediate or special cases.

*(Examiner's note: Any 3 hull forms with correct Froude range and one example each = 3 marks. Diagram optional but recommended.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**What is an LNG carrier? Mention the two main types of containment systems used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) carrier** is a specialized tanker designed to transport natural gas in its liquid form at approximately **$-163^\circ C$** and near-atmospheric pressure.

The two main containment systems are:

1. **Moss-Type (Spherical):** Uses independent self-supporting aluminium or 9%-nickel steel spherical tanks protruding above the deck. Each sphere is independent, offering high safety and partial-filling tolerance. Used in carriers above 70,000 m³.

2. **Membrane-Type:** Uses a thin primary barrier (stainless steel or Invar alloy) supported by the ship's hull insulation. More compact, with **higher cargo containment efficiency (~95–98%)** and lower construction cost. Two commercial systems: **GTT Mark III** and **No 96**.

*(Examiner's note: Mentioning the temperature $-163^\circ C$ and both system names correctly fetches full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**, Part B carries **14 marks** with sub-parts at **7 marks each**. Students may answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full internal choice).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) Classify merchant ships by cargo type, listing the main categories and giving one example of a vessel type in each category. Explain briefly the structural features of a VLCC and a Panamax bulk carrier. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Classification of merchant ships by cargo type:**

| Category | Vessel Type | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Liquid bulk** | Crude oil tanker | VLCC, Suezmax |
| **Liquid bulk (gas)** | LNG / LPG carrier | Moss-type LNG carrier |
| **Dry bulk** | Bulk carrier | Capesize, Panamax |
| **Container** | Cellular container ship | Post-Panamax (24,000+ TEU) |
| **General / Break-bulk** | General cargo ship | Multi-deck tweendecker |
| **Wheeled / Ro-Ro** | Ro-Ro, PCTC | Car carrier |
| **Refrigerated** | Reefer | Banana reefer |

*(1 mark for correct classification table)*

**Structural features of a VLCC (7 marks total — 3 marks for VLCC + 3 for Panamax + 1 for synthesis):**

A **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** is a double-hull oil tanker in the 200,000–320,000 DWT range, typically 330 m long, 58 m beam, and 20+ m draft. Key structural features:
- **Double hull** (mandatory under **MARPOL Annex I** since 2010 phase-in).
- **Engine room** located aft for safety and cargo space.
- **Inert gas system (IGS)** to inert cargo tanks, preventing explosion.
- **Segregated ballast tanks (SBT)** in double-bottom and wing tanks.
- **COW (Crude Oil Washing)** system to clean tanks with cargo instead of water.
- **Slamming-resistant bow** with high freeboard.
- **Heavy ballast capacity** — VLCCs carry up to 100,000 tonnes of ballast.

**Structural features of a Panamax bulk carrier** (60,000–80,000 DWT, max dimensions ~225 m × 32.3 m to fit old Panama locks):
- **Single deck** with large hatches.
- **Tweendecks** (removable) for grain/coal flexibility.
- **Hopper tanks** in double-bottom for ballast.
- **Topside tanks** and **side tanks** for stability.
- **Engine room** typically aft.
- **Cargo cranes** or shore-loader discharge.
- **Strengthened for hold loading** (high local loads on hatch covers).

*(1 mark for synthesis/closure statement connecting both to IMO regulations)*

---

**(b) A bulk carrier has principal dimensions L = 220 m, B = 32.2 m, draft T = 13.5 m, and block coefficient $C_B = 0.81$. Calculate the displacement in tonnes. If the lightship weight is 12,500 tonnes, find the Deadweight Tonnage (DWT). Take $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute displaced volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \\
&= 0.81 \times 220 \times 32.2 \times 13.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute stepwise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
220 \times 32.2 &= 7084 \text{ m}^2 \\
7084 \times 13.5 &= 95{,}634 \text{ m}^3 \\
0.81 \times 95{,}634 &= 77{,}463.54 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\nabla \approx 77{,}463.5$ m³. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Convert to mass (displacement)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \rho_{sw} \times \nabla \\
&= 1.025 \times 77{,}463.5 \\
&= 79{,}400.1 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the displacement is **$\Delta \approx 79{,}400$ tonnes** at the loaded draft. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Compute DWT**

By definition:

$$DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWT &= 79{,}400 - 12{,}500 \\
&= 66{,}900 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Interpretation and conclusion**

The vessel has a cargo capacity of **66,900 DWT**, placing it in the **Panamax bulk carrier** category (60,000–80,000 DWT). The size is constrained by the Panama Canal lock dimensions. *(2 marks for interpretation + correct units throughout)*

**Final Answer:** $\Delta = 79{,}400$ t; $DWT = 66{,}900$ t.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a) What is the Froude Number? Explain its significance in classifying ships by hull form. With neat sketches (block diagrams), describe the displacement, semi-displacement, and planing hull forms. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**

The **Froude Number ($F_n$)** is a dimensionless number that compares inertial forces to gravitational forces in free-surface flow around a ship:

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$$

where $V$ is ship speed (m/s), $g$ is acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²), and $L$ is waterline length (m).

**Significance in hull-form classification (2 marks):**

The Froude number determines the **dominant resistance regime** and therefore the **type of hull** best suited for the operating speed. It is the key discriminant between buoyancy-supported and lift-supported vessels. Higher $F_n$ means wave resistance dominates, requiring finer hulls or planning surfaces.

**Hull-form descriptions (4 marks — distributed across the three forms):**

- **Displacement Hull** ($F_n < 0.4$): Hull is always in contact with water; buoyancy alone supports weight. Wavy stern, full midsection. Examples: tankers, bulkers.
- **Semi-Displacement Hull** ($0.4 < F_n < 0.6$): Transitional regime; dynamic lift begins to assist buoyancy. Higher deadrise angle, slightly finer entry.
- **Planing Hull** ($F_n > 0.6$): Hydrodynamic lift raises the hull clear of water at high speed. Hard-chine hull, flat or V-bottom, small wetted area. Used in speedboats and racing craft.

*(The KTU examiner expects a labelled block diagram or schematic for at least one of the three forms. Without a sketch, deduct 0.5–1 mark.)*

---

**(b) A naval frigate has principal dimensions L = 130 m, B = 14.5 m, T = 5.0 m. The vessel operates at a service speed of 28 knots. Determine: (i) the Froude Number, (ii) the hull-form regime, and (iii) the speed-length ratio. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $L = 130$ m, $B = 14.5$ m, $T = 5.0$ m, $V = 28$ knots

**Step 1 — Convert speed to SI units**

$$
V = 28 \times 0.5144 = 14.4032 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Compute Froude Number**

$$
F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}} = \frac{14.4032}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 130}}
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
\sqrt{9.81 \times 130} = \sqrt{1275.3} = 35.71
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_n = \frac{14.4032}{35.71} = 0.403
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Identify hull-form regime**

Since $F_n = 0.403$ lies in the range $0.4 < F_n < 0.6$, the vessel operates in the **semi-displacement regime**. This is typical for fast frigates, which need both good seakeeping and high speed. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Speed-length ratio**

Using V in knots and L in feet (1 m = 3.281 ft):

$$
L = 130 \times 3.281 = 426.5 \text{ ft}
$$

$$
\frac{V}{\sqrt{L}} = \frac{28}{\sqrt{426.5}} = \frac{28}{20.65} = 1.356
$$

Alternatively, in SI units:

$$
\frac{V}{\sqrt{L}} = \frac{14.4032}{\sqrt{130}} = \frac{14.4032}{11.402} = 1.263 \text{ (SI)}
$$

*(2 marks — accept either SI or Imperial form; clearly state which convention)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistakes Students Make (Examiner's Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing knots with metres (or vice versa) in $\frac{V}{\sqrt{L}}$ — the dimensionless form $\frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ always uses SI; the naval form $\frac{V}{\sqrt{L}}$ requires **knots & feet** OR a clear statement that the ratio is in SI.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert knots to m/s** before computing $F_n$ — this is the most common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Stating only one hull form** when 3 are asked — answer must list all three.
> 4. **Wrong tank type in LNG question** — confusing "Moss" with "Membrane" is a typical 1-mark loss.
> 5. **No sketch in hull-form question** — KTU 2024 examiner pattern deducts 0.5–1 mark for missing schematic.
> 6. **DWT vs. Displacement confusion** — DWT is *cargo weight*, not *total mass*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1, Types of Ships**

- **Ships are classified along THREE axes:** By purpose, by hull form, and by propulsion.
- **By purpose**, the main categories are: **Merchant, Passenger, Naval, and Special**.
- **Merchant sub-categories to memorize:** General Cargo, Bulk Carrier, Container, Tanker, Ro-Ro, Reefer.
- **Tanker size classes (small → large):** Product → Aframax → Suezmax → VLCC → ULCC. (Note: **Suezmax fits the Suez Canal**, **Panamax fits old Panama locks**.)
- **Bulk carrier size classes:** Handysize → Handymax/Supramax → Panamax → Capesize → VLOC. **Capesize vessels cannot transit Suez or Panama.**
- **LNG carriers** operate at **$-163^\circ C$** with **Moss-type (spherical)** or **Membrane-type (Mark III / No 96)** containment.
- **Chemical tankers** are IMO **Type 1, 2, or 3** based on hazard, with **stainless steel tanks** and **double hulls**.
- **Naval ship types:** Carrier, Destroyer (DDG), Frigate (FFG), Corvette, Submarine (SSN = nuclear, SSK = diesel, SSBN = nuclear ballistic).
- **Naval propulsion codes:** **COGAG** (Combined Gas and Gas), **CODOG** (Combined Diesel or Gas), **CODAG** (Combined Diesel and Gas).
- **Hull form regimes** are determined by the **Froude Number** $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$:
  - $F_n < 0.4$ → **Displacement**
  - $0.4 < F_n < 0.6$ → **Semi-Displacement**
  - $F_n > 0.6$ → **Planing**
  - **Hydrofoils, Catamarans, SWATH, Air-Cushion Vehicles** are special high-speed forms.
- **SWATH (Small Waterplane Area Twin Hull):** Slender submerged struts, excellent seakeeping, low payload.
- **Catamaran vs. SWATH:** Catamaran has two full slender hulls partially above water; SWATH has only thin struts below.
- **Ice-class notation:** Finnish-Swedish (FSIC) and **Polar Class (PC 1–7)** per IMO Polar Code.
- **Classification societies to know:** **IRS** (India), **Lloyd's Register** (UK), **DNV** (Norway-Germany merged), **ABS** (USA), **BV** (France).
- **Key tonnage definitions:** **GT** = enclosed volume in m³/2.83; **DWT** = cargo + stores + crew + fuel = $\Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$; **NRT** = GT minus non-revenue spaces.
- **Block coefficient range:** Tankers $\sim 0.80$–$0.85$ (full), container ships $\sim 0.55$–$0.65$ (fine).
- **Displacement formula:** $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ and $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$ with $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³.
- **Mandatory regulations to mention:** **SOLAS**, **MARPOL Annex I** (oil), **MARPOL Annex VI** (air), **STCW** (crew), **Polar Code**, **BC Code** (bulk cargoes).
- **High-yield numerical patterns:** (1) Find displacement from $C_B, L, B, T$. (2) Find DWT given lightship. (3) Find $F_n$ and classify hull. (4) Compute speed-length ratio with unit conversion.
- **Unit conversions to memorize:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s; 1 m = 3.281 ft; 1 GT = 100 ft³; 1 nautical mile = 1852 m.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terms and Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Terms and Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Ship Geometry & Naval Architecture Terminology

Welcome to the foundational language of **Naval Architecture**. Before we can analyze how a ship floats, moves, or carries cargo, we must first agree upon a standardized vocabulary that naval architects, marine engineers, and classification societies (like **IRS – Indian Register of Shipping**, **Lloyd's Register**, **ABS – American Bureau of Shipping**) use worldwide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Terms and Definitions" Matter in KTU Examinations:**
> Module 1 is the **gateway module** of PCNST205. Almost every numerical problem in Modules 2–5 (Hydrostatics, Stability, Resistance, Propulsion) requires the student to correctly **identify**, **define**, and **apply** the geometry and form coefficients introduced here. A single misidentified perpendicular or wrong coefficient definition can cascade into a 14-mark numerical error.

---

## 1.2 Ship Dimensions — The Big Four (L, B, D, T)

The geometry of every ship — from a fishing trawler to a **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** — is described by four principal linear dimensions.

### (a) Length (L)
A ship has *three different lengths*, and the examiner will deliberately try to confuse you between them.

| Symbol | Full Name | Definition | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Lpp** | Length Between Perpendiculars | Horizontal distance between the **Forward Perpendicular (FP)** and the **Aft Perpendicular (AP)**. | Used in all hydrostatic and resistance calculations. |
| **LOA** | Length Overall | Maximum length of the ship from the **most forward point of the bow** to the **most aft point of the stern** (including bulbous bow, rudder, etc.). | Used for berth allocation, canal transit, and registration. |
| **LWL** | Length on Waterline | Horizontal length of the hull at the **design waterline (DWL)**. | Used in resistance and form-effect estimations. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Think of `Lpp` as the *engineering length* — it excludes the curvy bow and stern overhangs. `LOA` is the *legal length* — the actual physical extent of the vessel. `LWL` is the *hydrodynamic length* — the length the water actually "sees" when the ship moves.

### (b) Breadth / Beam (B)
The **maximum horizontal width** of the ship measured **amidships** (at the middle station) at the **widest point** of the hull, usually at or near the **design waterline**.

> **Typical Units:** **Metres (m)** in SI; **Feet (ft)** in Imperial.

### (c) Depth (D)
The **vertical distance** measured from the **keel (base line)** to the **moulded deck** at the side (the **sheer line**), measured at **amidships**.

> **Key Distinction:** Depth is *not* the same as draft. Depth is a *fixed* structural dimension; draft is a *variable* operational quantity.

### (d) Draft / Draught (T)
The **vertical distance** from the **design waterline (DWL)** down to the **keel (base line)**. Since ships can be loaded or unloaded, **T changes constantly**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Freeboard (F):** $F = D - T$. It is the visible portion of the hull above water. A cargo ship with heavy cargo has **less freeboard** and is therefore *less safe* in rough seas — this is why **IMO Load Line Convention** regulates minimum freeboard.

---

## 1.3 The Perpendiculars and Reference Lines

To unambiguously describe any point on a ship, naval architects use a **3-D Cartesian coordinate system** with three mutually perpendicular reference planes.

| Reference | Plane | Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **X-axis** | Longitudinal (Base Line) | Horizontal, runs from **AP to FP** (forward is positive). |
| **Y-axis** | Transverse | Horizontal, runs **port to starboard** (starboard positive). |
| **Z-axis** | Vertical | Vertical, runs from **keel to main deck** (upward positive). |

### Critical Reference Lines
* **Base Line (BL):** A horizontal line running through the lowest point of the hull (usually the keel). All vertical measurements (Depth, Draft) are taken from this line.
* **Forward Perpendicular (FP):** A vertical line at the point where the **design waterline** meets the **forward side of the stem** (the front face of the ship).
* **Aft Perpendicular (AP):** A vertical line passing through the **after side of the rudder post** (or the centreline of the rudder stock for ships with no rudder post).
* **Amidships (½ Lpp):** The midpoint between FP and AP. This is the **station number zero** for hull form measurements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often wrongly assume the FP is at the very tip of the bow. It is **NOT**. The FP is *at the waterline* — it is the intersection of DWL with the stem contour. The bow can extend *beyond* the FP (this overhang is what gives a ship a raked bow).

---

## 1.4 Displacement & Tonnage — "How Heavy is the Ship?"

This is one of the most confusing topics for beginners because the word **"tonnage"** actually has *three different meanings* in naval architecture.

### (a) Displacement (Δ)
The **weight of water displaced** by the ship's hull. By **Archimedes' Principle**, this equals the **weight of the ship** (including cargo, fuel, crew, etc.).

$$\boxed{\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla}$$

where $\rho$ is the density of water (seawater ≈ **1025 kg/m³**; freshwater ≈ **1000 kg/m³**) and $\nabla$ is the **volume of displacement** (submerged volume of the hull in m³).

> Units: **tonnes (t)** or **metric tons (1000 kg)**. In Imperial: **Long Tons (LT)** = 1016 kg.

### (b) Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)
The **total weight** the ship can carry — cargo + fuel + fresh water + ballast + crew + provisions + stores.

$$DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$$

> **Lightship Weight:** Weight of the empty ship with machinery, equipment, and ballast in operational condition but **no cargo or consumables**.

### (c) Gross Tonnage (GT) & Net Tonnage (NT)
These are **volume-based regulatory measures** (not weights) defined by the **IMO Tonnage Convention 1969**, used for port dues and safety regulations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Despite the word "tonnage," GT and NT have **units of volume (m³)** expressed as a *unitless number*. They are *not* weights and *cannot* be compared directly with DWT.

* **GT:** Measure of the *total enclosed volume* of the ship.
* **NT:** GT minus deductions for non-revenue spaces (crew quarters, machinery, navigation spaces). Used for **port charges**.

---

## 1.5 Form Coefficients — "How 'Fat' is the Ship?"

Real ships are not rectangular boxes. The **form coefficients** (also called *form factors* or *shape coefficients*) are *dimensionless ratios* that describe how the hull's shape compares to an idealized primitive shape (rectangle, triangle, or prism).

| Coefficient | Ratio | Geometric Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Block Coefficient (Cb)** | $\nabla / (L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | How "blocky" or "full" the hull is. |
| **Midship Coefficient (Cm)** | $A_m / (B \cdot T)$ | Fullness of the *midship section* only. |
| **Prismatic Coefficient (Cp)** | $\nabla / (A_m \cdot L_{pp})$ | How the volume is distributed along the length. |
| **Waterplane Coefficient (Cwp)** | $A_w / (L_{wl} \cdot B)$ | Fullness of the *waterplane area*. |

Where:
* $\nabla$ = Volume of displacement
* $A_m$ = Area of the **midship section** (cross-section at amidships below DWL)
* $A_w$ = Area of the **waterplane** (hull's horizontal slice at DWL)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** A **tanker** (full-form ship) has a *high* Cb (~0.80–0.85) — bulky, carries maximum cargo. A **destroyer** (fine-form ship) has a *low* Cb (~0.40–0.55) — sleek, built for speed. A mathematical identity links them: **$C_b = C_m \times C_p$**.

---

## 1.6 Stability-Related Definitions

### (a) Centre of Gravity (G)
The point where the **entire weight** of the ship (W = mg) is assumed to act vertically downward.

### (b) Centre of Buoyancy (B)
The point where the **buoyancy force** ($\rho g \nabla$) is assumed to act vertically upward. It is the **centroid of the submerged volume** of the hull.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** As a ship heels (rolls), the shape of the submerged volume changes, so **B moves**. G, however, only moves when cargo is shifted, added, or removed.

### (c) Metacentre (M)
The point where the **vertical line through the new centre of buoyancy (B')** (when the ship is slightly inclined) intersects the **original vertical centreline** of the ship.

### (d) Metacentric Height (GM)
The **vertical distance** between G and M. This single number (in metres) tells you *how stiff* or *tender* a ship is.

> **Rule of Thumb:** $GM > 0 \Rightarrow$ **Stable**. $GM < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Unstable (capsizes)**. $GM = 0 \Rightarrow$ **Neutral**.

### (e) Righting Lever (GZ)
The **horizontal distance** between G and the new vertical buoyancy line. It is the actual *arm* producing the righting moment.

$$\text{Righting Moment} = W \times GZ$$

---

## 1.7 Hull Form & Appendage Terminology

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Stem** | The forward-most structural member (the "edge" of the bow). |
| **Stern** | The after-most structural region. |
| **Bulbous Bow** | A bulb-shaped protrusion below the FP that reduces wave-making resistance. |
| **Sheer** | The upward curve of the deck line (profile) towards bow and stern. |
| **Camber** | The upward curvature of the deck *transversely* (athwartships). |
| **Flare** | The outward outward slope of the hull above the waterline (provides reserve buoyancy). |
| **Tumblehome** | The inward slope of the hull above the waterline (opposite of flare). |
| **Bilge** | The curved region where the **bottom (flat of keel)** meets the **side shell**. |
| **Chine** | A hard, sharp corner where the bottom meets the side (typical of planing/semi-planing hulls). |
| **Transom** | A flat, vertical plate closing the stern (typical of small craft, tugboats). |
| **Rudder** | A movable foil at the stern for directional control. |
| **Superstructure** | Decked structure on the freeboard deck (not the hull). |
| **Bulkhead** | A vertical transverse or longitudinal watertight partition. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Floating Rectangle:**
> Imagine a **rectangular shoebox** floating in a bathtub. Now tilt it slightly to one side. The waterline shifts, the submerged volume changes shape, and a *new* centroid of the submerged part appears. The distance from the box's centre of gravity to this "metacentre" determines whether the shoebox rights itself (like a cruise ship) or capsizes (like an upside-down cup). The **GM** is exactly that distance for a real ship.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Hierarchy of Ship Dimensions

To avoid confusion, the KTU examiner expects students to understand that ship dimensions are organized in a **logical hierarchy of operations**:

### Step 1 — Identify the Reference Frame
Every measurement begins with three orthogonal planes: **Base Plane** (keel), **Centreline Plane** (longitudinal vertical), and **Transverse Plane** (athwartships vertical). The **midship section** is the intersection of the centreline plane with a transverse plane at amidships.

### Step 2 — Apply the "Why" Behind Each Length
* Why three different lengths? Because each is used in a *different engineering domain*:
  * **Lpp** → used in **hydrostatic calculations** (displacement, form coefficients) because it excludes the dynamic bow and stern.
  * **LOA** → used in **regulatory and logistic** calculations (berthing, canal transit, towing).
  * **LWL** → used in **resistance and powering** calculations (Friction, Froude number).

### Step 3 — The Draft Triad
* **Forward Draft (Tf)** and **Aft Draft (Ta)** may differ (when the ship is **trimmed** by unequal loading).
* **Mean Draft (Tm)** = $(T_f + T_a) / 2$ is what is used in hydrostatic tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Moulded" vs. "Extreme" Distinction:**
> "Moulded" dimensions refer to the *theoretical* surface defined by the ship's lines plan (excluding plate thickness). "Extreme" dimensions include the *outermost* plating. KTU problems almost always use **moulded dimensions**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All tables below use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the raw pipe character `|` inside cells to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Range | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Length Between Perpendiculars | $L_{pp}$ | Distance from FP to AP | 50 – 400 | m |
| 2 | Length Overall | $L_{OA}$ | Extreme fore-aft distance | $L_{pp} + \text{overhangs}$ | m |
| 3 | Length on Waterline | $L_{WL}$ | Length of hull at DWL | $\approx L_{pp}$ for fine ships | m |
| 4 | Breadth (Moulded) | $B$ | Max width at amidships | 0.10–0.20 of $L_{pp}$ | m |
| 5 | Depth (Moulded) | $D$ | Keel to sheer line at amidships | 0.10–0.20 of $L_{pp}$ | m |
| 6 | Draft (Design) | $T$ | Keel to DWL | Variable | m |
| 7 | Freeboard | $F$ | $F = D - T$ | 0.5 – 5 | m |
| 8 | Displacement | $\Delta$ | $\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | 500 – 600,000 | tonnes |
| 9 | Displacement Volume | $\nabla$ | Submerged volume of hull | — | m³ |
| 10 | Deadweight Tonnage | $DWT$ | $\Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{light}$ | — | tonnes |
| 11 | Block Coefficient | $C_b$ | $\nabla \;\vert\; (L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | 0.40 – 0.90 | — |
| 12 | Midship Coefficient | $C_m$ | $A_m \;\vert\; (B \cdot T)$ | 0.85 – 0.99 | — |
| 13 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_p$ | $\nabla \;\vert\; (A_m \cdot L_{pp})$ | 0.55 – 0.90 | — |
| 14 | Waterplane Coefficient | $C_{wp}$ | $A_w \;\vert\; (L_{WL} \cdot B)$ | 0.65 – 0.95 | — |
| 15 | Form Identity | — | $C_b = C_m \cdot C_p$ | Always true | — |
| 16 | Righting Moment | $RM$ | $RM = W \cdot GZ$ | — | kN·m |
| 17 | Displacement → Weight | $W$ | $W = \Delta \cdot g$ | — | kN |
| 18 | Buoyancy Force | $F_B$ | $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | — | kN |
| 19 | Equilibrium | — | $W = F_B$ when floating | — | — |
| 20 | Trim | — | $Trim = T_a - T_f$ | — | m |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Definitions

### (a) Ship Design Phase
* **Cb** is the *first* parameter a naval architect selects. It determines resistance, powering, and cargo capacity simultaneously.
* A higher Cb → more cargo but higher resistance → more fuel.

### (b) Classification & Safety
* **Freeboard** is enforced by **IMO Load Line Rules** to ensure reserve buoyancy in rough seas.
* **DWT** is the basis for charter parties and insurance valuations (a $50,000/day VLCC has DWT ≈ 200,000–320,000 t).

### (c) Port & Logistics
* **LOA**, **B**, and **T (max)** determine which **Panamax / Suezmax / Capesize** category the vessel belongs to.
* **GT/NT** determine port dues at every berth the ship visits.

### (d) Stability & Seakeeping
* **GM** is the *primary stability indicator* used by the ship's Master and the Classification Society surveyor.
* **GZ vs. Heel Angle curve** is the basis of the **IMO intact stability code** criteria.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Form Coefficient

### Block Coefficient (Cb)
* **Why does it exist?** Because comparing two ships of equal $L \times B \times T$ but different shapes, their *volumes* differ. Cb captures this shape efficiency.
* **Tanker** (Cb ≈ 0.85): Maximizes cargo volume.
* **Warship** (Cb ≈ 0.50): Minimizes resistance.
* **Cargo ship** (Cb ≈ 0.70): Balanced compromise.

### Prismatic Coefficient (Cp)
* **Why does it exist?** Two ships can have the *same* Cb (same total volume) but distribute it differently. Cp tells you whether the volume is concentrated at midships (Cp low → "full midship") or distributed evenly (Cp high → "fine ends").

### Waterplane Coefficient (Cwp)
* **Why does it exist?** A full waterplane (high Cwp) gives good initial stability (high BM) but poor resistance performance. A fine waterplane (low Cwp) reduces wave-making resistance but reduces stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hidden Identity:** For fine ships (where $L_{WL} \approx L_{pp}$), $C_b \approx C_{wp} \times C_p$ approximately. But the *exact* identity is always $C_b = C_m \times C_p$.

---

## 2.5 Tonnage — The Three Distinct Concepts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Student Pitfall #1 — Tonnage Confusion:** Many KTU answer sheets erroneously interchange DWT and GT. They are *fundamentally different*:
> * **DWT** = a *weight* (in tonnes, related to cargo capacity).
> * **GT** = a *volume-derived regulatory number* (unitless, used for port dues).
> * **Displacement (Δ)** = a *weight* (the actual weight of the ship at any instant).

### Why Three Concepts?
1. **Δ (Displacement)** is for the **naval architect** (design, stability).
2. **DWT** is for the **commercial operator** (cargo, charter).
3. **GT/NT** is for the **regulator and port authority** (safety, dues).

This three-tier system is a deliberate engineering convention to separate technical, commercial, and regulatory concerns.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Displacement Equation from Archimedes' Principle

We start from the fundamental principle of floating bodies.

### Step 1 — Statement of Archimedes' Principle
> "A floating body displaces a volume of fluid whose weight equals the total weight of the body."

Mathematically, the **buoyancy force** $F_B$ equals the weight of the displaced fluid.

$$F_B = m_{fluid} \cdot g = (\rho \cdot \nabla) \cdot g$$

where:
* $\rho$ = density of the fluid (water) in **kg/m³**
* $\nabla$ = volume of displaced fluid (= submerged volume of the hull) in **m³**
* $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

### Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition (Floating)
For a ship floating in **static equilibrium**, the net vertical force must be zero. Therefore, the downward **weight (W)** equals the upward **buoyancy (F_B)**.

$$W = F_B$$

### Step 3 — Expressing Weight in Terms of Displacement
The weight of the ship is the product of its total mass and gravity.

$$W = m_{ship} \cdot g$$

By Archimedes' principle, the mass of the displaced water equals the mass of the ship.

$$m_{ship} = m_{fluid} = \rho \cdot \nabla$$

### Step 4 — Substituting Back
Substituting $m_{ship} = \rho \cdot \nabla$ into the weight equation:

$$W = (\rho \cdot \nabla) \cdot g$$

And since $\Delta$ is defined as the *mass* of the displaced water (or the *mass* of the ship):

$$\boxed{\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla \quad \text{(in mass units, e.g., tonnes)}}$$

Or, in *weight* units:

$$\boxed{W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla \quad \text{(in force units, e.g., kN)}}$$

### Step 5 — Unit Consistency Check
Let's verify the dimensions.

$$\Delta [\text{tonnes}] = \rho [\text{tonnes/m}^3] \times \nabla [\text{m}^3]$$

For seawater with $\rho = 1.025$ tonnes/m³ and a submerged volume $\nabla = 100{,}000$ m³:

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 100{,}000 = 102{,}500 \text{ tonnes}$$

This is consistent with a mid-sized cargo ship. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Form Coefficient Identity ($C_b = C_m \cdot C_p$)

This identity is one of the most frequently tested derivations in KTU Module 1.

### Step 1 — Recall the Definitions

$$C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

$$C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T}$$

$$C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}}$$

### Step 2 — Multiply $C_m$ and $C_p$

$$C_m \cdot C_p = \left(\frac{A_m}{B \cdot T}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Cancel the Common Term $A_m$

$$C_m \cdot C_p = \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T} \cdot \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}} = \frac{\nabla}{B \cdot T \cdot L_{pp}}$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Result

$$\frac{\nabla}{B \cdot T \cdot L_{pp}} = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T} = C_b$$

### Step 5 — Final Identity

$$\boxed{C_b = C_m \cdot C_p}$$

> **QED** — Quod Erat Demonstrandum (proven). This identity holds for *every* ship at *every* draft.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style 3-Mark Problem

> **Problem:** A ship has the following particulars:
> * Length between perpendiculars: $L_{pp} = 150$ m
> * Breadth: $B = 22$ m
> * Draft: $T = 8$ m
> * Block coefficient: $C_b = 0.72$
> * Midship coefficient: $C_m = 0.95$
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Displacement in tonnes, (b) Prismatic coefficient, (c) Volume of displacement, in seawater ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³).

### Part (a) — Displacement

$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$$

Substitute:

$$\nabla = 0.72 \times 150 \times 22 \times 8$$

$$\nabla = 0.72 \times 26{,}400 = 19{,}008 \text{ m}^3$$

Then:

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 19{,}008 = 19{,}483.2 \text{ tonnes}$$

### Part (b) — Prismatic Coefficient

From the identity $C_b = C_m \cdot C_p$:

$$C_p = \frac{C_b}{C_m} = \frac{0.72}{0.95} = 0.7579 \approx 0.758$$

### Part (c) — Volume of Displacement

Already calculated in (a):

$$\nabla = 19{,}008 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Result Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Volume of Displacement | $\nabla$ | 19,008 | m³ |
| Displacement | $\Delta$ | 19,483.2 | tonnes |
| Prismatic Coefficient | $C_p$ | 0.758 | — |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Stability-Related Quantities

> **Problem:** A ship of displacement $\Delta = 10{,}000$ tonnes has $KG = 6.5$ m and $KB = 3.0$ m. The transverse BM is $BM = 1.8$ m.
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Metacentric height GM, (b) Righting lever GZ at a heel angle of 8° (use small-angle approximation: $GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin\theta$).

### Part (a) — GM Calculation

The relationship between $KG$, $KB$, and $BM$ is:

$$KM = KB + BM$$

$$GM = KM - KG = (KB + BM) - KG$$

Substitute:

$$GM = (3.0 + 1.8) - 6.5 = 4.8 - 6.5 = -1.7 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative GM!** This ship is in a **statically unstable** condition. KTU problems often test whether students recognize this and can comment on it. A negative GM means the ship will capsize unless ballast is shifted to lower G.

### Part (b) — GZ at 8° Heel

Using the small-angle approximation:

$$GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin(8°) = -1.7 \times 0.1392 = -0.2366 \text{ m}$$

The **negative** sign confirms the ship is unstable (righting lever is in the *wrong* direction, i.e., a *capsizing* lever).

> **Real-World Lesson:** Naval architects must always ensure $GM > 0$ at all operational loading conditions. The **IMO intact stability code** requires a minimum GM of **0.15 m** for passenger ships.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Tonnage Comparison

| Parameter | DWT (Deadweight) | Δ (Displacement) | GT (Gross Tonnage) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type** | Weight (capacity) | Weight (actual) | Volume-based index |
| **Units** | Tonnes | Tonnes | Unitless |
| **Includes Cargo?** | Yes (max) | Yes (current) | No (volume only) |
| **Used By** | Commercial operator | Naval architect | Port authority |
| **Regulated By** | Charter parties | Physics (Archimedes) | IMO Tonnage Conv. 1969 |
| **Typical Order (Tanker)** | 200,000 t | 250,000 t | 80,000 (unitless) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference Plane System of a Ship

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **3-D orthogonal reference frame** that naval architects use to describe every point on a ship.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Z_Axis["Z-AXIS VERTICAL (Keel to Deck)"]
        A1[Base Line BL<br/>at the keel]
        A2[Design Waterline DWL<br/>at the load condition]
        A3[Sheer Line SL<br/>at the main deck]
    end

    subgraph Y_Axis["Y-AXIS TRANSVERSE (Port to Starboard)"]
        B1[Port Side<br/>Y negative]
        B2[Centreline CL<br/>Y zero]
        B3[Starboard Side<br/>Y positive]
    end

    subgraph X_Axis["X-AXIS LONGITUDINAL (AP to FP)"]
        C1[Aft Perpendicular AP<br/>X zero]
        C2[Amidships<br/>X equals half of Lpp]
        C3[Forward Perpendicular FP<br/>X equals Lpp]
    end

    Z_Axis --- Y_Axis
    Y_Axis --- X_Axis

    O[ORIGIN at AP<br/>on the centreline<br/>on the base line]

    O --> A1
    O --> A2
    O --> A3
    O --> B1
    O --> B2
    O --> B3
    O --> C1
    O --> C2
    O --> C3
```

---

The following block diagram shows the **hierarchical relationships** between the principal term groups in Module 1.


## 4.3 Functional Flow of a Form Coefficient Calculation

This diagram illustrates **how the form coefficients interact** during a typical hydrostatic analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hull Geometry Input<br/>Lpp B T Am Aw] --> B[Compute Volume of Displacement]
    B --> C[nabla equals Integral of cross sections]

    A --> D[Compute Block Coefficient]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Cb equals nabla over Lpp B T]

    A --> F[Compute Midship Coefficient]
    F --> G[Cm equals Am over B T]

    A --> H[Compute Waterplane Coefficient]
    H --> I[Cwp equals Aw over Lwl B]

    G --> J[Derive Prismatic Coefficient]
    E --> J
    J --> K[Cp equals Cb divided by Cm]

    K --> L[Validation Check<br/>Cb should equal Cm times Cp]
    E --> L
    G --> L
    L --> M[Final Hydrostatic Outputs]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
Define the following terms with one practical example each:
(i) **Length Between Perpendiculars (Lpp)**
(ii) **Breadth (B)**
(iii) **Depth (D)**

#### Model Answer:
* **(i) Length Between Perpendiculars (Lpp):** It is the horizontal distance measured along the design waterline between the **Forward Perpendicular (FP)** — defined as the vertical line through the point where the DWL meets the forward side of the stem — and the **Aft Perpendicular (AP)** — the vertical line through the after side of the rudder post. *Example:* For a Panamax bulk carrier, $L_{pp} = 229$ m.
* **(ii) Breadth (B):** The maximum horizontal width of the ship measured **amidships** at the **widest point** of the hull, usually at the design waterline. It is measured *moulded* (excluding plate thickness). *Example:* For the same Panamax bulk carrier, $B = 32.3$ m.
* **(iii) Depth (D):** The vertical distance measured at **amidships** from the **keel (base line)** to the **moulded deck line at the side (sheer line)**. *Example:* For the same vessel, $D = 19.9$ m.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
Differentiate between:
* (a) **Displacement (Δ)** and **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)**
* (b) **Block Coefficient (Cb)** and **Prismatic Coefficient (Cp)**

#### Model Answer:
* **(a) Δ vs. DWT:**
  * **Displacement (Δ)** is the *actual weight* of the ship at a given loading condition, equal to the weight of water displaced. It is **variable** (changes with loading) and is used by naval architects.
  * **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** is the *maximum weight* the ship can carry — cargo, fuel, water, stores, and crew. It is the difference between loaded and lightship displacement. It is used commercially.
* **(b) Cb vs. Cp:**
  * **Cb** is the ratio of the displaced volume to the volume of an enclosing rectangular box ($L_{pp} \times B \times T$). It measures the *overall fullness* of the hull.
  * **Cp** is the ratio of the displaced volume to the volume of a *prism* having the midship section area and length $L_{pp}$. It measures the *longitudinal distribution* of volume.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
(a) **[7 Marks]** Define the four principal form coefficients of a ship. State the mathematical relationship that connects them and prove it.
(b) **[7 Marks]** A ship has the following particulars:
* $L_{pp} = 120$ m, $B = 18$ m, $T = 6.5$ m
* $C_b = 0.70$, $C_m = 0.93$
* Density of seawater $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³

Calculate: (i) Volume of displacement, (ii) Displacement in tonnes, (iii) Prismatic coefficient, (iv) Area of the midship section.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Definitions and Proof:**

The four principal form coefficients are:

1. **Block Coefficient ($C_b$):** Ratio of the volume of displacement ($\nabla$) to the volume of a rectangular block having length $L_{pp}$, breadth $B$, and draft $T$.

$$C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

2. **Midship Coefficient ($C_m$):** Ratio of the area of the midship section ($A_m$) to the area of a rectangle of breadth $B$ and draft $T$.

$$C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T}$$

3. **Prismatic Coefficient ($C_p$):** Ratio of the volume of displacement ($\nabla$) to the volume of a prism having the midship area $A_m$ and length $L_{pp}$.

$$C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}}$$

4. **Waterplane Coefficient ($C_{wp}$):** Ratio of the waterplane area ($A_w$) at the design waterline to the area of a rectangle of length $L_{WL}$ and breadth $B$.

$$C_{wp} = \frac{A_w}{L_{WL} \cdot B}$$

**Proof of the identity $C_b = C_m \cdot C_p$:**

Multiplying the right-hand sides:

$$C_m \cdot C_p = \left(\frac{A_m}{B \cdot T}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}}\right)$$

The $A_m$ terms cancel:

$$C_m \cdot C_p = \frac{\nabla}{B \cdot T \cdot L_{pp}} = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T} = C_b$$

Therefore, $C_b = C_m \cdot C_p$. **QED.**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Volume of Displacement ($\nabla$):**

$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$$

$$\nabla = 0.70 \times 120 \times 18 \times 6.5$$

$$\nabla = 0.70 \times 14{,}040 = 9{,}828 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) Displacement ($\Delta$):**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 9{,}828$$

$$\Delta = 10{,}073.7 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(iii) Prismatic Coefficient ($C_p$):**

$$C_p = \frac{C_b}{C_m} = \frac{0.70}{0.93} = 0.7527 \approx 0.753$$

**(iv) Area of Midship Section ($A_m$):**

From $C_m = A_m / (B \cdot T)$:

$$A_m = C_m \cdot B \cdot T = 0.93 \times 18 \times 6.5 = 108.81 \text{ m}^2$$

**Result Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| $\nabla$ | 9,828 m³ |
| $\Delta$ | 10,073.7 tonnes |
| $C_p$ | 0.753 |
| $A_m$ | 108.81 m² |

---

### Question Choice B — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**Question B (14 Marks):**
(a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the terms **displacement**, **deadweight tonnage**, **lightship weight**, and **gross tonnage**. Why are different tonnage concepts needed in naval architecture?
(b) **[7 Marks]** With the aid of a sketch, define **Forward Perpendicular (FP)**, **Aft Perpendicular (AP)**, **Base Line (BL)**, and **Design Waterline (DWL)**. A ship of $L_{pp} = 200$ m, $B = 30$ m, $T = 12$ m has a block coefficient of 0.80. Find the displacement in tonnes (in seawater).

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Tonnage Concepts:**

* **Displacement ($\Delta$):** The actual weight of the ship at any instant, equal to the weight of the displaced water. Units: **tonnes**.
* **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT):** The maximum total weight of cargo, fuel, fresh water, ballast, crew, and stores that a ship can carry. Units: **tonnes**.
* **Lightship Weight ($\Delta_L$):** The weight of the empty ship with all permanent equipment, machinery, and operational ballast — but **without** cargo or consumables. Units: **tonnes**.
* **Gross Tonnage (GT):** A **unitless, volume-derived regulatory measure** under the **IMO Tonnage Convention (1969)**, used for port dues and safety regulations.

**Why different tonnage concepts?**
Different stakeholders require different measures:
1. **Naval architect** needs **displacement** for stability and design.
2. **Commercial operator** needs **DWT** for chartering and cargo planning.
3. **Port authority** needs **GT/NT** for charging port dues and applying safety regulations.
4. **Insurance** uses DWT to value the ship.

These concepts are *complementary, not interchangeable*.

---

**Part (b) — Reference Lines and Numerical Calculation:**

* **Base Line (BL):** A horizontal reference line running fore-aft through the *lowest point* of the hull, i.e., along the **keel**.
* **Forward Perpendicular (FP):** A vertical line at the point where the **design waterline (DWL)** meets the **forward side of the stem**.
* **Aft Perpendicular (AP):** A vertical line passing through the **after side of the rudder post** (or the centreline of the rudder stock for ships with no rudder post).
* **Design Waterline (DWL):** The waterline at the ship's **design draft**, used as the reference for all hydrostatic calculations.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $L_{pp} = 200$ m, $B = 30$ m, $T = 12$ m, $C_b = 0.80$, $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³

$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.80 \times 200 \times 30 \times 12$$

$$\nabla = 0.80 \times 72{,}000 = 57{,}600 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 57{,}600$$

$$\boxed{\Delta = 59{,}040 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Module 1 — Critical Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Interchanging Lpp, LOA, and LWL:** The KTU examiner will *deliberately* mix these in a 14-mark question. A 7-mark sub-part may be marked *zero* if the wrong length is used in the formula. **Always read the question's symbol carefully.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Density of Seawater:** By default, KTU problems assume **seawater with $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³** unless stated otherwise. Using 1.0 (freshwater) when the question says "seawater" costs 1 mark.
> 3. **DWT vs. Displacement Confusion:** DWT is **not** the same as displacement. DWT is the *capacity*; displacement is the *actual weight*. Saying "the ship's displacement is 50,000 DWT" will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Units of GT:** Writing GT in "tonnes" is wrong. GT is a **unitless regulatory number**.
> 5. **The $C_b = C_m \cdot C_p$ Identity:** In derivation questions, examiners check whether the student *cancels terms explicitly* (showing $A_m$ in numerator and denominator). A skipped cancellation step loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Freeboard vs. Depth:** Freeboard = D - T. Students often confuse the *visible* part of the hull (freeboard) with the *total* depth. Showing both with a clear sketch gains easy marks.
> 7. **The "Moulded" Specification:** If the problem states "moulded breadth," do not add plating thickness. If "extreme" is specified, then add plating. KTU defaults are usually **moulded**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> **Principal Dimensions:**
> * **Lpp** = FP to AP distance (engineering length).
> * **LOA** = extreme fore-aft length (legal/logistics length).
> * **LWL** = length at design waterline (hydrodynamic length).
> * **B** = maximum breadth at amidships.
> * **D** = depth, keel to sheer line (fixed).
> * **T** = draft, keel to DWL (variable).
> * **F = D − T** (freeboard).
>
> **Form Coefficients:**
> * **Cb** = ∇ / (Lpp · B · T) — overall fullness.
> * **Cm** = Am / (B · T) — midship fullness.
> * **Cp** = ∇ / (Am · Lpp) — longitudinal volume distribution.
> * **Cwp** = Aw / (LWL · B) — waterplane fullness.
> * **Key identity:** Cb = Cm · Cp (always).
> * Tanker Cb ≈ 0.80–0.85; warship Cb ≈ 0.40–0.55.
>
> **Tonnages:**
> * **Δ = ρ · ∇** (displacement, weight).
> * **DWT = Δloaded − Δlight** (cargo capacity, weight).
> * **GT / NT** = volume-based regulatory numbers (unitless).
> * **Lightship weight** = empty ship with machinery, no cargo.
>
> **Stability Nomenclature:**
> * **G** = centre of gravity (fixed unless loading changes).
> * **B** = centre of buoyancy (moves with heel).
> * **M** = metacentre (intersection of new buoyancy vertical with original centreline).
> * **GM** = KM − KG (metacentric height; must be > 0 for stability).
> * **GZ** = righting lever = horizontal distance from G to buoyancy vertical.
> * **Righting Moment = W × GZ**.
>
> **Hull Form Terms:**
> * **Stem** (forward edge), **Stern** (after region), **Bulbous bow** (sub-FP protrusion).
> * **Sheer** (longitudinal deck curvature), **Camber** (transverse deck curvature).
> * **Flare** (outward hull slope above WL), **Tumblehome** (inward above WL).
> * **Bilge** (bottom-to-side curve), **Chine** (hard corner), **Transom** (flat stern plate).
> * **Rudder** (steering foil), **Superstructure** (decked structure above freeboard deck), **Bulkhead** (vertical partition).
>
> **Universal Equations to Memorize:**
> * $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ (in mass units)
> * $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ (in force units)
> * $C_b = C_m \cdot C_p$
> * $RM = W \cdot GZ$
> * $GM = KB + BM - KG$
> * $F = D - T$
> * $DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Archimedes' Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Archimedes' Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Archimedes' Principle** states that *when a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid at rest, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.*
>
> Mathematically: **Buoyant Force ($F_B$) = Weight of Displaced Fluid = $\rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$**
>
> Where:
> - $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid ($kg/m^3$)
> - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity ($9.81\ m/s^2$)
> - $\nabla$ = Volume of fluid displaced ($m^3$) (also read as "del")

This principle is the **foundational pillar of naval architecture**. Without an intuitive and rigorous understanding of buoyancy, no ship can be designed, evaluated, or safely operated.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Swimming Pool Feeling"

Imagine you step into a swimming pool. The instant the water reaches your waist, you suddenly feel **noticeably lighter**. This is not a trick of the mind — it is Archimedes' Principle in action. The water "pushed aside" by your submerged body has weight, and the water *reacts* by pushing you upward with a force exactly equal to that weight.

In the same way, when a steel hull of a 200,000-tonne ship glides into the ocean, the vessel weighs the same as it did on land. However, the **enormous volume of sea water displaced by the hull pushes back upward** with a colossal buoyant force capable of suspending the entire ship. That is why a hollow steel vessel — vastly heavier than water per cubic centimetre — can still float.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:**
> The buoyant force depends **only on the volume of fluid displaced**, NOT on the material of which the body is made. A solid block of iron and a hollow steel box of identical outer dimensions will experience the **same buoyant force** when fully submerged.

---

### 1.3 Important Terminology (High-Yield KTU Definitions)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Buoyancy** | $F_B$ | Upward force exerted by a fluid on a submerged body |
| **Displacement** | $\Delta$ or $W$ | The **weight** of water displaced by a floating vessel (in tonnes) |
| **Displacement Volume** | $\nabla$ | The **volume** of water displaced by the hull (in $m^3$) |
| **Draft** | $T$ | Vertical distance from the waterline to the bottom of the keel (in $m$) |
| **Center of Buoyancy** | $B$ | Centroid of the submerged (displaced) volume |
| **Center of Gravity** | $G$ | Point through which the total weight of the ship acts |
| **Reserve Buoyancy** | $F_{RB}$ | Volume of the hull **above** the waterline (in $m^3$); the safety margin of a ship |
| **Waterplane Area** | $A_{WP}$ | Area of the hull at the waterline ($m^2$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Densities to Memorize:**
> - Fresh Water: $\rho_{FW} = 1000\ kg/m^3 = 1.000\ t/m^3$
> - Sea Water (standard): $\rho_{SW} = 1025\ kg/m^3 = 1.025\ t/m^3$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does Archimedes' Principle Work? (Pressure-Based Explanation)

Consider any body submerged in a static fluid. The fluid exerts pressure on every square centimetre of the body. The key observation is that **pressure increases linearly with depth**.

- On the **top face** of a submerged rectangular plate: small downward pressure $P_{top} = P_{atm} + \rho g h_1$
- On the **bottom face** of the same plate: large upward pressure $P_{bot} = P_{atm} + \rho g h_2$

Because $h_2 > h_1$, the **net vertical force is upward** and is given by:

$$F_{B,\text{upward}} = (P_{bot} - P_{top}) \cdot A = \rho g (h_2 - h_1) \cdot A = \rho g \cdot V$$

The horizontal pressure forces on the four sides of the body **cancel out in pairs** (by symmetry). This leaves a **net upward buoyant force** equal to the weight of the displaced fluid.

---

### 2.2 Conditions for Floating, Submerging, and Rising

A body in a fluid has **only two vertical forces** acting on it:

1. **Weight ($W$)** acting downward through $G$
2. **Buoyant Force ($F_B$)** acting upward through $B$

The behavior is governed by comparing the body's **average density** ($\rho_{body}$) to the **fluid density** ($\rho_{fluid}$):

| Condition | Density Comparison | Result |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho_{body} < \rho_{fluid}$ | Body is lighter per unit volume than fluid | **Floats** (partially submerged) |
| $\rho_{body} = \rho_{fluid}$ | Body has same average density as fluid | **Neutral equilibrium** (fully submerged, neither sinks nor rises) |
| $\rho_{body} > \rho_{fluid}$ | Body is heavier per unit volume than fluid | **Sinks** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a steel ship floats but a steel nail sinks:**
> A ship is mostly **hollow air-filled volume**. Its *average density* (steel + air + cargo divided by total volume) is much less than water. A nail is solid steel, so its average density is $7850\ kg/m^3$, which exceeds water's $1025\ kg/m^3$, and it sinks.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (All Key Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE BEFORE ANY EXAM.** Every numerical problem in Module 1 of PCNST205 reduces to one or more of these formulas.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Fundamental buoyant force | Newtons ($N$) |
| 2 | $W = \Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Weight = Displacement (equilibrium for floating body) | $N$ |
| 3 | $\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Displaced volume from displacement | $m^3$ |
| 4 | $\Delta_{\text{tonnes}} = \rho_{t/m^3} \cdot \nabla_{m^3}$ | Displacement in **tonnes** (mass equivalent, $1\ t = 1000\ kg$) | tonnes ($t$) |
| 5 | $\nabla_{FW} = \dfrac{\Delta}{1.000}$ | Volume displaced in **fresh water** | $m^3$ |
| 6 | $\nabla_{SW} = \dfrac{\Delta}{1.025}$ | Volume displaced in **sea water** | $m^3$ |
| 7 | $F_{RB} = V_{\text{hull}} - \nabla$ | Reserve buoyancy = hull volume minus displaced volume | $m^3$ |
| 8 | $\text{TPC} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot A_{WP}}{100}$ | Tonnes Per Centimetre immersion (change in displacement per 1 cm draft change) | $t/cm$ |
| 9 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\Delta \nabla}{A_{WP}}$ | Draft change from change in displaced volume | $m$ |
| 10 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\Delta W}{\text{TPC} \times 100}$ | Draft change from added/removed weight using TPC | $m$ |
| 11 | $\nabla_{FW} = \nabla_{SW} \times \dfrac{1.025}{1.000}$ | Equivalent volume in fresh water for same displacement (mass) | $m^3$ |

> **Convention:** Throughout naval architecture, $\Delta$ is often quoted in **tonnes-force (tonf)** or **metric tonnes (t)**, where $1\ t \equiv 1000\ kg \equiv 9810\ N$. Since the same numerical value applies whether you treat $1\ t$ as $1000\ kg$ or $9810\ N$, the gravitational constant $g$ is **often absorbed** into the unit "tonne." KTU examiners consistently follow this convention.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Ship Loading and Stability:** Every cargo officer must compute how much weight can be loaded before the ship exceeds its Plimsoll (load) line. This is a direct Archimedes' Principle calculation.
2. **Submarine Operations:** Ballast tanks are flooded to *increase* displacement (submerge) or blown with high-pressure air to *decrease* displacement (surface) — directly manipulating $\nabla$ and hence $F_B$.
3. **Dock and Lock Design:** The upward force on lock gates and dock caissons is calculated using Archimedes' Principle to prevent structural failure.
4. **Offshore Platforms:** Semi-submersibles use submerged pontoons whose buoyancy supports enormous topside weights.

---
