## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Archimedes' Principle from First Principles

Consider a body of arbitrary shape fully submerged in a static fluid of density $\rho$. Imagine replacing the body with the **same volume of fluid** occupying the identical region. Since this hypothetical "fluid parcel" is in static equilibrium with the surrounding fluid, the surrounding fluid must exert an upward force exactly equal to the weight of that parcel.

**Step 1:** Consider the equilibrium of the hypothetical fluid parcel.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_B^{\text{(on parcel)}} &= \text{Weight of parcel} \\
F_B^{\text{(on parcel)}} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{\text{parcel}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Since the parcel occupies the *exact same region* as the actual body, the pressure distribution on the boundary is identical. Therefore, the upward force the surrounding fluid exerts on **any object** occupying that region is the same.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_B^{\text{(on body)}} &= F_B^{\text{(on parcel)}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Conclude the formal statement:

$$\boxed{F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla}$$

This elegant proof requires **only the assumption of static equilibrium** — making Archimedes' Principle universally valid for all fluids (liquids and gases).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of TPC (Tonnes Per Centimetre)

**TPC** is the most frequently used operational metric in naval architecture and is almost guaranteed to appear in KTU numerical questions.

**Step 1:** Define TPC — it is the change in displacement (in tonnes) per 1 cm change in mean draft.

**Step 2:** When a ship sinks by an additional small depth $\delta T$ (in metres), the additional volume submerged equals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta \nabla = A_{WP} \cdot \delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The corresponding additional weight (displacement) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \delta \nabla = \rho \cdot g \cdot A_{WP} \cdot \delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Express $\delta T$ in centimetres: $\delta T \text{ (in cm)} = 100 \cdot \delta T \text{ (in m)}$. So if the ship sinks by 1 cm ($\delta T = 0.01\ m$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta W_{(1\,cm)} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A_{WP} \cdot 0.01
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to tonnes (where $1\ t = 1000\ kg$). Using $\rho$ in $kg/m^3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TPC} &= \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A_{WP} \cdot 0.01}{1000 \cdot g} \\
\text{TPC} &= \frac{\rho \cdot A_{WP}}{100\,000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** When $\rho$ is expressed in $t/m^3$ (so $\rho_{SW} = 1.025$, $\rho_{FW} = 1.000$):

$$
\boxed{\text{TPC} = \frac{\rho_{(t/m^3)} \cdot A_{WP\,(m^2)}}{100}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Draft Change When Density of Water Changes

A ship of fixed displacement $\Delta$ moves from sea water to fresh water. The mass of the ship does not change, but the buoyancy required per unit volume decreases.

**Step 1:** In sea water:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta = \rho_{SW} \cdot \nabla_{SW} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \nabla_{SW} = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{SW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** In fresh water (same $\Delta$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{FW} &= \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{FW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Increase in displaced volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta \nabla = \nabla_{FW} - \nabla_{SW} = \Delta \left( \frac{1}{\rho_{FW}} - \frac{1}{\rho_{SW}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Change in draft (since $\delta \nabla = A_{WP} \cdot \delta T$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta T = \frac{\delta \nabla}{A_{WP}} = \frac{\Delta}{A_{WP}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho_{FW}} - \frac{1}{\rho_{SW}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting TPC expression, $A_{WP} = \dfrac{\text{TPC} \cdot 100}{\rho}$:

$$
\boxed{\delta T = \frac{\Delta}{\text{TPC} \cdot 100} \cdot \rho_{SW} \left( \frac{1}{\rho_{FW}} - \frac{1}{\rho_{SW}} \right) = \frac{\Delta}{\text{TPC}} \cdot \left( \frac{\rho_{SW}}{\rho_{FW}} - 1 \right) \times \frac{1}{100}}
$$

This expression is widely used for **Dock Water Allowance (DWA)** calculations in ship operations.

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** A rectangular pontoon of dimensions $6\ m\ (L) \times 4\ m\ (B) \times 2\ m\ (D)$ weighs $200\ kN$. It floats in sea water of density $1025\ kg/m^3$. Determine:
> (a) The equilibrium draft $T$
> (b) The reserve buoyancy $F_{RB}$ (in $m^3$)
> (c) The new equilibrium draft if it sails into fresh water

#### (a) Equilibrium Draft

**Step 1:** Apply Archimedes' Principle. For a floating body, weight = buoyant force.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= F_B \\
200 \times 10^3 &= \rho_{SW} \cdot g \cdot \nabla
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values ($\rho = 1025$, $g = 9.81$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
200\,000 &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times \nabla \\
200\,000 &= 10\,055.25 \times \nabla
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\nabla$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla = \frac{200\,000}{10\,055.25} = 19.891\ m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Relate displaced volume to draft. Since the pontoon floats upright, the submerged shape is a rectangular block of length $L$, breadth $B$, and depth $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= L \times B \times T \\
19.891 &= 6 \times 4 \times T = 24\,T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for draft:

$$
\boxed{T = \frac{19.891}{24} = 0.829\ m \approx 0.83\ m}
$$

#### (b) Reserve Buoyancy

**Step 1:** Total hull volume = $L \times B \times D = 6 \times 4 \times 2 = 48\ m^3$.

**Step 2:** Reserve buoyancy volume = volume above waterline:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{RB} &= V_{\text{hull}} - \nabla \\
F_{RB} &= 48 - 19.891 = 28.109\ m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{RB} \approx 28.11\ m^3}
$$

#### (c) Draft in Fresh Water

**Step 1:** Mass of pontoon remains $m = \dfrac{W}{g} = \dfrac{200\,000}{9.81} = 20\,387.36\ kg$.

**Step 2:** Volume displaced in fresh water:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{FW} = \frac{m}{\rho_{FW}} = \frac{20\,387.36}{1000} = 20.387\ m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** New draft:

$$
\boxed{T_{FW} = \frac{20.387}{24} = 0.849\ m \approx 0.85\ m}
$$

**Observation:** The pontoon sits about **2 cm deeper** in fresh water — a critical consideration when a ship transits from the ocean into an estuary.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1. State and explain Archimedes' Principle. How is it applied in determining the draft of a ship?**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Statement:** *When a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid at rest, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.*
>
> **Mathematical Form:** $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$
>
> **Application in Ship Draft:** For a ship floating in equilibrium, the weight of the ship $W$ must equal the buoyant force $F_B$. Hence:
>
> $$W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot g}$$
>
> Knowing the waterplane area $A_{WP}$ and shape of the hull near the waterline, the draft $T$ can be computed from $\nabla = A_{WP} \cdot T$ (for a rectangular approximation) or from hydrostatic curves for an actual ship.
>
> *(Examiner's note: Full 3 marks require the formal statement + mathematical form + one application sentence.)*

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q2. Define the following terms with neat sketches: (i) Buoyancy (ii) Center of Buoyancy (iii) Reserve Buoyancy.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **(i) Buoyancy:** The upward force exerted by a fluid on a body immersed in it. Numerically, $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$.
>
> **(ii) Center of Buoyancy (B):** The centroid of the displaced volume of fluid. It is the point through which the buoyant force is considered to act.
>
> **(iii) Reserve Buoyancy ($F_{RB}$):** The volume of the ship **above** the waterline that is not submerged. It represents the additional buoyancy the ship would obtain if loaded further or the water became denser. Mathematically:
>
> $$F_{RB} = V_{\text{hull}} - \nabla$$
>
> *(Examiner's note: Each definition ≈ 1 mark. Sketches must clearly label $B$ and the waterline.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — With Internal Choice)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **Question A:**

**(a)** *A rectangular barge has plan dimensions $20\ m \times 8\ m$ and a uniform depth of $3\ m$. It weighs $1500\ kN$ when empty and floats in sea water ($\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$). Determine:*
*(i) The mean draft of the empty barge.*
*(ii) The load (in tonnes) that can be placed on the barge such that the deck just touches the waterline (i.e., maximum permissible load).*
*(iii) The reserve buoyancy at the loaded condition in $m^3$.*
*(7 Marks)*

**(b)** *A ship of displacement $12\,000$ tonnes floats in sea water with a TPC of $20$ tonnes/cm. Find the change in draft when the ship sails from sea water to fresh water.* *(7 Marks)*

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### **(a) Sub-parts (i), (ii), (iii)**

**(i) Mean draft of empty barge:**

**Step 1:** Apply equilibrium: weight = buoyancy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla \\
1500 \times 10^3 &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times \nabla
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for displaced volume.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla = \frac{1\,500\,000}{10\,055.25} = 149.18\ m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For a rectangular barge, $\nabla = L \times B \times T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
149.18 &= 20 \times 8 \times T = 160\,T \\
T &= \frac{149.18}{160} = 0.932\ m
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 0.932\ m}
$$

**(ii) Maximum permissible load (deck just at waterline):**

**Step 1:** When deck just touches waterline, $T = D = 3\ m$, so $\nabla_{\max} = 20 \times 8 \times 3 = 480\ m^3$.

**Step 2:** Maximum displacement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\max} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{\max} \\
W_{\max} &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 480 = 4\,826\,520\ N = 4826.52\ kN
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{load}} = W_{\max} - W_{\text{empty}} = 4826.52 - 1500 = 3326.52\ kN
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to tonnes (using $1\ t = 9.81\ kN$):

$$
\boxed{W_{\text{load}} = \frac{3326.52}{9.81} \approx 339.1\ \text{tonnes}}
$$

**(iii) Reserve buoyancy at loaded condition:**

At loaded condition, $T = 3\ m$, so $\nabla = 480\ m^3$ and $V_{\text{hull}} = 20 \times 8 \times 3 = 480\ m^3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{RB} &= V_{\text{hull}} - \nabla = 480 - 480 = 0\ m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{RB} = 0\ m^3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Reserve buoyancy is **zero** when the deck is at the waterline. This is the absolute limit — any further load will cause sinking. Operating near this condition is **unsafe**, and in real ships the Plimsoll line ensures a healthy reserve buoyancy (typically 20–40% of total hull volume).

---

#### **(b) Draft change from sea water to fresh water**

**Step 1:** Identify given values: $\Delta = 12\,000\ t$, $\text{TPC}_{SW} = 20\ t/cm$.

**Step 2:** Use the Dock Water Allowance formula derived in §3.3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta T = \frac{\Delta}{\text{TPC}_{SW} \times 100} \cdot \left( \frac{\rho_{SW}}{\rho_{FW}} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta T &= \frac{12\,000}{20 \times 100} \cdot \left( \frac{1.025}{1.000} - 1 \right) \\
&= \frac{12\,000}{2000} \cdot (1.025 - 1) \\
&= 6 \times 0.025 \\
&= 0.15\ m
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta T = 0.15\ m = 15\ cm \text{ (sinks deeper)} }
$$

---

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **Question B (Alternative Choice):**

**(a)** *State Archimedes' Principle. With a neat sketch, show the forces acting on a floating body and derive the expression for buoyant force.* *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** *A wooden block of density $750\ kg/m^3$ and volume $0.5\ m^3$ floats in water of density $1000\ kg/m^3$. Find:*
*(i) The depth to which the block is submerged.*
*(ii) The buoyant force acting on the block.*
*(iii) The minimum weight of a metal piece to be attached to the bottom of the block so that it is just completely submerged.* *(7 Marks)*

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### **(a) Statement, Sketch & Derivation**

**Statement:** Archimedes' Principle — *when a body is fully or partially immersed in a fluid at rest, the fluid exerts an upward buoyant force on the body equal to the weight of fluid displaced by the body.*

**Sketch description:** A rectangular body floating in water. Two arrows: $W$ acting downward through $G$ at the centroid of the body; $F_B$ acting upward through $B$, the centroid of the submerged volume.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Consider a fluid element of arbitrary shape at depth $h_1$ at the top and $h_2$ at the bottom, with horizontal cross-sectional area $A$.

**Step 2:** Pressure at top face:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{top}} = P_{atm} + \rho g h_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Pressure at bottom face:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{bot}} = P_{atm} + \rho g h_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Net upward force from these two faces (other faces cancel horizontally):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{net, vertical}} &= (P_{\text{bot}} - P_{\text{top}}) \cdot A \\
&= \rho g (h_2 - h_1) \cdot A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Recognizing $(h_2 - h_1) \cdot A$ as the volume of fluid displaced:

$$
\boxed{F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla}
$$

*(Full marks for stating, sketching with clear labels, and a complete derivation.)*

---

#### **(b) Wooden Block Numerical**

**Given:** $\rho_{\text{wood}} = 750\ kg/m^3$, $V_{\text{total}} = 0.5\ m^3$, $\rho_{FW} = 1000\ kg/m^3$.

**(i) Submerged depth $h$:**

**Step 1:** Weight of block:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{wood}} = \rho_{\text{wood}} \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{total}} = 750 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 = 3678.75\ N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** For floating equilibrium, $W_{\text{wood}} = F_B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3678.75 &= \rho_{FW} \cdot g \cdot \nabla \\
3678.75 &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times \nabla \\
\nabla &= 0.375\ m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** If the block has uniform horizontal cross-sectional area $A$, then $\nabla = A \cdot h$. Also $V_{\text{total}} = A \cdot H$, so $h/H = \nabla/V_{\text{total}} = 0.375/0.5 = 0.75$.

Thus the block is submerged to **75% of its height**:

$$
\boxed{h = 0.75\ H}
$$

where $H$ is total height. (Numerically, $h = 0.375\ m$ if we use $V/A$ ratio.)

**(ii) Buoyant force:**

$$
\boxed{F_B = 3678.75\ N \approx 3.68\ kN}
$$

(Already calculated — equal to the weight of the block.)

**(iii) Minimum weight of metal to fully submerge block:**

**Step 1:** For the block to be *just* fully submerged, total submerged volume = $0.5\ m^3$.

**Step 2:** Maximum buoyant force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{B,\max} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 = 4905\ N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** This must equal total weight (wood + metal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{wood}} + W_{\text{metal}} &= 4905\ N \\
W_{\text{metal}} = 4905 - 3678.75 &= 1226.25\ N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to mass:

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{metal}} = \frac{1226.25}{9.81} = 125\ kg}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Read Carefully):**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Disaster (4 marks lost):** Mixing $kN$ with $tonnes$ without converting via $g = 9.81$. Always state: "Using $1\ t = 9.81\ kN$" before any conversion.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by $g$:** When weight is given in $kN$ and density in $kg/m^3$, students often write $W = \rho \cdot \nabla$, dropping $g$. This is the **single most common mistake**.
> 3. **TPC vs TPF confusion:** TPC (Tonnes per Centimetre) is defined using $\rho$ in $t/m^3$. If $\rho$ is given in $kg/m^3$, divide by 1000 first or use the formula with $g$ explicitly.
> 4. **Reserve Buoyancy = 0 Trap:** Many students answer "no safety margin" without explaining that this is the *unsafe operating limit*.
> 5. **Density Confusion:** Writing $\rho_{FW}/\rho_{SW}$ when the formula requires $\rho_{SW}/\rho_{FW}$ — sign of the answer will invert, costing full marks.
> 6. **Draft vs Displacement:** Some students confuse "draft change" (in cm or m) with "displacement" (in tonnes). Always double-check units.
> 7. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** KTU examiners *do* deduct marks for missing or incorrect units, even if the numerical value is correct.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Archimedes' Principle** states that buoyant force = weight of displaced fluid: $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$.
- A body floats if its **average density** < fluid density; sinks if greater; neutral if equal.
- For a **floating ship in equilibrium**: $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$, hence **displacement = weight of water displaced**.
- **Standard densities (KTU convention):** Fresh water = $1000\ kg/m^3$; Sea water = $1025\ kg/m^3$.
- **Displacement ($\Delta$)** is conventionally reported in **tonnes** (metric tons, where $1\ t = 1000\ kg \equiv 9.81\ kN$).
- **TPC (Tonnes Per Centimetre)** = $\rho_{(t/m^3)} \cdot A_{WP\,(m^2)} / 100$. Indicates change in displacement per 1 cm draft change.
- **Reserve Buoyancy ($F_{RB}$)** = $V_{\text{hull}} - \nabla$ (volume above waterline). A **safety margin**; never operate with $F_{RB} = 0$.
- **Center of Buoyancy ($B$)** is the centroid of the displaced volume — the line of action of $F_B$.
- **Center of Gravity ($G$)** is where the ship's total weight acts vertically downward.
- When moving from **sea water to fresh water**, a ship **sinks deeper** because fresh water provides less buoyancy per unit volume.
- The change in draft due to density change is given by: $\delta T = (\Delta / \text{TPC}) \cdot (\rho_{SW}/\rho_{FW} - 1) / 100$.
- The **Plimsoll line** on the ship's hull visually marks the maximum safe draft for various water densities and seasonal conditions — a direct practical application of Archimedes' Principle.
- **Memorize the constant:** $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$ and the **density ratio** $\rho_{SW}/\rho_{FW} = 1.025$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laws of Floatation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Laws of Floatation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Laws of Floatation** govern the equilibrium of a body partially or wholly immersed in a fluid. They are direct consequences of **Archimedes' Principle** (c. 250 BC), which states:

> A body wholly or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body, and this force acts vertically upward through the centroid of the displaced volume (the **Center of Buoyancy**, denoted **B**).

For naval architecture, the principle is re-stated as **The Three Laws of Floatation** (often attributed to Archimedes, formalised for ship design):

1. **First Law (Weight-Displacement Equality):** The weight of a floating body equals the weight of the fluid it displaces. Formally, $W = \rho \cdot \nabla \cdot g$, where $\nabla$ is the displaced volume.
2. **Second Law (Vertical Alignment):** The Center of Gravity (**G**) and the Center of Buoyancy (**B**) of a floating body in equilibrium must lie on the same vertical line.
3. **Third Law (Reserve Buoyancy):** The submerged volume of a floating vessel is the minimum required for weight support; the additional volume between the waterplane and the upper deck is the **Reserve Buoyancy**, vital for survivability in waves.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a bath tub experiment:** Push a small wooden block into a full bath. The water that overflows is exactly equal in **weight** (not volume) to the block. If that block weighed 1 kg, exactly 1 kg of water spills out. This is the **First Law**. The reason the block tilts back up to a stable orientation is that the "lift" force (buoyancy) acts at the geometric center of the underwater part (**B**), while the weight acts at the block's mass center (**G**). The vertical alignment of these two points is the **Second Law**. A ship must displace more water than its minimum needed — that extra capacity is the **Third Law** (reserve buoyancy), which is what keeps a ship from sinking when waves crash over its deck.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (Must Memorise for KTU Exams)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of fresh water | $\rho_{fw}$ | **1000** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Density of standard sea water | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Density of pure water (4 °C reference) | $\rho_w$ | 999.97 | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Standard gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| Specific weight of sea water | $\gamma_{sw}$ | 10056.75 | $\text{N/m}^3$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In numerical problems, unless stated otherwise, KTU examiners expect $\rho_{sea} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. Using $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ without justification will cost a half-mark deduction.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

- **Displacement ($\Delta$ or $W$):** The total weight of water a vessel displaces, equal to the vessel's own weight when floating. Often quoted in **tonnes** (metric).
- **Displacement Volume ($\nabla$):** The actual volume of underwater hull, in $\text{m}^3$.
- **Center of Buoyancy (B):** Centroid of the submerged volume.
- **Center of Gravity (G):** Centroid of the ship's mass.
- **Waterplane:** The horizontal plane cutting the hull at the waterline.
- **Freeboard:** Vertical distance from waterline to the upper deck.
- **Reserve Buoyancy:** Volumetric capacity of the hull above the waterline.
- **Draft / Draught ($T$):** Vertical distance from keel to waterline.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Theory: Pressure Integration → Buoyancy

A fluid exerts hydrostatic pressure that increases linearly with depth. For a fully submerged body, integrate the pressure over the entire wetted surface. The horizontal pressure components cancel out by symmetry; the vertical components add up to give a resultant upward force — the **buoyant force**.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

- **Step 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure Law:** At any depth $h$ below the free surface, the gauge pressure is $P = \rho g h$. This is a *linear* depth function.
- **Step 2 — Force on a Small Element:** A horizontal patch of area $dA$ at depth $h$ experiences a normal pressure force $dF = P\,dA = \rho g h\,dA$.
- **Step 3 — Vertical Component Summation:** Integrating vertically over the body's surface, the upward push on the bottom face is *greater* than the downward push on the top face because the bottom is deeper. The net result is $F_B$ acting through the centroid of the displaced volume.
- **Step 4 — Identifying the Centre of Application:** The line of action of $F_B$ always passes through the centroid of the submerged region (in naval terms, the **center of flotation** for the waterplane and **B** for the underwater volume).
- **Step 5 — Equilibrium Condition:** For a body in static floating equilibrium, $\sum F_z = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$ — yielding Archimedes' result $F_B = W$ and the collinearity of **B** and **G** on a vertical axis.

## 2.2 The Three Conditions of Submersion

A body immersed in a fluid can be in one of three states, governed by the ratio of its mean density to the fluid's density:

| Condition | Density Comparison | Behaviour | Ship-Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sinks (Negative Buoyancy)** | $\rho_{\text{body}} > \rho_{\text{fluid}}$ | $F_B < W$, body accelerates downward | A solid steel ingot dropped overboard |
| **Floats (Positive Buoyancy)** | $\rho_{\text{body}} < \rho_{\text{fluid}}$ | $F_B = W$ at partial immersion | A ship, a wooden log |
| **Neutral (Neutral Buoyancy)** | $\rho_{\text{body}} = \rho_{\text{fluid}}$ | $F_B = W$ at full submersion, remains suspended | A submarine hovering at trim depth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Naval Architecture:** A ship is built from **steel** ($\rho \approx 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$, which is $\approx 7.7$ times denser than water) but floats because the hull encloses a large air-filled volume. The **mean density** (total mass / total enclosed volume) becomes less than that of water.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_B = \rho_f \cdot \nabla \cdot g$ | Buoyant force (Archimedes) | N ($\text{kg·m/s}^2$) | Any fluid, any immersion depth |
| 2 | $W = m \cdot g = \rho_b \cdot V \cdot g$ | Weight of the body | N | Rigid body, constant $g$ |
| 3 | $\Delta = \rho_f \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement (mass) | kg or tonnes | Floating equilibrium only |
| 4 | $\nabla = \dfrac{W}{\rho_f \cdot g}$ | Displaced volume (solve for $\nabla$) | $\text{m}^3$ | Floating equilibrium |
| 5 | $\dfrac{V_{\text{sub}}}{V_{\text{body}}} = \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f}$ | Fraction submerged | dimensionless | Homogeneous body, simple geometry |
| 6 | $\text{Reserve Buoyancy} = V_{\text{deck}} - \nabla$ | Volumetric safety margin | $\text{m}^3$ | Naval architecture, intact stability |
| 7 | $\text{TPC} = \dfrac{\Delta_{\text{per cm}}}{1}$ | Tonnes Per Centimetre immersion | tonnes/cm | Ship loading |
| 8 | $P_{\text{hydrostatic}} = \rho_f g h$ | Gauge pressure at depth $h$ | Pa | Static fluid, open surface |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Pitfall (Markdown-Safe LaTeX):** When writing $\vert x \vert$ in exam sheets, **never** use the bare `|` inside a markdown table cell — it breaks the table. Always use $\lvert \dots \rvert$ or $\vert \dots \vert$ with proper LaTeX delimiters.

## 2.4 Real-World Naval / Shipping Utility

- **Load-line Assignment (Plimsoll Line):** Regulators compute reserve buoyancy from Laws of Floatation to mark safe-loading waterlines for various sea zones (tropical, summer, winter, freshwater).
- **Submarine Depth Control:** Ballast tanks are flooded/emptied to alter mean density, switching between **positive** and **negative** buoyancy regimes on demand.
- **Damage Stability & Flooded Compartments:** Naval architects use the displacement formula iteratively to predict the new waterline after hull breach.
- **Port-of-Entry Surveys:** Draft surveys use Archimedes' law to weigh a ship by observing its draft before and after cargo discharge — saving the cost of a shore-side weighbridge.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Buoyant Force from First Principles (Pressure Integration)

**Statement to prove:** A body fully immersed in a static fluid of density $\rho_f$ experiences a net upward force equal to the weight of the displaced fluid.

**Setup:** Consider a small vertical column of cross-section $dA$ slicing through the body from top face (depth $h_1$) to bottom face (depth $h_2$).

**Step 1 — Pressure at the top face:**

$$P_{\text{top}} = \rho_f \, g \, h_1$$

**Step 2 — Pressure at the bottom face:**

$$P_{\text{bottom}} = \rho_f \, g \, h_2$$

**Step 3 — Net vertical force on the column (downward positive convention):**

$$dF_z = \left( P_{\text{bottom}} - P_{\text{top}} \right) dA = \rho_f g (h_2 - h_1) dA$$

**Step 4 — Recognise that the column height equals the local body thickness:**

$$h_2 - h_1 = d(\text{vertical slice height})$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over the entire horizontal waterplane area $A_{\text{WP}}$:**

$$F_B = \int_{A_{\text{WP}}} \rho_f g (h_2 - h_1)\, dA$$

**Step 6 — By geometry, $\int (h_2 - h_1)\, dA = V_{\text{displaced}} = \nabla$:**

$$F_B = \rho_f \, g \, \nabla$$

> **Conclusion:** This is the **Archimedes buoyant force** — derived purely from hydrostatic pressure integration. The line of action passes through the centroid of the displaced volume, which we call **B**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Equilibrium Condition for a Floating Body

**Statement:** A rigid body of mass $m$ and volume $V$ placed gently in a fluid of density $\rho_f$ will float with a submerged volume fraction equal to the density ratio.

**Step 1 — Two forces act on the body in the vertical direction:** weight $W$ (down) and buoyancy $F_B$ (up).

**Step 2 — Write the equilibrium condition $\sum F_z = 0$:**

$$F_B = W$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the standard expressions:**

$$\rho_f \, V_{\text{sub}} \, g = \rho_b \, V \, g$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the common factor $g$:**

$$\rho_f \, V_{\text{sub}} = \rho_b \, V$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the submerged volume ratio:**

$$\dfrac{V_{\text{sub}}}{V} = \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation of the result:**

$$\begin{aligned}
& \text{If } \rho_b < \rho_f \Rightarrow V_{\text{sub}} < V \Rightarrow \textbf{body floats (partially immersed).} \\
& \text{If } \rho_b > \rho_f \Rightarrow V_{\text{sub}} > V \Rightarrow \textbf{impossible, body sinks fully.} \\
& \text{If } \rho_b = \rho_f \Rightarrow V_{\text{sub}} = V \Rightarrow \textbf{neutral buoyancy, fully submerged in equilibrium.}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Geometric corollary for simple shapes:** A vertical cylinder of height $H$ and uniform cross-section $A$ floats with submersion depth $h$. Then $h/H = \rho_b / \rho_f$. This is the most-tested KTU geometry.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Draft of a Rectangular Barge (Worked from Scratch)

A flat-bottomed barge has length $L$, breadth $B$, and total mass $M$ (including cargo). It floats in sea water ($\rho_{sw}$). Derive the draft $T$.

**Step 1 — Volume submerged is rectangular block:**

$$\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium:**

$$W = F_B \Rightarrow M g = \rho_{sw} \cdot (L B T) \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $g$:**

$$M = \rho_{sw} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T$:**

$$T = \dfrac{M}{\rho_{sw} \cdot L \cdot B}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check (KTU-style):** If $M = 5 \times 10^6\ \text{kg}$, $L = 100\ \text{m}$, $B = 20\ \text{m}$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$:

$$T = \dfrac{5 \times 10^6}{1025 \times 100 \times 20} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^6}{2.05 \times 10^6} \approx 2.44\ \text{m}$$

> This is consistent with realistic Panamax-class feeder barge drafts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — What KTU Examiners Love to Test

| Concept | Direct Definition | Equation Form | Common Test Trap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Archimedes' Principle | Buoyancy = weight displaced | $F_B = \rho_f \nabla g$ | Confusing weight (N) vs mass (kg) |
| Center of Buoyancy (B) | Centroid of underwater volume | geometric integration | Saying it's the centroid of the *whole ship* |
| Center of Gravity (G) | Centroid of ship's mass | $G = \frac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{M}$ | Confusing with metacenter M |
| Floating Condition | $W = F_B$ | $\rho_b V = \rho_f \nabla$ | Forgetting to convert tonnes to kg |
| Reserve Buoyancy | Volumetric safety above WL | $V_{\text{deck}} - \nabla$ | Confusing with freeboard (a *length*, not a *volume*) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the equilibrium analysis for a floating body — replacing hand-drawn hydrostatic force diagrams with a logically equivalent schematic that complies with Mermaid v10 syntax rules (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved-keyword nodes, plain text labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Body and Fluid Parameters] --> B[Body Mass m]
    A --> C[Fluid Density rho_f]
    A --> D[Body Volume V]
    A --> E[Submerged Geometry]

    B --> F[Compute Weight W = m g]
    C --> G[Compute Buoyant Force F_B = rho_f times displaced volume times g]
    E --> G
    D --> H[Compute Mean Density rho_b = m divided by V]

    F --> I[Force Balance Check]
    G --> I

    I --> J{F_B compared to W}
    J -->|F_B greater than W| K[STATE: Positive Buoyancy - Body Rises]
    J -->|F_B less than W| L[STATE: Negative Buoyancy - Body Sinks]
    J -->|F_B equals W| M[STATE: Neutral Floating Equilibrium]

    M --> N[Locate Centre of Buoyancy B]
    M --> O[Locate Centre of Gravity G]
    N --> P[Verify B and G are on same vertical line]
    O --> P

    P --> Q[OUTPUT: Equilibrium Waterline and Draft T]
    K --> R[OUTPUT: Body Partially Emersed, Continue Rising]
    L --> S[OUTPUT: Body Fully Submerged, Settles on Bottom]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#312e81,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.1 Reading the Diagram

- **Top band (A → B, C, D, E):** Data ingestion stage. Every KTU problem starts here.
- **Middle band (F, G, H):** Parallel computation of the three critical scalars — weight, buoyant force, mean density.
- **Decision block (J):** The classic three-way Archimedean classification. **Memorise this branch.**
- **Verification node (P):** Implements the *Second Law of Floatation* — the **B–G verticality** check.
- **Terminal outputs (Q, R, S):** Match the three submersion regimes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Q1a]`
**State Archimedes' Principle. Explain any two practical applications in naval architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Archimedes' Principle states that *when a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body, and this force acts vertically upward through the centroid of the displaced volume.*

**Naval applications (any two, 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Load-line (Plimsoll mark) assignment:** The maximum permissible draft of a ship is computed from the Laws of Floatation so that reserve buoyancy is preserved in any ocean zone.
2. **Submarine depth control:** Flooding/emptying ballast tanks alters mean density; this switches the submarine between positive, negative, and neutral buoyancy.
3. **Draft surveys:** Cargo weight is measured by observing changes in a vessel's draft — Archimedes' principle in commercial use.
4. **Stability of floating docks and pontoons:** Designed using displaced-volume computations.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1b]`
**Define (i) Center of Buoyancy, and (ii) Reserve Buoyancy. Why is reserve buoyancy critical for ship safety?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Center of Buoyancy (B):** It is the centroid of the volume of water displaced by the immersed portion of the hull. The buoyant force is considered to act vertically upward through this point. *(1 mark)*

(ii) **Reserve Buoyancy:** It is the volume of the watertight hull lying *above* the waterline, i.e., the difference between the total enclosed volume of the hull and the displacement volume $\nabla$. *(1 mark)*

**Critical for safety:** Reserve buoyancy provides the extra displacement capacity that prevents the ship from sinking when waves, flooding, or shifting cargo increase the effective weight or submerge the deck. Without it, any green-water loading could cause total loss of buoyancy. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Q2 OR]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *Derive the condition for a body to float, submerge, or be in neutral equilibrium, starting from the basic force-balance equation.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A ship of displacement 25,000 tonnes floats in sea water of density $1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Find (i) the volume of water displaced, and (ii) the new draft if the ship sails into a freshwater port ($\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$). The ship has a uniform waterplane area of $4500\ \text{m}^2$.*

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1:** For a rigid body of mass $m$ and total volume $V$ immersed in fluid of density $\rho_f$, two vertical forces act: weight $W$ downward and buoyancy $F_B$ upward. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** Write force expressions:
$W = m g = \rho_b V g$ and $F_B = \rho_f V_{\text{sub}} g$, where $\rho_b$ is mean body density. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** Apply $\sum F_z = 0$ for equilibrium:
$\rho_f V_{\text{sub}} g = \rho_b V g$ ⇒ $\dfrac{V_{\text{sub}}}{V} = \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f}$. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4:** Case analysis: *(3 marks)*
- If $\rho_b > \rho_f$ ⇒ $V_{\text{sub}} > V$ ⇒ **impossible, body sinks** (negative buoyancy).
- If $\rho_b < \rho_f$ ⇒ $V_{\text{sub}} < V$ ⇒ **body floats** with fraction $\rho_b/\rho_f$ submerged (positive buoyancy).
- If $\rho_b = \rho_f$ ⇒ **neutral buoyancy** — body remains suspended at any depth.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Displacement volume in sea water:** *(3 marks)*

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}}$$

$$\nabla = \dfrac{25{,}000 \times 1000\ \text{kg}}{1025\ \text{kg/m}^3} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^7}{1025} \approx 24{,}390.24\ \text{m}^3$$

**(ii) New draft in fresh water:** *(4 marks)*

The mass (weight) of the ship is unchanged; therefore the displaced volume must increase (because fresh water is less dense).

$$\nabla_{\text{fw}} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho_{fw}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^7}{1000} = 25{,}000\ \text{m}^3$$

With uniform waterplane area $A_{WP} = 4500\ \text{m}^2$:

$$T_{\text{fw}} = \dfrac{\nabla_{\text{fw}}}{A_{WP}} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{4500} \approx 5.56\ \text{m}$$

**Comparison with sea-water draft:**

$$T_{sw} = \dfrac{24{,}390.24}{4500} \approx 5.42\ \text{m}$$

The ship sits **about 14 cm deeper** in fresh water — a critical figure for vessels negotiating river entrances with shallow under-keel clearance.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the three laws of floatation with neat diagrams. State the conditions for a body to be in stable equilibrium while floating.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A solid cylinder of diameter $0.4\ \text{m}$, height $1.5\ \text{m}$, and density $780\ \text{kg/m}^3$ is gently placed in water ($\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$) with its axis vertical. Find: (i) the depth of immersion, and (ii) the magnitude of the buoyant force.*

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**The Three Laws of Floatation:**

**Law 1 — Weight equals weight of displaced fluid:** A floating body displaces a volume of fluid whose weight is exactly equal to the weight of the body. Mathematically, $W = \rho_f \nabla g$. *(2 marks)*

**Law 2 — Verticality of B and G:** For a body in floating equilibrium, the centre of buoyancy (B) and the centre of gravity (G) must lie on the same vertical line. Otherwise, a couple is produced that rotates the body. *(2 marks)*

**Law 3 — Minimum Submerged Volume:** The volume of fluid displaced is the *minimum* required to support the body; the remaining hull volume (above the waterline) constitutes the **reserve buoyancy**. *(2 marks)*

**Conditions for stable equilibrium (1 mark):** When slightly tilted, the body's metacentre **M** must lie *above* **G**, i.e., **GM > 0**. (This connects to Module 2 stability analysis.)

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** Diameter $D = 0.4\ \text{m}$ ⇒ Radius $r = 0.2\ \text{m}$, Height $H = 1.5\ \text{m}$, $\rho_b = 780\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \pi r^2 = \pi (0.2)^2 = 0.04\pi\ \text{m}^2 \approx 0.1257\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total volume of cylinder:**

$$V = A H = 0.04\pi \times 1.5 = 0.06\pi\ \text{m}^3 \approx 0.1885\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Depth of immersion** (using $V_{\text{sub}}/V = \rho_b/\rho_f$): *(3 marks)*

Let immersion depth = $h$. Submerged volume $V_{\text{sub}} = A h$.

$$\dfrac{A h}{A H} = \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = H \cdot \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_f} = 1.5 \times \dfrac{780}{1000} = 1.17\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Buoyant force magnitude:** *(2 marks)*

$$F_B = \rho_w \cdot V_{\text{sub}} \cdot g = 1000 \times (0.04\pi \times 1.17) \times 9.81$$

$$F_B = 1000 \times 0.1470 \times 9.81 \approx 1442.07\ \text{N}$$

**Cross-check (2 marks):** $W = \rho_b V g = 780 \times 0.1885 \times 9.81 \approx 1442.07\ \text{N}$. The values match, confirming the floating condition. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic — 2024 pattern analysis:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing tonnes (mass) with tonnes-force (force). $1$ tonne = $1000$ kg (mass), NOT $1000$ kgf. Always convert to SI newtons via $W = mg$.
> 2. **Density of sea water mis-quoted (–1 mark):** Use $1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ unless the problem specifies otherwise. Some students default to $1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ even when "sea" is clearly stated.
> 3. **Forgetting the gravity term in buoyancy but not in weight (–2 marks):** Either include $g$ in **both** $W$ and $F_B$ or cancel it out algebraically. Mixing the two conventions is a fatal flaw.
> 4. **Confusing Reserve Buoyancy with Freeboard (–1 mark):** Reserve buoyancy is a *volume* ($\text{m}^3$). Freeboard is a *length* (m). They are related but never interchangeable.
> 5. **B and G mislabelled in diagrams (–1 mark):** B is *centroid of submerged volume*, G is *centroid of ship's mass*. Examiners look for this distinction explicitly.
> 6. **No unit in final answer (–½ mark):** Always write the unit. A bare number with no unit is incomplete in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Laws of Floatation" (Module 1):**

- [x] **Archimedes' Principle:** $F_B = \rho_f \nabla g$, acts vertically upward through **B**.
- [x] **Three Laws:** (1) $W = \rho_f \nabla g$; (2) **B** and **G** on the same vertical line; (3) reserve buoyancy exists.
- [x] **Three submersion regimes:** $\rho_b > \rho_f$ (sinks), $\rho_b < \rho_f$ (floats), $\rho_b = \rho_f$ (neutral).
- [x] **Fraction submerged:** $V_{\text{sub}}/V = \rho_b / \rho_f$.
- [x] **Draft equation for rectangular barge:** $T = W / (\rho_f g A_{WP})$.
- [x] **Constants to memorise:** $\rho_{fw} = 1000$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$, $g = 9.81$.
- [x] **Key acronyms:** TPC (Tonnes Per Centimetre), FWA (Fresh Water Allowance), DWT (Deadweight).
- [x] **Equilibrium of a floating body:** requires both force balance and moment balance — these two conditions together give the *three* laws of floatation.
- [x] **Ship floats despite steel density $\approx 7.85 \times$ water density** — because mean density of the *whole vessel including enclosed air* is less than water.
- [x] **Stability hook (Module 2 preview):** Initial transverse stability requires the metacentre **M** to lie above **G**, quantified by the metacentric height **GM**.
- [x] **Engineering intuition:** Everything in naval architecture — from Plimsoll marks to submarine trim — is governed by these three laws.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weight and Buoyancy; The Ship's Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Weight and Buoyancy; The Ship's Form # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Weight (W) in Ship Theory **Weight (W)** of a ship is the gravitational force exerted on the entire vessel, including its hull, machinery, outfit, cargo, fuel, water, crew, supplies, and ballast. It is conventionally expressed in **tonnes-force (tf)** or **kilonewtons (kN)**, and is the vector sum of every downward-acting mass component of the vessel. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > Weight of a ship, denoted by **W**, is the total mass of the ship and all its contents multiplied by the acceleration due to gravity. In shipbuilding practice, because we are working with very large masses at sea, the unit **tonne-force (1 tf ≈ 9.81 kN)** is universally adopted, where 1 tonne corresponds to a mass of **1000 kg**. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Heavy Boat on a Lake" Imagine you load a small wooden boat with camping gear, food, and friends. The boat sinks deeper into the water. Why? Because the **total weight pushing down** has increased. The ship in the ocean behaves identically — every additional tonne of cargo causes the ship to sink fractionally deeper, displacing more water. The ship is essentially a "floating weighing machine" — its **depth of immersion (draft)** is a direct, visual readout of its total weight. --- ## 1.2 Buoyancy (B / Δ) in Ship Theory **Buoyancy** is the upward vertical force exerted on a submerged or partially submerged body by the hydrostatic pressure of the surrounding fluid. By **Archimedes' Principle** (3rd century BC), this upward force is equal in magnitude to the **weight of the fluid displaced by the body**. In naval architecture, the buoyancy force is referred to as the **Displacement (Δ)** — so called because the ship is said to "displace" a volume of water whose weight equals the buoyancy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Archimedes' Principle — The Cornerstone of Ship Hydrostatics** > A floating vessel displaces a volume of water whose weight is **exactly equal to the weight of the vessel** at that instant. This is the single most important equilibrium equation in ship theory: > > $$\text{Weight of Ship } (W) \;=\; \text{Buoyancy / Displacement } (\Delta)$$ ### Conceptual Analogy — The Bathtub Experiment Fill a bathtub to the brim. Gently lower a brick into it — water overflows equal to the brick's volume. That overflowing water's weight is exactly the upward force pushing the brick back up. A ship in the ocean does the same thing, only on a colossal scale: a fully loaded VLCC displaces more than **300,000 tonnes** of seawater. --- ## 1.3 The Ship's Form The **Ship's Form** refers to the three-dimensional geometry of the hull — its length, breadth, depth, curvature of the sides, sharpness of the ends, and the shape of the underwater volume. Naval architects describe this form using a small set of dimensionless ratios called **Form Coefficients** (Block, Midship, Prismatic, and Waterplane coefficients), which are the heart of every hull-form design. > [!NOTE] > **Why Form Matters** > Two ships with identical displacement can have radically different resistance, stability, speed, and seakeeping purely due to their form. A tanker (blocky, full) behaves nothing like a frigate (slender, fine) even if they weigh the same. The **Ship's Form** is what differentiates them. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Shape of a Spoon vs. a Knife" Push a spoon through water — it pushes a lot of water aside, you feel heavy drag. Push a knife edge-on — almost no resistance. This is exactly the design problem: how to choose the hull's cross-sectional shape (and longitudinal taper) so that the ship moves efficiently through water at the desired speed, while still carrying the required cargo. The **form coefficients** are simply the mathematical toolset that lets us quantify this "spoon vs. knife" choice. --- ## 1.4 Key Standard Reference Values | Quantity | Sea Water (SW) | Fresh Water (FW) | |---|---|---| | Mass density $\rho$ | **1.025 t/m³** | **1.000 t/m³** | | Specific weight $\gamma$ | **10.05 kN/m³** | **9.81 kN/m³** | > [!IMPORTANT] > A ship floats **higher in salt water** than in fresh water (because salt water is denser, so less volume needs to be displaced to support the same weight). The difference is called the **Fresh Water Allowance (FWA)** and is a routine cargo-planning calculation. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Floating Equilibrium Condition A ship in still water is in static equilibrium under two opposing vertical forces: 1. **Weight W** — acting downward through the **Centre of Gravity (G)**. 2. **Buoyancy B (≡ Δ)** — acting upward through the **Centre of Buoyancy (B)** (the centroid of the displaced volume). For **translational equilibrium**: $$W = B = \Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$ where $\nabla$ (pronounced "del" or "nabla") is the **volume of displacement** in cubic metres, $\rho$ is the water mass density, and $g$ is gravitational acceleration. For **rotational equilibrium** (about any horizontal axis), the moments must balance — this is the basis of the **Statical Stability** theory covered later in Module 1. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How a Ship Floats 1. **Hull is constructed empty** (lightweight condition). The volume of the underwater hull displaces a certain weight of water. 2. **Loads are added** (cargo, fuel, crew). The total weight $W$ increases. 3. **The ship sinks** until the new displaced volume $\nabla'$ satisfies $\rho g \nabla' = W'$. 4. **Equilibrium is reached** at a new, deeper **draft** $T'$. 5. The waterline plane that just touches the hull at this condition is called the **Waterline of Floatation (WLF)**. --- ## 2.3 Reserve of Buoyancy The **Reserve of Buoyancy (RoB)** is the additional buoyancy that would become available if the ship were submerged down to a deeper reference level (usually the upper deck or the load line). It is the safety margin against sinking. $$\text{Reserve of Buoyancy} = \frac{\nabla_{\text{above WLF}}}{\nabla_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Typical merchant ships carry a **Reserve of Buoyancy between 30 % and 60 %** of the total displacement volume. Warships, by deliberate design, have higher reserves (often **> 60 %**) for survivability in damage conditions. --- ## 2.4 Tonnage vs. Displacement — A Critical Distinction | Term | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---| | **Displacement (Δ)** | Actual weight of water displaced by the ship at a given loading condition. | tonnes (t) | | **Deadweight (DWT)** | Total weight the ship can carry (cargo + fuel + water + stores + crew). | tonnes (t) | | **Lightweight (LWT)** | Weight of the empty ship (hull + machinery + outfit, no cargo/fuel). | tonnes (t) | | **Gross Tonnage (GT)** | Internal volume-based measure for commercial/regulatory use. | dimensionless (m³-based) | Relationship: $\Delta = LWT + DWT$ --- ## 2.5 Form Coefficients — The Ship Designer's Toolkit The four form coefficients quantify the fullness/fineness of the hull: $$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} \quad \text{(Block Coefficient)}$$ $$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} \quad \text{(Midship Coefficient)}$$ $$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L} = \frac{C_B}{C_M} \quad \text{(Prismatic Coefficient)}$$ $$C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B} \quad \text{(Waterplane Area Coefficient)}$$ Where: - $\nabla$ = displaced volume (m³) - $L$ = length between perpendiculars (m) - $B$ = moulded breadth (m) - $T$ = mean draft (m) - $A_M$ = area of the midship section (m²) - $A_{WP}$ = area of the waterplane (m²) > [!NOTE] > **Exam Pearl** — KTU examiners love to ask: *"Why is $C_P = C_B / C_M$?"* The proof comes from simple substitution, which is shown in Section 3. --- ## 2.6 Typical $C_B$ Values (for KTU context) | Ship Type | $C_B$ Range | $C_P$ Range | Comment | |---|---|---|---| | Tanker (VLCC) | 0.80 – 0.85 | 0.80 – 0.85 | Very full form, low speed | | Bulk Carrier | 0.75 – 0.82 | 0.78 – 0.82 | Full, moderate speed | | Container Ship | 0.55 – 0.65 | 0.62 – 0.70 | Fine for high speed | | Frigate / Warship | 0.40 – 0.55 | 0.55 – 0.65 | Very fine for high speed | | Submarine (surfaced) | 0.40 – 0.50 | 0.55 – 0.65 | Streamlined | --- ## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 EXAM** | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement from displaced volume | tonnes (t) | | 2 | $W = \Delta$ | Floating equilibrium | t | | 3 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | dimensionless | | 4 | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Midship coefficient | dimensionless | | 5 | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L} = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M}$ | Prismatic coefficient | dimensionless | | 6 | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$ | Waterplane coefficient | dimensionless | | 7 | $\Delta = LWT + DWT$ | Weight breakdown | t | | 8 | $\text{RoB} = \dfrac{\nabla_{above}}{\nabla_{total}} \times 100$ | Reserve of Buoyancy | % | | 9 | $FWA = \dfrac{\Delta}{100 \cdot \text{TPC per cm in SW - TPC per cm in FW}}$ | Fresh Water Allowance (approx.) | cm | | 10 | $TPC = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{100} \cdot \rho$ | Tonnes per Centimetre Immersion | t/cm | --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are the foundation of: - **Cargo loading / loading manual** (every ship carries an approved Loading Manual using these relationships). - **Stability booklets** (used by the ship's master to check $G$, $B$, and righting moment). - **Resistance & powering prediction** ($C_B$ and $C_P$ are the primary inputs to the Holtrop-Mennen and similar statistical resistance methods). - **Damage stability** (RoB gives the residual buoyancy after flooding). - **Statutory surveys and Load Line assignment** (Plimsoll Mark). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Buoyancy from Integration of Hydrostatic Pressure Consider a ship floating at waterline $W\!L$. Pick a small horizontal element of the immersed hull surface of area $dA$ at depth $z$ below the waterline. The hydrostatic pressure at that depth is $p = \rho g z$. The vertical component of the pressure force on that element is $dF = p \cdot dA \cdot \cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the outward normal to the hull and the vertical. For a closed surface, integrating the vertical pressure forces gives the **total upward buoyancy**. The mathematical identity (Green's Theorem, applied to hydrostatics) yields: $$B = \iint_{S_{\text{immersed}}} p \cdot \cos\theta \, dA = \rho g \int_{0}^{T} A(z) \, dz = \rho g \cdot \nabla$$ where $A(z)$ is the cross-sectional area of the hull at depth $z$, and $\nabla$ is the integral of $A(z)$ from $z=0$ (waterline) to $z=T$ (keel) — i.e., the displaced volume. $$\boxed{B = \rho g \nabla}$$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. Pressure at depth $z$ is $p = \rho g z$ (basic hydrostatics). 2. Integrate this pressure over the immersed surface. 3. By the divergence theorem, the surface integral collapses to a volume integral of $\rho g$ over the immersed volume. 4. The result is the **weight of the displaced fluid**, $B = \rho g \nabla$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship $C_P = C_B / C_M$ Starting from the definitions: $$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} \quad \text{and} \quad C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$ The prismatic coefficient is defined as: $$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$$ Now substitute $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ from the first equation: $$C_P = \frac{C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T}{A_M \cdot L} = \frac{C_B \cdot (B \cdot T)}{A_M}$$ But from the second equation, $\dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T} = C_M$, so $\dfrac{B \cdot T}{A_M} = \dfrac{1}{C_M}$. Therefore: $$\boxed{C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** $C_P$ measures how much the end portions of the hull are "cut away" relative to a prism of constant cross-section $A_M$. A $C_P = 1$ means a uniform "barge" shape; $C_P < 1$ means the ends are fine and tapered. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fresh Water Allowance (FWA) — Full Step-by-Step A ship in **salt water** at draft $T_{SW}$ displaces: $$\Delta = \rho_{SW} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{SW}$$ The same ship, when moved to **fresh water**, will float at a shallower draft $T_{FW}$ because the water is less dense. Equating the weight: $$\rho_{SW} \cdot \nabla_{SW} = \rho_{FW} \cdot \nabla_{FW}$$ (Assuming $g$ cancels; we work in mass-tonnes.) For small draft differences, $\nabla$ is approximately $A_{WP} \cdot T$ (waterplane area × mean draft): $$\rho_{SW} \cdot A_{WP} \cdot T_{SW} \approx \rho_{FW} \cdot A_{WP} \cdot T_{FW}$$ $$\frac{T_{FW}}{T_{SW}} = \frac{\rho_{SW}}{\rho_{FW}} = \frac{1.025}{1.000} = 1.025$$ Hence: $$T_{FW} = 1.025 \cdot T_{SW}$$ The **Fresh Water Allowance (FWA)** is the difference in draft: $$FWA = T_{SW} - T_{FW} = T_{SW} - 1.025 \cdot T_{SW}$$ $$FWA = T_{SW} \cdot \left(1 - 1.025\right)$$ Wait — this gives a **negative** value because $T_{FW} > T_{SW}$. The correct convention is: $$T_{FW} = T_{SW} \cdot \frac{\rho_{SW}}{\rho_{FW}}$$ A more practical formula, used in loading manuals, is: $$\boxed{FWA \;(\text{in cm}) = \frac{\Delta \cdot (\rho_{SW} - \rho_{FW})}{100 \cdot \rho_{SW} \cdot \rho_{FW}} \cdot \frac{1}{A_{WP}} \approx \frac{\Delta}{4 \cdot A_{WP} \cdot 25}}$$ A simpler approximate working formula (commonly used on board ship): $$FWA \;(\text{cm}) \approx \frac{W \;(\text{tonnes})}{4 \times TPC_{SW}}$$ where $TPC_{SW}$ is the tonnes-per-centimetre of immersion in salt water. > [!TIP] > **Working rule of thumb:** $FWA \approx \dfrac{\Delta}{100 \cdot TPC}$ (in cm), or the value is **read directly from the ship's Hydrostatic Table** for the given draft. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: TPC (Tonnes per Centimetre Immersion) If the ship sinks by an infinitesimal depth $\delta T$, the additional displaced volume is $A_{WP} \cdot \delta T$. The additional mass displaced is $\rho \cdot A_{WP} \cdot \delta T$. If $\delta T = 1 \text{ cm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$: $$TPC = \rho \cdot A_{WP} \cdot 0.01 = \frac{\rho \cdot A_{WP}}{100}$$ In salt water ($\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$): $$TPC_{SW} = \frac{1.025 \cdot A_{WP}}{100} = \frac{A_{WP}}{97.56} \;\; \text{(t/cm)}$$ $$\boxed{TPC = \frac{\rho \cdot A_{WP}}{100} \;\; \text{t/cm}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Form Coefficients on Ship Behaviour | Property | Increasing $C_B$ does… | Increasing $C_P$ does… | Increasing $C_M$ does… | |---|---|---|---| | Cargo Capacity | Increases | Slight increase | Marginal | | Resistance at low $F_n$ | Increases (more wetted surface) | Increases (less fine ends) | Marginal | | Resistance at high $F_n$ | Decreases (less wave-making) | Decreases (less end interference) | Marginal | | Stability (GM) | Marginal | Marginal | Marginal | | Seakeeping (motion) | Worsens (slower, stiffer) | Worsens | Marginal | | Typical Tanker | High $C_B$, High $C_P$ | — | — | | Typical Frigate | Low $C_B$, Low $C_P$ | — | — | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Force Balance on a Floating Ship ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Weight W
Downward force
Acts at Centre of Gravity G"]:::down node2["Buoyancy Delta
Upward force
Acts at Centre of Buoyancy B"]:::up node3["Hull of the Ship
Displaces water volume"]:::hull node4["Waterline WLF
Defines displaced volume"]:::wl node5["Equilibrium Check
W equals Delta"]:::eq node6["Floating Condition
Ship remains at rest"]:::result node1 --> node3 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node3 --> node5 node1 --> node5 node2 --> node5 node5 --> node6 classDef down fill:#ffd1d1,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef up fill:#d1e8ff,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 classDef hull fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b9770e,color:#000 classDef wl fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#239b56,color:#000 classDef eq fill:#e8d1ff,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000 classDef result fill:#d1fff0,stroke:#117864,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Form Coefficients Hierarchy (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1["GEOMETRIC INPUTS"] a1["L Length between perpendiculars"] a2["B Moulded Breadth"] a3["T Mean Draft"] a4["AM Midship Section Area"] a5["AWP Waterplane Area"] end subgraph S2["FORM COEFFICIENTS"] b1["CB = nabla divided by LBT"] b2["CM = AM divided by BT"] b3["CP = nabla divided by AML"] b4["CWP = AWP divided by LB"] end subgraph S3["ENGINEERING OUTPUTS"] c1["Resistance"] c2["Powering"] c3["Stability"] c4["Seakeeping"] c5["Cargo Capacity"] end a1 --> b1 a2 --> b1 a3 --> b1 a4 --> b2 a2 --> b2 a3 --> b2 a4 --> b3 a1 --> b3 a5 --> b4 a1 --> b4 a2 --> b4 b1 --> c1 b1 --> c5 b2 --> c3 b3 --> c1 b3 --> c2 b4 --> c3 b4 --> c4 classDef geom fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000 classDef coef fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 classDef out fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000 class a1,a2,a3,a4,a5 geom class b1,b2,b3,b4 coef class c1,c2,c3,c4,c5 out ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Concept Map: Weight vs Buoyancy at Various Loading States ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Lightweight Ship LWT"] --> A1["Hull plus Machinery plus Outfit"] B["Deadweight Cargo DWT"] --> A1 A1 --> C["Total Weight W"] C --> D["Ship Sinks Deeper"] D --> E["Larger Displaced Volume nabla"] E --> F["Larger Buoyancy Delta"] F --> G{"W equals Delta ?"} G -- YES --> H["Equilibrium Established"] G -- NO --> I["Ship continues to sink or rise"] J["Salt Water rho 1.025"] --> K["Less Volume Needed"] L["Fresh Water rho 1.000"] --> M["More Volume Needed"] K --> N["Lower Draft in Salt Water"] M --> O["Higher Draft in Fresh Water"] N --> P["FWA Positive Allowance"] O --> P classDef state fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#a04000,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000 classDef density fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 class A,A1,B,C,D,E,F,H,I state class G decision class J,K,L,M,N,O,P density ``` > [!NOTE] > The above block diagrams substitute for a physical lines-plan drawing. In an actual textbook, this would be illustrated by the **Body Plan, Sheer Plan, and Half-Breadth Plan** views of a ship — collectively called the **Lines Plan**. The form coefficients are numerical summaries of what those drawings depict graphically. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions --- ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** **Define the following terms with reference to a floating ship:** **(a)** Displacement **(b)** Reserve of Buoyancy **(c)** Tonnes per Centimetre Immersion (TPC) **Model Answer:** **(a) Displacement (Δ):** The weight of water displaced by the ship's hull at a given loading condition. By Archimedes' Principle, it equals the total weight of the ship. Unit: tonnes. Symbol: $\Delta$. **(b) Reserve of Buoyancy (RoB):** The additional volume of water that the ship could displace if submerged down to a deeper reference level (typically the upper deck), expressed as a percentage of the total displacement volume. It represents the safety margin against sinking. Typical values: 30 % to 60 %. **(c) TPC:** The change in displacement (in tonnes) caused by a 1 cm change in mean draft. Formula: $TPC = \dfrac{\rho \cdot A_{WP}}{100}$ t/cm. In salt water of density 1.025 t/m³, $TPC_{SW} \approx \dfrac{A_{WP}}{97.6}$ t/cm. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** **State the four principal form coefficients used in ship theory. Write their formulae in terms of the ship's principal dimensions and explain the physical meaning of the Block Coefficient $C_B$.** **Model Answer:** The four principal form coefficients are: 1. **Block Coefficient:** $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ 2. **Midship Coefficient:** $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ 3. **Prismatic Coefficient:** $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L} = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M}$ 4. **Waterplane Area Coefficient:** $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$ **Physical meaning of $C_B$:** The Block Coefficient expresses the ratio of the actual displaced volume of the hull to the volume of a rectangular box having the same length, breadth, and draft. A value of $C_B = 1.0$ would mean a perfect rectangular block (barge); $C_B = 0.0$ would mean no volume at all. Typical values range from 0.40 (fine warship) to 0.85 (full tanker). $C_B$ is the single most important indicator of hull fullness and is directly used in resistance and powering calculations. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** **(a)** A ship of length $L = 150$ m, breadth $B = 22$ m, and mean draft $T = 8.5$ m has a Block Coefficient $C_B = 0.72$. Calculate the **displacement volume** $\nabla$, the **displacement in salt water** (in tonnes), and the **TPC** in salt water, given the waterplane area coefficient $C_{WP} = 0.78$. Take $\rho_{SW} = 1.025$ t/m³. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the relationship between the four form coefficients. Show that $C_P = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M}$. Discuss how $C_B$ and $C_P$ influence the resistance of a ship at low and high Froude numbers. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A (a) **Step 1: Compute the displaced volume $\nabla$.** $$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} \implies \nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$$ Substituting values: $$\nabla = 0.72 \times 150 \times 22 \times 8.5 \; \text{m}^3$$ Compute step by step: $$150 \times 22 = 3300 \;\; \text{m}^2$$ $$3300 \times 8.5 = 28{,}050 \;\; \text{m}^3$$ $$\nabla = 0.72 \times 28{,}050 = 20{,}196 \;\; \text{m}^3$$ **Step 2: Compute the displacement in salt water.** $$\Delta = \rho_{SW} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 20{,}196$$ $$\Delta = 20{,}700.9 \;\; \text{tonnes}$$ **Step 3: Compute the waterplane area $A_{WP}$.** $$C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B} \implies A_{WP} = C_{WP} \cdot L \cdot B$$ $$A_{WP} = 0.78 \times 150 \times 22 = 0.78 \times 3300 = 2574 \;\; \text{m}^2$$ **Step 4: Compute the TPC in salt water.** $$TPC_{SW} = \frac{\rho_{SW} \cdot A_{WP}}{100} = \frac{1.025 \times 2574}{100}$$ $$TPC_{SW} = \frac{2638.35}{100} = 26.38 \;\; \text{t/cm}$$ **Final Answers:** - $\nabla = 20{,}196$ m³ - $\Delta = 20{,}700.9$ t (salt water) - $TPC_{SW} = 26.38$ t/cm --- #### Model Solution to Q.A (b) **Derivation of $C_P = C_B / C_M$:** Starting from the definitions: $$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} \quad \text{and} \quad C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$ The prismatic coefficient is defined as $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$. Rearranging the $C_B$ definition: $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$. Substituting: $$C_P = \frac{C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T}{A_M \cdot L} = \frac{C_B \cdot B \cdot T}{A_M}$$ Since $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$, we have $\dfrac{B \cdot T}{A_M} = \dfrac{1}{C_M}$. Therefore: $$\boxed{C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M}}$$ **Influence on resistance:** | Speed Regime | Effect of High $C_B$ | Effect of High $C_P$ | Physical Reason | |---|---|---|---| | Low $F_n$ (< 0.20) | Higher frictional + form resistance | Higher residual resistance | Larger wetted surface and blunt form | | High $F_n$ (> 0.30) | Lower wave resistance (slender form helps less) | Lower interference at ends | Smoother wave system at bow/stern | In practice, tankers (high $C_B$, high $C_P$) are optimised for low-speed cargo efficiency, while container ships (moderate $C_B$, low $C_P$) are fine-ended for high-speed wave reduction. --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** **(a)** A ship floats in **salt water** ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³) at a mean draft of $T_{SW} = 9.20$ m with a displacement of $\Delta = 25{,}000$ tonnes. The waterplane area is $A_{WP} = 2400$ m². If the ship enters a freshwater port ($\rho = 1.000$ t/m³), calculate the **new draft** $T_{FW}$ and the **Fresh Water Allowance (FWA)**. Also compute the **TPC in both waters**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Define the following and state their typical values for a VLCC tanker: - (i) Block Coefficient - (ii) Prismatic Coefficient - (iii) Reserve of Buoyancy - (iv) Deadweight Also explain the difference between **Displacement Tonnage** and **Deadweight Tonnage**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B (a) **Step 1: TPC in salt water.** $$TPC_{SW} = \frac{\rho_{SW} \cdot A_{WP}}{100} = \frac{1.025 \times 2400}{100} = \frac{2460}{100} = 24.60 \;\; \text{t/cm}$$ **Step 2: TPC in fresh water.** $$TPC_{FW} = \frac{\rho_{FW} \cdot A_{WP}}{100} = \frac{1.000 \times 2400}{100} = 24.00 \;\; \text{t/cm}$$ **Step 3: New draft in fresh water.** The displaced volume in salt water: $$\nabla_{SW} = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{SW}} = \frac{25{,}000}{1.025} = 24{,}390.24 \;\; \text{m}^3$$ In fresh water, the same weight requires a larger volume: $$\nabla_{FW} = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{FW}} = \frac{25{,}000}{1.000} = 25{,}000 \;\; \text{m}^3$$ Assuming $A_{WP}$ remains approximately constant over the small draft change, the new draft is: $$T_{FW} = \frac{\nabla_{FW}}{A_{WP}} = \frac{25{,}000}{2400} = 10.4167 \;\; \text{m}$$ **Step 4: Fresh Water Allowance.** $$FWA = T_{FW} - T_{SW} = 10.4167 - 9.20 = 1.2167 \;\; \text{m} = 121.67 \;\; \text{cm}$$ **Alternative verification using TPC:** A draft increase of 121.67 cm corresponds to an added displacement of: $$\Delta_{\text{added}} = 121.67 \times 24.60 \approx 2993 \;\; \text{t}$$ This extra buoyancy is what supports the ship in fresh water — it is conceptually the **weight of the extra volume of fresh water displaced** compared with the same mass of salt water. **Final Answers:** - $T_{FW} = 10.42$ m - $FWA = 121.67$ cm - $TPC_{SW} = 24.60$ t/cm - $TPC_{FW} = 24.00$ t/cm --- #### Model Solution to Q.B (b) **(i) Block Coefficient $C_B$:** Ratio of displaced volume to the product $L \cdot B \cdot T$. For a VLCC, $C_B \approx 0.80$ to $0.85$ (very full form, slow-speed efficient). **(ii) Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$:** Ratio of displaced volume to $A_M \cdot L$. For a VLCC, $C_P \approx 0.80$ to $0.85$. The ends are nearly as full as the midship section — characteristic of a "block" cargo carrier. **(iii) Reserve of Buoyancy:** The percentage of the total hull volume that lies above the waterline. For a VLCC, typical RoB is **30 % to 50 %**. **(iv) Deadweight (DWT):** The total variable load the ship can carry — cargo + fuel (HFO/MDO) + lubricating oil + fresh water + ballast + crew + stores + provisions. For a VLCC, DWT is typically **200,000 to 320,000 tonnes**. **Displacement Tonnage vs Deadweight Tonnage:** | Aspect | Displacement Tonnage (Δ) | Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) | |---|---|---| | Definition | Total weight of the ship and everything on board at a given condition | Maximum variable load the ship can carry | | Includes | Lightweight + Deadweight | Cargo + fuel + water + stores + crew | | Variable? | Yes — changes with loading | Fixed by design (design DWT) | | Measured by | Draft reading (from Hydrostatic tables) | Cargo manifest + tank soundings | | Warship usage | Used as the principal tonnage measure | Rarely quoted | | Merchant usage | Used for stability/draft checks | Used commercially (chartering, port dues) | The fundamental relationship: $\Delta = LWT + DWT$. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MISTAKES — KTU Board Valuation Patterns** > > 1. **Unit Errors in TPC:** TPC is in **t/cm**, NOT t/m. Converting incorrectly loses full marks. > 2. **Forgetting the waterplane area:** Many students incorrectly use $A_M$ (midship area) instead of $A_{WP}$ in TPC formulas. The two are different. > 3. **Sign of FWA:** FWA is the **draft increase** when moving from salt to fresh water. Some students write it as a negative value. By KTU convention, FWA is reported as a **positive number** indicating how much higher the ship floats in salt water than in fresh water. > 4. **Confusing TPC with displacement:** TPC is a *rate* (tonnes per centimetre), not an absolute weight. Multiplying TPC by draft does NOT give displacement. > 5. **In formula derivations**, examiners check whether the student can clearly state the *initial assumption* (e.g., "assuming $A_{WP}$ is approximately constant over a small draft change"). Omitting this loses 1–2 marks. > 6. **Sloppy sketches in 7-mark questions** that ask for diagrams: a missing arrow showing direction of $W$ vs $\Delta$ typically costs 1 mark. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Displacement $\Delta$** = weight of water displaced = ship's weight at that condition (Archimedes). - [x] **$W = \Delta$** is the **floating equilibrium** — remember this equation cold; it appears in nearly every question. - [x] **$\nabla$** denotes the **displaced volume** (m³), NOT the displacement. Don't confuse the two. - [x] **Standard densities:** SW = **1.025 t/m³**, FW = **1.000 t/m³**. Memorise. - [x] **Block Coefficient** $C_B = \nabla / (LBT)$ is the most important single descriptor of hull fullness. - [x] **$C_P = C_B / C_M$** is a frequently-derived identity — show the substitution step-by-step in the exam. - [x] **TPC = $\rho \cdot A_{WP} / 100$** — units **t/cm** — and it varies with water density. - [x] **FWA** is positive when the ship moves from SW to FW; ship sits **deeper in fresh water**. - [x] **Reserve of Buoyancy** = submerged volume above waterline as % of total submerged volume. - [x] **Deadweight** = total variable load; **Lightweight** = empty ship; **$\Delta = LWT + DWT$**. - [x] **Typical $C_B$ ranges:** Tanker ≈ 0.80–0.85; Bulk Carrier ≈ 0.75–0.82; Container ≈ 0.55–0.65; Warship ≈ 0.40–0.55. - [x] **High $C_B$ → low-speed, high-cargo**; **Low $C_B$ → high-speed, low-cargo**. - [x] **Plimsoll Mark / Load Line** markings: TF (Tropical Fresh), F (Fresh), T (Tropical), S (Summer — Standard), W (Winter), WNA (Winter North Atlantic). - [x] Always write the **unit** of the final answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless numerical answers. - [x] In derivation questions, explicitly state the **physical assumption** (e.g., "small draft change, $A_{WP}$ constant") before proceeding.

topics.title: Main Dimensions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Main Dimensions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Main Dimensions

In the discipline of **Naval Architecture**, the term **Main Dimensions** refers to the standardized set of geometric parameters that completely and unambiguously describe the **size and form** of a ship's hull envelope. These parameters serve as the **primary input variables** for every subsequent calculation in ship design, hydrostatics, stability analysis, resistance estimation, structural scantling, and statutory regulation.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCNST205 (Module 1)**, the Main Dimensions of a ship comprise the following six fundamental quantities:

1. **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP or Lₚₚ)** — The length measured between the **Forward Perpendicular (FP)** and the **Aft Perpendicular (AP)**.
2. **Length Overall (LOA or L_OA)** — The maximum length of the ship measured along the **design waterline** between the extreme forward and aft points of the hull (including any projections such as the bulbous bow or stern overhang).
3. **Length at Waterline (LWL)** — The length of the ship's waterline plane at the **design draft**.
4. **Breadth (B)** — The maximum **molded breadth** of the hull measured at the **midship section** (also called the design waterline beam).
5. **Depth (D)** — The **molded depth** measured vertically from the **baseline (keel)** to the **midship freeboard deck** at the side.
6. **Draft (T or d)** — The vertical distance from the **waterline** to the **baseline (keel)** at the midship section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The Main Dimensions are always expressed in **meters (m)** for international standards (IMO, IACS) and in **feet (ft)** for legacy British/Imperial classification. The standard conversion factors to be remembered are:
> **1 Nautical Mile = 1852 m = 6080 ft**
> **1 Foot = 0.3048 m**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body" Approach

Imagine you are describing a person for a tailor. You do not just say "make a shirt." You specify the **height, chest width, arm length, and waist depth**. The tailor can then build a perfectly fitting garment.

Similarly, a ship is a complex 3-D structure, but the Naval Architect does not need to describe every curve. The six Main Dimensions act as the "**vital statistics**" of the ship, providing a robust geometric envelope on which the entire hydrodynamic, structural, and regulatory analysis is anchored. Without these six numbers, no further analysis is mathematically possible.

## 1.3 Auxiliary Reference Dimensions

Beyond the six primary main dimensions, the following **auxiliary dimensions** are also considered part of the hull geometry in KTU Module 1:

- **Length on the Load Waterline (LWL)** — used in resistance estimation.
- **Molded Draft (T_M)** and **Extreme Draft (T_E)** — the latter includes any appendages below the keel.
- **Freeboard (F)** — the vertical distance from the waterline to the **upper deck** at side: **F = D − T**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Perpendiculars" Concept (Board-Examiner Favourite):**
> - **Forward Perpendicular (FP):** A vertical line passing through the point where the **design waterline (DWL)** intersects the **forward side of the stem** (for ships without a bulbous bow) or the **centerline of the rudder stock** axis extended (legacy IACS rule).
> - **Aft Perpendicular (AP):** A vertical line passing through the **centerline of the rudder stock** at the design waterline.
> - **LBP** is therefore **independent of any actual bow or stern shape overhang**, making it a clean, repeatable reference for all hydrostatic computations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Breakdown of Each Main Dimension

### (a) Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)

- **Geometric Origin:** Defined by FP and AP as explained above.
- **Use Case:** Used in **all hydrostatic and resistance calculations** because it is the horizontal reference over which form coefficients (Cb, Cp) are computed.
- **Typical Range:** **0.90 L_OA to 0.97 L_OA** depending on bow and stern overhang.
- **KTU Key Fact:** A bulbous bow extends the LOA beyond FP but does **not** change LBP.

### (b) Length Overall (LOA)

- **Geometric Origin:** The maximum hull extent in the longitudinal direction.
- **Use Case:** Used for **port berth allocation, canal transits (Panamax, Suezmax), and statutory length regulations**.
- **KTU Note:** LOA includes the bulbous bow's projection forward of FP.

### (c) Length at Waterline (LWL)

- **Geometric Origin:** The length of the waterplane at the **design draft**.
- **Use Case:** Critical for **wave-making resistance** estimation (Holtrop, Havelock formulas).
- **Relationship to LBP:** LWL differs from LBP due to **trim** and the **shape of the bow and stern waterlines**. For a ship in even keel (no trim): **LWL ≈ LBP + (bow overhang projection + stern overhang projection at WL)**.

### (d) Breadth (B)

- **Geometric Origin:** Maximum molded width at midship.
- **Use Case:** Determines **stability (GMT)**, **hull girder section modulus**, and **payload capacity**.
- **Typical B/LBP Ratios:**
  - Tankers: **B/LBP ≈ 0.15 to 0.18**
  - Container Ships: **B/LBP ≈ 0.20 to 0.32** (very high for stability under stacked containers)
  - Warships: **B/LBP ≈ 0.10 to 0.13** (slim for speed)

### (e) Depth (D)

- **Geometric Origin:** Keel to **midship freeboard deck** at the side.
- **Use Case:** Determines **hull girder strength**, **freeboard (F = D − T)**, and **number of decks**.
- **Regulation:** Governed by the **IMO International Load Line Convention (1966)**.

### (f) Draft (T)

- **Geometric Origin:** Baseline (keel) to design waterline at midship.
- **Use Case:** Determines **displacement**, **under-keel clearance**, and **port accessibility**.
- **Types:**
  - **Design Draft (T_D):** The draft at which the ship is designed for optimum performance.
  - **Scantling Draft (T_S):** The maximum draft allowed by structural strength.
  - **Load Draft (T_L):** The actual draft when fully loaded.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the single most important revision artifact for the KTU Module 1 exam.** Memorize every entry and its units.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Standard Value / Unit |
