|---|-----------|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Length Between Perpendiculars | $L_{BP}$ | Distance from FP to AP | meters (m) |
| 2 | Length Overall | $L_{OA}$ | Maximum longitudinal hull extent | m |
| 3 | Length at Waterline | $L_{WL}$ | Length of waterplane at design draft | m |
| 4 | Molded Breadth | $B$ | Max beam at midship section | m |
| 5 | Molded Depth | $D$ | Keel to freeboard deck at side | m |
| 6 | Design Draft | $T$ | Keel to design waterline | m |
| 7 | Freeboard | $F$ | $F = D - T$ | m |
| 8 | Block Coefficient | $C_B$ | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | 0.55 (fine) to 0.85 (full) |
| 9 | Midship Section Coefficient | $C_M$ | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | 0.90 to 0.99 |
| 10 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P$ | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}} = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M}$ | 0.55 to 0.85 |
| 11 | Waterplane Area Coefficient | $C_{WP}$ | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}$ | 0.65 to 0.90 |
| 12 | Volume of Displacement | $\nabla$ | $\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$ | m³ |
| 13 | Displacement (Mass) | $\Delta$ | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes (t) |
| 14 | Sea Water Density | $\rho_{sw}$ | Standard seawater | **1.025 t/m³** |
| 15 | Fresh Water Density | $\rho_{fw}$ | Standard freshwater | **1.000 t/m³** |
| 16 | Deadweight | $DWT$ | Total variable load (cargo + fuel + stores) | tonnes (t) |
| 17 | Wetted Surface Area | $S$ | Total underwater hull area in contact with water | m² |
| 18 | Length-to-Beam Ratio | $L/B$ | $L = L_{BP}$ | 4.5 (bulk) to 9.0 (warship) |
| 19 | Beam-to-Draft Ratio | $B/T$ | Stability vs. resistance indicator | 2.0 (tanker) to 4.0 (container) |
| 20 | Length-to-Depth Ratio | $L/D$ | Hull girder longitudinal strength | 10 to 15 |

## 2.3 Why These Dimensions Matter — Engineering Utility

- **Resistance and Powering:** The **slenderness ratio L/∇^(1/3)** and **B/T** directly control frictional, wave-making, and eddy resistance.
- **Stability:** The **metacentric height (GM)** is heavily dependent on B and the **vertical center of gravity (KG)**. Wider ships have higher initial GM.
- **Structural Strength:** The **hull girder bending moment** scales as $M \propto L^2 \cdot B \cdot T$, making L the dominant variable.
- **Statutory Compliance:** **Load Line Rules (Freeboard)** depend directly on D and T. **Tonnage Rules (TONNAGE 1969)** use a complex function of V and the molded dimensions.
- **Cargo Capacity:** **TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit)** capacity of a container ship is geometrically driven by L, B, and the number of decks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **C_B** (uses $L_{BP}$) with **C_V** (uses $L_{WL}$). The block coefficient **C_B is defined with $L_{BP}$**, while some resistance formulations use a "volumetric coefficient" $C_V$ based on $L_{WL}$. Always check the reference length in the formula.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between L_OA, L_BP, and Bow/Stern Overhangs

Let:
- $L_{bow}$ = Longitudinal projection of the bow beyond FP at the **design waterline**.
- $L_{stern}$ = Longitudinal projection of the stern beyond AP at the **design waterline**.

**Step 1:** Define the geometric envelope. The extreme forward point of the hull at the design waterline is $L_{bow}$ ahead of FP. The extreme aft point is $L_{stern}$ behind AP.

**Step 2:** Total length is the sum of all longitudinal segments between the two extreme points.

$$
L_{OA} = L_{bow} + L_{BP} + L_{stern}
$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging for L_BP:

$$
L_{BP} = L_{OA} - (L_{bow} + L_{stern})
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical Example. A bulk carrier has $L_{OA} = 230$ m, $L_{bow} = 4$ m, $L_{stern} = 6$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{BP} &= 230 - (4 + 6) \\
L_{BP} &= 230 - 10 \\
L_{BP} &= 220 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** For a modern bulk carrier, $L_{BP}/L_{OA} \approx 0.95$. The 5% difference is the cumulative bow and stern overhang, which is hydrodynamic but excluded from the hydrostatic baseline to keep form coefficients clean.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Block Coefficient (C_B) from First Principles

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of the **Block Coefficient** as the ratio of the **actual underwater volume** of the hull to the volume of the **rectangular box (L × B × T)** that just encloses it.

$$
C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

**Step 2:** The volume of displacement ∇ is the volume of water displaced by the underwater hull. By Archimedes' principle, the **mass of this water equals the displacement mass Δ**.

$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for ∇ from Step 1.

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the displacement is:

$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

**Step 5:** Numerical Example. A general cargo ship has $L_{BP} = 150$ m, $B = 22$ m, $T = 9$ m, $C_B = 0.68$, sailing in seawater ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= 0.68 \times 150 \times 22 \times 9 \\
\nabla &= 0.68 \times 29700 \\
\nabla &= 20196 \text{ m}^3 \\
\Delta &= 1.025 \times 20196 \\
\Delta &= 20700.9 \text{ tonnes} \approx 20701 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange the same formula to **solve for unknown C_B** if displacement is measured by load-line reading or draft survey:

$$
C_B = \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Prismatic Coefficient (C_P) and the "C_B = C_P × C_M" Identity

**Step 1:** Define the **Midship Section Coefficient** as the ratio of the actual transverse area at midship $A_M$ to the area of the bounding rectangle at midship:

$$
C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}
$$

**Step 2:** Define the **Prismatic Coefficient** as the ratio of the displaced volume to the volume obtained by extruding the midship section along the entire LBP:

$$
C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}}
$$

**Step 3:** Now substitute the expression for $\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$ and $A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T$ into the C_P expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= \frac{C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}{(C_M \cdot B \cdot T) \cdot L_{BP}} \\
C_P &= \frac{C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}{C_M \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} \\
C_P &= \frac{C_B}{C_M}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The well-known identity emerges:

$$
\boxed{C_B = C_P \times C_M}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerical Example. A container ship has $C_B = 0.65$ and $C_M = 0.97$. Find $C_P$.

$$
C_P = \frac{0.65}{0.97} \approx 0.670
$$

**Engineering Insight:** A **low C_P** (around 0.55–0.60) indicates a **fine, taper-ended hull** (warships, fast ferries). A **high C_P** (around 0.80–0.85) indicates a **full, blocky hull** (tankers, bulkers). C_P is the **best single indicator of hull slenderness**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Density Conversion (Fresh Water to Sea Water Draft)

When a ship moves from seawater ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³) to freshwater ($\rho = 1.000$ t/m³), it must sink deeper to displace the same weight.

**Step 1:** Displacement is conserved: $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_{sw} = \rho_{fw} \cdot \nabla_{fw}$.

**Step 2:** Assume the **block coefficient and the waterplane area** are approximately constant for small draft changes, so $\nabla \propto T$ and $A_{WP} \cdot \Delta T \approx \Delta \nabla$.

**Step 3:** The change in draft for the **same displacement** between two densities is given by the **Plimsoll Mark correction**:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP}} \cdot \left( \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}} - 1 \right)
$$

A simpler **practical approximation** often used in KTU problems is:

$$
\frac{T_{fw}}{T_{sw}} = \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}} = \frac{1.025}{1.000} = 1.025
$$

So $T_{fw} \approx T_{sw} \times 1.025$ (about a **2.5% increase in draft**).

**Numerical Example:** A ship has $T_{sw} = 10.0$ m. Its freshwater draft is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{fw} &= 10.0 \times 1.025 \\
T_{fw} &= 10.25 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Freeboard and the "Reserve Buoyancy" Concept

**Step 1:** Freeboard $F$ is defined as the **vertical distance** from the waterline to the **upper continuous deck** at the side:

$$
F = D - T
$$

**Step 2:** The **Reserve Buoyancy** is the additional volume of water the ship **could** displace before water floods the main deck. It is approximately:

$$
\text{Reserve Buoyancy} \approx A_{WP} \cdot F
$$

**Step 3:** As a fraction of total displacement volume:

$$
\text{Reserve Buoyancy Ratio} = \frac{A_{WP} \cdot F}{\nabla}
$$

**Step 4:** Typical IMO minimum freeboard values for cargo ships are in the range **0.05 D to 0.10 D**, ensuring a reserve buoyancy of roughly **20% to 50%** of the displacement volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** The relationship $F = D - T$ is a 2-second conceptual question, but it is **frequently asked** as a 3-mark problem. Students are expected to also identify which of the **Load Line Marks (TF, F, T, S, W, WNA)** corresponds to the calculated freeboard. Memorize the **Summer Freeboard** and **Tropical Freeboard** difference of approximately **1/48 × D** per zone.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Typical Values for Different Ship Types

| Ship Type | L_BP (m) | B (m) | T (m) | C_B | C_P | C_M | L/B | B/T |
|-----------|---------|-------|-------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Panamax Tanker | 220 | 32.2 | 12.5 | 0.81 | 0.83 | 0.98 | 6.8 | 2.6 |
| VLCC | 320 | 58.0 | 20.0 | 0.83 | 0.85 | 0.98 | 5.5 | 2.9 |
| Capesize Bulker | 285 | 45.0 | 18.0 | 0.82 | 0.84 | 0.98 | 6.3 | 2.5 |
| Container (8000 TEU) | 300 | 42.8 | 14.0 | 0.65 | 0.67 | 0.97 | 7.0 | 3.1 |
| Destroyer (Warship) | 145 | 18.0 | 6.0 | 0.50 | 0.61 | 0.82 | 8.1 | 3.0 |
| LNG Carrier (Moss) | 280 | 43.0 | 12.0 | 0.72 | 0.74 | 0.97 | 6.5 | 3.6 |
| Fishing Vessel | 30 | 7.5 | 3.5 | 0.55 | 0.65 | 0.85 | 4.0 | 2.1 |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation for KTU Exams:** A low $C_B$ combined with a high $C_M$ is the signature of a **fast warship**. A high $C_B$ and $C_M$ both near 0.98 is the signature of a **slow, full-form merchant ship**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Main Dimensions

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how each **Main Dimension** is geometrically located on the ship hull envelope and how they interact to feed downstream Naval Architecture calculations. This is a **fallback functional architecture map** (as mandated by the protocol for complex physical shapes that cannot be rendered directly in Mermaid).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Geometry Envelope] --> B[Length Family]
    A --> C[Breadth Family]
    A --> D[Depth-Draft Family]

    B --> B1[LOA: Extreme forward to extreme aft]
    B --> B2[LBP: FP to AP]
    B --> B3[LWL: Length at design waterline]

    C --> C1[Molded Breadth B at midship]
    C --> C2[Extreme Breadth: With shell plating]
    C --> C3[Waterplane Breadth]

    D --> D1[Molded Depth D: Keel to deck]
    D --> D2[Design Draft T: Keel to WL]
    D --> D3[Freeboard F: D minus T]

    B2 --> E[Hydrostatic Form Coefficients]
    C1 --> E
    D2 --> E

    E --> E1[Block Coefficient CB]
    E --> E2[Prismatic Coefficient CP]
    E --> E3[Midship Coefficient CM]
    E --> E4[Waterplane Coefficient CWP]

    E1 --> F[Displacement Volume Nabla]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    B2 --> F
    C1 --> F
    D2 --> F

    F --> G[Mass Displacement Delta = rho times Nabla]
    G --> H[Resistance and Powering Analysis]
    G --> I[Stability Analysis]
    G --> J[Structural Hull Girder Analysis]
    G --> K[Statutory Tonnage and Loadline]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Vertical Sectional View (Schematic Block Mapping)

This diagram is a **block-level functional mapping** of the vertical arrangement of D, T, and F on a ship cross-section, since Mermaid cannot physically draw curves.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TopDeck[Upper Deck Level] --> KeelDir[Baseline or Keel]
    KeelDir -->|Distance equals D| TopDeck
    KeelDir -->|Distance equals T| WaterLine[Design Waterline]
    WaterLine -->|Distance equals F| TopDeck
    WaterLine --> UnderwaterHull[Underwater Hull Volume Nabla]
    UnderwaterHull -->|Multiply by rho| MassDispl[Mass Displacement Delta]
    FreeboardVolume[Reserve Buoyancy = AWP times F] --> WaterLine
```

## 4.3 Schematic Plan View (Block-Level Mapping of Lengths)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Extreme Bow Point] -->|L bow| FP[Forward Perpendicular FP]
    FP -->|L BP| AP[Aft Perpendicular AP]
    AP -->|L stern| A2[Extreme Stern Point]
    A1 -->|L OA| A2
    WL[Length at Waterline LWL] -.->|Approximately equals LBP| FP
    WL -.-> AP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings:**
> The Main Dimensions form an **interconnected web of geometric and physical relationships**. A block diagram captures the **logical flow from raw dimensions → form coefficients → displacement → downstream analysis** far more rigorously than a physical sketch. KTU 2024 examiners often award marks for showing the **derivation chain**, not just the picture.

## 4.4 Schematic of the Perpendiculars and Waterline Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Forward Perpendicular FP] -->|Vertical line at design WL, stem intersection or rudder stock axis| P1[Point A]
    H2[Aft Perpendicular AP] -->|Vertical line at design WL, rudder stock axis| P2[Point B]
    P1 -->|LBP| P2
    H3[Design Waterline DWL] -->|Intersects hull at extreme forward and aft| P1
    H3 --> P2
    H3 --> H4[Length at Waterline LWL]
    H5[Baseline or Keel Line] -->|Molded Depth D| H6[Freeboard Deck]
    H5 -->|Design Draft T| H3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define Block Coefficient and state its typical range for a general cargo ship. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 1]

**Model Answer:**

The **Block Coefficient ($C_B$)** is the ratio of the **volume of displacement ($\nabla$)** of the ship to the volume of a **rectangular box (L_BP × B × T)** that just encloses the underwater hull at the design waterline.

$$
C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

**Typical Range:** For a general cargo ship, $C_B$ lies between **0.60 and 0.72**, with the most common design value being approximately **0.65 to 0.68**.

A **high $C_B$** (e.g., 0.80) indicates a **full, bluff hull** typical of slow-speed tankers, maximizing cargo capacity. A **low $C_B$** (e.g., 0.50) indicates a **fine, slender hull** typical of fast warships, minimizing wave-making resistance.

---

### Question 2. What is the difference between Length Overall (LOA) and Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)? Why are both used in ship design? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 1]

**Model Answer:**

- **Length Overall (LOA)** is the **maximum longitudinal extent** of the ship, measured from the **extreme forward point** (e.g., tip of a bulbous bow) to the **extreme aft point** (e.g., stern transom or rudder tip) along the centerline. It includes all projections and overhangs.
- **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)** is the length measured between the **Forward Perpendicular (FP)** and the **Aft Perpendicular (AP)**, which are **geometric reference lines** at the design waterline. It excludes bow and stern overhangs.

**Why Both Are Used:**

- **LOA** is used for **statutory and port regulations** (e.g., Panama Canal tolls, berth allocation, IMO tonnage rules for ship length categories).
- **LBP** is used for **all hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, and resistance calculations** because it provides a clean, repeatable geometric reference independent of the variable shape of the bow and stern.

The relationship is: $L_{OA} = L_{BP} + L_{bow} + L_{stern}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]

**A bulk carrier has the following main dimensions:**
- $L_{BP} = 200$ m
- $B = 32$ m
- $T = 12$ m
- $C_B = 0.78$
- $C_M = 0.98$

**Calculate:**

**(a)** The volume of displacement ($\nabla$) and the mass displacement ($\Delta$) in seawater. (**7 Marks**)

**(b)** The prismatic coefficient ($C_P$), and discuss how the calculated form coefficients compare with typical values for a full-form merchant vessel. (**7 Marks**)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Use the block coefficient definition.

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= 0.78 \times 200 \times 32 \times 12 \\
\nabla &= 0.78 \times 76800 \\
\nabla &= 59904 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Use Archimedes' principle with seawater density $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \rho \cdot \nabla \\
\Delta &= 1.025 \times 59904 \\
\Delta &= 61401.6 \text{ tonnes} \\
\Delta &\approx 61402 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $\nabla = 59904$ m³ and $\Delta = 61402$ tonnes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Use the identity $C_P = C_B / C_M$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= \frac{0.78}{0.98} \\
C_P &\approx 0.7959 \\
C_P &\approx 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Interpret the form coefficients.

- **$C_B = 0.78$** — High block coefficient indicates a **full, bluff hull form**, which is typical of slow-speed bulk carriers. The high value maximizes cargo-carrying capacity per unit length.
- **$C_M = 0.98$** — Very high midship coefficient indicates that the midship cross-section is **almost rectangular** (very little deadrise or flare), maximizing internal volume for cargo holds.
- **$C_P = 0.80$** — High prismatic coefficient indicates that the **end taper is very mild**, meaning the hull maintains nearly its full midship area for most of its length before tapering to the bow and stern. This is the signature of a **full-form merchant vessel**.

**Step 3:** Comparison with typical values (from Section 3.6). The calculated values ($C_B = 0.78$, $C_P = 0.80$, $C_M = 0.98$) are **very consistent** with the typical values for a Capesize Bulker ($C_B \approx 0.82$, $C_P \approx 0.84$, $C_M \approx 0.98$). The slightly lower $C_B$ in this problem suggests a **marginally finer** bulker optimized for a slightly higher service speed (~14–15 knots) compared to standard ~12-knot designs.

> **Answer (b):** $C_P = 0.80$. The form coefficients confirm a full-form merchant vessel with characteristics consistent with a modern bulk carrier.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]

**A general cargo ship has the following particulars:**
- $L_{OA} = 175$ m
- Bow overhang at DWL: $L_{bow} = 3.5$ m
- Stern overhang at DWL: $L_{stern} = 4.5$ m
- $B = 24$ m
- $D = 14$ m
- $T = 8.5$ m
- $C_B = 0.66$
- $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³

**Calculate:**

**(a)** The Length Between Perpendiculars ($L_{BP}$), the Freeboard ($F$), and the mass displacement ($\Delta$). (**7 Marks**)

**(b)** The midship section area ($A_M$) given that $C_M = 0.96$, and the reserve buoyancy ratio. (**7 Marks**)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Apply the length relationship $L_{OA} = L_{BP} + L_{bow} + L_{stern}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{BP} &= L_{OA} - L_{bow} - L_{stern} \\
L_{BP} &= 175 - 3.5 - 4.5 \\
L_{BP} &= 167 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the Freeboard.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= D - T \\
F &= 14 - 8.5 \\
F &= 5.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the volume of displacement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \\
\nabla &= 0.66 \times 167 \times 24 \times 8.5 \\
\nabla &= 0.66 \times 34068 \\
\nabla &= 22484.88 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the mass displacement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \rho \cdot \nabla \\
\Delta &= 1.025 \times 22484.88 \\
\Delta &= 23047.0 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $L_{BP} = 167$ m, $F = 5.5$ m, $\Delta = 23047$ t.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Calculate the midship section area.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_M &= C_M \cdot B \cdot T \\
A_M &= 0.96 \times 24 \times 8.5 \\
A_M &= 195.84 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the waterplane area (assume $C_{WP} = 0.78$ as a typical value, since not given).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{WP} &\approx C_{WP} \cdot L_{WL} \cdot B \\
A_{WP} &\approx 0.78 \times 167 \times 24 \\
A_{WP} &\approx 3126.24 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the reserve buoyancy volume.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reserve Buoyancy} &= A_{WP} \cdot F \\
&= 3126.24 \times 5.5 \\
&= 17194.32 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the reserve buoyancy ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reserve Buoyancy Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Reserve Buoyancy}}{\nabla} \\
&= \frac{17194.32}{22484.88} \\
&\approx 0.765 \text{ or } 76.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (b):** $A_M = 195.84$ m², Reserve Buoyancy Ratio ≈ 76.5%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong reference length in C_B:** The most common mistake is using $L_{OA}$ in the block coefficient formula. C_B is **defined using L_BP**, not L_OA. Loss of **2 marks** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing Draft and Depth:** $T$ is below the waterline, $D$ is the full molded depth. Freeboard $F = D - T$, not the other way around. Loss of **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting units of density:** $\Delta$ must be expressed in **tonnes** (metric tonnes = 1 Mg), and density in **t/m³**. Mixing kg/m³ with tonnes will cause a **factor of 1000 error** worth **3 marks**.
> 4. **Not using even-approx for seawater density:** Always use $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³ unless the problem explicitly states a different value.
> 5. **In the prismatic coefficient derivation:** Forgetting to divide $C_B$ by $C_M$ (or doing it as $C_M / C_B$) is the single most common error in 14-mark problems.
> 6. **Reserve Buoyancy not asked explicitly:** In some questions, students are asked to "comment on the safety of the ship" — this is a veiled request for the **reserve buoyancy ratio**. Failing to compute it loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Main Dimensions**

- [ ] **Six Main Dimensions:** $L_{BP}, L_{OA}, L_{WL}, B, D, T$ — know the geometric origin of each.
- [ ] **FP and AP Definition:** FP is at the forward intersection of DWL with the stem; AP is at the rudder stock centerline at DWL.
- [ ] **Length Relationship:** $L_{OA} = L_{BP} + L_{bow} + L_{stern}$ — memorize this identity.
- [ ] **Freeboard Identity:** $F = D - T$. This is non-negotiable.
- [ ] **Block Coefficient:** $C_B = \nabla / (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$. Uses **LBP**, not LOA.
- [ ] **Midship Coefficient:** $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$. Typical range 0.90–0.99.
- [ ] **Prismatic Coefficient:** $C_P = C_B / C_M = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L_{BP})$. Best indicator of slenderness.
- [ ] **Waterplane Coefficient:** $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (L_{WL} \cdot B)$.
- [ ] **Displacement Mass:** $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$. Use $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³ and $\rho_{fw} = 1.000$ t/m³.
- [ ] **Fresh vs. Sea Water Draft:** $T_{fw} \approx T_{sw} \times 1.025$ (2.5% increase).
- [ ] **Reserve Buoyancy:** $A_{WP} \cdot F$ is the safety volume above the waterline.
- [ ] **Tonnage Categories:** Always remember that **GRT and NRT** are volumetric (in GT and NT) and are **not** the same as **DWT or Displacement**. A common KTU trick is to ask the student to compare.
- [ ] **Unit Conversions:** 1 Nautical Mile = 1852 m = 6080 ft. 1 ft = 0.3048 m. Memorize.
- [ ] **Typical Ratios:** $L/B = 5$ to 9, $B/T = 2$ to 4, $L/D = 10$ to 15.
- [ ] **Form Coefficient Trifecta Identity:** $\boxed{C_B = C_P \times C_M}$ — never forget this, it appears in almost every Module 1 numerical.
- [ ] **Hull Form Recognition:** Low $C_B$ + low $C_M$ = warship. High $C_B$ + high $C_M$ = tanker/bulker. Medium $C_B$ + high $C_M$ + high $C_{WP}$ = container ship.
- [ ] **Statutory Bodies:** IMO (Load Line, Tonnage), IACS (Classification), ISO (Standards), Classification Societies (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS) — know the roles.

> **End of Module 1 — Main Dimensions Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lines Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lines Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Lines Plan** (also called the *Lines Drawing* or *Sheer Drawing*) is the most fundamental graphical representation in naval architecture. It is a set of **three mutually perpendicular, orthographic projections** that completely define the three-dimensional form (geometry) of a ship's hull by intersecting the hull surface with three families of mutually perpendicular parallel cutting planes.

The three classical views constituting the Lines Plan are:

| View Name | Plane of Projection | What it shows |
|---|---|---|
| **Profile / Sheer Plan** | Vertical longitudinal plane (centerline) | Side elevation; waterlines & buttock lines |
| **Body Plan** | Transverse plane at midships | Fore & aft transverse sections (stations) |
| **Half-Breadth Plan** | Horizontal (water) plane | Top view; waterlines & stations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** The Lines Plan is *not* a single drawing — it is a **complete drawing set** in which the intersection of three families of cutting planes (waterlines, buttocks, and stations) with the hull produces a curvilinear network whose coordinates (offsets) define the hull form unambiguously.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Watermelon Slicer"

Imagine a **watermelon** sitting on a table. To describe its exact shape on paper, a chef would:

1. **Slice it horizontally** at several heights → you get circles of different sizes (these are the **Waterlines**, WL).
2. **Slice it vertically, length-wise** from top to bottom in several places → you get curved profiles (these are the **Buttock Lines**, BL).
3. **Slice it vertically, side-to-side** like bread slices at several stations → you get D-shapes (these are the **Stations / Frame Sections**, S).

Now stack all these slices neatly on a table and look at them from three directions (side, front, top). The result is exactly a **Lines Plan** of the watermelon — except a ship hull is more pointed at the bow, fuller amidships, and tapered at the stern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world parallel:** A ship is a *hollow* watermelon with a flat bottom (or nearly so). The Lines Plan is the naval architect's "unrolled MRI scan" of the hull — once you have it, you can roll the ship back into 3D and know every curve of every plate.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

The Lines Plan is referenced to three **mutually perpendicular reference planes** that intersect at a single point called the **Frame Station Zero** (or sometimes amidships):

- **Baseline Plane (BL)** — horizontal plane passing through the lowest point of the hull (the top of the keel plate). All vertical dimensions (**heights**) are measured from this.
- **Centerline Plane (CL)** — vertical plane of symmetry dividing the hull into port and starboard halves. All transverse dimensions (**half-breadths**) are measured from this.
- **Midship Section Plane** — vertical transverse plane at mid-length, used as origin for **station numbers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard shipbuilding coordinate convention used in Lines Plans:
> - **X-axis:** Longitudinal — positive **forward** from amidships (or from the after perpendicular).
> - **Y-axis:** Transverse — positive to **starboard** (right hand side).
> - **Z-axis:** Vertical — positive **upward** from the baseline.
>
> Offsets are conventionally expressed as **(Half-Breadth, Height)** pairs at numbered stations.

---

## 1.4 Why the Lines Plan is the "DNA" of Every Ship

Every downstream naval-architecture activity depends on the Lines Plan:

- **Hydrostatics** → displacement, KB, BM, TPC are computed from the offsets.
- **Stability** → cross-curves of stability use the same offsets.
- **Resistance & powering** → wetted surface, form coefficients use the offsets.
- **Steel cutting** → plate profiles, frame bending schedules, shell plating expansions.
- **Strength** → section modulus at every station is derived from offsets.

> [!TIP]
> If the Lines Plan is wrong, **everything downstream is wrong**. In KTU valuation, examiners treat the Lines Plan as the *foundation document* and award marks for correctly identifying all three views and their relationships.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Families of Cutting Planes

The hull surface $\Sigma(x, y, z) = 0$ is intersected by three families of parallel planes:

### 2.1.1 Waterlines (WL) — Horizontal Family

Planes **parallel to the baseline** at constant heights $z = z_i$.

Each waterline produces a closed (or nearly closed) curve that represents the hull's girth at that height.

Typical numbering: $WL_0$ (baseline) up to $WL_{n}$ (deck edge), at equal vertical spacing $\Delta z$ (commonly 500 mm or 1 ft in practice).

### 2.1.2 Buttock Lines (BL) — Longitudinal Vertical Family

Planes **parallel to the centerline** at constant offsets $y = y_j$.

In the Sheer Plan, buttocks appear as **nearly straight, gently curving lines** sloping down from bow to stern at the design waterline.

Typical numbering: $BL_0$ (centerline), $BL_1, BL_2, \ldots$ to starboard, mirrored on port side.

### 2.1.3 Stations (S) — Transverse Vertical Family

Planes **perpendicular to the longitudinal axis** at constant $x = x_k$.

Each station produces a **transverse section** — a closed 2D curve that is what the shipwright actually frames with steel ribs.

Numbering: typically 20 stations (Station 0 at AP, Station 10 at midships, Station 20 at FP), or finer (every 1 m or every frame spacing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three and not four?** Mathematically, a smooth closed surface in 3D is *fully determined* by just **two** families of intersecting curves (since the third family is then automatically defined by intersection). However, naval architects use **three families** for:
> 1. **Cross-checking** (consistency of offsets).
> 2. **Ease of lofting** (long flexible wooden battens, called *lofting battens*, are faired smoothly through each set of points).
> 3. **Practical drafting** (the three views fit naturally on a drawing board).

---

## 2.2 The Offset Table — Numerical Backbone of the Lines Plan

The Lines Plan on paper is supported by a numerical **Offset Table** (also called the *Table of Offsets*). For every station $k$ and every waterline $i$, the table lists two values:

$$\text{Offset}(S_k, WL_i) = \left(\tfrac{B}{2}_{k,i},\ H_{k,i}\right)$$

where:
- $\tfrac{B}{2}_{k,i}$ = **half-breadth** of the hull at station $k$ on waterline $WL_i$ (measured from the centerline).
- $H_{k,i}$ = **height** of the same point above the baseline.

The port-side values are obtained by symmetry: $\tfrac{B}{2}_{port} = \tfrac{B}{2}_{stbd}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Offsets are **half-breadths**, not full breadths! Students frequently lose 1–2 marks by writing breadth instead of half-breadth in the offset table.

---

## 2.3 Principal Dimensions Read from the Lines Plan

| Dimension | Symbol | Read From | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length Between Perpendiculars | $L_{BP}$ | Sheer Plan (horizontal) | Distance between FP and AP |
| Length Over All | $L_{OA}$ | Sheer Plan (horizontal) | Extreme bow to extreme stern |
| Breadth (moulded) | $B$ | Body Plan (max) | Max half-breadth × 2 |
| Depth (moulded) | $D$ | Sheer Plan (vertical) | Baseline to strength deck at side |
| Draft (moulded) | $T$ | Sheer Plan (vertical) | Baseline to design waterline |
| Freeboard | $F$ | Sheer Plan | $F = D - T$ |
| Camber | $c$ | Half-Breadth Plan | Crown of deck from CL to side |
| Sheer | $s$ | Sheer Plan | Height of deck above WL along length |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Table

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Half-breadth symmetry | $y_{port} = y_{stbd}$ | m | Hull is mirror-symmetric about CL |
| 2 | Displacement (volumetric) | $\nabla = \int_0^L 2\,A(x)\,dx$ | m³ | $A(x)$ = sectional area at station $x$ |
| 3 | Sectional area (Simpson's 1/3) | $A \approx \frac{h}{3}[y_0 + 4(y_1+y_3+\dots) + 2(y_2+y_4+\dots) + y_n]$ | m² | $h$ = spacing of half-ordinates |
| 4 | Block coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP}\cdot B \cdot T}$ | – | Typical 0.55–0.85 |
| 5 | Prismatic coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}}$ | – | $A_M$ = midship sectional area |
| 6 | Waterplane coefficient | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_W}{L_{BP}\cdot B}$ | – | $A_W$ = waterplane area |
| 7 | Midship section coefficient | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | – | Typical 0.85–0.98 |
| 8 | Wetted surface (approx.) | $S \approx 1.7\,L\,\sqrt{L\cdot T}$ | m² | Denny's formula |
| 9 | Tons per cm immersion | $TPC = \dfrac{A_W \cdot \rho_{sw}}{100}$ | t/cm | $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³ |
| 10 | Freeboard | $F = D - T$ | m | Read off sheer plan |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All integration/summation for hydrostatics ultimately starts from the **Offset Table**, which is the numerical equivalent of the Lines Plan.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where the Lines Plan Goes in Real Life

1. **Lofting Floor** → the Lines Plan is laid out at full scale on a wooden floor; steel plates are cut to the expanded shapes derived from it.
2. **CAD/CAM** → modern shipyards digitize the Lines Plan into a NURBS surface model; offsets become a 3D mesh.
3. **Regulatory** → Classification Societies (IRS, LR, DNV) approve the Lines Plan as the basis for all strength and stability calculations.
4. **Model Testing** → hull models for towing tanks are manufactured directly from the Lines Plan (typically to 1:20–1:40 scale).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because "Lines Plan" is fundamentally a **graphical and descriptive** topic, the "derivations" here are analytical transitions: how one moves between the three views, how offsets generate hydrostatic quantities, and how geometric relationships are derived from the coordinate system.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — View-to-View Mapping

| From / To | Sheer Plan | Body Plan | Half-Breadth Plan |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sheer Plan** | — | Shares **height (Z)** and **station (X)** info | Shares **station (X)** and **waterline (Z)** info |
| **Body Plan** | Forward half = **forward stations**; Aft half = **aft stations** | — | Shares **half-breadth (Y)** and **waterline (Z)** info |
| **Half-Breadth Plan** | WLs appear as straight horizontal lines | Stations appear as straight lines radiating from CL | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory anchor for KTU exams:**
> - Sheer Plan ↔ Body Plan share **Heights** (Z-axis).
> - Sheer Plan ↔ Half-Breadth Plan share **Lengths** (X-axis).
> - Body Plan ↔ Half-Breadth Plan share **Half-Breadths** (Y-axis).

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Deriving the Displacement Volume from Offsets

The displacement volume $\nabla$ is the integral of the cross-sectional area $A(x)$ along the length:

$$\nabla = \int_{AP}^{FP} A(x)\,dx$$

Each sectional area $A(x_k)$ is computed by integrating the half-breadths along the waterlines at that station:

$$A(x_k) = 2 \int_{BL}^{Deck} \tfrac{B}{2}(z)\,dz$$

Discretizing with Simpson's 1/3 rule across $n+1$ waterlines with spacing $h_z$:

$$A(x_k) \approx \frac{2 h_z}{3}\left[y_0 + 4(y_1+y_3+\dots) + 2(y_2+y_4+\dots) + y_n\right]$$

Then integrating along the length using Simpson's 1/3 rule across the stations:

$$\nabla \approx \frac{h_x}{3}\left[A_0 + 4(A_1+A_3+\dots) + 2(A_2+A_4+\dots) + A_N\right]$$

This is the **double-Simpson's-rule** workflow that every KTU numerical question on hydrostatics assumes.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Coordinate Transformation Between Views

A point $P$ on the hull has coordinates $(x, y, z)$ in 3D. Its projections are:

| View | Coordinates Plotted | What you see |
|---|---|---|
| Sheer Plan | $(x, z)$ | Side view (port or starboard edge) |
| Body Plan | $(y, z)$ | Transverse section |
| Half-Breadth Plan | $(x, y)$ | Top view |

**Worked micro-example:**

Given offsets of a single point:
- Station: $S_{10}$ (midships), so $x = 0$
- Waterline: $WL_5$, so $z = 5.0$ m
- Half-breadth: $y = 4.2$ m

In the **Sheer Plan**: plot the point at $(x = 0,\ z = 5.0)$ → lies on the centreline of the sheer drawing.

In the **Body Plan**: plot at $(y = 4.2,\ z = 5.0)$ → lies on the starboard-side half of the body plan at midship section.

In the **Half-Breadth Plan**: plot at $(x = 0,\ y = 4.2)$ → lies on the centerline of the half-breadth view at the station line.

The **mirror image** at $(-4.2, 5.0)$ in the Body Plan and Half-Breadth Plan represents the port-side counterpart.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Identifying the Three Views from a Single Drawing

This is a frequent KTU Part A question. The procedure is:

1. **Look for a centreline (vertical line).** The view showing the centreline clearly with deck edge, waterlines, and buttock lines sloping down toward bow and stern is the **Sheer Plan**.
2. **Look for symmetry about a vertical axis** with curved sections resembling the ship's cross-section (D-shaped, U-shaped). The view showing **overlapping forward and aft sections, mirrored** is the **Body Plan**.
3. **Look for a horizontal centreline** with curved waterlines and transverse station lines. The view showing **the waterlines as nearly straight lines meeting at the bow** is the **Half-Breadth Plan**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Breakdown of Key Ship Form Terms

| Term | Definition | Read From |
|---|---|---|
| **Sheer** | Fore-and-aft curvature of the deck edge | Sheer Plan |
| **Camber** | Transverse curvature of the deck | Half-Breadth Plan |
| **Deadrise** | Rise of the bottom from CL to side at a station | Body Plan |
| **Tumblehome** | Inward curvature of the side above WL | Body Plan (upper part) |
| **Flare** | Outward curvature of the side above WL | Body Plan (upper part) |
| **Transom** | Flat aft-facing surface (aft of sternpost) | Sheer Plan / Half-Breadth Plan |
| **Stem** | Forward-most structural member | Sheer Plan |
| **Sternpost** | Aft-most vertical structural member | Sheer Plan |
| **Keel** | Lowest longitudinal structural member | Sheer Plan / Half-Breadth Plan |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** *Camber* is measured transversely (deck crown); *Sheer* is measured longitudinally. Do **not** interchange these in Part A answers — examiners routinely deduct marks for this confusion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Relationships Among the Three Views

The Lines Plan is a **3-view projection** of a 3D hull. The mermaid diagram below maps how the three cutting-plane families intersect and how their data flows between the views.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[3D Hull Surface sigma xyz] --> B[Waterline Planes z const]
    A --> C[Buttock Planes y const]
    A --> D[Station Planes x const]

    B --> E[Sheer Plan View xz]
    B --> F[Half Breadth Plan View xy]
    C --> E
    D --> G[Body Plan View yz]
    C --> F
    D --> E

    E --> H[Offset Table Numerical Data]
    G --> H
    F --> H

    H --> I[Hydrostatics Displacement TPC]
    H --> J[Stability Cross Curves]
    H --> K[Hull Strength Section Modulus]
    H --> L[Lofting and Plate Cutting]

    classDef planeStyle fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#f8fafc,stroke-width:2px
    classDef viewStyle fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0f172a,color:#f8fafc,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dataStyle fill:#b45309,stroke:#0f172a,color:#f8fafc,stroke-width:2px
    classDef appStyle fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#0f172a,color:#f8fafc,stroke-width:2px

    class A,B,C,D planeStyle
    class E,F,G viewStyle
    class H dataStyle
    class I,J,K,L appStyle
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — How an Offset Becomes a 3D Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Offset Table Row Station Sk, Waterline WLi] --> P2[Read Half Breadth y and Height z]
    P2 --> P3[Determine station number x from station table]
    P3 --> P4[Construct 3D coordinates x, y, z]
    P4 --> P5[Plot point in Sheer Plan using x and z]
    P4 --> P6[Plot point in Body Plan using y and z]
    P4 --> P7[Plot point in Half Breadth Plan using x and y]

    classDef inputStyle fill:#0369a1,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#f8fafc,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef procStyle fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,color:#f8fafc,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef plotStyle fill:#a16207,stroke:#713f12,color:#f8fafc,stroke-width:1.5px

    class P1,P2,P3 inputStyle
    class P4 procStyle
    class P5,P6,P7 plotStyle
```

---

## 4.3 Reference-Plane Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP["Reference Planes"]
        BL[Baseline Plane z equals 0]
        CL[Centerline Plane y equals 0]
        MS[Midship Plane x equals 0]
    end

    BL --> D1[Origin O at intersection of three planes]
    CL --> D1
    MS --> D1

    D1 --> E1[X axis forward positive]
    D1 --> E2[Y axis starboard positive]
    D1 --> E3[Z axis upward positive]

    E1 --> F1[Stations numbered from AP]
    E2 --> F2[Half Breadths measured from CL]
    E3 --> F3[Heights measured from Baseline]

    classDef planeStyle fill:#1e293b,stroke:#020617,color:#f8fafc,stroke-width:2px
    classDef axisStyle fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0e7490,color:#f8fafc,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef dimStyle fill:#65a30d,stroke:#365314,color:#f8fafc,stroke-width:1.5px

    class BL,CL,MS,D1 planeStyle
    class E1,E2,E3 axisStyle
    class F1,F2,F3 dimStyle
```

---

## 4.4 Lines-Plan Layout — Typical Sheet Organization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ST["Standard Drawing Sheet Layout"]
        ST1[Top Left: Sheer Plan Profile]
        ST2[Top Right: Body Plan]
        ST3[Bottom Right: Half Breadth Plan]
        ST4[Bottom Left: Offset Table and Principal Dimensions]
    end

    ST1 <--> ST2
    ST2 <--> ST3
    ST3 <--> ST4
    ST4 <--> ST1

    classDef sheetStyle fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#581c87,color:#f8fafc,stroke-width:1.5px
    class ST1,ST2,ST3,ST4 sheetStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to *sketch* a Lines Plan arrangement, place the Sheer Plan top-left, Body Plan top-right (so it shares heights with the Sheer Plan), and Half-Breadth Plan bottom-right (so it shares lengths with the Sheer Plan). This is the **standard naval-architectural layout** and is what examiners expect.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define the Lines Plan and name its three views.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Lines Plan is the complete set of three mutually perpendicular orthographic projections of a ship's hull, generated by intersecting the hull surface with three families of cutting planes — waterlines (horizontal), buttocks (longitudinal-vertical), and stations (transverse-vertical). The three views are:

1. **Profile / Sheer Plan** — side elevation in the centerline plane.
2. **Body Plan** — view in the transverse plane at midships, showing all station sections.
3. **Half-Breadth Plan** — top view in the horizontal (water) plane.

Each view shows two families of lines; the third family is shared between views.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** What is meant by an *offset* in the context of a ship's Lines Plan? Why are offsets expressed as half-breadths and not full breadths?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **offset** is the numerical coordinate $(y, z)$ defining a single point on the hull surface, given as **(half-breadth, height)** at a particular station and waterline.

Offsets are expressed as **half-breadths** (rather than full breadths) for the following reasons:
- **Symmetry:** Since the hull is symmetric about the centerline, one value automatically defines both port and starboard sides, **halving the size of the offset table**.
- **Drafting convenience:** Plotted against the centerline as the reference, half-breadths directly give the distance from the symmetry plane in the Body Plan and Half-Breadth Plan.
- **Numerical economy:** Fewer digits, fewer transcription errors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **three views** of a ship's Lines Plan. State clearly what each view shows and the reference planes involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship has the following principal dimensions: $L_{BP} = 120$ m, $B = 18$ m, $T = 6$ m, $C_B = 0.70$. Compute the **displacement volume** $\nabla$ and the **midship sectional area** $A_M$, assuming $C_P = 0.72$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — The Three Views (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The Lines Plan is a graphical representation of the hull's form obtained by projecting onto three mutually perpendicular planes.

**Step 2 (2 marks):**
- **Sheer Plan (Profile View):** Drawn in the **centerline vertical plane**. Shows the side elevation of the hull. Displays **waterlines** (horizontal lines at constant heights) and **buttock lines** (lines of constant half-breadth projected onto the centerline). The stem, sternpost, keel, design waterline, and sheer line are all read off this view.

- **Body Plan:** Drawn in the **transverse vertical plane at midships**. Shows the **transverse sections (stations)** of the hull. By convention, **forward stations are drawn on the right side** of the centerline and **aft stations on the left side** (or vice-versa, depending on the yard).

- **Half-Breadth Plan:** Drawn in the **horizontal (water) plane**. Shows a top view. Displays **waterlines as horizontal curves** that converge toward the bow and **station lines as nearly vertical curves**.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The three views share common information:
- Sheer Plan ↔ Body Plan share **heights (Z)**.
- Sheer Plan ↔ Half-Breadth Plan share **lengths (X)**.
- Body Plan ↔ Half-Breadth Plan share **half-breadths (Y)**.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** **Sketch** of the standard Lines Plan arrangement (Sheer Plan top-left, Body Plan top-right, Half-Breadth Plan bottom-right with offset table bottom-left).

---

#### Part (b) — Hydrostatic Calculations (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $L_{BP} = 120$ m
- $B = 18$ m
- $T = 6$ m
- $C_B = 0.70$
- $C_P = 0.72$

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Displacement Volume $\nabla$:**

By definition of block coefficient:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP}\cdot B\cdot T}$$

Therefore:

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP}\cdot B\cdot T$$

$$\nabla = 0.70 \times 120 \times 18 \times 6$$

$$\nabla = 0.70 \times 12960 = 9072\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Displacement Mass $\Delta$:**

Using seawater density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³:

$$\Delta = \nabla \cdot \rho_{sw} = 9072 \times 1.025 = 9298.8\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Midship Sectional Area $A_M$:**

By definition of prismatic coefficient:

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}}$$

Therefore:

$$A_M = \frac{\nabla}{C_P \cdot L_{BP}}$$

$$A_M = \frac{9072}{0.72 \times 120} = \frac{9072}{86.4} = 105\ \text{m}^2$$

**Verification (1 mark):** Check $C_M$:

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B\cdot T} = \frac{105}{18\times 6} = \frac{105}{108} = 0.972$$

This is a reasonable value for a merchant ship (typical range 0.85–0.98). ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **coordinate system** and **three reference planes** used in preparing a ship's Lines Plan. Define the terms *baseline*, *centerline plane*, and *midship section*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The offset table for a ship at station $S_5$ (located 25 m forward of amidships) gives the following half-breadths at successive waterlines (spacing 1 m): $0.00,\ 3.10,\ 5.20,\ 6.40,\ 6.80,\ 6.50,\ 5.40,\ 3.20$ m at heights $z = 0,\ 1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6,\ 7$ m. Compute the **sectional area** at $S_5$ using **Simpson's 1/3 rule**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Coordinate System and Reference Planes (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Three mutually perpendicular reference planes define the Lines Plan coordinate system. They intersect at a single point called the **Frame Station Zero** or **Reference Origin** $O$.

**Step 2 (3 marks):**

- **Baseline Plane (BL):** A horizontal plane tangent to the lowest point of the hull (top of the keel plate or the garboard line). All vertical dimensions — *heights, drafts, depths* — are measured **upward** from this plane.
- **Centerline Plane (CL):** A vertical plane of symmetry dividing the hull into port (left) and starboard (right) halves. All transverse dimensions — *half-breadths, waterline girths* — are measured **outward** from this plane to either side.
- **Midship Section Plane:** A vertical transverse plane located at the mid-length of the ship (midway between FP and AP). It serves as the **origin for station numbering** in the longitudinal direction.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The coordinate axes are:
- **X-axis:** longitudinal, positive **forward** (measured from amidships or AP).
- **Y-axis:** transverse, positive to **starboard**.
- **Z-axis:** vertical, positive **upward** from the baseline.

Any point $P$ on the hull is identified by offsets $(x, y, z)$, with $y$ given as half-breadth.

---

#### Part (b) — Sectional Area by Simpson's 1/3 Rule (7 marks)

**Given:** 8 half-ordinates (so $n = 7$ intervals, which is suitable for Simpson's 1/3 rule since $n$ is odd → 8 ordinates is even → applicable).

| $i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $z_i$ (m) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| $y_i$ (m) | 0.00 | 3.10 | 5.20 | 6.40 | 6.80 | 6.50 | 5.40 | 3.20 |

Common spacing $h = 1$ m.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply Simpson's 1/3 rule for the half-area first:

$$A_{half} = \frac{h}{3}\left[y_0 + 4(y_1+y_3+y_5) + 2(y_2+y_4+y_6) + y_7\right]$$

Substituting:

$$A_{half} = \frac{1}{3}\bigl[0.00 + 4(3.10+6.40+6.50) + 2(5.20+6.80+5.40) + 3.20\bigr]$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}\bigl[0.00 + 4(16.00) + 2(17.40) + 3.20\bigr]$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}\bigl[0.00 + 64.00 + 34.80 + 3.20\bigr]$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}(102.00) = 34.00\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Full sectional area (doubling for both halves of the hull by symmetry):

$$A_{S_5} = 2 \times A_{half} = 2 \times 34.00 = 68.00\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Verification by **Trapezoidal Rule** (sanity check):

$$A_{half,trap} = h\left[\tfrac{y_0+y_7}{2} + (y_1+y_2+y_3+y_4+y_5+y_6)\right]$$

$$= 1 \times \bigl[\tfrac{0+3.20}{2} + (3.10+5.20+6.40+6.80+6.50+5.40)\bigr]$$

$$= 1.60 + 33.40 = 35.00\ \text{m}^2$$

$$A_{trap} = 2 \times 35.00 = 70.00\ \text{m}^2$$

The two values (68.00 m² from Simpson's, 70.00 m² from Trapezoidal) are within **~3%** of each other, which is reasonable for a smoothly curved ship section. Simpson's 1/3 rule is the **more accurate** estimator for such smooth curves and should be preferred for KTU numerical answers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Half-breadths vs. full breadths:** Offsets are *half*-breadths. Marks lost if you forget the factor of 2 when computing sectional area.
> 2. **Simpson's rule requires an *even* number of intervals (odd number of ordinates + 1).** With 8 ordinates you have 7 intervals — **not applicable** unless you drop the last ordinate or use a modified rule. Always check this before applying Simpson's 1/3.
> 3. **Wrong view identification:** Many students confuse Body Plan (transverse sections) with Half-Breadth Plan (top view). Memorize: *Body Plan = "Bread Loaf Slices"*.
> 4. **Coordinate sign convention:** Offsets are conventionally given as positive half-breadths; symmetry automatically gives the port-side counterpart.
> 5. **Freeboard vs. Depth:** $D$ = baseline to deck at side; $T$ = baseline to WL; $F = D - T$. Examiners deduct marks if these are confused.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **Lines Plan** is a **set of three mutually perpendicular orthographic projections** of the hull, not a single drawing.
- [x] The three views are: **Sheer Plan** (side, in CL plane), **Body Plan** (transverse, at midships), **Half-Breadth Plan** (top, in horizontal plane).
- [x] Three families of cutting planes: **Waterlines** (constant $z$), **Buttock Lines** (constant $y$), **Stations** (constant $x$).
- [x] Three reference planes intersect at the origin: **Baseline**, **Centerline Plane**, **Midship Plane**.
- [x] Coordinate convention: **X forward, Y starboard, Z upward**.
- [x] Offsets are tabulated as **(half-breadth, height)** pairs — not full breadths.
- [x] The hull is **mirror-symmetric** about the centerline → port-side values are mirrored from starboard.
- [x] The Offset Table is the **numerical database** underlying the Lines Plan; hydrostatics, stability, and strength all derive from it.
- [x] **Block coefficient** $C_B = \nabla/(L_{BP}BT)$ — relates displacement volume to principal dimensions.
- [x] **Prismatic coefficient** $C_P = \nabla/(A_M L_{BP})$ — relates $\nabla$ to midship area.
- [x] **Midship coefficient** $C_M = A_M/(BT)$ — typical 0.85–0.98 for merchant ships.
- [x] **Sheer** = longitudinal deck curvature; **Camber** = transverse deck curvature. Do **not** interchange.
- [x] **Simpson's 1/3 Rule** requires an **odd number of ordinates** (i.e., an even number of intervals).
- [x] Standard drawing-sheet layout: Sheer Plan top-left, Body Plan top-right, Half-Breadth Plan bottom-right, Offset Table bottom-left.
- [x] The Lines Plan is the **single most important document** in naval architecture — every downstream calculation (hydrostatics, stability, resistance, strength, plate cutting) starts from it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coefficients and Their Meaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Coefficients and Their Meaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Form Coefficients?

In Naval Architecture, a ship's hull is an extremely complex three-dimensional curved surface. To numerically describe the "fatness" or "fineness" of this shape, naval architects use dimensionless ratios known as **Form Coefficients** (or simply *Ship Coefficients*). These coefficients are the single most important set of parameters used to compare the geometric efficiency of one ship against another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *Form coefficients are dimensionless ratios that compare the actual volume/area of a ship's hull to the volume/area of standard reference geometric shapes (prisms, cylinders, or rectangular blocks) that circumscribe it.*

### 1.2 The Six Principal Form Coefficients

The complete set of form coefficients covered in **Module 1** of the PCNST205 syllabus are:

| # | Coefficient | Compares | Symbol |
|---|------------|----------|--------|
| 1 | Block Coefficient | Displacement Volume vs. Rectangular Block (L × B × T) | $C_B$ |
| 2 | Midship Coefficient | Midship Section Area vs. Rectangle (B × T) | $C_M$ |
| 3 | Prismatic Coefficient | Displacement Volume vs. Longitudinal Prism (L × A_M) | $C_P$ |
| 4 | Waterplane Coefficient | Waterplane Area vs. Rectangle (L × B) | $C_{WP}$ |
| 5 | Longitudinal Coefficient | Displacement Volume vs. Prism (L × A_W) | $C_L$ |
| 6 | Vertical Prismatic Coefficient | Displacement Volume vs. Vertical Prism (T × A_W) | $C_{VP}$ |

### 1.3 The "Ice-Cream Scoop" Analogy

Imagine you have a rectangular block of ice-cream. If you scoop it out into a long, thin, streamlined shape (like a torpedo), the resulting shape has a **low** block coefficient. If you barely scoop it — leaving it almost as a chunky box — it has a **high** block coefficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:**
> - A **full** ship (tanker, bulk carrier) → Coefficients are **high** (close to 1.0).
> - A **fast/lean** ship (destroyer, frigate, container ship) → Coefficients are **low** (closer to 0.5).
> - The block coefficient is the **"fatness index"** of the entire hull.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Dimensions

Every form coefficient uses the ship's **Principal Dimensions** as the reference. Per KTU 2024 syllabus notation:

- **L** = Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP) — measured in **metres (m)**
- **B** = Moulded Breadth — measured in **m**
- **T** = Moulded Draft — measured in **m**
- $\nabla$ = Volume of Displacement (or Displacement Volume) — measured in **m³**
- $A_M$ = Area of the Midship Section (Section at amidships) — measured in **m²**
- $A_W$ = Area of the Waterplane (at the design waterline) — measured in **m²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All form coefficients are **dimensionless** (pure numbers). They are independent of the ship's size and depend *only* on the hull shape. Two ships of completely different sizes can have the same $C_B$ if they share a similar hull form.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Coefficients — Exhaustive Theoretical Breakdown

#### (A) Block Coefficient ($C_B$)

**Definition:** The ratio of the volume of displacement ($\nabla$) of the ship to the volume of a rectangular block having the ship's length (L), breadth (B), and draft (T).

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \times B \times T}$$

- **Physical Meaning:** It indicates the "fullness" of the underwater hull. It is the **most widely used** form coefficient in ship design.
- **Typical Range:** 0.40 (very fine warships) to 0.90 (full-form tankers).
- **High $C_B$** → Ship carries more cargo for a given L, B, T → Better cargo capacity, but higher resistance and lower speed.
- **Low $C_B$** → Sleek, fine hull → Lower resistance, higher speed, but less cargo capacity.

#### (B) Midship Coefficient ($C_M$)

**Definition:** The ratio of the cross-sectional area of the ship at amidships ($A_M$) to the area of its circumscribing rectangle (B × T).

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \times T}$$

- **Physical Meaning:** Describes the "fullness" of the **largest cross-section** of the ship.
- **Typical Range:** 0.90 to 0.99. Most ship designs use values between **0.92 and 0.98**.
- The midship coefficient is always the **highest** of all form coefficients because the midship section is the bulkiest part of the ship.

#### (C) Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$)

**Definition:** The ratio of the volume of displacement ($\nabla$) to the volume of a prism whose cross-section is the midship area and whose length is L.

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \times L}$$

- **Physical Meaning:** Indicates the **longitudinal taper** of the hull. It tells us how much the hull "tapers" from the midship section towards the bow and stern.
- **Typical Range:** 0.55 (fine ends) to 0.85 (full ends).
- A low $C_P$ means the ship has very fine, sharp ends (a needle-like hull). A high $C_P$ means the ends are full (almost box-shaped).

#### (D) Waterplane Coefficient ($C_{WP}$)

**Definition:** The ratio of the waterplane area ($A_W$) at the design waterline to the area of its circumscribing rectangle (L × B).

$$C_{WP} = \frac{A_W}{L \times B}$$

- **Physical Meaning:** Indicates the "fullness" of the waterplane (the flat deck-level slice through the ship at the waterline).
- **Typical Range:** 0.65 to 0.90.
- This coefficient is critical because it directly affects the **stability** of the ship. A higher $C_{WP}$ gives a larger initial moment of inertia of the waterplane area, which improves the ship's stiffness in roll.

#### (E) Longitudinal Coefficient ($C_L$)

**Definition:** The ratio of the volume of displacement ($\nabla$) to the volume of a prism whose length is L and cross-section is the waterplane area $A_W$.

$$C_L = \frac{\nabla}{A_W \times L}$$

- **Physical Meaning:** Describes the longitudinal taper of the hull **with reference to the waterplane** rather than the midship area.
- **Typical Range:** 0.55 to 0.85.

#### (F) Vertical Prismatic Coefficient ($C_{VP}$)

**Definition:** The ratio of the volume of displacement ($\nabla$) to the volume of a vertical prism whose height is T and cross-section is the waterplane area $A_W$.

$$C_{VP} = \frac{\nabla}{A_W \times T}$$

- **Physical Meaning:** Describes the vertical taper of the hull from the waterline down to the keel.
- **Typical Range:** 0.50 to 0.85.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — Memorise All Formulas Below (Board Favourite)**

| Coefficient | Formula | Compares (Numerator) | Reference (Denominator) | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Block | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \, B \, T}$ | Displacement Volume | Block $L \times B \times T$ | 0.55 – 0.85 |
| Midship | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \, T}$ | Midship Area | Rectangle $B \times T$ | 0.92 – 0.98 |
| Prismatic | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \, L}$ | Displacement Volume | Prism $L \times A_M$ | 0.60 – 0.85 |
| Waterplane | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_W}{L \, B}$ | Waterplane Area | Rectangle $L \times B$ | 0.70 – 0.90 |
| Longitudinal | $C_L = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_W \, L}$ | Displacement Volume | Prism $L \times A_W$ | 0.60 – 0.85 |
| Vertical Prismatic | $C_{VP} = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_W \, T}$ | Displacement Volume | Prism $T \times A_W$ | 0.60 – 0.85 |

### 2.3 Critical Inter-Coefficient Relationships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These relationships are **guaranteed exam questions**. KTU examiners ask students to derive these identities almost every year.

**Relationship 1 (The Master Identity):**
$$C_B = C_P \times C_M$$

**Relationship 2 (The Vertical Identity):**
$$C_B = C_{VP} \times C_{WP}$$

**Relationship 3 (Linking Longitudinal & Vertical Coefficients):**
$$C_L = C_{VP} \times \frac{T}{L}$$

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why Do We Need These Coefficients?

1. **Preliminary Design Stage:** When a naval architect first sketches a new ship, they don't draw the hull. They pick typical $C_B$ and $C_M$ values from statistical data, and then they know the displacement and the midship area **instantly**.
2. **Resistance Estimation:** $C_B$ is the most important input in **Holtrop-Mennen** and **ITTC** statistical resistance estimation methods. A 1% change in $C_B$ can change the required EHP by 5–10%.
3. **Stability Analysis:** $C_{WP}$ directly governs the ship's initial GM (metacentric height) and roll period.
4. **Comparison Tool:** Allows fair comparison of hull forms across different ship sizes.
5. **Propulsion & Hull Interaction:** $C_P$ and $C_M$ together indicate how the water flows past the hull, affecting propulsive efficiency.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $C_B = C_P \times C_M$

This is the most important derivation in Module 1. The KTU board examiner will expect every line.

**Step 1 — Write down the three fundamental definitions.**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \, B \, T}$$

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \, L}$$

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \, T}$$

**Step 2 — Form the product $C_P \times C_M$.**

$$C_P \times C_M = \left( \frac{\nabla}{A_M \, L} \right) \times \left( \frac{A_M}{B \, T} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the common term $A_M$ (midship area) appearing in the numerator and denominator.**

$$C_P \times C_M = \frac{\nabla \, \cancel{A_M}}{\cancel{A_M} \, L \, B \, T}$$

**Step 4 — Observe that the resulting expression is exactly the definition of $C_B$.**

$$C_P \times C_M = \frac{\nabla}{L \, B \, T} = C_B$$

$$\boxed{C_B = C_P \times C_M} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of $C_B = C_{VP} \times C_{WP}$

**Step 1 — Write down the two relevant definitions.**

$$C_{VP} = \frac{\nabla}{A_W \, T}$$

$$C_{WP} = \frac{A_W}{L \, B}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply the two coefficients.**

$$C_{VP} \times C_{WP} = \left( \frac{\nabla}{A_W \, T} \right) \times \left( \frac{A_W}{L \, B} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the waterplane area $A_W$ from numerator and denominator.**

$$C_{VP} \times C_{WP} = \frac{\nabla \, \cancel{A_W}}{\cancel{A_W} \, L \, B \, T}$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the result as the block coefficient definition.**

$$C_{VP} \times C_{WP} = \frac{\nabla}{L \, B \, T} = C_B$$

$$\boxed{C_B = C_{VP} \times C_{WP}} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of $C_L = C_{VP} \times \dfrac{T}{L}$

**Step 1 — Express $C_L$ using its definition.**

$$C_L = \frac{\nabla}{A_W \, L}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply and divide by $T$ (a smart algebraic manipulation).**

$$C_L = \frac{\nabla}{A_W \, L} \times \frac{T}{T} = \frac{\nabla}{A_W \, T} \times \frac{T}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the first fraction as $C_{VP}$.**

$$C_L = C_{VP} \times \frac{T}{L}$$

$$\boxed{C_L = C_{VP} \times \frac{T}{L}} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Model Numerical Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical:** A ship has the following particulars: L = 200 m, B = 28 m, T = 9 m. Its midship section area is 235 m² and its waterplane area is 4200 m². If the displacement volume is 47,880 m³, calculate: (i) $C_B$, (ii) $C_M$, (iii) $C_P$, (iv) $C_{WP}$, and (v) verify the identity $C_B = C_P \times C_M$.

**Given Data:**
- $L = 200 \text{ m}$, $\quad B = 28 \text{ m}$, $\quad T = 9 \text{ m}$
- $A_M = 235 \text{ m}^2$, $\quad A_W = 4200 \text{ m}^2$, $\quad \nabla = 47{,}880 \text{ m}^3$

**Part (i) — Block Coefficient:**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \, B \, T}$$

$$C_B = \frac{47{,}880}{200 \times 28 \times 9}$$

$$C_B = \frac{47{,}880}{50{,}400}$$

$$\boxed{C_B = 0.95}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A $C_B$ of 0.95 is extremely high (typical of a slow, full-form bulk carrier or tanker).

**Part (ii) — Midship Coefficient:**

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \, T} = \frac{235}{28 \times 9} = \frac{235}{252}$$

$$\boxed{C_M = 0.933}$$

**Part (iii) — Prismatic Coefficient:**

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \, L} = \frac{47{,}880}{235 \times 200} = \frac{47{,}880}{47{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{C_P = 1.019}$$

> **Engineering Check:** Wait — a prismatic coefficient greater than 1.0 is **physically impossible** ($C_P \le 1$ by definition). This means there is a calculation or data error in the problem (a classic KTU trap). The values are internally inconsistent, which is itself a learning point about how these coefficients are used to **validate** ship data.

> *(For a realistic case, the problem would need $A_M \ge 239.4 \text{ m}^2$ to keep $C_P \le 1$.)*

**Part (iv) — Waterplane Coefficient:**

$$C_{WP} = \frac{A_W}{L \, B} = \frac{4200}{200 \times 28} = \frac{4200}{5600}$$

$$\boxed{C_{WP} = 0.75}$$

**Part (v) — Verification of $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ (using our calculated $C_B = 0.95$):**

$$C_P \times C_M = 1.019 \times 0.933 = 0.951 \approx C_B = 0.95$$

The small discrepancy (0.951 vs 0.95) is due to rounding. The identity holds.

---

### 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — Coefficient Selection for Different Ship Types

| Ship Type | $C_B$ | $C_M$ | $C_P$ | $C_{WP}$ | Hull Form Character |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tanker / Bulk Carrier** | 0.80 – 0.85 | 0.98 – 0.99 | 0.80 – 0.85 | 0.85 – 0.90 | Very full, blocky |
| **General Cargo** | 0.65 – 0.75 | 0.95 – 0.98 | 0.68 – 0.78 | 0.75 – 0.85 | Medium fullness |
| **Container Ship** | 0.55 – 0.65 | 0.92 – 0.96 | 0.60 – 0.70 | 0.70 – 0.80 | Fine, sharp ends |
| **Naval Frigate / Corvette** | 0.45 – 0.55 | 0.85 – 0.92 | 0.55 – 0.65 | 0.65 – 0.75 | Very fine, high-speed |
| **Aircraft Carrier** | 0.55 – 0.65 | 0.90 – 0.95 | 0.60 – 0.70 | 0.70 – 0.80 | Fine for size |
| **Sailing Yacht** | 0.30 – 0.45 | 0.75 – 0.85 | 0.40 – 0.55 | 0.60 – 0.70 | Ultra-fine, slender |

### 4.3 ASCII Sectional Sketch (Conceptual)

The sketch below shows the **midship section** (a "slice" of the ship at amidships, viewed from the bow). The shaded region is the actual hull cross-section ($A_M$), and the dashed rectangle is the circumscribing rectangle ($B \times T$).

```
        <--------- B (Breadth) --------->
        +-------------------------------+   ^
        |                               |   |
        |        Ship's                 |   |
        |       Waterline              |   |
        +-------------------------------+   |
        |                              /|   |
        |   A_M (Shaded)              / |   |
        |  (Midship Section)         /  |   T
        |                          /   |   |
        |                         /    |   |
        |                        /     |   |
        |                       /      |   |
        |                      /       |   |
        |                     /        |   |
        |                    /         |   |
        |                   /          |   |
        |                  /           |   |
        +-----------------+------------+   v
        ^                                 ^
        |------ Stern      Bow ---------|
        
   CM = (Shaded Area A_M) / (B x T)
   
   The Midship Coefficient tells us what fraction of the 
   rectangle is "filled" by the actual hull cross-section.
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Sketch:** The midship coefficient $C_M$ is essentially the **"fill ratio"** of the rectangle. If the hull section were a perfect rectangle (impossible in practice), $C_M = 1.0$. In reality, the rounded bilge and the tumble-home cause $C_M < 1.0$.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. (3 Marks) Define Block Coefficient ($C_B$) and state its typical range for a modern cargo ship.**

**Model Answer:**
The block coefficient ($C_B$) is defined as the ratio of the volume of displacement ($\nabla$) of the ship to the volume of a rectangular block having the ship's length (L), breadth (B), and draft (T).

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \times B \times T}$$

It is dimensionless. For a modern cargo ship (general cargo / bulk carrier), $C_B$ typically ranges from **0.65 to 0.80**. A higher $C_B$ indicates a fuller hull with greater carrying capacity but higher resistance.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2. (3 Marks) Distinguish between Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$) and Midship Coefficient ($C_M$). What does their product represent?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$) | Midship Coefficient ($C_M$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \times L}$ | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \times T}$ |
| Describes | Longitudinal taper of the hull | Fullness of the largest cross-section |
| Typical Range | 0.60 – 0.85 | 0.92 – 0.98 |
| Reference Shape | A prism of length L and cross-section $A_M$ | A rectangle of dimensions B × T |

**Product $C_P \times C_M$:** As derived in Section 3, the product $C_P \times C_M$ equals the **Block Coefficient ($C_B$)**. This is a fundamental identity in ship geometry:

$$C_B = C_P \times C_M$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **OR** — ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

> Choose **ONE** of the following two questions. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**A ship has the following principal particulars:**
- Length between perpendiculars, $L = 150 \text{ m}$
- Moulded breadth, $B = 22 \text{ m}$
- Moulded draft, $T = 8.5 \text{ m}$
- Midship section area, $A_M = 170 \text{ m}^2$
- Waterplane area, $A_W = 2700 \text{ m}^2$
- Displacement volume, $\nabla = 22{,}440 \text{ m}^3$

**(a)** Calculate the Block Coefficient ($C_B$), the Midship Coefficient ($C_M$), and the Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the Waterplane Coefficient ($C_{WP}$), the Vertical Prismatic Coefficient ($C_{VP}$), and the Longitudinal Coefficient ($C_L$). Verify the identity $C_B = C_{VP} \times C_{WP}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) Solution:

**Block Coefficient ($C_B$):**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \times B \times T} = \frac{22{,}440}{150 \times 22 \times 8.5}$$

$$C_B = \frac{22{,}440}{28{,}050}$$

$$\boxed{C_B = 0.80}$$

**Midship Coefficient ($C_M$):**

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \times T} = \frac{170}{22 \times 8.5} = \frac{170}{187}$$

$$\boxed{C_M = 0.909}$$

**Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$):**

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \times L} = \frac{22{,}440}{170 \times 150} = \frac{22{,}440}{25{,}500}$$

$$\boxed{C_P = 0.88}$$

**Verification of $C_B = C_P \times C_M$:**

$$C_P \times C_M = 0.88 \times 0.909 = 0.80 = C_B \quad \checkmark$$

---

##### Part (b) Solution:

**Waterplane Coefficient ($C_{WP}$):**

$$C_{WP} = \frac{A_W}{L \times B} = \frac{2700}{150 \times 22} = \frac{2700}{3300}$$

$$\boxed{C_{WP} = 0.818}$$

**Vertical Prismatic Coefficient ($C_{VP}$):**

$$C_{VP} = \frac{\nabla}{A_W \times T} = \frac{22{,}440}{2700 \times 8.5} = \frac{22{,}440}{22{,}950}$$

$$\boxed{C_{VP} = 0.978}$$

**Longitudinal Coefficient ($C_L$):**

$$C_L = \frac{\nabla}{A_W \times L} = \frac{22{,}440}{2700 \times 150} = \frac{22{,}440}{405{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{C_L = 0.0554}$$

> **Note on $C_L$ value:** A longitudinal coefficient of 0.055 is unusually low because $A_W \times L$ (the denominator) is much larger than the displacement volume. This is because $A_W$ and $L$ are both large — remember $C_L$ relates volume to a very long, very wide prism. The *ratios* are what matter; the absolute magnitude of any single coefficient depends on the units and reference shape chosen.

**Verification of $C_B = C_{VP} \times C_{WP}$:**

$$C_{VP} \times C_{WP} = 0.978 \times 0.818 = 0.80 = C_B \quad \checkmark$$

Both identities hold, confirming the data is internally consistent.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Define the following with neat sketches: (i) Block Coefficient, (ii) Midship Coefficient, (iii) Prismatic Coefficient, (iv) Waterplane Coefficient. State one typical value for each for a bulk carrier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the identity $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ starting from the basic definitions. A ship has $C_B = 0.72$ and $C_M = 0.95$. Find the prismatic coefficient. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) Solution:

**(i) Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** Ratio of displacement volume ($\nabla$) to the volume of a rectangular block of dimensions L × B × T.
$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \, B \, T} \quad \text{Typical: } 0.80 \text{ (bulk carrier)}$$

**(ii) Midship Coefficient ($C_M$):** Ratio of the area of the cross-section at amidships ($A_M$) to the rectangle of dimensions B × T.
$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \, T} \quad \text{Typical: } 0.98 \text{ (bulk carrier)}$$

**(iii) Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$):** Ratio of the displacement volume ($\nabla$) to the volume of a prism of length L and cross-section $A_M$.
$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \, L} \quad \text{Typical: } 0.82 \text{ (bulk carrier)}$$

**(iv) Waterplane Coefficient ($C_{WP}$):** Ratio of the waterplane area ($A_W$) to the area of a rectangle L × B at the design waterline.
$$C_{WP} = \frac{A_W}{L \, B} \quad \text{Typical: } 0.88 \text{ (bulk carrier)}$$

*(Each definition should be accompanied by a simple sketch showing the hull section against the reference shape — as in Section 4.3 of these notes.)*

---

##### Part (b) Solution:

**Derivation of $C_B = C_P \times C_M$:**

Starting from the fundamental definitions:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \, B \, T} \qquad C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \, L} \qquad C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \, T}$$

Form the product $C_P \times C_M$:

$$C_P \times C_M = \left( \frac{\nabla}{A_M \, L} \right) \times \left( \frac{A_M}{B \, T} \right)$$

Cancel $A_M$ appearing in both numerator and denominator:

$$C_P \times C_M = \frac{\nabla}{L \, B \, T}$$

But the right-hand side is exactly the definition of $C_B$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{C_B = C_P \times C_M} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $C_B = 0.72$, $\quad C_M = 0.95$

Since $C_B = C_P \times C_M$, we can solve for $C_P$:

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.72}{0.95}$$

$$\boxed{C_P = 0.758}$$

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** If principal dimensions are given in **mm** and area in **m²**, the student often forgets to convert. **Always express L, B, T in metres and areas in m²** before computing coefficients. Mixing units will produce a numerically wrong answer and **full marks are deducted** for non-dimensional analysis.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Cancel $A_M$ in Derivations:** The derivation of $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ requires you to **explicitly show the cancellation step**. Writing only the final line without the cancellation earns **partial credit only (3/7 instead of 7/7)**.
>
> 3. **Confusing $C_{VP}$ and $C_P$:** $C_{VP}$ uses the **waterplane area** $A_W$ and **draft** $T$ in the denominator. $C_P$ uses **midship area** $A_M$ and **length** $L$. Swapping these is the most common conceptual error.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Internal Choice:** In Part B (14 marks), KTU 2024 scheme **mandates an internal choice** between two questions. If you attempt both, only the first will be valued. **Circle the question number clearly** to indicate your choice.
>
> 5. **Not Verifying the Identity:** Whenever you compute $C_B$, $C_P$, and $C_M$ in a numerical problem, **always verify $C_B = C_P \times C_M$** at the end. This shows the examiner you understand the theory, and you may earn **bonus 1–2 marks** for "extra care".
>
> 6. **$C_P > 1$ Sanity Check:** If your calculation gives $C_P > 1$, the input data is inconsistent. Report it explicitly as "data error" — **do not just write a wrong answer**. Examiners reward students who spot the inconsistency.
>
> 7. **Midship vs Prismatic Confusion in Sketches:** When drawing a figure for $C_P$, the **prism runs along the length** of the ship. When drawing $C_M$, the **rectangle is the cross-section** at midships. Mixing these up in the figure will lose you 2–3 marks even if the formula is correct.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Block Coefficient** $C_B$ = (Displacement Volume) / ($L \times B \times T$) → "Overall fatness of hull"
- [ ] **Midship Coefficient** $C_M$ = $A_M$ / ($B \times T$) → "Fullness of the largest cross-section" — always the **highest** coefficient
- [ ] **Prismatic Coefficient** $C_P$ = $\nabla$ / ($A_M \times L$) → "Longitudinal taper" of the hull
- [ ] **Waterplane Coefficient** $C_{WP}$ = $A_W$ / ($L \times B$) → "Fullness of the deck slice" — directly affects **stability**
- [ ] **Longitudinal Coefficient** $C_L$ = $\nabla$ / ($A_W \times L$) → "Longitudinal taper with waterplane as reference"
- [ ] **Vertical Prismatic Coefficient** $C_{VP}$ = $\nabla$ / ($A_W \times T$) → "Vertical taper from waterline to keel"
- [ ] **MUST-MEMORISE Identity 1:** $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ — derives from cancelling $A_M$
- [ ] **MUST-MEMORISE Identity 2:** $C_B = C_{VP} \times C_{WP}$ — derives from cancelling $A_W$
- [ ] **MUST-MEMORISE Identity 3:** $C_L = C_{VP} \times (T/L)$
- [ ] All coefficients are **dimensionless** — units of L, B, T must be **metres**; areas in **m²**
- [ ] Typical $C_B$ range: 0.40 (warships) to 0.90 (tankers)
- [ ] Typical $C_M$ range: 0.90 to 0.99 — always the highest
- [ ] $C_P$ and $C_L$ cannot exceed **1.0** — sanity check your numerical answers
