This is the **energy criterion** equivalent to the classical metacentric stability criterion $\overline{GM} > 0$. The energy approach is more general and remains valid at large heel angles, whereas the metacentric method is strictly valid only for small angles. [3 marks]

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**Q:** Starting from the principle of minimum potential energy, derive the condition for stable equilibrium of a floating body. Show that the righting moment is $W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$ for small angles of heel.

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Stability Condition (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Consider a floating body of weight $W$ displaced volume $\nabla$, with centres of gravity $G$ and buoyancy $B$ at heights $\overline{KG}$ and $\overline{KB}$ above the keel datum.

The total potential energy in the upright condition is:

$$PE_0 = W \cdot \overline{KG} - \rho g \nabla \cdot \overline{KB}$$

When the ship heels by an angle $\theta$, the centre of buoyancy moves on a circular arc of radius $\overline{BM}$ about the metacentre $M$. The vertical drop of $B$ is $\overline{BM}(1 - \cos\theta)$. Therefore:

$$PE(\theta) = W \cdot \overline{KG} - W\left[\overline{KB} - \overline{BM}(1-\cos\theta)\right]$$

$$PE(\theta) = PE_0 + W \cdot \overline{BM}(1 - \cos\theta)$$

**First-Order Equilibrium Condition:**

$$\frac{dPE}{d\theta} = W \cdot \overline{BM} \cdot \sin\theta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 0 \text{ (upright)}$$

**Second-Order Stability Condition:**

$$\frac{d^2 PE}{d\theta^2} = W \cdot \overline{BM} \cdot \cos\theta \quad \text{(positive at } \theta = 0\text{)}$$

The energy of the G–B system relative to $G$ is the change in PE measured from $G$:

$$\Delta PE = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)$$

(using $BM = GM + BG$ and the geometry of the heeled configuration).

**Stability Condition:** $\overline{GM} > 0$, i.e., $M$ lies above $G$.

[7 marks]

#### Part (b) — Righting Moment Derivation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The righting moment is the negative gradient of the potential energy:

$$M_{righting} = -\frac{dPE}{d\theta} = -W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

For a small heel angle, this acts in the **opposite direction** to the imposed heel, so it is a true restoring moment. The magnitude is:

$$\boxed{\vert M_{righting} \vert = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** This moment is zero at $\theta = 0$ (no restoring force needed when upright), increases with $\theta$ for small angles, and reaches a maximum near the angle of maximum $GZ$, beyond which it decreases until vanishing at the angle of vanishing stability $\theta_v$.

For the limiting case $\theta \to 0$ with $\sin\theta \approx \theta$:

$$M_{righting} \approx W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$$

This is the linearised righting moment valid for small heel angles (typically $\theta < 10°$).

[7 marks]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students frequently **omit the sign convention** and lose 1–2 marks. The righting moment, by convention, is reported as a **positive restoring moment** acting to return the ship to upright. The expression $\sin\theta$ is positive for positive heel, and the moment opposes the heel — so the **magnitude** is what is quoted. Do not write "$M = -W \overline{GM} \sin\theta$" without explicitly noting the sign convention of your coordinate system.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]`

**Q:** A ship of displacement 10,000 tonnes floats in sea water ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). The following data are given:

- $\overline{KG} = 6.0$ m
- $\overline{KB} = 2.5$ m
- $I_T = 12{,}500$ m⁴ (transverse second moment of waterplane area)

#### Part (a) — Compute $\overline{BM}$ and $\overline{GM}$ (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

$$\overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{12{,}500}{10{,}000} = 1.25 \text{ m}$$

$$BG = \overline{KG} - \overline{KB} = 6.0 - 2.5 = 3.5 \text{ m}$$

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} - BG = 1.25 - 3.5 = -2.25 \text{ m}$$

Since $\overline{GM} < 0$, the metacentre lies **below** the centre of gravity, and the ship is in **unstable equilibrium** at the upright position.

[7 marks]

#### Part (b) — Using the Energy Method, Verify the Stability State and Compute the Work Done to Heel the Ship to $\theta = 15°$ (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The potential energy change as a function of heel angle is:

$$\Delta PE(\theta) = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)$$

With $W = \rho g \nabla = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 10{,}000 = 100{,}542{,}500$ N $\approx 1.0054 \times 10^8$ N.

At $\theta = 15°$:

$$\cos(15°) = 0.96593$$

$$1 - \cos(15°) = 0.03407$$

$$\Delta PE = (1.0054 \times 10^8) \times (-2.25) \times (0.03407)$$

$$\Delta PE = -7.708 \times 10^6 \text{ J} \approx -7.71 \text{ MJ}$$

**Interpretation:** The **negative** change in potential energy means the system **loses** potential energy as the ship heels. This is the energy criterion for **unstable equilibrium** — the ship rolls spontaneously to lower its PE, and the work done by external agents (such as the calm sea surface) is **negative**, i.e., the system does work on the surroundings. The ship would capsize.

**Verification via Second Derivative Test:**

$$\left.\frac{d^2 PE}{d\theta^2}\right|_{\theta=0} = W \cdot \overline{BM} = 1.0054 \times 10^8 \times 1.25 = 1.257 \times 10^8 \text{ J/rad}^2 > 0$$

This is positive, but the relevant test for a ship with $G$ above $M$ involves the $GM$-based formulation. Since $\overline{GM} < 0$, the **second derivative of the system potential energy referenced to the metacentre geometry** is effectively negative, confirming **instability**.

**Remedial Action Suggested:** The vessel must either reduce $\overline{KG}$ (lower the cargo) or increase $\overline{KB}$ (shift buoyancy upward by trimming) so that $\overline{GM} \geq +0.15$ m.

[7 marks]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Energy vs Moment Methods):**
> Many students confuse the second-derivative test using $\overline{BM}$ (which is always positive for a positively buoyant waterplane area) with the **true stability test** that uses $\overline{GM}$. The local minimum of PE for the ship as a whole is governed by $\overline{GM}$, not by $\overline{BM}$. Always state explicitly which formulation you are using in your answer, and show the connection to $G$ in the energy expression.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- [x] **Total PE of floating body** = $W \bar{z}_G - \rho g \nabla \bar{z}_B$. Datum choice does not affect energy *differences*.
- [x] **First-order equilibrium** requires $\dfrac{dPE}{d\theta} = 0$ at the angle of interest — always satisfied at $\theta = 0$ for an upright ship.
- [x] **Stable equilibrium** requires $\dfrac{d^2 PE}{d\theta^2} > 0$ at that angle, equivalent to $\overline{GM} > 0$.
- [x] **Unstable equilibrium** corresponds to $\dfrac{d^2 PE}{d\theta^2} < 0$, equivalent to $\overline{GM} < 0$ (ship capsizes).
- [x] **Neutral equilibrium** corresponds to $\dfrac{d^2 PE}{d\theta^2} = 0$, equivalent to $\overline{GM} = 0$ (metacentre coincides with $G$).
- [x] **Work–Energy Link:** Work done to heel from $0$ to $\theta$ is $W \cdot \int_0^\theta \overline{GZ}(\phi)\, d\phi = W \times$ (area under $GZ$ curve). This is the **stability energy**.
- [x] **Small-angle approximation:** $\overline{GZ} \approx \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$, valid for $\theta \lesssim 10°$–$15°$.
- [x] **Energy approach is valid for ALL angles** — use it for large-angle stability, damage cases, and IMO compliance checks.
- [x] **Units discipline:** $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\nabla$ in m³, $W$ in N. Distinguish **mass displacement** (tonnes) from **weight** (N or tonnes-force).
- [x] **Sign of $GM$** is the universal stability indicator: positive = stable, negative = unstable, zero = neutral.
- [x] **Numerical integration** of $GZ$ curves in exam answers: use trapezoidal rule (3 ordinates) or Simpson's 1/3 rule (even number of intervals). Always quote the result in J or kJ.
- [x] **Cross-check** any energy-based stability conclusion with the classical metacentric method for small angles.

> **Final Note from Examiner's Desk:** In the KTU 2024 scheme ESE, the energy approach is favoured for **Part B 14-mark** questions where derivation of the righting moment or stability energy is required. Always **draw the geometry**, **state the datum**, **write the PE function explicitly**, and **perform the derivative tests in order** — this is the exact 7+7 mark split that examiners follow.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability of Ships - Stable and Unstable Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stability of Ships - Stable and Unstable Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ship Stability (KTU 2024 Official Definition):** The ability of a floating vessel, when subjected to external heeling moments caused by wind, waves, cargo shift, or turning maneuvers, to develop a **righting moment** that restores it to its original upright equilibrium position is termed *Transverse Stability of a Ship*.

The geometric and physical pivots that govern this property are three cardinal points:
- **Center of Gravity (G)** — the point through which the total weight *W* of the ship acts vertically downward.
- **Center of Buoyancy (B)** — the centroid of the displaced water volume, through which the total buoyant force *Δ* (equal to *W* in equilibrium) acts vertically upward.
- **Metacenter (M)** — the instantaneous center of curvature of the path traced by the Center of Buoyancy *B* when the ship is heeled by a small angle *θ*.

## 1.2 The Three Equilibrium States

A floating body in still water may exist in any of the three classical equilibrium conditions:

| Condition | Mathematical Criterion | Physical Outcome |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stable Equilibrium** | $GM \gt 0$ | Ship returns to upright after disturbance |
| **Unstable Equilibrium** | $GM \lt 0$ | Ship heels further and ultimately capsizes |
| **Neutral Equilibrium** | $GM = 0$ | Ship remains at the heeled angle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In naval architecture, the symbol **GM** is universally called the **Metacentric Height** and is the *single most critical parameter* in determining initial transverse stability of a ship.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Marble, the Bowl, and the Hilltop

Imagine three identical marbles placed in three different scenarios:

1. **A Marble in a Bowl (Stable Equilibrium):** The bowl's curved walls push the marble back to the bottom whenever it is nudged. Even if you let go, the marble rolls back to the center. *A ship with a positive metacentric height behaves exactly like this marble — wind pushes it over, but its own buoyancy pulls it back upright.*

2. **A Marble Balanced on a Hilltop (Unstable Equilibrium):** The slightest disturbance causes the marble to roll down and never return. *A ship with negative GM behaves like this — once it heels beyond a critical angle, gravity and buoyancy combine to flip it completely.*

3. **A Marble on a Perfectly Flat Glass Table (Neutral Equilibrium):** A push moves the marble to a new spot, and it stays there without any tendency to return or move further. *A ship with GM = 0 stays at whatever angle it is heeled to — extremely dangerous because any further disturbance pushes it into instability.*

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *Positive GM = Positive comeback. Negative GM = No comeback.* The "M" stands for the Metacenter, and the sign tells the entire story of safety.

## 1.4 Standard Metric Conventions

The following constants and sign conventions are universally used in KTU naval architecture problems:

- Displacement $\Delta$ is expressed in **tonnes (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)** ($1 \text{ t} \approx 9.81 \text{ kN}$).
- Metacentric height GM is measured in **metres (m)**.
- Heel angles are measured in **degrees (°)**.
- The metacentric height GM is measured *positive upward* from G to M.
- The distance KB (Keel to Center of Buoyancy) is always measured from the keel baseline.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Geometry of a Heeled Ship

When an upright ship with center of buoyancy at $B_0$ is tilted by a small angle $\theta$ about its longitudinal axis:

1. The center of buoyancy migrates from $B_0$ to a new position $B_1$.
2. The vertical line through $B_1$ intersects the original vertical centerline at point **M** — this point is the **transverse metacenter**.
3. The horizontal displacement of $B$ is denoted by $\overline{B_0 B_1}$.
4. The righting lever is the horizontal distance $GZ$, measured from $G$ to the line of action of buoyant force through $B_1$.

## 2.2 Why a Ship Rights Itself — The Physics

The righting action arises because when the ship heels:
- Weight *W* continues to act vertically downward through *G*.
- Buoyancy *Δ* continues to act vertically upward through the *new* center of buoyancy $B_1$.
- These two equal and opposite forces are now separated by a horizontal distance **GZ**, forming a **couple**.
- This couple creates the **Righting Moment** = $W \times \overline{GZ}$.
- If G lies below M, this couple restores the ship; if G lies above M, the couple overturns the ship.

## 2.3 Conditions for Stable, Unstable, and Neutral Equilibrium

| Position of G relative to M | Sign of GM | Nature of Equilibrium | Practical Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| G is **below** M | $GM \gt 0$ (positive) | **Stable** | Ship returns to upright |
| G is **above** M | $GM \lt 0$ (negative) | **Unstable** | Ship capsizes |
| G **coincides** with M | $GM = 0$ | **Neutral** | Ship remains at heeled angle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Naval architects always design vessels with a **positive GM**. Typical merchant ships have $GM$ between $0.15\,\text{m}$ and $1.5\,\text{m}$. Warships require even larger values (often $1$ to $3\,\text{m}$) for survivability in damaged condition.

## 2.4 The Sign of GM — The Decisive Criterion

The metacentric height is a vector quantity measured from G to M:

$$GM = KM - KG$$

- **Stable Condition:** $KM \gt KG \Rightarrow GM \gt 0$
- **Unstable Condition:** $KM \lt KG \Rightarrow GM \lt 0$
- **Neutral Condition:** $KM = KG \Rightarrow GM = 0$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations form the **core computational toolkit** for Module 3 stability problems in KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.

| Sl. No. | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ | Transverse metacentric radius | m |
| 2 | $KM = KB + \overline{BM}$ | Keel to metacenter distance | m |
| 3 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height | m |
| 4 | $\overline{GZ} = GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | Righting lever (small angles $\le 10^\circ$) | m |
| 5 | $\overline{GZ} = \overline{KN} - \overline{KG}\sin\theta$ | Righting lever (general, large angles) | m |
| 6 | $\text{Righting Moment} = W \times \overline{GZ}$ | Restoring couple | kN·m or t·m |
| 7 | $W = \rho g V = \Delta$ | Weight = Displacement | kN or t |
| 8 | $I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | Second moment of waterplane area (rectangle) | m$^4$ |
| 9 | $\sin\theta_{max} = \dfrac{GM_{\text{eff}}}{\text{specific lever}}$ | Angle of maximum righting moment | degrees |
| 10 | $T_{eff} = 2 \cdot (KM - KG) \cdot \sin\theta$ | Effective metacentric height formula | m |

Where:
- $I_T$ = Transverse second moment of waterplane area (m$^4$)
- $V$ = Submerged volume / Displaced volume (m$^3$)
- $\Delta$ = Displacement of the ship (tonnes)
- $W$ = Total weight of ship (kN)
- $\theta$ = Angle of heel (degrees or radians)
- $L$ = Length of waterplane (m)
- $B$ = Breadth of waterplane (m)
- $KG$ = Height of center of gravity above keel (m)
- $KB$ = Height of center of buoyancy above keel (m)

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of the Stability Criterion

The metacentric height is not just an academic number — it directly determines:

1. **Rolling Period:** $T_{\text{roll}} \approx \dfrac{2\pi \cdot k}{\sqrt{GM}}$ — higher GM means a shorter, stiffer, and more uncomfortable roll.
2. **Cargo Loading Decisions:** Bunkers, ballast tanks, and heavy lifts must be positioned to keep G low.
3. **Survival in Damage:** A warship that loses buoyancy on one side needs *positive residual GM* to survive flooding.
4. **Passenger Comfort:** Cruise ships deliberately maintain moderate GM ($\approx 0.3$ to $0.6\,\text{m}$) for a smoother ride.
5. **Tender Ships:** If GM is very small ($\lt 0.15\,\text{m}$), the ship rolls slowly and dangerously (called a "tender" ship).
6. **Stiff Ships:** Excessively high GM ($\gt 2\,\text{m}$) causes jerky, rapid rolls that can damage cargo and injure crew.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transverse Metacentric Radius $\overline{BM} = I_T / V$

**Statement to Prove:** For a ship heeled by a small angle $\theta$, the distance between the original center of buoyancy $B_0$ and the metacenter $M$ is given by $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$.

### Step 1 — Initial Setup

Consider a ship floating upright at waterline WL. The center of buoyancy is at $B_0$, and the ship displaces a volume $V$ of water. The displaced weight equals the buoyancy force $\Delta = \rho g V$.

### Step 2 — Heeling Action

The ship is now heeled by a small angle $\theta$ about its longitudinal axis. Two wedge-shaped volumes emerge:

- **Wedge $W_1$** is submerged on the lee side (newly immersed).
- **Wedge $W_2$** is emerged on the weather side (newly uncovered).

These two wedges are equal in volume because the displaced volume $V$ remains constant for an unchanging waterline area (constant $\Delta$).

$$V_1 = V_2$$

### Step 3 — Center of Buoyancy Shifts

The center of buoyancy migrates from $B_0$ to $B_1$, moving laterally toward the immersed side by a distance $\overline{B_0 B_1}$.

By the principle of moments (taking moments about the original centerline):

$$\Delta \cdot \overline{B_0 B_1} = W_1 \cdot x_1 + W_2 \cdot x_2$$

where $x_1$ and $x_2$ are the centroids of the wedges measured from the centerline.

### Step 4 — Geometry of the Wedges

For a small angle $\theta$, each elementary strip $dx$ at distance $x$ from the centerline has height $x \tan\theta$. The volume of the wedge $W_1$ is:

$$V_1 = \int x \tan\theta \cdot dx = \tan\theta \int x \, dx$$

The moment of this wedge about the centerline is:

$$\int x \cdot (x \tan\theta) \, dx = \tan\theta \int x^2 \, dx$$

### Step 5 — Recognizing the Second Moment

The integral $\int x^2 \, dx$ over the waterplane area is precisely the **Transverse Second Moment of Area** of the waterplane:

$$I_T = \int x^2 \, dA$$

By symmetry of the wedges, the moment equation reduces to:

$$\Delta \cdot \overline{B_0 B_1} = 2 \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \frac{I_T}{2}$$

$$\Delta \cdot \overline{B_0 B_1} = \tan\theta \cdot I_T$$

### Step 6 — Geometric Link to the Metacenter

From the geometry of the heeled ship, since $M$ is the intersection of the original vertical and the new vertical through $B_1$:

$$\overline{B_0 M} = \frac{\overline{B_0 B_1}}{\tan\theta}$$

Substituting $\overline{B_0 B_1} = \dfrac{I_T \tan\theta}{\Delta}$ and noting $\Delta = \rho g V$:

$$\overline{B_0 M} = \frac{I_T \tan\theta}{\Delta \tan\theta} = \frac{I_T}{\Delta} = \frac{I_T}{\rho g V}$$

Since $\overline{B_0 M}$ is conventionally written as $\overline{BM}$:

$$\boxed{\overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{V}}$$

**Verification of Units:** $[I_T] = \text{m}^4$, $[V] = \text{m}^3$, so $\overline{BM}$ is in metres ✓.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Righting Lever for Small Heel Angles

**Statement to Prove:** For a ship with metacentric height $GM$ heeled by a small angle $\theta$, the righting lever $\overline{GZ} = GM \cdot \sin\theta$.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Triangle

From the geometry of the heeled ship, triangle $GMZ$ is a right-angled triangle with the right angle at $Z$ (where the buoyant force line of action meets the horizontal through $G$). The hypotenuse is $GM$ and the angle at $M$ is the heel angle $\theta$.

### Step 2 — Apply Trigonometry

In the right triangle $GMZ$:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{\overline{GZ}}{\overline{GM}}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange

$$\boxed{\overline{GZ} = GM \cdot \sin\theta}$$

### Step 4 — Righting Moment

The righting moment that restores the ship to upright is:

$$\boxed{M_R = W \times \overline{GZ} = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stability Criterion via Moment Analysis

**Statement to Prove:** A floating body is stable if and only if $GM > 0$.

### Step 1 — Heeling Moment Applied

Suppose an external heeling moment $M_H$ tilts the ship by a small angle $\theta$ from upright.

### Step 2 — Righting Moment Developed

The metacentric height produces a restoring couple:

$$M_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

### Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition

At equilibrium (when heeling moment equals righting moment):

$$M_H = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

### Step 4 — Three Cases Examined

**Case A:** If $GM > 0$, then $M_R$ opposes the heel $\Rightarrow$ **Stable**.

**Case B:** If $GM < 0$, then $M_R$ acts in the *same direction* as the heel $\Rightarrow$ **Unstable** (capsize).

**Case C:** If $GM = 0$, then $M_R = 0$ for any finite $\theta$ $\Rightarrow$ **Neutral**.

### Step 5 — Conclusion

$$\boxed{\text{Ship Stability} \iff GM > 0}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Righting Moment Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A ship of displacement $\Delta = 5000\,\text{t}$ floats with $KG = 4.8\,\text{m}$ and $KB = 2.5\,\text{m}$. The waterplane area is $1200\,\text{m}^2$ with transverse second moment $I_T = 60000\,\text{m}^4$. Calculate: (i) metacentric height, (ii) righting moment at $\theta = 6^\circ$, (iii) state stability condition.

**Given:** $\Delta = 5000\,\text{t}$, $KG = 4.8\,\text{m}$, $KB = 2.5\,\text{m}$, $I_T = 60000\,\text{m}^4$, $\theta = 6^\circ$.

### Solution

**Step (i) — Compute the displaced volume**

$$V = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{5000}{1.025} \approx 4878\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step (ii) — Compute $\overline{BM}$**

$$\overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{60000}{4878} \approx 12.30\,\text{m}$$

**Step (iii) — Compute $KM$**

$$KM = KB + \overline{BM} = 2.5 + 12.30 = 14.80\,\text{m}$$

**Step (iv) — Compute $GM$**

$$GM = KM - KG = 14.80 - 4.80 = 10.00\,\text{m}$$

**Step (v) — Compute $\overline{GZ}$ at $\theta = 6^\circ$**

$$\overline{GZ} = GM \cdot \sin\theta = 10.00 \times \sin(6^\circ) = 10.00 \times 0.1045 = 1.045\,\text{m}$$

**Step (vi) — Compute Righting Moment**

$$M_R = W \times \overline{GZ} = (5000 \times 9.81) \times 1.045 = 49050 \times 1.045 \approx 51257\,\text{kN·m}$$

$$\boxed{M_R \approx 51257\,\text{kN·m} \approx 5125.7\,\text{t·m}}$$

**Stability Verdict:** Since $GM = 10\,\text{m} > 0$, the ship is in **Stable Equilibrium** ✓.

## 3.5 Cross-Verification Matrix

| Quantity | Computed Value | Sanity Check |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\overline{BM}$ | $12.30\,\text{m}$ | Plausible for a large waterplane ✓ |
| $KM$ | $14.80\,\text{m}$ | Consistent with KB + BM ✓ |
| $GM$ | $10.00\,\text{m}$ | Positive $\Rightarrow$ Stable ✓ |
| $\overline{GZ}$ | $1.045\,\text{m}$ | Less than GM (since $\sin\theta \lt 1$) ✓ |
| $M_R$ | $51257\,\text{kN·m}$ | Large restoring moment for large ship ✓ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ship Stability Decision Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *logical decision architecture* used by naval architects when assessing the stability state of a vessel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ship1[Ship Floating at Equilibrium]
    inputA[Identify Points: G, B0, M]
    calcA[Compute BM using I_T divided by V]
    calcB[Compute KM = KB plus BM]
    calcC[Compute GM = KM minus KG]
    decisionA{Is GM greater than 0?}
    decisionB{Is GM equal to 0?}
    stableOut[Stable Equilibrium: Ship Returns Upright]
    neutralOut[Neutral Equilibrium: Ship Stays at Heel Angle]
    unstableOut[Unstable Equilibrium: Ship Capsizes]
    warningOut[CRITICAL WARNING: Immediate Ballasting Required]
    ship1 --> inputA
    inputA --> calcA
    calcA --> calcB
    calcB --> calcC
    calcC --> decisionA
    decisionA -->|Yes| stableOut
    decisionA -->|No| decisionB
    decisionB -->|Yes| neutralOut
    decisionB -->|No| unstableOut
    unstableOut --> warningOut
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Components of the Righting Moment System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    weightW[Total Weight W acting at G downward]
    buoyDelta[Buoyant Force Delta acting at B1 upward]
    leverGZ[Righting Lever GZ equals GM times sin theta]
    rightingMoment[Righting Moment MR equals W times GZ]
    externalHeel[External Heeling Moment from Wind Waves or Cargo Shift]
    equilibrium[Equilibrium State Restored or Capsized]
    weightW --> rightingMoment
    buoyDelta --> leverGZ
    leverGZ --> rightingMoment
    rightingMoment --> equilibrium
    externalHeel --> equilibrium
```

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — The Three Stability States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Upright: Initial Loading
    Upright --> StableDisturbance: External Heel Applied
    Upright --> NeutralDisturbance: External Heel Applied
    Upright --> UnstableDisturbance: External Heel Applied
    StableDisturbance --> Upright: Positive GM restores position
    NeutralDisturbance --> HeeledAtAngle: GM equals zero so ship stays
    HeeledAtAngle --> NeutralDisturbance: Any further heel treated similarly
    UnstableDisturbance --> Capsized: Negative GM causes progressive heel
    Capsized --> [*]: Vessel Loss
```

## 4.4 Geometric Schematic of a Heeled Ship (ASCII Representation)

```
                       M  (Metacenter)
                       |
                       |  GM (Metacentric Height - positive upward)
                       |
                       G  (Center of Gravity)  -----> GZ (Righting Lever)
                      /|
                     / |
                    /  |
                   /   |        Delta (Buoyancy, upward)
                  /    |        ^
                 /     |        |
                B1     |        |
               /       |        |
              / theta  |        |
             /---------+--------+-----> Waterline (Heeled)
            B0         KB       
            |
            |
          ----- Keel Baseline
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- $B_0$ = Original center of buoyancy (upright).
- $B_1$ = Shifted center of buoyancy after heel by $\theta$.
- The vertical through $B_1$ meets the original vertical (through $B_0$) at $M$.
- $G$ lies *below* $M$, so $GM > 0 \Rightarrow$ Stable.
- $\overline{GZ}$ is the horizontal distance from $G$ to the buoyant force line — this is the righting lever.
- Righting moment $= W \times \overline{GZ}$, restoring the ship upright.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Mermaid Adaptation:** Physical ship geometry with three-dimensional perspective cannot be accurately rendered in Mermaid syntax. The block diagrams above provide a faithful functional and logical representation of the stability computation workflow. For actual free-body diagrams of heeled ships, students should refer to standard naval architecture textbooks such as *Principles of Naval Architecture* (SNAME).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **Q:** Define the term **metacentric height** of a floating body. Mention its significance in ship stability.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The metacentric height $\overline{GM}$ of a floating body is defined as the distance between the **center of gravity (G)** of the body and the **metacenter (M)**. Mathematically:

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{KM} - \overline{KG}$$

**Significance (1 Mark):** It is the single most important parameter that determines the **initial transverse stability** of a ship. A positive value of $\overline{GM}$ indicates a stable condition, while a negative value indicates an unstable (capsizing) condition.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q:** Differentiate between **stable**, **unstable**, and **neutral equilibrium** of a floating body with reference to metacentric height.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Equilibrium Type | Criterion | Behaviour on Disturbance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stable** | $GM \gt 0$ (G lies below M) | Body returns to upright position |
| **Unstable** | $GM \lt 0$ (G lies above M) | Body heels further and eventually capsizes |
| **Neutral** | $GM = 0$ (G coincides with M) | Body remains at the disturbed position |

In all three cases, the weight and buoyancy forces remain equal and opposite, but their relative positions determine the nature of the equilibrium.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students frequently confuse the *transverse* ($\overline{BM}$) with the *longitudinal* metacentric radius ($\overline{BM_L}$). The transverse formula uses the breadth dimension; the longitudinal uses the length dimension.
> - Forgetting to convert displacement in tonnes to volume in m$^3$ when computing $\overline{BM} = I_T / V$.
> - Computing $GM$ as $KG - KM$ instead of $KM - KG$ (sign error = entire answer wrong).
> - Using $W$ in tonnes in the moment equation without multiplying by $g = 9.81$ when the answer must be in kN·m.
> - Mixing up the **righting lever** $GZ$ (small-angle formula $GM \sin\theta$) with the **general** righting lever $KN - KG \sin\theta$ which is only needed for large heel angles ($> 10^\circ$).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the transverse metacentric radius $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ for a ship heeled by a small angle. Explain all steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

When a ship floating upright is heeled by a small angle $\theta$ about its longitudinal axis, two wedge-shaped volumes are formed:
- A wedge $W_1$ submerged on the lee side
- A wedge $W_2$ emerged on the weather side

Since the displacement remains constant, $V_1 = V_2$ and the center of buoyancy shifts from $B_0$ to $B_1$.

Taking moments of buoyancy about the original centerline:

$$\Delta \cdot \overline{B_0 B_1} = 2 \cdot W_1 \cdot x_1$$

For a small angle $\theta$, the elementary strip area is $x \, d\theta \, dx$ at distance $x$ from the centerline, so:

$$W_1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \theta \int x \, dx$$

and its moment about the centerline is:

$$W_1 \cdot x_1 = \frac{\theta}{3} \int x^2 \, dx$$

The transverse second moment of the waterplane area is:

$$I_T = \int x^2 \, dA$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\Delta \cdot \overline{B_0 B_1} = I_T \cdot \theta$$

From the geometry of the heeled ship:

$$\overline{BM} = \frac{\overline{B_0 B_1}}{\tan\theta} \approx \frac{\overline{B_0 B_1}}{\theta}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{V}}$$

where $V$ is the displaced volume and $I_T$ is the transverse second moment of the waterplane area. **(7 Marks)**

---

> **(b)** A ship of displacement $8000\,\text{tonnes}$ floats with $KG = 5.2\,\text{m}$. The waterplane area is $1500\,\text{m}^2$ and its transverse second moment is $I_T = 90000\,\text{m}^4$. If the ship heels by $5^\circ$, calculate the **(i)** metacentric height, **(ii)** righting lever, and **(iii)** righting moment. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:** $\Delta = 8000\,\text{t}$, $KG = 5.2\,\text{m}$, $A_W = 1500\,\text{m}^2$, $I_T = 90000\,\text{m}^4$, $\theta = 5^\circ$.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Metacentric Height (3 Marks):**

The displaced volume is computed using seawater density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025\,\text{t/m}^3$:

$$V = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{8000}{1.025} \approx 7804.88\,\text{m}^3$$

Transverse metacentric radius:

$$\overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{90000}{7804.88} \approx 11.53\,\text{m}$$

For a typical merchant ship at moderate draft, $KB \approx 0.53 \cdot d$ where $d$ is the draft. Assuming $d = 4\,\text{m}$:

$$KB \approx 2.12\,\text{m}$$

$$KM = KB + \overline{BM} = 2.12 + 11.53 = 13.65\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{GM = KM - KG = 13.65 - 5.20 = 8.45\,\text{m}}$$

**(ii) Righting Lever (2 Marks):**

$$\overline{GZ} = GM \cdot \sin(5^\circ) = 8.45 \times 0.0872 \approx 0.737\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{GZ} \approx 0.737\,\text{m}}$$

**(iii) Righting Moment (2 Marks):**

$$M_R = W \times \overline{GZ} = (8000 \times 9.81) \times 0.737 \approx 57842\,\text{kN·m}$$

$$\boxed{M_R \approx 57842\,\text{kN·m} \approx 5896\,\text{t·m}}$$

**Stability Verdict:** Since $GM = 8.45\,\text{m} > 0$, the ship is in **Stable Equilibrium**. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **stable**, **unstable**, and **neutral equilibrium** conditions of a floating body. Derive the mathematical criterion for each case. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Stable Equilibrium (3 Marks):**
When the metacenter $M$ lies **above** the center of gravity $G$, i.e., $GM > 0$, the floating body is in stable equilibrium. On being tilted by a small angle $\theta$, the buoyant force through $B_1$ and the weight through $G$ form a couple that **opposes** the tilt and restores the body to its original upright position.

*Sketch Elements:* Upright ship $\rightarrow$ heeled ship $\rightarrow$ M above G $\rightarrow$ righting couple restoring ship.

**Unstable Equilibrium (2 Marks):**
When $M$ lies **below** $G$, i.e., $GM < 0$, the couple formed by the weight and buoyancy **aids** the disturbance. The body continues to heel until it capsizes.

*Sketch Elements:* M below G $\rightarrow$ couple increases tilt $\rightarrow$ capsize.

**Neutral Equilibrium (2 Marks):**
When $M$ and $G$ **coincide**, i.e., $GM = 0$, no couple is generated. The body remains at whatever angle it is tilted to, with no tendency to return or to capsize.

*Sketch Elements:* M coincides with G $\rightarrow$ ship stays at heeled angle indefinitely.

**Mathematical Criteria Summary:**
- Stable: $\overline{GM} > 0$
- Unstable: $\overline{GM} < 0$
- Neutral: $\overline{GM} = 0$

---

> **(b)** A rectangular barge has length $L = 90\,\text{m}$, breadth $B = 14\,\text{m}$, and floats at a draft $d = 3.2\,\text{m}$ in seawater ($\rho = 1.025\,\text{t/m}^3$). The center of gravity is at $KG = 4.0\,\text{m}$ above the keel. Determine **(i)** the displacement in tonnes, **(ii)** the metacentric height, and **(iii)** whether the barge is stable. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:** $L = 90\,\text{m}$, $B = 14\,\text{m}$, $d = 3.2\,\text{m}$, $\rho = 1.025\,\text{t/m}^3$, $KG = 4.0\,\text{m}$.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Displacement (2 Marks):**

The displaced volume of the rectangular barge is:

$$V = L \times B \times d = 90 \times 14 \times 3.2 = 4032\,\text{m}^3$$

The displacement in tonnes:

$$\Delta = \rho \times V = 1.025 \times 4032 = 4132.8\,\text{t}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta = 4132.8\,\text{tonnes}}$$

**(ii) Metacentric Height (3 Marks):**

The waterplane area of the rectangular barge is:

$$A_W = L \times B = 90 \times 14 = 1260\,\text{m}^2$$

The transverse second moment of area for a rectangle is:

$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{90 \times 14^3}{12} = \frac{90 \times 2744}{12} = 20580\,\text{m}^4$$

The transverse metacentric radius:

$$\overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{20580}{4032} \approx 5.104\,\text{m}$$

For a rectangular barge, the center of buoyancy is at mid-draft:

$$KB = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{3.2}{2} = 1.6\,\text{m}$$

The metacenter distance from the keel:

$$KM = KB + \overline{BM} = 1.6 + 5.104 = 6.704\,\text{m}$$

The metacentric height:

$$GM = KM - KG = 6.704 - 4.0 = 2.704\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{GM = 2.704\,\text{m}}$$

**(iii) Stability Verdict (2 Marks):**

Since $GM = 2.704\,\text{m} > 0$, the center of gravity $G$ lies **below** the metacenter $M$. Therefore, the rectangular barge is in **Stable Equilibrium**. ✓

**Note:** This positive GM is appropriate for a cargo barge — large enough to ensure stability but not so large as to make the rolling period uncomfortably short for any crew on board.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3, Stability Topic**

- **Three Cardinal Points:** G (center of gravity, fixed), B (center of buoyancy, shifts with heel), M (metacenter, fixed for small angles).
- **The Magic Formula:** $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ — must always be computed first in stability problems.
- **The Master Criterion:** $GM > 0$ means **Stable**; $GM < 0$ means **Unstable**; $GM = 0$ means **Neutral**. This single number decides everything.
- **Three Sequential Formulas** (in order of computation):
  1. $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$
  2. $KM = KB + \overline{BM}$
  3. $GM = KM - KG$
- **Righting Lever for Small Angles:** $\overline{GZ} = GM \cdot \sin\theta$ — only valid for $\theta \leq 10^\circ$.
- **Righting Lever for Large Angles:** $\overline{GZ} = \overline{KN} - \overline{KG}\sin\theta$ — must be used beyond $10^\circ$.
- **Righting Moment Equation:** $M_R = W \times \overline{GZ} = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$.
- **Unit Conversion Essentials:**
  - $\Delta$ in tonnes $\times\,9.81$ = $W$ in kN.
  - $\Delta / \rho_{sw}$ = $V$ in m$^3$ (use $\rho_{sw} = 1.025\,\text{t/m}^3$ for seawater).
- **Waterplane Second Moment for Rectangle:** $I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$.
- **KB for Rectangular Hull:** $KB = d/2$ (mid-draft).
- **Practical Stability Range:** Merchant ships $GM \approx 0.15$ to $1.5\,\text{m}$; passenger ships $0.3$ to $0.6\,\text{m}$; warships $1$ to $3\,\text{m}$.
- **Rolling Period:** $T_{roll} \propto 1/\sqrt{GM}$ — higher GM = shorter, stiffer, jerkier roll.
- **Free Surface Effect:** Loose liquid in partially filled tanks effectively *raises* G, reducing GM — always account for this in stability booklets.
- **Sign Convention:** Always measure GM positive upward from G to M; if M is below G, GM is negative (unstable).
- **KTU Examiner Loves:** Asking students to derive $\overline{BM} = I_T / V$ and then apply it to numerical problems — practice this derivation at least three times before the exam.
- **Common Trap:** The formula $\overline{GZ} = GM \sin\theta$ applies to small heel angles only; for angles beyond $10^\circ$, the righting lever must be computed using the cross-curve of stability or the more general $KN - KG\sin\theta$ formula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note:** The stability of a ship is a *geometric and physical* concept, not a thermodynamic one. Master the geometry of the three points G, B, and M — and every numerical problem reduces to applying the same three formulas in sequence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability Terms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stability Terms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Introduction to Ship Stability Terminology

In Naval Architecture, **Ship Stability** is the single most important subject for the safety of life at sea. A ship floats because of a delicate balance between **Gravity** (pulling it down) and **Buoyancy** (pushing it up). When a ship tilts due to wind, waves, or cargo shift, several geometric points inside the ship's cross-section dictate whether it returns upright or capsizes. These points are called **Stability Terms**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Module 3 focuses on the *static stability terms* — students must be able to draw, label, and mathematically relate the following points: **G (Center of Gravity), B (Center of Buoyancy), M (Metacenter), F (Center of Flotation), and W (Waterline)**. Mastery of these is a guaranteed scoring area in the ESE.

### 1.2 Primary Stability Terms — Formal Definitions

Let us define each term with the exact precision expected in a KTU board exam answer.

**1. Center of Gravity (G / COG)**
The point through which the force of gravity (the weight **W** of the ship) is considered to act vertically downward. It is the *centroid* of the entire weight distribution of the vessel, including hull, machinery, cargo, fuel, and crew.

**2. Center of Buoyancy (B / COB)**
The point through which the force of buoyancy (the upward force equal to the weight of displaced water) is considered to act vertically upward. It is the *centroid of the underwater volume* (the displaced volume **∇**).

**3. Metacenter (M)**
The point of intersection of the ship's **centerline** (vertical axis) with the new vertical line passing through the **new Center of Buoyancy (B')** when the ship is tilted by a small angle **θ**. It is the *pivot point* of the ship's rolling motion in the transverse plane.

**4. Metacentric Height (GM)**
The vertical distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** and the **Metacenter (M)**. It is the *single most important numerical measure* of a ship's initial stability.

**5. Center of Flotation (F / COF)**
The centroid of the waterplane area. It is the point about which the ship **trims** (rotates longitudinally) when a weight is added or removed fore and aft. There is no equivalent for transverse heel.

**6. Waterplane (WP)**
The horizontal plane formed by the intersection of the hull surface with the water surface when the ship is in equilibrium.

**7. Draft (T)**
The vertical distance from the **keel** (bottom of the hull) to the **design waterline**. A ship has three drafts: **Forward (Tf)**, **Aft (Ta)**, and **Mean (Tm)**.

**8. Heel / List**
A transverse tilt of the ship. **Heel** is a *temporary* transverse tilt caused by external forces (wind, turning), while **List** is a *permanent* transverse tilt caused by an off-center weight (e.g., cargo shift).

**9. Trim**
The longitudinal inclination of the ship, expressed as the difference between forward and aft drafts: **Trim = Tf − Ta**.

**10. Freeboard**
The vertical distance from the **waterline** to the **uppermost continuous deck** (the weather deck). It is the ship's *reserve of buoyancy* measured in height.

**11. Reserve of Buoyancy**
The volume of the ship *above* the waterline that is available to provide buoyancy if the ship becomes additionally submerged. It is essentially the *integral of freeboard over the waterplane area*.

**12. Displacement (Δ or W)**
The total weight of water displaced by the ship, equal to the total weight of the ship. Units: **tonnes (t)** or **metric tons (MT)**.

**13. TPC — Tonnes Per Centimeter Immersion**
The additional weight (in tonnes) required to sink the ship by **1 cm** deeper in calm water of standard density (**ρ = 1.025 t/m³**).

**14. MCTC — Moment to Change Trim by 1 Centimeter**
The longitudinal moment (in tonne-meters) required to change the trim of the ship by **1 cm**. It governs the longitudinal stability and trim calculations.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Floating Pencil

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine balancing a short pencil on your fingertip (this is *unstable* — the pencil falls). Now glue a heavy disc to the bottom of the pencil and place it in a small boat. When you tilt the boat, the boat returns upright. **Why?** Because the heavy disc lowers the **Center of Gravity (G)** *below* the **Center of Buoyancy (B)**, and this makes a very large **Metacentric Height (GM)**, providing immense stability. In a ship, a low **KG** and a high **KB** = a safe ship.

### 1.4 Summary Callout — The Five Critical Points

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these five points like a road map of the ship's cross-section:
> - **G** — Center of Gravity (acts downward)
> - **B** — Center of Buoyancy (acts upward)
> - **M** — Metacenter (the pivot of roll)
> - **F** — Center of Flotation (the pivot of trim)
> - **K** — Keel (the reference point for **KG, KB, KM, KF**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Geometric Relationship Between G, B, M, and F

When a ship in upright equilibrium is tilted by a small angle **θ** (in the transverse plane):

1. The volume of water displaced **remains constant** (Archimedes' principle requires the ship to float at a new waterline of the same area integral for small heel).
2. The shape of the underwater volume **changes**, so the centroid **B** shifts to a new position **B'**.
3. The line of action of the buoyancy force is now the **vertical through B'**.
4. This vertical intersects the original centerline at the point **M** (Metacenter).
5. The distance **BM** is called the **Metacentric Radius** (denoted **KM** in many texts, or **BM** when measured from **B**).

### 2.2 Why These Terms Matter — The "Righting Moment" Concept

The stability of a ship is not measured by a single number but by the **Righting Lever (GZ)**. The moment that restores the ship to upright is:

**Righting Moment = W × GZ**

where **GZ** is the horizontal distance between **G** and the line of action of buoyancy.

For **small angles** of heel (typically **θ < 10°**), the relationship is linear:

**GZ ≈ GM × sin(θ)**

For **large angles** (θ > 10°), the relationship is non-linear and must be computed from the **GZ curve** (a plot of GZ vs. θ) — this is the **Large Angle Stability / Dynamical Stability** domain, covered in the next module.

### 2.3 Why "Heel" vs. "List" vs. "Trim" — The Engineering Logic

- **Heel** (transient): Caused by an *external couple* (wind on sail, centrifugal force in a turn). The ship will right itself when the force is removed.
- **List** (permanent): Caused by an *internal asymmetry* (improper loading, slack tanks, collision flooding). The ship *will not* right itself — this is a stability emergency.
- **Trim** (longitudinal): Caused by a *longitudinal weight imbalance* (cargo loaded too far forward or aft). The ship naturally floats at an angle — but in most cases, this is *intentional* and not a stability failure.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Term** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement | $\Delta$ or $W$ | $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes; $\rho$ = 1.025 t/m³ (sea) |
| Center of Buoyancy position | $KB$ | From integration of $KB = \dfrac{\int z \cdot dA}{A_{WP}}$ | meters (m) |
| Transverse Metacentric Radius | $BM_T$ | $BM_T = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | m; $I_T$ = 2nd moment of waterplane area about centerline |
| Longitudinal Metacentric Radius | $BM_L$ | $BM_L = \dfrac{I_L}{\nabla}$ | m; $I_L$ is huge (10× to 20× $I_T$) |
| Metacentric Height | $GM_T$ | $GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG$ | m; must be $> 0$ for stability |
| Longitudinal Metacentric Height | $GM_L$ | $GM_L = KB + BM_L - KG$ | m; usually 100s of meters |
| Righting Lever (small angle) | $GZ$ | $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | m; valid only for $\theta \leq 10^\circ$ |
| Righting Moment | $RM$ | $RM = W \cdot GZ$ | tonne-meter (t·m) |
| TPC | $TPC$ | $TPC = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{100}$ | t/cm; $A_{WP}$ in m² |
| MCTC | $MCTC$ | $MCTC = \dfrac{W \cdot LBP}{100 \cdot GM_L}$ | t·m/cm; $LBP$ = Length Between Perpendiculars |
| Trim | $Trim$ | $Trim = T_f - T_a$ | m; positive = trimmed by stern |
| Trim by Moment | $\Delta Trim$ | $\Delta Trim = \dfrac{M}{MCTC \cdot 100}$ | cm; $M$ = longitudinal moment |
| Freeboard | $F_b$ | $F_b = D - T$ | m; $D$ = depth (moulded) |
| Reserve of Buoyancy | $RB$ | $RB = \int_{WL}^{Deck} A(z) \, dz$ | m³; volume above waterline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The formula **$GM = KB + BM - KG$** is *the* most repeated equation in KTU naval exams. Note the sign convention: $KB$ and $BM$ are *above* $K$, while $KG$ is *above* $K$ — you subtract $KG$ because $G$ and $B$ are typically in the same region.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These terms are *not* academic abstractions — they are used **daily** on every commercial vessel:
- The **Chief Officer** of a ship uses **TPC** to determine how much draft change a given cargo parcel will cause.
- The **Master** uses **GM** to estimate the ship's rolling period ($T = 2\pi \cdot k / \sqrt{GM}$) and weather route the vessel.
- The **Loading Computer** on the bridge continuously calculates **SF (Shear Force)** and **BM (Bending Moment)** in real-time, which require **MCTC** for trim corrections.
- Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) mandate minimum **GM** values for safe service — typically **GM > 0.15 m** for passenger ships.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Metacentric Radius ($BM = I / \nabla$)

This is the **single most important derivation** in the entire Stability chapter and is a guaranteed question in KTU ESE.

**Setup:** Consider a ship floating in still water. Let the ship be tilted by a small angle **θ** in the transverse plane about a longitudinal axis through the **Center of Flotation (F)**.

**Step 1 — Volume Invariance:**
For a small heel, the waterplane shifts but the displaced volume remains the same. Therefore, the **weight of water displaced remains constant** (Archimedes' Principle).

**Step 2 — The Shifted Buoyancy Center:**
Let the **new Center of Buoyancy** be **B'**. The shift **BB'** is small but it lies along the inclined direction of the waterplane.

**Step 3 — Applying the First Moment of Area Theorem:**
The shift of the buoyancy center in the **transverse** direction (let's call it **x**) is given by the ratio of the **first moment of the waterplane area** about the centerline to the **underwater volume**:

$$
BB' = \dfrac{\int x \, dA_{WP}}{\nabla}
$$

**Step 4 — Small Angle Approximation (the "small heel" trick):**
In the triangle formed by **B, B', and M**, the angle at **B'** is **θ** (the heel angle). The geometry of this triangle gives:

$$
BM = \dfrac{BB'}{\tan \theta}
$$

For small angles, $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians), so:

$$
BM \approx \dfrac{BB'}{\theta}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting the First Moment of Area:**
The first moment of the waterplane area about the centerline can be rewritten in terms of the **second moment of area** (using the relationship between the first moment of a thin strip and the differential element of the waterplane):

$$
\int x \, dA_{WP} = I_T \cdot \sin\theta
$$

where $I_T$ is the **transverse second moment of area** of the waterplane about the longitudinal centerline.

**Step 6 — Final Substitution:**
Plugging this back into the equation for **BM**:

$$
BM = \dfrac{I_T \cdot \sin\theta}{\nabla \cdot \theta}
$$

For small $\theta$, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$, so:

$$
\boxed{BM_T = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}}
$$

**Q.E.D.** — The transverse metacentric radius depends only on the **second moment of the waterplane area** and the **displaced volume**. It is *independent* of the heel angle (for small angles).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common exam question asks: *"For a rectangular barge of breadth B and length L, find BM."* The student must compute $I_T = L \cdot B^3 / 12$ and substitute. This derivation is the foundation of that problem.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of $GM = KB + BM - KG$

**Step 1 — From the diagram (vertical line through G to the new buoyancy line):**
The metacenter **M** lies on the original centerline at height $KM$ above the keel **K**.

**Step 2 — Vertical Geometry:**
The **Center of Buoyancy B** is at height $KB$ above K. The **Center of Gravity G** is at height $KG$ above K. The **Metacenter M** is at height $KM$ above K.

**Step 3 — The Distance GM:**
Since all three points lie on (or very near) the vertical centerline for small heel:

$$
GM = KM - KG
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $KM = KB + BM$:**

$$
GM = (KB + BM) - KG
$$

$$
\boxed{GM = KB + BM - KG}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Note:** If $G$ lies *above* $M$, then $GM$ is *negative*, and the ship is **unstable** (the moment becomes a *capsizing moment*). This is the famous "**G above M** = capsize" rule.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of TPC

**Setup:** The TPC is defined as the **weight in tonnes** required to sink a ship by **exactly 1 cm** in standard sea water ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³).

**Step 1 — The Ship as a Floating Plate:**
A uniform sink of 1 cm over the entire waterplane area $A_{WP}$ corresponds to an **additional displaced volume** of:

$$
\Delta\nabla = A_{WP} \times \Delta T = A_{WP} \times 0.01 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — The Mass of This Volume:**
The mass of the extra displaced water (in tonnes) equals $\rho \times \Delta\nabla$:

$$
\Delta W = \rho \times A_{WP} \times 0.01
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³:**

$$
\Delta W = 1.025 \times A_{WP} \times 0.01
$$

**Step 4 — Final Formula (with the conventional 1.025 factor absorbed in the 100 denominator):**

$$
\boxed{TPC = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{100}}
$$

where $A_{WP}$ is in m² and TPC is in t/cm. This is the **standard KTU formula** for examination purposes.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of MCTC (Moment to Change Trim by 1 Centimeter)

**Step 1 — Definition of Trim:**
A ship trims about the **Center of Flotation (F)**. A change in trim **Δt** (in cm) by a longitudinal moment **M** (in t·m) is governed by the longitudinal stiffness of the waterplane.

**Step 2 — Analogy with a Beam:**
The ship's longitudinal floating condition is analogous to a simply-supported beam of length $LBP$ on an elastic foundation. The "stiffness" is given by the moment per unit trim change.

**Step 3 — Setting Δt = 1 cm and M = MCTC:**

$$
MCTC = \dfrac{W \cdot LBP}{100 \cdot GM_L}
$$

where $GM_L$ is the **longitudinal metacentric height** (typically 100s of meters, making MCTC very large — often 100s of t·m/cm).

**Step 4 — Final Expression:**

$$
\boxed{MCTC = \dfrac{W \cdot LBP}{100 \cdot GM_L}}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Stability vs. G Position

| **Position of G** | **Value of GM** | **Stability Type** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| G below M | $GM > 0$ | **Stable** | Ship returns to upright when tilted |
| G coincides with M | $GM = 0$ | **Neutral** | Ship remains at tilted angle (theoretical) |
| G above M | $GM < 0$ | **Unstable** | Ship continues to roll over → capsize |
| G above B but below M | $GM > 0$, $KB > KG$ | **Stable** | Common case — sufficient stability |
| G below B (very rare) | $GM > 0$ | **Very Stable** | Ballasted condition, e.g., submarines |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Relationship Between Stability Terms

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional relationships** between the five key points (G, B, M, K, F) and the four key levers (KG, KB, KM, BM, GM). It serves as a mental model for the entire module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K0[KEEL POINT K]:::anchor --> K1[Vertical Reference Line]
    K1 --> K2[Center of Gravity G]
    K1 --> K3[Center of Buoyancy B]
    K1 --> K4[Metacenter M]
    K1 --> K5[Center of Flotation F]

    K2 --> L1["KG = Height of G above K"]
    K3 --> L2["KB = Height of B above K"]
    K4 --> L3["KM = Height of M above K"]
    K5 --> L4["F lies on waterplane, x-direction matters"]

    L2 --> R1["BM = KM minus KB"]
    R1 --> R2["Metacentric Radius"]

    L1 --> E1["GM = KM minus KG"]
    E1 --> E2{"GM sign check"}
    E2 -->|GM positive| S1[STABLE: Righting Moment exists]
    E2 -->|GM zero| S2[NEUTRAL: No restoring force]
    E2 -->|GM negative| S3[UNSTABLE: Capsizing Moment]

    S1 --> P1[Righting Lever GZ approx GM sin theta]
    P1 --> P2[Righting Moment equals W times GZ]

    classDef anchor fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffd700,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff;
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stability Term Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Ship Inputs]
        A1[Weights and Loadings] --> C1
        A2[Hull Geometry] --> C2
        A3[Waterplane Area] --> C3
    end

    subgraph CENTERS[Geometric Centers]
        C1[Weight Distribution] --> CG[Center of Gravity G]
        C2[Underwater Volume] --> CB[Center of Buoyancy B]
        C3[Waterplane Centroid] --> CF[Center of Flotation F]
    end

    subgraph DERIVED[Derived Stability Terms]
        CG --> KG[KG]
        CB --> KB[KB]
        CF --> KF[KF]
        KB --> BM["BM equals I over Volume"]
        BM --> KM[KM equals KB plus BM]
        KG --> GM["GM equals KM minus KG"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS[Stability Outputs]
        GM --> TPC[TPC equals A WP over 100]
        GM --> MCTC[MCTC equals W times LBP over 100 GM]
        GM --> ROLL[Roll Period]
        GM --> RIGHT[Righting Lever GZ]
    end

    classDef centerNode fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#90caf9,color:#ffffff;
    classDef derivedNode fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#ffcdd2,color:#ffffff;
    classDef outputNode fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#a5d6a7,color:#ffffff;

    class CG,CB,CF centerNode;
    class KG,KB,KF,BM,KM,GM derivedNode;
    class TPC,MCTC,ROLL,RIGHT outputNode;
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Equilibrium of a Floating Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FORCES[Vertical Force Balance]
        W1[Weight W acting downward at G] --> NET{Net Force = 0}
        FB1[Buoyancy FB acting upward at B] --> NET
    end

    subgraph MOMENTS[Rotational Moment Balance]
        GM1[GM positive] --> RM[Righting Moment RM equals W times GZ]
        GM2[GM negative] --> CM[Capsizing Moment]
    end

    NET -->|Equilibrium| EQ[Ship Floats in Still Water]
    RM --> EQ2[Ship Returns to Upright after Heel]
    CM --> CAP[Capsize Event]

    EQ --> EQ2

    classDef safe fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#a5d6a7,color:#ffffff;
    classDef danger fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#ffcdd2,color:#ffffff;
    classDef neutral fill:#37474f,stroke:#cfd8dc,color:#ffffff;

    class EQ,EQ2 safe;
    class CAP danger;
    class NET,GM1,GM2,RM,CM neutral;
```

### 4.4 Transverse Stability Cross-Section — Conceptual Sketch

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **typical "B-G-M" cross-section** of a ship in a slightly heeled condition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph UPRIGHT[Upright Position]
        UPL[Original Centerline vertical] --> UPB[Buoyancy B]
        UPG[Weight G] --> UPL
    end

    subgraph HEELED[Heeled by angle theta]
        HL[New Centerline vertical] --> HB[New Buoyancy B prime]
        HL --> HM[Metacenter M at intersection of old and new verticals]
        HG[G remains at original location] --> HL
        HM --> RM[Righting Arm GZ]
    end

    UPRIGHT -->|Rotation about F| HEELED

    classDef stable fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#a5d6a7,color:#ffffff;
    classDef ref fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#90caf9,color:#ffffff;

    class HM,RM stable;
    class UPL,UPB,UPG,HL,HB,HG ref;
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define the following with neat sketches:**
**(a) Center of Buoyancy (B), (b) Metacenter (M), (c) Metacentric Height (GM).**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Center of Buoyancy (B):** The point through which the force of buoyancy, due to the displaced volume of water, is considered to act vertically upward. It is the *centroid of the underwater volume* ($\nabla$) of the ship.

(b) **Metacenter (M):** The point of intersection of the ship's vertical centerline (in the upright condition) with the new vertical line passing through the **new Center of Buoyancy (B')** when the ship is tilted by a small angle $\theta$. It is the *pivot point* about which the ship rolls.

(c) **Metacentric Height (GM):** The vertical distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** and the **Metacenter (M)**. Mathematically:

$$
GM = KM - KG
$$

It is the principal numerical measure of a ship's *initial transverse stability*. For a ship to be stable, $GM > 0$; a typical passenger ship has $GM$ between **0.15 m and 0.50 m**.

**Sketch (ASCII representation):**
```
              M
              |
              |  ← Centerline
              G
              |
              B
              |
              K (Keel)
```

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between "Heel" and "List" with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Heel** | **List** |
|---|---|
| Temporary transverse inclination of the ship | Permanent transverse inclination of the ship |
| Caused by *external* forces (wind, turning, passengers shifting) | Caused by *internal* asymmetry (cargo shift, slack tanks, flooding) |
| The ship *returns to upright* when the force is removed | The ship *remains tilted* even after the cause is removed — a stability emergency |
| Example 1: A ship turns sharply in a channel and heels outward | Example 1: A bulk carrier has improper cargo distribution, listing 3° to port |
| Example 2: A gust of wind heels a sailing vessel 5° to leeward | Example 2: A collision causes flooding in a port-side ballast tank, leading to a permanent list |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Define **Metacentric Height (GM)**. Derive the formula $BM_T = I_T / \nabla$ for a ship heeled by a small angle $\theta$ in the transverse plane. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular barge has a **length of 100 m, breadth of 20 m**, and floats at a draft of **5 m** in sea water ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³). The **Center of Gravity (G)** is located at **3.5 m** above the keel. Calculate:
(i) The transverse metacentric radius **$BM_T$**.
(ii) The metacentric height **$GM_T$**, given $KB = 2.5$ m.
(iii) The **righting moment** at $\theta = 6°$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition of GM:** The metacentric height is the vertical distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** and the **Metacenter (M)**. It is the most important measure of a ship's *initial transverse stability*.

**Derivation of $BM_T = I_T / \nabla$:**

Consider a ship in upright equilibrium floating at waterline WP. The Center of Buoyancy is at B. Now tilt the ship by a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis through the **Center of Flotation (F)**. The new Center of Buoyancy shifts to **B'**.

By the principle of **constant displaced volume** (Archimedes' Principle), the shift **BB'** lies along the inclined waterplane.

The shift in the transverse direction is given by the first moment of the waterplane area about the centerline, divided by the underwater volume:

$$
\text{transverse shift} = \dfrac{\int x \, dA_{WP}}{\nabla}
$$

In the right-angled triangle **B B' M**, the angle at **B'** is $\theta$ and the vertical leg is **BM**. From geometry:

$$
BM = \dfrac{BB'}{\sin\theta}
$$

The first moment $\int x \, dA_{WP}$ is related to the **second moment of area** $I_T$ of the waterplane about the longitudinal centerline. As the waterplane tilts by $\theta$, the lever arm of each strip $dA$ is approximately $x \cdot \sin\theta$, so:

$$
\int x \, dA_{WP} = I_T \cdot \sin\theta
$$

Substituting back:

$$
BM = \dfrac{I_T \cdot \sin\theta}{\nabla \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

$$
\boxed{BM_T = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}}
$$

This is the required formula, valid for small angles of heel. **Q.E.D.**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Length $L = 100$ m
- Breadth $B = 20$ m
- Draft $T = 5$ m
- $KG = 3.5$ m
- $KB = 2.5$ m
- $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³
- $\theta = 6°$

**(i) Calculation of $BM_T$:**

For a rectangular waterplane, the second moment of area about the longitudinal centerline is:

$$
I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{100 \times 20^3}{12} = \dfrac{100 \times 8000}{12} = 66{,}666.67 \text{ m}^4
$$

The displaced volume is:

$$
\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T = 100 \times 20 \times 5 = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

The transverse metacentric radius is:

$$
BM_T = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla} = \dfrac{66{,}666.67}{10{,}000} = 6.667 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{BM_T = 6.667 \text{ m}}
$$

**(ii) Calculation of $GM_T$:**

$$
GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG
$$

$$
GM_T = 2.5 + 6.667 - 3.5
$$

$$
\boxed{GM_T = 5.667 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A $GM_T$ of 5.67 m is **very high** — typical of a barge with a high metacenter. This means the ship will be very stiff, with a short roll period. For a passenger ship, this would be too stiff and uncomfortable; for a barge carrying cargo, it is acceptable.

**(iii) Calculation of Righting Moment at $\theta = 6°$:**

First, compute the displacement:

$$
W = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 10{,}000 = 10{,}250 \text{ tonnes}
$$

The righting lever at small angle:

$$
GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta = 5.667 \times \sin(6°)
$$

$$
\sin(6°) = 0.1045
$$

$$
GZ = 5.667 \times 0.1045 = 0.5922 \text{ m}
$$

The righting moment:

$$
RM = W \times GZ = 10{,}250 \times 0.5922
$$

$$
\boxed{RM = 6{,}070 \text{ t·m}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Define **TPC** and **MCTC**. Explain the engineering significance of each in ship operations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship has a **displacement $W = 20{,}000$ tonnes**, a **waterplane area $A_{WP} = 2500$ m²**, and a **length between perpendiculars $LBP = 150$ m**. The **longitudinal metacentric height** is $GM_L = 200$ m. Calculate:
(i) The TPC of the ship.
(ii) The MCTC of the ship.
(iii) The new draft if **500 tonnes** of cargo is loaded uniformly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**TPC (Tonnes Per Centimeter Immersion):**
TPC is the additional weight (in tonnes) required to sink a ship by **1 cm** deeper in standard sea water of density $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³.

$$
TPC = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{100}
$$

**Engineering Significance of TPC:**
- It is used during **loading and discharging** to predict the change in mean draft.
- Example: If TPC = 25 t/cm and 1000 tonnes of cargo is loaded, the ship sinks by $1000/25 = 40$ cm.
- It is essential for **draft survey** (calculating cargo quantity by draft readings).
- It helps the **Port State Control** verify the ship's loaded draft and Under-Keel Clearance (UKC).

**MCTC (Moment to Change Trim by 1 cm):**
MCTC is the longitudinal moment (in t·m) required to change the trim of the ship by **1 cm** in standard sea water.

$$
MCTC = \dfrac{W \cdot LBP}{100 \cdot GM_L}
$$

**Engineering Significance of MCTC:**
- It governs the **longitudinal stability** and is critical for **trim correction**.
- Loading computers use MCTC to compute trim changes due to fore-aft weight shifts.
- It is used to verify compliance with **class requirements** for longitudinal strength and trim limits.
- During **ballast operations**, MCTC determines how much ballast water to transfer to achieve the desired trim.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $W = 20{,}000$ tonnes
- $A_{WP} = 2500$ m²
- $LBP = 150$ m
- $GM_L = 200$ m
- Cargo loaded = 500 tonnes (uniform)

**(i) Calculation of TPC:**

$$
TPC = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{100} = \dfrac{2500}{100}
$$

$$
\boxed{TPC = 25 \text{ t/cm}}
$$

**(ii) Calculation of MCTC:**

$$
MCTC = \dfrac{W \cdot LBP}{100 \cdot GM_L} = \dfrac{20{,}000 \times 150}{100 \times 200}
$$

$$
MCTC = \dfrac{3{,}000{,}000}{20{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{MCTC = 150 \text{ t·m/cm}}
$$

**(iii) New Draft After Loading 500 Tonnes:**

Since the cargo is loaded **uniformly** (at the Center of Flotation), there is **no change in trim** — only a change in mean draft.

Draft change:

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{\text{Loaded Weight}}{TPC} = \dfrac{500}{25} = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{New draft} = T_{old} + 0.20 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the cargo had been loaded *forward* or *aft* of the Center of Flotation, an additional **trim calculation** would be required using MCTC and the moment arm of the cargo.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in TPC / MCTC:** Always write t/cm and t·m/cm respectively. A numerical answer without units gets **0 marks** in the answer key.
> 2. **Confusing $KM$ with $BM$:** $KM$ is from the *keel* to the metacenter; $BM$ is from *buoyancy center* to the metacenter. They are related by $KM = KB + BM$.
> 3. **Using $GZ = GM \cdot \theta$ (in radians) instead of $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta$:** Both are valid for small angles, but examiners expect the **sine form** for general cases.
> 4. **Sign convention errors in $GM = KB + BM - KG$:** Some students write $GM = KG - KB - BM$ and get a negative answer for an obviously stable ship. Always check the *physical location* of G, B, M.
> 5. **Forgetting $\rho = 1.025$ for sea water** when computing displacement: 1.5 marks are reserved for this constant in the KTU answer scheme. If the question says "fresh water", use $\rho = 1.000$.
> 6. **In derivations, students often skip the geometry of the triangle B B' M** and jump straight to the final formula. Examiners **specifically award 2 marks** for the geometric sketch and the angle identification.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **G (Center of Gravity)** — acts downward; depends on weight distribution.
- [ ] **B (Center of Buoyancy)** — acts upward; depends on underwater shape.
- [ ] **M (Metacenter)** — the pivot of small-angle roll.
- [ ] **F (Center of Flotation)** — the pivot of trim.
- [ ] **K (Keel)** — the universal reference for heights ($KB$, $KG$, $KM$, $KF$).
- [ ] **Master Formula:** $GM = KB + BM - KG$ (must be **positive**).
- [ ] **Metacentric Radius:** $BM_T = I_T / \nabla$ (transverse) and $BM_L = I_L / \nabla$ (longitudinal).
- [ ] **Righting Lever (small angle):** $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta$.
- [ ] **Righting Moment:** $RM = W \cdot GZ$.
- [ ] **TPC:** $TPC = A_{WP} / 100$, units t/cm.
- [ ] **MCTC:** $MCTC = (W \cdot LBP) / (100 \cdot GM_L)$, units t·m/cm.
- [ ] **Stability Conditions:**
  - $GM > 0$ → Stable (righting moment exists)
  - $GM = 0$ → Neutral (no restoring force)
  - $GM < 0$ → Unstable (capsizing moment)
- [ ] **Heel** = transient; **List** = permanent; **Trim** = longitudinal.
- [ ] **Sea water density** = 1.025 t/m³ (always use unless stated otherwise).
- [ ] **Freeboard** = Depth − Draft; **Reserve of Buoyancy** = volume above waterline.
- [ ] The second moment $I_T = L \cdot B^3 / 12$ for a **rectangular waterplane** — used in 80% of KTU numerical problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Exam Tip:** For the 14-mark derivation question on $BM = I / \nabla$, practice drawing the **triangle B B' M** and labeling the angle $\theta$ at B' — this 30-second sketch is what separates a 10-mark answer from a 14-mark answer in the KTU valuation scheme. Always conclude the derivation with a boxed final formula and the phrase "Q.E.D."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equal Volume Inclinations; Shift of C.O.B. Due to Inclinations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equal Volume Inclinations; Shift of C.O.B. Due to Inclinations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — EQUAL VOLUME INCLINATIONS & SHIFT OF C.O.B.

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equal Volume Inclination (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> *"When a floating body (ship) is tilted through a small angle of heel (θ), the volume of the wedge-shaped slice that emerges from the waterline on one side is exactly equal to the volume of the wedge-shaped slice that submerges on the opposite side. The total displaced volume remains constant, only its shape (and hence the position of the Center of Buoyancy) changes."*

**Shift of Center of Buoyancy (C.O.B.):**
The Center of Buoyancy (B) — the centroid of the underwater volume — is **not a fixed point** relative to the ship. As the ship inclines, the underwater hull geometry changes, and the C.O.B. migrates from its original vertical axis. The **transverse horizontal distance** through which B shifts is the *transverse shift of the Center of Buoyancy*, denoted as $\overline{BB_1}$ (where $B_1$ is the new COB after inclination).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3):**
> The B.Tech Naval Architecture syllabus explicitly requires understanding:
> (a) The **equal volume principle** governing inclined floating bodies.
> (b) The **transverse shift of COB** (denoted $GG_1$ or $\overline{BB_1}$).
> (c) The **locus of B** as inclination increases (the *B-K curve* or *metacentric locus*).
> (d) The **assumption limits**: equal volume theory is exact only for **small angles** (θ ≤ 10°–15°).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**The "Iceberg in a Bathtub" Analogy:**
Imagine a large rectangular block of wood floating in a bathtub (this represents our ship). Now tilt the block slightly to the right by pushing down on the left corner.

- **What happens to the underwater shape?** A small triangular **slice pops OUT** of the water on the right side, while simultaneously a mirror-image triangular **slice SINKS IN** on the left side.
- **Are these slices the same size?** For a **small** tilt, **YES** — the emerged wedge and the immersed wedge have **equal volume** (this is the *equal volume principle*).
- **What changes?** The **centroid (balance point) of the underwater shape** shifts sideways — this is the **Shift of COB**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Ship Context:** When a container ship heels to starboard (rolls right), the emerged wedge of water on the starboard side and the immersed wedge on the port side are **virtually equal in volume**. The COB slides along a curve — away from the ship's centerline toward the submerged (port) side. This sideways shift is what creates the **righting lever (GZ)**, which is the entire foundation of ship stability.

### Key Symbols & Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit (SI) |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $B$ | Original Center of Buoyancy (upright) | point |
| $B_1$ | New COB after inclination by angle $\theta$ | point |
| $G$ | Center of Gravity (fixed in ship) | point |
| $M$ | Transverse Metacenter | point |
| $K$ | Keel point (reference) | point |
| $\theta$ | Angle of heel / inclination | **radians** (or **degrees**) |
| $V$ | Volume of displacement | **m³** |
| $I_T$ | Transverse 2nd moment of waterplane area | **m⁴** |
| $BM$ | Metacentric radius | **m** |
| $GM$ | Metacentric height | **m** |
| $W$ | Weight of ship = $\rho g V$ | **tonnes-force** or **kN** |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $\rho$ | Density of seawater | **1.025 t/m³** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Equal Volume Inclination Principle — Structured Logic

When a ship heels through a small angle $\theta$ from the upright:

1. **The original waterplane (W.L.)** tilts with the ship by the same angle $\theta$.
2. **A wedge $\Delta v_1$** (emerged) appears on the high side (windward side, port).
3. **A wedge $\Delta v_2$** (immersed) appears on the low side (leeward side, starboard).
4. **By Archimedes' principle**, the total displaced volume must remain **constant** (weight doesn't change), so:
$$\Delta v_1 = \Delta v_2 = \Delta v$$
5. The **center of these two equal wedges** lies on the **original centerline** of the ship.
6. The **new COB ($B_1$)** is the centroid of the **original underwater volume** minus $\Delta v_1$ and plus $\Delta v_2$.
7. Therefore, the **shift of COB occurs perpendicular to the original centerline** (i.e., transversely).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the COB shift?** Because the underwater volume is no longer symmetric about the ship's centerline. The added wedge on the immersed side pulls the centroid (COB) toward that side. The magnitude of the shift depends on the **second moment of area ($I$)** of the waterplane and the **displaced volume ($V$)**.

---

## 2.2 Movement of COB — Small Angle Theory

### Step 1: The Metacentric Radius $BM$

The transverse metacentric radius is given by the classical Naval Architecture formula:

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V}$$

where:
- $I_T$ = 2nd moment of area of the waterplane about the **fore-aft centerline axis** (m⁴)
- $V$ = volume of displacement (m³)

### Step 2: Transverse Shift of COB

When the ship heels by a small angle $\theta$, the COB moves from $B$ to $B_1$ along a **circular arc of radius $BM$** centered at the metacenter $M$:

$$\overline{BB_1} = BM \cdot \sin(\theta) \approx BM \cdot \theta \quad \text{(for small } \theta \text{ in radians)}$$

The new COB ($B_1$) lies on the **new vertical line** through $B_1$ (i.e., the buoyant force is always vertical).

### Step 3: The Righting Lever (GZ) — Most Important KTU Concept

The transverse shift of the COB creates a **couple** with the ship's weight, generating the **righting moment**:

$$\text{Righting Moment (RM)} = W \times GZ$$

where the righting lever is:

$$\boxed{GZ = GM \cdot \sin(\theta)}$$

For **small angles** ($\theta \leq 10°$):

$$GZ \approx GM \cdot \theta \quad \text{(in radians)}$$

And:

$$GM = KM - KG = \left(KB + \frac{I_T}{V}\right) - KG$$

---

## 2.3 The Metacentric Locus (B-K Curve) — Large Angle Theory

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Distinction:** The assumption that $M$ is a **fixed point** is valid **only for small angles** (θ ≤ 10°). For larger angles, the metacenter $M$ itself moves, and the locus of $B$ becomes a **curve**, not a circle.

As the ship heels progressively:
- The metacenter $M$ traces a curve called the **metacentric locus** (or **B-K curve**, where $K$ is the keel point).
- The COB $B$ moves along this curve.
- $GZ$ must be calculated using **integration of wedge volumes**, not the small-angle formula.

For a ship with **vertical sides** in the vicinity of the waterline (typical rectangular cross-section), the locus is approximately circular up to large angles. For **fine-shaped hulls**, the locus deviates significantly.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — it covers 90% of Module 3 numerical questions in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Validity | Units |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $BM = I_T \div V$ | Metacentric radius | Exact | m |
| 2 | $KB + BM = KM$ | Distance from keel to metacenter | Exact | m |
| 3 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height | Exact | m |
| 4 | $\overline{BB_1} = BM \sin\theta$ | Transverse shift of COB | Small angle | m |
| 5 | $GZ = GM \sin\theta$ | Righting lever (lever arm) | Small angle (θ ≤ 10°) | m |
| 6 | $\text{RM} = W \times GZ$ | Righting moment | Small angle | kN·m |
| 7 | $I_T = \int y^2 \, dA$ | 2nd moment of waterplane | Definition | m⁴ |
| 8 | $I_T = \frac{L B^3}{12}$ | Rectangular waterplane | Special case | m⁴ |
| 9 | $I_T = \frac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | Circular waterplane | Special case | m⁴ |
| 10 | $\Delta = \rho g V$ | Displacement (weight) | Exact | tonnes |
| 11 | $\overline{KB_1} = \overline{KB} \cos\theta + \overline{BM} \sin\theta \tan\theta$ | Vertical shift of COB | For wedge analysis | m |
