When a ship is in upright equilibrium, the weight **W** acts through **G** (center of gravity) and the buoyancy force **ρg∇** acts through **B** (center of buoyancy), both on the ship’s centerline. If the ship is tilted by a small angle **θ** about a longitudinal axis, the underwater hull form changes — more volume is submerged on one side and less on the other. The center of buoyancy shifts laterally to a new position **B′**.

The vertical line through this new buoyancy force (which always acts vertically upward) meets the original ship centerline at a fixed point **M**, called the **metacentre**.

**Sketch (to be drawn by student):**
- Vertical line representing the original centerline.
- Points G, B, and M marked at correct relative heights (G between B and M for stable ship).
- After heeling by angle θ, mark B′ off the centerline, the new buoyancy line, and the heeling angle.

**Three Conditions of Equilibrium:**

| Condition | GM Value | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Stable | $GM > 0$ | Righting couple returns ship to upright |
| Neutral | $GM = 0$ | Ship remains at any heel angle |
| Unstable | $GM < 0$ | Capsizing couple, ship continues to heel over |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $W = 8000$ t, $KB = 3.0$ m, $BM = 1.8$ m, $KG = 4.5$ m, $w = 50$ t, $a = 8$ m (transverse).

**(i) New Metacentric Height:**

**Step 1 — Original KM:**

$$
KM = KB + BM = 3.0 + 1.8 = 4.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Original GM:**

$$
GM_0 = KM - KG = 4.8 - 4.5 = 0.3 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Shift of G due to Moved Weight:**

The center of gravity shifts transversely by:

$$
\overline{GG_1} = \frac{w \cdot a}{W} = \frac{50 \times 8}{8000} = \frac{400}{8000} = 0.05 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Effect on Vertical Position of G (no change, since motion is purely transverse):**

The vertical KG remains **4.5 m** (no vertical shift).

**Step 5 — New Metacentric Height:**

For the new condition, M remains at the same vertical position (4.8 m) because KM depends only on hull form and displacement (which is unchanged). However, G is no longer on the centerline — it has shifted by 0.05 m laterally. The *effective* metacentric height for stability about the new centerline is calculated by recognising that the new G₁ is on the centerline of the metacentre shifted by 0.05 m, giving a **virtual reduction in GM**:

$$
GM_1 = GM_0 - \frac{w \cdot a}{W \cdot \tan\theta} \cdot \cos\theta
$$

For small angles (which we are about to confirm), the new vertical GM remains effectively 0.3 m but the new position of G₁ is offset laterally by 0.05 m. The **effective metacentric height about the new vertical through G₁** is:

$$
\boxed{GM_{\text{eff}} \approx GM_0 = 0.3 \text{ m}}
$$

(For small transverse shifts, vertical GM is essentially unchanged; the key effect is the induced heel angle, computed next.)

**(ii) Angle of Heel Produced:**

**Step 1 — Heel Angle from Shift of G:**

The new G₁ is at a transverse distance of 0.05 m from the original centerline. The ship heels until the new buoyancy vertical passes through the new G₁. The geometry gives:

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{\overline{GG_1}}{GM_0} = \frac{0.05}{0.3} = 0.1667
$$

$$
\theta = \arctan(0.1667) = 9.46^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Verify Small-Angle Assumption:**

θ ≈ 9.46° is within the small-angle range (< 15°), so the linear approximation is **acceptable**.

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 9.46^\circ}
$$

**Discussion:** This 9.46° heel is significant — a real ship would require cargo redistribution to reduce this. If the heel exceeds ~15°, the actual GZ from the GZ curve must be used (beyond the scope of this KTU module).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing M with G or B:** Many students draw the metacentre at the position of G. **M is always above B** (for a ship with positive BM), and can be above OR below G. Remember: M is on the **original centerline**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Units in BM = I/∇:** I is in m⁴ and ∇ in m³, so BM is in m. KTU examiners **deduct 0.5 marks** for missing or wrong units.
> 
> 3. **Mixing KB, KG, KM, BM, GM:** Students often write $GM = KB + BM - KG$ as $GM = KB + KG - BM$. Use the **mnemonic "KGB-MGM"**: KG to B is KB; KG to M is KM; B to M is BM; G to M is GM.
> 
> 4. **Using Wrong Formula for B vs TPC:** B is the **breadth** of the ship, TPC is **tonnes per cm immersion**, A_W is waterplane area. Do not confuse.
> 
> 5. **Applying Small-Angle Formula at Large θ:** The formula $GZ = GM \sin\theta$ is valid only for θ < 10°–15°. Beyond that, the **actual GZ curve** must be used. KTU problems usually specify "small heel" — if not, state the assumption explicitly.
> 
> 6. **Sign of GM:** Always report **GM with sign**. Even if positive, write "+0.3 m" rather than "0.3 m" in tables to demonstrate clarity.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting ρ and g:** When computing W, always include both **ρ = 1025 kg/m³ for seawater** (or 1000 for freshwater) and **g = 9.81 m/s²**. Common pitfall: using ρ alone and forgetting g.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Card for KTU Viva / Exam:**

- ✅ **Metacentre (M):** Intersection of original and new buoyancy verticals after a small heel.
- ✅ **Metacentric Height (GM):** The single most important stability number; $GM = KM - KG = KB + BM - KG$.
- ✅ **Metacentric Radius:** $BM = I / \nabla$ — depends only on **hull form and displacement**, not on heel angle.
- ✅ **BMT << BML** (≈ 20–30× smaller) because ships are slender; hence **rolling is faster than pitching**.
- ✅ **Stable Ship:** $GM > 0$ (M above G). **Neutral:** $GM = 0$. **Unstable:** $GM < 0$.
- ✅ **Righting Lever:** $GZ = GM \sin\theta$ (small angle).
- ✅ **Righting Moment:** $M_R = W \cdot GZ = \rho g \nabla \cdot GM \sin\theta$.
- ✅ **Displacement Weight:** $W = \rho g \nabla$ — Archimedes' Principle.
- ✅ **Transverse 2nd Moment (rectangle):** $I_T = LB^3/12$.
- ✅ **Longitudinal 2nd Moment (rectangle):** $I_L = BL^3/12$.
- ✅ **GM Standard Range:** Passenger ships 0.15–0.5 m, cargo ships 0.3–1.0 m, tankers 0.3–0.6 m.
- ✅ **Sliding Weight Effect:** Moving a weight **w** by distance **a** shifts G by $\overline{GG_1} = wa/W$ and tilts the ship by $\tan\theta = \overline{GG_1}/GM$.
- ✅ **Free Surface Effect (FSE):** Slack liquid in tanks reduces effective GM (covered in advanced modules, but be aware).
- ✅ **Stability Booklet:** For angles > 15°, use **GZ curve** and **IMO weather criterion** (not in KTU Module 3).
- ✅ **Always draw a diagram** in descriptive answers — KTU examiners reward well-labelled sketches.
- ✅ **Box the final numerical answer** — this single step can earn you the **0.5–1 mark** difference between grade boundaries.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pro-metacentre and Metacentric Radius
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pro-metacentre and Metacentric Radius

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

In naval architecture and ship hydrostatics, the concepts of the **pro-metacentre** and the **metacentric radius** are fundamental geometric parameters used to quantify the **initial transverse stability** of a floating vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metacentric Radius (BM):**
> The *metacentric radius* is the perpendicular distance between the **Center of Buoyancy (B)** of the upright floating ship and the **Metacentre (M)**. It is determined exclusively by the geometry of the underwater hull (the waterplane area and the displaced volume), and is *independent* of the ship's loading condition or center of gravity position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pro-metacentre (M'):**
> The *pro-metacentre* is the point of intersection of two specific lines:
> (a) The new vertical line of action of buoyancy passing through the shifted **Center of Buoyancy (B')** when the ship is inclined at an angle of heel *θ*.
> (b) A line drawn through the **original Center of Gravity (G)** that is *parallel* to the new (inclined) waterline.

Mathematically, the metacentric radius is governed by the classical hydrostatic relation:

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V}$$

where $I_T$ is the **transverse second moment of area** of the waterplane about the ship's centerline and $V$ is the **submerged volume of displacement**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a tall, slender tumbler (a glass) standing on a table very gently sideways with one finger. As long as the tilt is *very* small, the glass rights itself the moment you let go. The point around which it appears to "rotate" when correcting itself is what naval architects call the **metacentre** — a virtual pivot point above the ship's center of gravity.

The **pro-metacentre** is like marking where your finger *would have* been if you had continued pushing the tumbler. The line through the center of gravity (your hand's pivot feel) parallel to the new tilted table-top, intersecting the new vertical through the buoyancy center, gives you this "anticipated" point. For *tiny* tilts, the pro-metacentre and the metacentre sit so close together that naval architects treat them as identical — this is the cornerstone of the **"metacentric method"** of ship stability analysis.

### 1.3 Standard Naval Architecture Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **W = ρgV** = Displacement weight (in **tonnes** or **kN**)
> - **ρ** = Mass density of sea water = **1.025 t/m³** (standard) or **1.000 t/m³** for fresh water
> - **g** = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
> - **V** = Submerged volume in **m³**
> - **I_T** = Transverse waterplane moment of inertia in **m⁴**
> - **BM** (metacentric radius) in **metres**
> - **GM** (metacentric height) in **metres**

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Logic

The derivation of BM rests on three observable physical realities when a ship heels by a small angle *θ*:

- **Step 1 — Mass Conservation of Displacement:**
  When a ship rolls by a small angle *θ*, a *wedge of immersion* (volume $v_1$) submerges on one side, while a *wedge of emersion* (volume $v_2$) emerges on the opposite side. Because the total weight (and hence the displaced volume) must remain constant:
  $$v_1 = v_2 = v$$

- **Step 2 — Shift in the Center of Buoyancy:**
  The original Center of Buoyancy $B$ migrates laterally to a new position $B'$. The horizontal shift $OB'$ is the *first moment* of the wedge volumes about the original centerline, divided by the total displacement:
  $$OB' = \frac{\sum v \cdot x}{V}$$

- **Step 3 — Intersection Geometry → Metacentre M:**
  Extending the vertical line through $B'$ upward until it meets the original ship centerline (the vertical through $B$) locates the **metacentre $M$**. From the right-triangle formed by $B$, $B'$, and $M$:
  $$BM = \frac{BB'}{\tan\theta} = \frac{OB' \cdot \cos\theta}{\tan\theta} = \frac{OB' \cdot \cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \frac{OB'}{\sin\theta \cdot \sec\theta}$$

  For small angles, $\cos\theta \approx 1$ and $\tan\theta \approx \sin\theta \approx \theta$, so the metacentric radius reduces to a **shape-only** constant.

### 2.2 The Pro-Metacentre Concept — Why It Matters

The metacentre $M$ is defined *only* in the limit of infinitesimally small angles. The pro-metacentre $M'$ is the corresponding point for a finite angle of heel $\theta$ that can be physically measured or computed.

- The vertical through $B'$ intersects the **line through $G$ parallel to the new waterline** at $M'$.
- The vertical distance $GZ$ (righting lever) is then:
  $$GZ = GM' \cdot \sin\theta$$
- For **small angles** ($\theta < \sim 10°$), the pro-metacentre $M'$ and the metacentre $M$ nearly coincide, so we replace $GM'$ by the constant $GM$. This is the **metacentric (Wallis) approximation**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ | Transverse metacentric radius (hull shape property) | **m** |
| 2 | $BM_L = \dfrac{I_L}{V}$ | Longitudinal metacentric radius | **m** |
| 3 | $I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | Transverse waterplane inertia (rectangular approximation) | **m⁴** |
| 4 | $I_L = \dfrac{B \cdot L^3}{12}$ | Longitudinal waterplane inertia (rectangular approximation) | **m⁴** |
| 5 | $KM = KB + BM$ | Distance of metacentre above keel | **m** |
| 6 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height (stability indicator) | **m** |
| 7 | $OB' = \dfrac{I_T \cdot \tan\theta}{V}$ | Lateral shift of B at angle $\theta$ | **m** |
| 8 | $BM = \dfrac{OB'}{\tan\theta}$ | Derivation identity | **m** |
| 9 | $GM' \approx GM + BM \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)$ | Approximate $GM'$ for moderate angles | **m** |
| 10 | $GZ = GM' \cdot \sin\theta$ | Righting lever (metacentric method) | **m** |
| 11 | $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot V$ | Displacement weight | **kN** or **t** |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, $I_T$ is calculated about the **centerline** (transverse axis) and $I_L$ about the **transverse axis through centroid of waterplane** (longitudinal axis). For non-rectangular waterplanes, integration or the use of standard naval architecture coefficient tables is required.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Initial Stability Assessment:** Naval architects compute $GM$ at the design stage to ensure that passenger ships typically have $GM > 0.15$ m and cargo vessels $GM > 0.30$ m.
- **Damage Stability:** IMO regulations use $GM$ to compute residual righting levers after flooding.
- **Loading Computer Outputs:** Every loading condition printed onboard modern ships lists $GMT$ (transverse) and $GML$ (longitudinal) metacentric heights for the Master to verify compliance with the Trim and Stability Booklet.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of BM = I/V (Metacentric Radius)

**Step 0 — Setup:**
Consider a ship floating upright with center of buoyancy at $B$. Let the ship be heeled through a small angle $\theta$ to starboard about its longitudinal centerline. The new center of buoyancy is $B'$, and the new waterline is $W_1L_1$.

**Step 1 — Volume of Wedge Immersed / Emersed:**
Let $l$ be the length of the ship at the waterline. For an elemental strip of the waterplane at distance $y$ from the centerline, of width $dy$ and length $l$:

$$\text{Elemental wedge volume} = l \cdot y \cdot \tan\theta \cdot dy$$

**Step 2 — Total Wedge Volume $v$:**

$$v = \int_0^{B/2} l \cdot y \cdot \tan\theta \, dy$$

where $B$ is the beam (breadth) at the waterline. This represents the volume of water that has entered (immersed side) on one side. By symmetry of mass conservation, an equal volume has emerged on the opposite side.

**Step 3 — Lateral Shift of Center of Buoyancy $OB'$:**
Taking the first moment of the wedge volumes about the original centerline (the *y* = 0 axis), and equating it to the moment produced by the shift $OB'$ acting over the full displacement $V$:

$$V \cdot OB' = \int_0^{B/2} l \cdot y \cdot \tan\theta \cdot y \, dy = \tan\theta \int_0^{B/2} l \cdot y^2 \, dy$$

**Step 4 — Recognition of the Second Moment of Area:**
The integral $\int l \cdot y^2 \, dy$ is, by definition, the **transverse second moment of area** $I_T$ of the waterplane about the centerline. Therefore:

$$V \cdot OB' = I_T \cdot \tan\theta$$

$$OB' = \frac{I_T \cdot \tan\theta}{V}$$

**Step 5 — Geometric Construction of Metacentre:**
The metacentre $M$ is the intersection of:
- the vertical through the original $B$ (the ship's centerline), and
- the vertical through the new $B'$.

In the right triangle $\triangle B B' M$, the angle at $B'$ is $\theta$ (since the new vertical through $B'$ is tilted by $\theta$ from the original vertical through $B$). Therefore:

$$BM = \frac{OB'}{\tan\theta}$$

**Step 6 — Substituting the Result of Step 4:**

$$BM = \frac{1}{\tan\theta} \cdot \frac{I_T \cdot \tan\theta}{V} = \frac{I_T}{V}$$

$$\boxed{BM = \frac{I_T}{V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The metacentric radius is **independent of the angle of heel** (so long as the angle is small enough that the approximation holds). It is purely a function of hull form ($I_T$) and the loading draft ($V$).

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Pro-Metacentre Geometry

**Step 0 — Setup:**
Let the ship be heeled to a finite angle $\theta$. The new center of buoyancy is $B'$. The center of gravity $G$ has shifted only *transversely* by a small amount and remains essentially on the original centerline (assuming $G$ stays in the same transverse plane for a small heel).

**Step 1 — Construction Lines for the Pro-Metacentre:**
Two lines are drawn:
- **Line 1:** The vertical through $B'$ (this is the new line of action of the buoyant force).
- **Line 2:** A line through $G$ that is **parallel to the new (inclined) waterline**.

The point of intersection of these two lines is defined as the **pro-metacentre $M'$**.

**Step 2 — Constructing the Righting Lever $GZ$:**
Drop a perpendicular from $G$ onto the new vertical through $B'$. The length of this perpendicular is the **righting lever** $GZ$. From the geometry of the triangle $\triangle GZ M'$ (which contains the angle $\theta$ at the vertex $M'$):

$$GZ = GM' \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — True Position of $M'$ vs. $M$ (the Metacentre):**
Whereas the metacentre $M$ lies on the original vertical through $B$, the pro-metacentre $M'$ lies on the new vertical through $B'$. For *small* angles $\theta$ (typically $\theta \le 10°$), the offset between $M$ and $M'$ is negligibly small, and we may set $GM' \approx GM$.

**Step 4 — Expression for $GM'$ in Terms of $GM$ and $BM$ at Larger Angles:**
For a ship of block form (i.e., where $B$ is approximately stationary in the vertical $KB$ direction for small heeling), the pro-metacentric height can be approximated as:

$$GM' = GM + BM(1 - \cos\theta)$$

This correction arises because the new vertical through $B'$ is offset horizontally by approximately $BM \sin\theta$ from the original centerline, while the line through $G$ parallel to the new waterline is offset by a corresponding geometric amount.

**Step 5 — Final Stability Equation (Metacentric Method):**

$$\boxed{GZ = \left[ GM + BM(1 - \cos\theta) \right] \sin\theta}$$

When $\theta$ is very small, $\cos\theta \to 1$ and the bracketed term reduces to $GM$, giving the simple result:

$$GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx GM \cdot \theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The simplification $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta$ is valid only for **small angles of heel** (typically $\theta \le 10°$ for first-approximation naval architectural work). For larger angles, the full pro-metacentric formula or direct integration (Simpson's rules applied to the ship's Bonjean data) must be used.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Relationship Summary

| Quantity | Definition | Key Relation | Validity Range |
|----------|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| $B$ | Original center of buoyancy (upright) | Located at centroid of underwater volume | All drafts |
| $B'$ | New center of buoyancy (heeled by $\theta$) | $OB' = I_T \tan\theta / V$ | Small $\theta$ |
| $M$ | Metacentre (intersection of original & new verticals) | $BM = I_T / V$ | Infinitesimal $\theta$ |
| $M'$ | Pro-metacentre (intersection of new vertical & line through $G$ parallel to new WL) | $GZ = GM' \sin\theta$ | Finite $\theta$ |
| $G$ | Center of gravity (loading condition) | $KG$ from stability booklet | All conditions |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geometry of the Pro-Metacentre and Metacentric Radius

The following block-level schematic maps the geometric construction of the metacentre $M$, the pro-metacentre $M'$, and the relationship between all key stability reference points.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["ORIGINAL POSITION (theta equals 0)"]
    nodeB["B : Original Center of Buoyancy on Centerline"]
    nodeC["G : Center of Gravity on Centerline"]
    nodeD["K : Keel Reference Point at Bottom of Ship"]
    nodeE["HEELED POSITION (angle equals theta)"]
    nodeF["B prime : New Center of Buoyancy after Heel"]
    nodeG["WL1 : New Inclined Waterline"]
    nodeH["CONSTRUCTION LINES"]
    nodeI["Vertical through B prime - New Line of Buoyancy Force"]
    nodeJ["Line through G parallel to New Waterline"]
    nodeK["KEY POINTS DERIVED"]
    nodeL["M : Metacentre - Intersection of B vertical and B prime vertical"]
    nodeM["M prime : Pro-metacentre - Intersection of B prime vertical and line through G parallel to WL1"]
    nodeN["Z : Foot of Perpendicular from G to B prime vertical - gives GZ lever"]
    nodeO["KEY DISTANCES"]
    nodeP["BM : Metacentric Radius equals I_T divided by V"]
    nodeQ["GM : Metacentric Height equals KM minus KG"]
    nodeR["GZ : Righting Lever equals GM prime times sin theta"]
    nodeS["INPUTS FROM HULL FORM"]
    nodeT["I_T : Transverse Waterplane Second Moment of Area in m to the 4th"]
    nodeU["V : Submerged Displacement Volume in m cubed"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeN
    nodeL --> nodeO
    nodeM --> nodeO
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeQ
    nodeO --> nodeR
    nodeS --> nodeP
    nodeT --> nodeS
    nodeU --> nodeS
```

### 4.2 Mermaid ASCII-Art Cross-Section View (Conceptual Reconstruction)

For a clearer mental picture, the cross-section of the heeled ship looks approximately as follows (G, B, M, and M' all in the same transverse plane):

```mermaid
graph LR
    K1["K (Keel)"] --> B1["B (original Center of Buoyancy)"]
    B1 --> G1["G (Center of Gravity)"]
    G1 --> M1["M (Metacentre)"]
    M1 --> Mprime1["M prime (Pro-metacentre, very near M)"]
    Mprime1 --> WL1["New Inclined Waterline WL1"]
    B1 -. heeled by angle theta .-> B2["B prime (new Center of Buoyancy)"]
    B2 --> Mprime1
    G1 -.-> Z1["Z - foot of perpendicular from G onto B prime vertical"]
    Z1 --> B2
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow for Stability Calculation

```mermaid
graph TD
    inA["Input: Hull Lines Plan and Draft T"] --> proc1["Compute Waterplane Area Awp"]
    inB["Input: Length L and Beam B of Waterplane"] --> proc1
    proc1 --> proc2["Compute I_T = L times B cubed divided by 12 for rectangular approximation"]
    inC["Input: Displacement Volume V from Curves of Form"] --> proc3["Compute BM = I_T divided by V"]
    proc2 --> proc3
    proc3 --> proc4["Read KB from Hydrostatic Curves"]
    inD["Input: KG from Loading Manual"] --> proc5["Compute GM = KB + BM - KG"]
    proc4 --> proc5
    proc5 --> proc6["Stability Check: GM greater than 0.15 m (positive stability)"]
    proc5 --> proc7["At heel angle theta: GZ = GM times sin theta"]
    proc5 --> proc8["At heel angle theta (corrected): GZ = GM prime times sin theta where GM prime = GM + BM(1 minus cos theta)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above are **block-level functional representations** of the geometric and computational relationships. They are not literal physical drawings (Mermaid is not ideally suited for free-form technical sketches), but they correctly capture the dependencies, sequencing, and logical flow required for KTU-level problem solving.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the terms "metacentric radius" and "pro-metacentre" as applied to a floating ship. State the fundamental relationship between metacentric radius and the waterplane area.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Metacentric radius (BM):** The distance measured along the ship's centerline between the original Center of Buoyancy (B) and the Metacentre (M). It is a property of the hull form only. **(1 Mark)**
- **Pro-metacentre (M'):** The intersection of the vertical line of action of the buoyant force through the new Center of Buoyancy (B') and a line drawn through the original Center of Gravity (G) parallel to the inclined waterline. **(1 Mark)**
- **Fundamental relationship:** $BM = I_T / V$, where $I_T$ is the transverse second moment of area of the waterplane and $V$ is the displaced volume. **(1 Mark)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Why is the metacentric radius BM independent of the angle of heel (for small angles), whereas the position of the pro-metacentre varies with the angle of heel?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- $BM = I_T/V$ depends only on the **waterplane geometry** ($I_T$) and the **displacement volume** ($V$), both of which remain essentially constant for small angles of heel. **(1 Mark)**
- Therefore BM is a **hull-form constant** for any given loading condition. **(1 Mark)**
- The pro-metacentre $M'$, however, is defined by the intersection of a line through $G$ parallel to the *inclined* waterline with the vertical through $B'$. As the heel angle $\theta$ changes, both the waterline inclination and the position of $B'$ change, so $M'$ migrates relative to $M$. **(1 Mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question – 14 Marks (Module 3, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Derivation Track** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Starting from first principles, derive the expression for the transverse metacentric radius of a ship:
$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V}$$
clearly stating the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular pontoon of length 60 m, breadth 18 m, and depth 4 m floats in seawater (ρ = 1.025 t/m³) at a draft of 2.5 m. Calculate:
- (i) The transverse metacentric radius $BM$;
- (ii) The position of the metacentre above the keel ($KM$), given that the center of buoyancy is at mid-depth;
- (iii) The righting lever $GZ$ at a heel angle of $8°$, assuming the center of gravity is at a height $KG = 3.2$ m above the keel. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of BM (7 Marks):**

*Assumptions:* (1 mark)
- The angle of heel $\theta$ is infinitesimally small (so that wedge volumes and shifts can be linearized).
- The ship is wall-sided (vertical sides) in the vicinity of the waterline.
- The shift in center of gravity $G$ with heel is negligible (G is fixed on the centerline).

*Step 1:* When the ship heels by $\theta$, a wedge of volume $v$ is immersed on one side and an equal wedge of volume $v$ is emerged on the other side, to conserve the total displaced weight. (1 mark)

*Step 2:* The horizontal shift of the center of buoyancy from $B$ to $B'$ is given by equating moments of the wedge volumes about the original centerline:
$$V \cdot OB' = \int \text{(elemental volume)} \cdot y = I_T \tan\theta$$
(2 marks)

*Step 3:* The metacentric radius is the ratio of the shift $OB'$ to the tangent of the heel angle:
$$BM = \frac{OB'}{\tan\theta} = \frac{I_T \tan\theta / V}{\tan\theta} = \frac{I_T}{V}$$
(2 marks)

*Step 4:* Conclude that BM is purely a function of the waterplane geometry and the displacement volume, and is independent of $\theta$. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

*Given:*
- $L = 60$ m, $B = 18$ m, $T = 2.5$ m (draft)
- $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³
- Mid-depth buoyancy: $KB = T/2 = 1.25$ m
- $KG = 3.2$ m
- $\theta = 8°$

**(i) Transverse Metacentric Radius BM (3 Marks):**
For a rectangular waterplane:
$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{60 \times 18^3}{12} = \frac{60 \times 5832}{12} = \frac{349920}{12} = 29160 \text{ m}^4$$

Displaced volume:
$$V = L \cdot B \cdot T = 60 \times 18 \times 2.5 = 2700 \text{ m}^3$$

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{29160}{2700} = 10.8 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Position of Metacentre KM (2 Marks):**
$$KM = KB + BM = 1.25 + 10.8 = 12.05 \text{ m above keel}$$

**(iii) Righting Lever GZ at θ = 8° (2 Marks):**
$$GM = KM - KG = 12.05 - 3.2 = 8.85 \text{ m}$$

For small angles, use the metacentric method:
$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta = 8.85 \times \sin 8° = 8.85 \times 0.13917 = 1.232 \text{ m}$$

Alternatively, using the more accurate pro-metacentric formula:
$$GM' = GM + BM(1 - \cos\theta) = 8.85 + 10.8 \times (1 - \cos 8°)$$
$$= 8.85 + 10.8 \times (1 - 0.99027) = 8.85 + 10.8 \times 0.00973 = 8.85 + 0.1051 = 8.955 \text{ m}$$
$$GZ = GM' \sin\theta = 8.955 \times 0.13917 = 1.246 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** $BM = 10.8$ m, $KM = 12.05$ m, $GZ \approx 1.246$ m (using pro-metacentric correction).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Application / Problem-Solving Track** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain, with the help of a neat sketch, the geometric construction of the pro-metacentre M' for a ship heeled by a finite angle θ. State clearly the difference between the metacentre M and the pro-metacentre M'. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship of length 120 m, breadth 20 m, floats at a draft of 6 m in seawater (ρ = 1.025 t/m³). The center of buoyancy is at $KB = 3.2$ m, and the center of gravity is at $KG = 5.5$ m above the keel. Calculate:
- (i) The displacement of the ship;
- (ii) The metacentric height $GM$ (using a rectangular waterplane approximation);
- (iii) The righting moment when the ship heels by $6°$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Pro-Metacentre Construction (7 Marks):**

*Sketch description (textual):* (3 marks for diagram and explanation)
- Draw the ship's cross-section in upright and heeled positions.
- Mark $B$ (original center of buoyancy on centerline) and $G$ (center of gravity on centerline).
- After heeling through angle $\theta$, mark $B'$ (new center of buoyancy, displaced laterally).
- Draw the new vertical line of action of buoyancy through $B'$.
- Draw a line through $G$ that is **parallel to the new inclined waterline** $W_1L_1$.
- The intersection of these two lines is the **pro-metacentre M'**.

*Differences (4 marks):*
| Aspect | Metacentre M | Pro-Metacentre M' |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| Defining lines | Intersection of original vertical through B and new vertical through B' | Intersection of new vertical through B' and line through G parallel to new WL |
| Validity | Infinitesimal $\theta$ | Finite $\theta$ |
| Position | On the original ship centerline | On a line offset by $BM \sin\theta$ from the centerline |
| Use | First-order (metacentric) stability | Higher-order stability at finite angles |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

*Given:* $L = 120$ m, $B = 20$ m, $T = 6$ m, $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³, $KB = 3.2$ m, $KG = 5.5$ m, $\theta = 6°$.

**(i) Displacement (2 Marks):**
$$V = L \cdot B \cdot T = 120 \times 20 \times 6 = 14400 \text{ m}^3$$
$$W = \rho \cdot V = 1.025 \times 14400 = 14760 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(ii) Metacentric Height GM (3 Marks):**
$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times 20^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times 8000}{12} = 80000 \text{ m}^4$$

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{80000}{14400} = 5.556 \text{ m}$$

$$KM = KB + BM = 3.2 + 5.556 = 8.756 \text{ m}$$

$$GM = KM - KG = 8.756 - 5.5 = 3.256 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Righting Moment at 6° (2 Marks):**
$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin 6° = 3.256 \times 0.10453 = 0.3404 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Righting Moment} = W \cdot GZ = 14760 \times 0.3404 = 5024.3 \text{ tonne-metres}$$

Or equivalently in kN·m:
$$\text{Righting Moment} = 14760 \times 9.81 \times 0.3404 = 49289 \text{ kN·m} \approx 49.3 \text{ MN·m}$$

**Final Answer:** $W = 14{,}760$ t, $GM = 3.256$ m, Righting Moment $\approx 4929$ kN·m (using $W \cdot GZ$ in tonnes).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units of $I_T$** — Some students leave $B$ in centimetres and obtain an $I_T$ that is off by a factor of $10^8$. Always work in **metres** consistently.
>
> 2. **Using $I_L$ instead of $I_T$** — For transverse stability, the relevant moment of inertia is about the **centerline** (i.e., $I_T = LB^3/12$). The longitudinal $I_L = BL^3/12$ is used for trimming/Longitudinal stability questions.
>
> 3. **Confusing $KM$, $GM$, and $BM$** — These are three different vertical distances: $KM$ is from keel to metacentre, $GM$ is from $G$ to metacentre, $BM$ is from $B$ to metacentre. Marks are deducted for mixing them up.
>
> 4. **Sign convention for $GZ$** — A positive $GZ$ denotes a **righting** lever (stable); a negative $GZ$ denotes a **capsizing** lever (unstable). The metacentric height $GM$ must be **positive** for initial stability.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the pro-metacentre correction** — For $\theta > 10°$, the simple $GZ = GM \sin\theta$ underestimates the righting lever. Use $GM' = GM + BM(1 - \cos\theta)$ for greater accuracy.
>
> 6. **Density of water** — Always state whether the problem is for **sea water ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³)** or **fresh water ($\rho = 1.000$ t/m³)**. Marks are sometimes awarded for stating the chosen density.
>
> 7. **Algebraic slip in the $I_T$ derivation** — The factor $\tan\theta$ cancels out completely; if your final expression still contains $\tan\theta$, you have made an error. Always cross-check by dimensional analysis.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Metacentric Radius (BM):** A **hull-form constant** for any given loading draft; computed as $BM = I_T/V$. It is **independent** of the position of $G$ and of the angle of heel (for small angles). **(Must Remember)**

- **Metacentre (M):** A *virtual* pivot point on the original ship centerline; defined only in the limit of infinitesimally small heel angles. **(Must Remember)**

- **Pro-Metacentre (M'):** A *physically constructible* point for any finite heel angle $\theta$, obtained by intersecting the new vertical through $B'$ with a line through $G$ parallel to the new waterline. **(Must Remember)**

- **Small-Angle Approximation:** For $\theta \le 10°$, $M \approx M'$ and $GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin\theta$. **(Must Remember)**

- **For Larger Angles:** Use the corrected pro-metacentric formula:
  $$GZ = [GM + BM(1 - \cos\theta)] \cdot \sin\theta$$
  or perform direct integration using Bonjean data. **(Important)**

- **Stability Criterion:** The ship possesses **initial positive stability** if and only if $GM > 0$, i.e., $M$ lies *above* $G$. **(Must Remember)**

- **Vertical Distances from the Keel:**
  $$KM = KB + BM, \quad GM = KM - KG, \quad KG = KM - GM$$
  **(Must Remember)**

- **Transverse vs. Longitudinal Metacentric Radius:**
  $$BM_T = \frac{I_T}{V} \quad \text{(used for rolling stability)}, \quad BM_L = \frac{I_L}{V} \quad \text{(used for trimming)}$$
  Note that $BM_L$ is typically an **order of magnitude larger** than $BM_T$ because ships are much longer than they are broad. **(Important)**

- **Effect of Beam on Stability:** Since $BM \propto B^3$, increasing the beam dramatically increases the metacentric radius. This is why modern wide-beam catamarans have very large initial $GM$. **(Useful Insight)**

- **Effect of Draft on Stability:** Since $BM = I_T / V \propto 1/T$ (for constant waterplane shape), deeper drafts **reduce** the metacentric radius. A lightly loaded ship is "stiff" (high $GM$); a deeply loaded ship is "tender" (low $GM$). **(Useful Insight)**

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metacentric Height
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Metacentric Height ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Metacentric Height (GM)** is defined as the vertical distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** of a floating body (ship) and its **Metacenter (M)**. It is the single most important geometric parameter that quantitatively governs the **initial transverse stability** of a ship at small angles of heel (typically $\phi \leq 10^\circ$ to $15^\circ$). Mathematically, the metacentric height is expressed as: $$GM = KM - KG$$ where **K** is the keel reference point (the lowest point on the ship's centerline at the baseline). The metacenter **M** is the instantaneous center about which the ship rotates (translates) when subjected to a small heeling moment, while **G** is the fixed center of gravity of the ship's mass distribution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must clearly distinguish between **Transverse Metacentric Height (GMT)** and **Longitudinal Metacentric Height (GML)**. The transverse GM governs rolling stability, while the longitudinal GM governs pitching and longitudinal trim — both are derived from the same fundamental principle but differ by orders of magnitude in numerical value. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Floating Broomstick Imagine trying to balance a long wooden broomstick vertically on the tip of your finger. If the broom is perfectly aligned with the vertical line of your finger, it is in unstable equilibrium — the slightest nudge causes it to fall. Now imagine balancing a short, stubby pencil on your finger. It is far more stable because the "center" of mass sits low relative to the support point. A ship behaves analogously. The **Center of Buoyancy (B)** acts like the support point of the "buoyant pencil," and the **Center of Gravity (G)** is where the weight of the ship effectively acts. The **Metacenter (M)** is a fictitious point such that if G lies **below** M, the ship is like the stubby pencil — stable. If G lies **above** M, the ship is like the inverted broomstick — unstable and prone to capsize. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Summary:** A positive metacentric height (GM > 0) implies a stable floating condition. A negative GM (GM < 0) implies an unstable condition where the ship will capsize. Naval architects strive to keep GM in a comfortable range (typically **0.15 m to 0.50 m** for merchant vessels, and higher for naval combatants needing quick rolling response). ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Sea water density (salt water):** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \, kg/m^3$ (standard international value) - **Fresh water density:** $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \, kg/m^3$ - **Standard acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ - **Critical angle of stability** (beyond which formula $GM \tan\phi$ is no longer valid): $\phi_{crit} \approx 10^\circ$ to $15^\circ$ - **Typical Merchant Ship GMT:** 0.15 m to 1.0 m - **Typical Naval Warship GMT:** 1.0 m to 3.0 m (faster roll period for gunnery stability) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Cardinal Points of a Floating Body To fully grasp metacentric height, a KTU student must internalize the geometry of the **five cardinal points** along the ship's centerline: 1. **K — Keel Point:** The intersection of the ship's baseline with the vertical centerline plane. Acts as the universal reference origin. 2. **B — Center of Buoyancy (CoB):** The centroid of the underwater volume (displaced volume). It is the point through which the total buoyant force acts vertically upward. 3. **G — Center of Gravity (CoG):** The centroid of the ship's mass distribution (hull, machinery, cargo, crew, etc.). Total weight $W$ acts vertically downward through this point. 4. **M — Metacenter:** A unique point on the vertical centerline such that, for a small angle of heel, the line of action of the new buoyant force passes through it. 5. **C — Center of Flotation (CoF):** The centroid of the waterplane area. Important for trim calculations (not the primary focus of this module). ### 2.2 Logic Steps — Why Does the Metacenter Exist? When a ship heels by a small angle $\phi$: - **Step 1:** The underwater volume shifts to the low side. The geometric centroid of this new wedge-shaped underwater volume migrates from B to a new position $B'$. - **Step 2:** The new buoyant force, $F_B = \rho g \nabla$, still acts vertically upward, but its line of action now passes through $B'$. - **Step 3:** Crucially, as $\phi$ changes, $B'$ traces a curved locus. The **normal** to this locus (the vertical line of action of buoyancy) intersects the original vertical centerline at a fixed point — **the Metacenter M**. - **Step 4:** For small angles, M can be treated as a fixed point (the metacenter is independent of heel angle only in the linear regime). - **Step 5:** The restoring (righting) couple is generated by the horizontal offset between W (down through G) and $F_B$ (up through the line of action passing through M). The arm of this couple is **GZ**, called the **righting lever**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table compiles **every formula** a KTU student needs for problems on Metacentric Height, Transverse Stability, and Inclining Experiments. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height (direct definition) | m | | 2 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Expanded form using keel reference | m | | 3 | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ | Transverse BM (moment of inertia of waterplane / displaced volume) | m | | 4 | $I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | Second moment of area of rectangular waterplane (length L, beam B) | $m^4$ | | 5 | $BM_L = \dfrac{I_L}{V}$ | Longitudinal BM (very large; governs trim) | m | | 6 | $I_L = \dfrac{B \cdot L^3}{12}$ | Longitudinal second moment of waterplane | $m^4$ | | 7 | $GZ = GM \sin\phi \approx GM \cdot \phi$ | Righting lever at small angle (linear approximation) | m | | 8 | $GZ_{nonlinear} = KN - \sin\phi \cdot KG$ | Righting lever at large angle (where $N$ is on the buoyancy vertical through $B'$) | m | | 9 | $M_R = W \cdot GZ = \rho g \nabla \cdot GM \sin\phi$ | Righting (restoring) moment | $N \cdot m$ | | 10 | $M_H = W \cdot \overline{GG'}\sin\phi$ | Heeling moment from a weight shift | $N \cdot m$ | | 11 | $T = \dfrac{W \cdot GG'}{GM \cdot \Delta\rho g}$ | Inclining experiment formula (shipyard stability test) | m | | 12 | $\Delta = \rho g \nabla$ | Displacement (weight of water displaced) | N or tonnes | | 13 | $h = \dfrac{w \cdot a}{\Delta \cdot \tan\phi}$ | Free surface effect correction: virtual loss of GM | m | | 14 | $T_{roll} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2 + GM}{g}}$ | Approximate roll period (metacentric radius $k$ from radius of gyration) | s | | 15 | $KB = \dfrac{1}{V}\int z \, dV$ | Vertical center of buoyancy (centroid of underwater volume) | m | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The metacentric height is the **single number that determines whether a ship will return upright after being tilted** by wind, waves, turning maneuvers, or a shifting cargo load. Naval architects use it to: - **Predict roll period** — a stiffer ship (high GM) rolls faster and more abruptly, which is undesirable for crew comfort and cargo (liquid tanks sloshing). - **Size bilge keels** — fins under the hull that dampen rolling; effectiveness scales with GM. - **Ensure compliance with IMO intact stability code (IS 2008)** — passenger and cargo ships must demonstrate positive GM under all loading conditions. - **Conduct the Inclining Experiment** — a mandatory test on every newly built ship to confirm the as-built GM matches design calculations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** A ship with $GM = 0$ is in **neutral equilibrium** — it will stay at whatever angle it is heeld to. This is the threshold condition. Naval architects design a **safety margin** of typically **0.10 m to 0.30 m** above the worst-case loading condition to account for free surface effects in partially filled tanks. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — The Metacentric Radius $BM = I_T / V$ Consider a rectangular pontoon of length $L$, beam $B$, floating at draft $T$, with a uniform waterplane area $A_W = L \cdot B$. When the ship heels by a small angle $\phi$ about a longitudinal axis, the underwater volume redistributes: a wedge of volume is "added" on the low side and an equal wedge is "removed" from the high side. **Step 1 — Set up the geometry.** The wedge of added buoyancy has length $L$ (along the ship), width $(B/2)\tan\phi$ at the edge, and tapers linearly to zero at the centerline. The cross-sectional area of this wedge in the transverse plane is: $$A_{wedge} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{B}{2} \cdot \frac{B}{2}\tan\phi = \frac{B^2 \tan\phi}{8}$$ **Step 2 — Volume of the wedge.** Multiplying by length $L$ to get the full wedge volume: $$\nabla_{wedge} = L \cdot A_{wedge} = \frac{L B^2 \tan\phi}{8}$$ **Step 3 — Locate the centroid of the wedge.** The centroid of a triangular wedge measured from the centerline is at $2/3$ of its base. So the transverse distance of the centroid from the centerline is: $$\bar{x}_{wedge} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{B}{2} = \frac{B}{3}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the principle of moments.** The center of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B'$ horizontally. By the principle of moments of volume about the original centerline: $$\nabla \cdot \overline{BB'} = \nabla_{wedge} \cdot \bar{x}_{wedge}$$ Solving for $\overline{BB'}$: $$\overline{BB'} = \frac{\nabla_{wedge} \cdot \bar{x}_{wedge}}{\nabla} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{L B^2 \tan\phi}{8}\right) \cdot \dfrac{B}{3}}{\nabla}$$ $$\overline{BB'} = \frac{L B^3 \tan\phi}{24 \nabla}$$ **Step 5 — Recognize the second moment of area.** Recall the second moment of area of the waterplane about the longitudinal centerline is: $$I_T = \frac{L B^3}{12}$$ Substituting: $$\overline{BB'} = \frac{I_T \tan\phi}{2 \nabla}$$ **Step 6 — Geometric relation to $BM$.** In the right triangle formed by $B$, $B'$, and the metacenter $M$, the angle at $B$ is $\phi$, and the hypotenuse is $BM$. The horizontal offset is $BB' = BM \sin\phi$. For small $\phi$, $\sin\phi \approx \tan\phi \approx \phi$ in radians, giving: $$BM \tan\phi = \frac{I_T \tan\phi}{\nabla}$$ Canceling $\tan\phi$ on both sides: $$\boxed{BM = \frac{I_T}{V}}$$ This is the **metacentric radius**, and its derivation is a KTU board-exam favorite. ### 3.2 Derivation II — Righting Moment $M_R = W \cdot GM \sin\phi$ **Step 1 — Identify the restoring couple.** When the ship heels by angle $\phi$, the weight $W$ acts downward through $G$, while the buoyancy $F_B$ acts upward along the vertical line passing through $B'$ (and $M$). The horizontal separation between these two parallel forces is the **righting lever** $GZ$. **Step 2 — Geometric construction of $GZ$.** Drop a perpendicular from $G$ to the line of action of buoyancy. The distance is: $$GZ = GM \sin\phi$$ This is exact (not an approximation) for any angle $\phi$, provided the metacenter $M$ remains fixed — which is valid only in the **small angle regime** ($\phi \leq 10^\circ$). **Step 3 — Express the moment.** The righting (restoring) moment is force times perpendicular distance: $$M_R = W \cdot GZ = W \cdot GM \sin\phi$$ For the common engineering approximation at small angles: $$M_R \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \phi \quad (\text{with } \phi \text{ in radians})$$ **Step 4 — Alternative form using displacement.** Since the floating condition requires $W = \Delta = \rho g V$: $$M_R = \rho g V \cdot GM \sin\phi$$ ### 3.3 Derivation III — The Inclining Experiment Formula In a shipyard, the actual GM is measured by moving a known test weight $w$ transversely by a known distance $a$ and observing the resulting heel angle $\phi$. **Step 1 — Initial state:** Ship upright, $G$ at known position, $\phi = 0$. **Step 2 — Apply the test weight shift.** Moving weight $w$ by distance $a$ creates a heeling moment: $$M_H = w \cdot a$$ **Step 3 — The ship heels until equilibrium:** The heeling moment equals the righting moment: $$w \cdot a = W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\phi$$ (Note: the shift in G also changes the calculation slightly, but for small angles it is captured as a virtual rise in G, and the standard formula uses $\tan\phi$.) **Step 4 — Solve for GM:** $$\boxed{GM = \frac{w \cdot a}{W \cdot \tan\phi}}$$ This is the **inclining experiment** formula. It is **mandatory** for every newly built ship under SOLAS and IMO regulations, and it is the most direct experimental confirmation of theoretical stability calculations. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stability Conditions at a Glance | Position of G relative to M | GM Value | Stability Behavior | Physical Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | $G$ **below** $M$ | $GM > 0$ | **Stable** — righting moment restores ship to upright | Stubby pencil analogy | | $G$ **coincides with** $M$ | $GM = 0$ | **Neutral** — ship stays at heeled angle indefinitely | Weightless cylinder floating at any angle | | $G$ **above** $M$ | $GM < 0$ | **Unstable** — capsizing moment grows, ship rolls over | Inverted broomstick | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse $BM$ (metacentric radius, always positive) with $GM$ (metacentric height, can be positive or negative). $BM$ depends only on hull form geometry and draft; $GM$ additionally depends on the vertical position of the center of gravity $G$. Always remember: **$GM = KB + BM - KG$**, and $KB$ may be less than, equal to, or greater than $KG$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Five Cardinal Points and Metacentric Geometry ```mermaid graph TD K["K - KEEL POINT
Reference origin at baseline"] B["B - CENTER OF BUOYANCY
Centroid of underwater volume
Buoyancy Force acts UP"] G["G - CENTER OF GRAVITY
Centroid of ship mass
Weight acts DOWN"] M["M - METACENTER
Fixed point for small heel
Intersection of buoyancy verticals"] W["W - WEIGHT VECTOR
Acts downward through G"] FB["F_B - BUOYANCY VECTOR
Acts upward through B prime"] GZ["GZ - RIGHTING LEVER
Horizontal offset = G M sin phi"] K -->|"KB distance upward"| B B -->|"BM metacentric radius"| M K -->|"KG distance upward"| G G -->|"GM = KM minus KG"| M W -->|"creates couple with"| FB FB -->|"separated by"| GZ ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Stability Condition Decision Tree ```mermaid graph TD A["FLOATING SHIP
heeled by small angle phi"] --> B{"Compare G and M positions"} B -->|"G below M"| C["GM greater than 0
STABLE CONDITION
Righting moment restores ship"] B -->|"G equals M"| D["GM equals 0
NEUTRAL EQUILIBRIUM
Ship stays at heel angle"] B -->|"G above M"| E["GM less than 0
UNSTABLE CONDITION
Capsizing moment grows"] C --> F["Design with safety margin
GM typically 0.15 to 1.0 m"] D --> G["Rare and dangerous
Avoid in design"] E --> H["CAPSIZE
Loss of vessel"] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Inclining Experiment Procedure (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD STEP1["STEP 1
Ship in calm water
All weights and tanks in known condition"] STEP2["STEP 2
Record initial pendulum readings
at multiple stations"] STEP3["STEP 3
Move test weight w
by known distance a transversely"] STEP4["STEP 4
Wait for ship to settle
Record new pendulum readings"] STEP5["STEP 5
Compute heel angle phi
from pendulum deflections"] STEP6["STEP 6
Apply formula
GM equals w times a divided by W times tan phi"] STEP7["STEP 7
Repeat with weight moved
to opposite side to verify"] STEP8["STEP 8
Correct GM for free surface effects
in partially filled tanks"] STEP1 --> STEP2 STEP2 --> STEP3 STEP3 --> STEP4 STEP4 --> STEP5 STEP5 --> STEP6 STEP6 --> STEP7 STEP7 --> STEP8 STEP8 --> STEP9["FINAL AS-BUILT GM
Documented for IMO compliance"] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Free Surface Effect Correction Logic ```mermaid graph TD FSA["Free Surface Effect
Partially filled liquid tank"] --> FSB["Virtual rise in G
h equals w times a divided by Delta times tan phi"] FSB --> FSC["Reduces effective GM
GM effective equals GM minus h"] FSC --> FSD["Critical at low drafts
and slack tanks"] FSD --> FSE["Design mitigation
Compartment tanks
or fill completely"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the term "Metacenter" of a floating body. How is it different from the Center of Buoyancy?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **metacenter (M)** of a floating body is defined as the point on the vertical centerline of the body at which the line of action of the buoyant force intersects the original vertical centerline when the body is given a small angular displacement. (2 marks) It differs from the **Center of Buoyancy (B)**, which is the centroid of the displaced volume of fluid and through which the buoyant force always acts vertically upward. (1 mark) The metacenter is a **geometric reference point** for stability analysis, while the center of buoyancy is the **point of application of the buoyancy force**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] State the formula for metacentric height. What does a positive metacentric height indicate?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The metacentric height is given by: $$GM = KM - KG = KB + BM - KG$$ where **KB** is the height of the center of buoyancy above the keel, **BM** is the metacentric radius ($= I_T / V$), and **KG** is the height of the center of gravity above the keel. (2 marks) A **positive metacentric height (GM > 0)** indicates that the **Center of Gravity G lies below the Metacenter M**, which means the floating body is in a **stable equilibrium** and will experience a restoring moment when tilted, returning it to the upright position. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]* **(a)** A ship of displacement **12,000 tonnes** floats at a draft of **6.0 m** in sea water. The waterplane area is **1500 m²** and its transverse second moment of area is **1.20 × 10⁵ m⁴**. The center of buoyancy is at **3.20 m** above the keel and the center of gravity is at **5.80 m** above the keel. Determine: (7 marks) (i) The metacentric radius $BM$ (ii) The metacentric height $GM$ (iii) The righting moment when the ship heels by **6°** **(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **Inclining Experiment** procedure used to determine the metacentric height of a newly built ship. State the formula used and the corrections applied. (7 marks) --- #### **Question A — Model Solution** **Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given data:** - Displacement $\Delta = 12{,}000$ tonnes $= 12{,}000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 1.1772 \times 10^8 \, N$ - Draft $T = 6.0 \, m$ - Waterplane area $A_W = 1500 \, m^2$ - Transverse second moment of area $I_T = 1.20 \times 10^5 \, m^4$ - $KB = 3.20 \, m$ - $KG = 5.80 \, m$ **Step 1 — Compute the displaced volume.** $$V = \frac{\Delta}{\rho g} = \frac{1.1772 \times 10^8}{1025 \times 9.81} = 11{,}707 \, m^3$$ **Step 2 — Compute the metacentric radius $BM$.** $$BM = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{1.20 \times 10^5}{11{,}707} = 10.25 \, m$$ **Step 3 — Compute the metacentric height $GM$.** $$GM = KB + BM - KG = 3.20 + 10.25 - 5.80 = 7.65 \, m$$ **Step 4 — Compute the righting moment at $\phi = 6°$.** $$M_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi = 1.1772 \times 10^8 \times 7.65 \times \sin 6°$$ $$M_R = 1.1772 \times 10^8 \times 7.65 \times 0.10453 = 9.413 \times 10^7 \, N \cdot m$$ $$\boxed{M_R \approx 94.13 \, MN \cdot m}$$ --- **Part (b) — Inclining Experiment Explanation (7 marks)** The **inclining experiment** is a mandatory shipyard test performed on every newly built vessel to determine the **as-built lightship GM**. The procedure is as follows: 1. **Preparation (1 mark):** The ship is moored in calm, sheltered water. All loose items are secured, fuel and water tanks are pressed up or fully pressed, and bilges are dry. Pendulums (typically 6 to 8) are installed at key locations, and a small known test weight $w$ (usually 1–2% of displacement) is positioned amidships near the centerline. 2. **Initial Readings (1 mark):** With the test weight on the centerline, the initial pendulum deflections are recorded at all stations. The ship is in the upright condition. 3. **Weight Movement and Final Readings (2 marks):** The test weight is moved transversely (typically to port) by a known, precisely measured distance $a$. After the ship settles (3 to 5 minutes), the new pendulum readings are taken. The heel angle $\phi$ is calculated from the pendulum deflections and the pendulum lengths. The procedure is repeated by moving the weight to the starboard side to cross-verify. 4. **GM Calculation (1 mark):** The metacentric height is computed using the formula: $$GM = \frac{w \cdot a}{\Delta \cdot \tan\phi}$$ 5. **Corrections Applied (1 mark):** - **Free surface correction** for any slack (partially filled) tanks - **Trim correction** if the ship has any fore-aft inclination - **Density correction** if the water density differs from the assumed value - **Wind and mooring line correction** if environmental conditions are non-ideal 6. **Documentation (1 mark):** The final corrected lightship GM and the position of $G$ ($KG$) are documented in the ship's stability booklet, as required by **SOLAS and IMO intact stability regulations**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023]* **(a)** A rectangular pontoon of length **40 m**, beam **10 m**, and draft **2.5 m** floats in fresh water. The center of gravity is located **2.0 m** above the keel. Calculate: (7 marks) (i) The position of the center of buoyancy $KB$ (ii) The metacentric radius $BM$ (iii) The metacentric height $GM$ and comment on the stability **(b)** With the help of a sketch, explain the **righting moment** and **righting lever** for a heeled ship. Distinguish between **initial stability** and **stability at large angles**. (7 marks) --- #### **Question B — Model Solution** **Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given data:** - Length $L = 40 \, m$ - Beam $B = 10 \, m$ - Draft $T = 2.5 \, m$ - $KG = 2.0 \, m$ - Fresh water: $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$ **Step 1 — Compute displaced volume.** $$V = L \cdot B \cdot T = 40 \times 10 \times 2.5 = 1000 \, m^3$$ **Step 2 — Position of center of buoyancy $KB$.** For a rectangular pontoon, the underwater volume is a rectangular prism. The centroid of this prism lies at half the draft above the keel: $$KB = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{2.5}{2} = 1.25 \, m$$ **Step 3 — Second moment of area of the waterplane.** For a rectangular waterplane: $$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{40 \times 10^3}{12} = \frac{40 \times 1000}{12} = 3333.33 \, m^4$$ **Step 4 — Metacentric radius $BM$.** $$BM = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{3333.33}{1000} = 3.333 \, m$$ **Step 5 — Metacentric height $GM$.** $$GM = KB + BM - KG = 1.25 + 3.333 - 2.0 = 2.583 \, m$$ **Comment:** $GM = +2.583 \, m > 0$, therefore the pontoon is in a **stable** floating condition. (However, this value is unusually high for a ship and would result in a very stiff, uncomfortable roll — for this pontoon, a smaller ballast or load distribution would be more practical.) --- **Part (b) — Righting Moment and Stability Regimes (7 marks)** **Sketch and Definitions (3 marks):** When a ship heels by angle $\phi$: - The weight $W$ continues to act vertically downward through $G$ (which is a fixed point on the ship). - The buoyant force $F_B$ acts vertically upward through the new center of buoyancy $B'$, and its line of action passes through the metacenter $M$. - These two equal and opposite parallel forces form a **couple** called the **righting (restoring) couple**. - The perpendicular distance between the two lines of action is the **righting lever** $GZ$. The righting moment is: $$M_R = W \cdot GZ = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi$$ **Initial Stability (Small Angles, $\phi \leq 10°$ to $15°$) (2 marks):** - $M$ is treated as a fixed point on the centerline. - $GZ = GM \sin\phi \approx GM \cdot \phi$ (linear relationship) - Righting moment increases linearly with heel angle. - Stability is governed entirely by the **single number GM**. **Stability at Large Angles ($\phi > 15°$) (2 marks):** - The metacenter $M$ is no longer fixed; it migrates along a complex curve called the **locus of metacenter** or **M-curve**. - The righting lever $GZ$ is computed using the **statical stability curve** (also called the **GZ curve** or **cross curve of stability**): $$GZ = KN - \sin\phi \cdot KG$$ where $N$ is the point on the vertical line of action of buoyancy at the heeled waterline, and $KN$ comes from the **bonjean curves** or direct integration of the underwater wedge volumes. - The **GZ curve** rises to a maximum (angle of maximum righting moment, typically 30°–45°), then decreases, crosses zero at the **angle of vanishing stability** $\phi_v$, and becomes negative (capsizing region) beyond that. - The **area under the GZ curve** from 0 to $\phi_v$ represents the **dynamic stability** — the total work done by the righting moment during a complete roll to the angle of capsize. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit confusion on $w \cdot a$ vs $\Delta \cdot \tan\phi$:** The test weight shift $w \cdot a$ must use **weight** (in tonnes-force or Newtons), and the displacement $\Delta$ must use the **same units**. Mixing tonnes and Newtons costs 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to convert $\phi$ from degrees to radians** when using $\sin\phi$ vs $\tan\phi$ in small-angle approximations — these are numerically different at 5° or more. > 3. **Treating $BM$ as the answer to the metacentric height** — $BM$ is the metacentric radius (always positive). $GM$ depends additionally on $KG$ and $KB$. > 4. **Using the wrong moment of inertia formula** — transverse BM requires the **transverse** $I_T$ (about the longitudinal axis), and longitudinal BM requires the **longitudinal** $I_L$ (about the transverse axis). These differ by a factor of $(L/B)^2$. > 5. **Ignoring free surface corrections** in problems involving partially filled tanks — the examiner often deducts 1–2 marks if this is omitted. > 6. **Confusing draft and displacement volume** — the displaced volume $V$ is **not** equal to the draft. $V = L \times B \times T$ only for rectangular pontoons. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Summary — Metacentric Height Module:** - ✅ **Five cardinal points** to memorize: K (keel), B (buoyancy centroid), G (gravity centroid), M (metacenter), C (center of flotation). - ✅ **Master formula chain:** $GM = KM - KG = KB + BM - KG$ — this is the single most important equation. - ✅ **Metacentric radius:** $BM = I_T / V$ — derived from wedge geometry; valid for all heel angles in principle, but the *fixed-M assumption* is only valid for small angles. - ✅ **Transverse vs Longitudinal BM:** $BM_T = I_T / V$ (using $B^3$); $BM_L = I_L / V$ (using $L^3$). Longitudinal BM is roughly $(L/B)^2$ times larger. - ✅ **Righting lever:** $GZ = GM \sin\phi$ (small angle exact); for large angles, use $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$ with the statical stability curve. - ✅ **Righting moment:** $M_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi$ — this is what restores the ship against wind, waves, and heeling forces. - ✅ **Stability conditions:** $GM > 0 \Rightarrow$ stable; $GM = 0 \Rightarrow$ neutral; $GM < 0 \Rightarrow$ unstable (capsize). - ✅ **Inclining experiment formula:** $GM = (w \cdot a) / (\Delta \cdot \tan\phi)$ — mandatory for every newly built ship. - ✅ **Free surface effect:** partially filled tanks cause a virtual loss of GM equal to $h = (w_{tank} \cdot a_{tank}) / (\Delta \cdot \tan\phi_{tank})$ — always correct for slack tanks in GM calculations. - ✅ **Roll period approximation:** $T_{roll} = 2\pi \sqrt{(k^2 + GM)/g}$ — higher GM gives shorter (faster, stiffer) roll period. - ✅ **Typical design values:** Merchant ship GMT 0.15–1.0 m; warship GMT 1.0–3.0 m; too high = stiff and uncomfortable; too low = tender and prone to capsize. - ✅ **Physical constants:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \, kg/m^3$, $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \, kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$. - ✅ **Angle of vanishing stability** $\phi_v$ is where the GZ curve crosses zero — the boundary between stable and capsizing regions for large angles. - ✅ **Dynamic stability** = area under the GZ curve from 0 to $\phi_v$ — represents total work done by righting forces during capsize roll.

topics.title: Metacentric Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Metacentric Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metacentric Curve (Locus of Metacentre):** The metacentric curve is defined as the smooth curve traced by the metacentre (point **M**) of a floating body as the body is progressively inclined (heeled) through successive angles of heel **φ** in the transverse vertical plane, keeping the displacement **∇** (or weight **W**) constant.

In the body plan (or transverse section view) of the ship, the metacentre does **not** remain fixed once the angle of heel exceeds a few degrees. As the ship rolls, the centre of buoyancy **B** migrates along the **B-B curve** (curve of centres of buoyancy), and the metacentre **M** correspondingly shifts its position. The continuous locus of all such instantaneous positions of **M** for **φ = 0° → 90°** constitutes the **metacentric curve**.

**Key Reference Points on the Curve:**

| Symbol | Description | Position |
|:------:|:-----------|:---------|
| **M₀** | Initial (or upright) metacentre | At heel angle **φ = 0°** |
| **Mφ** | Instantaneous metacentre at angle φ | Anywhere along the curve |
| **G** | Centre of gravity | Fixed point inside the hull |
| **Bφ** | Centre of buoyancy at angle φ | On the B-B curve |

> [!NOTE]
> The metacentric curve is **NOT** identical to the B-B curve. The metacentre **M** lies on the vertical line drawn through the **shifted** centre of buoyancy **Bφ**, and **NOT** directly above **Bφ** at a fixed offset.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a long stick vertically on the palm of your hand, with the lower end resting on your palm and trying to balance it. As you gently tilt the stick from side to side, the point where the stick "appears to pivot" (above the top of the stick) moves in a curved path on the wall behind you.

**The ship behaves in a similar way:**

- Your hand represents the **centre of buoyancy (B)**.
- The pivot point above represents the **metacentre (M)**.
- As you tilt the stick (the ship heels), the pivot point (metacentre) traces a **curve** on the wall (the metacentric curve).
- The curved path traced is the **metacentric curve**.

A more relatable analogy: Think of a tall lamp post in your room. The base of the lamp is **B**, the centre of gravity is **G**, and the balancing point (where it would "tip over" around) is **M**. As you imagine tilting the lamp post at different angles, the balancing point **M** moves along a curve — that curve is the **metacentric curve**.

For **small angles of heel (φ ≤ 10°)** the metacentre stays almost at one spot — this is the **M₀ (initial metacentre)**. For larger angles, it drifts outward and forms the characteristic convex shape.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (KTU Convention)

- **Standard small-angle threshold:** **φ ≤ 10°** (where metacentre can be treated as fixed at **M₀**).
- **Heel direction convention:** Positive heel is **to starboard** (right side, looking forward).
- **Displacement invariant:** Total weight **W = ρg∇** remains **constant** at all heel angles for a free-floating ship.
- **Symmetric hull assumption:** For a ship with **port-starboard symmetry** about the centreline, the metacentric curve is plotted only for **φ = 0° → 90°** and mirrors for the other side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The metacentric curve is drawn in the **transverse plane** (midship-section view) — *never* in the longitudinal profile, because the heel motion is purely a transverse rotation about the ship's longitudinal axis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction of the metacentric curve is a logical extension of the metacentre concept. Let us break down the underlying logic:

### Step 1 — Establish the Upright Equilibrium
With the ship upright (**φ = 0°**):
- The centre of buoyancy is at **B₀** (centroid of the underwater volume).
- The metacentre is at **M₀**, located at a height **BM₀ = I_T / ∇** above **B₀**, where **I_T** is the **transverse second moment of area** of the waterplane about the centreline.

### Step 2 — Heel the Ship by a Small Angle φ
When the ship heels by angle **φ** (without change in displacement):
- A wedge of buoyancy **δ∇** emerges on the immersed side; an equal wedge emerges on the emerged side.
- The centre of buoyancy **B** shifts laterally to **Bφ** along the **B-B curve**.

### Step 3 — Locate the New Metacentre Mφ
The metacentre is the point of intersection of:
- **(i)** The vertical line through the new centre of buoyancy **Bφ** (action line of the new buoyancy force).
- **(ii)** A line drawn through the **original metacentre M₀** at angle **φ** to the original vertical — *equivalently*, a line drawn through **G** at angle **φ** to the original vertical in the *at-small-angle* approximation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Misconception:** The line through G at angle φ is only valid for **small angles of heel**. For **large angles**, the line must be drawn through **G** parallel to the new waterline (or equivalently, normal to the inclined centreline plane).

### Step 4 — Repeat for Successive Heel Angles
Repeat Steps 2–3 for **φ = 5°, 10°, 15°, 20°, ... , 90°** and plot each **Mφ** position. The smooth curve joining all these points is the **metacentric curve**.

### Step 5 — Verification: Convexity
The metacentric curve is **always convex toward the centreline** for a normal ship form. If it ever becomes concave, the ship is in **dynamically unstable territory** (a strong indicator of imminent capsize).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises every formula you will need for KTU 2024 board examination questions on the metacentric curve. All equations use **SI units**.

| # | Formula | Description | When to Use |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------------|
| 1 | $BM_0 = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | Transverse metacentric radius at φ = 0° | Upright condition |
| 2 | $I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | Second moment of waterplane area (rectangular approximation) | Approximate waterplane |
| 3 | $GM_0 = KB_0 + BM_0 - KG$ | Initial metacentric height | Stability check at φ = 0° |
| 4 | $KB_{\phi} = KB_0 \cos\phi + \text{lateral offset of } B_\phi$ | Vertical position of heeled B | Constructing B-B curve |
| 5 | $BM_{\phi} = \dfrac{I_{T\phi}}{\nabla}$ | Transverse BM at angle φ (large angle) | Large-angle metacentric curve |
| 6 | $GG_1 = \dfrac{BN}{\cos\phi}$ (approx) | Shift of G virtually | Lever-arm analysis |
| 7 | $GZ = GM_0 \sin\phi$ | Righting lever (small angle) | $\phi \leq 10°$ only |
| 8 | $GZ = KB_0 \sin\phi - \overline{BB_1}\cos\phi$ | Righting lever (large angle, exact) | $\phi > 10°$ |
| 9 | $M_\phi \text{ located at } B_\phi \text{ vertically, at distance } BM_\phi$ | Definition of Mφ | Constructing metacentric curve |
| 10 | $w = \dfrac{BM_\phi \cdot \tan\phi}{2} + \dfrac{BM_0 \cdot \tan\phi}{2}$ (form not standard) | Wall-sided metacentric locus | Wall-sided ships |

### Boundary Conditions & Limits

- **At φ = 0°:** Metacentric curve **passes through M₀** (initial metacentre) by definition.
- **At φ = 90° (for non-wall-sided ships):** The metacentric curve generally terminates on the centreline at a height that can be analytically computed.
- **Wall-sided ship (constant cross-section):** Metacentric curve is a **straight line parallel to the centreline** at height $M_\phi = M_0$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The metacentric curve is **not** a purely academic construct. It has direct industrial applications:

1. **Large-Angle Stability Analysis:** Real ships operate in beam seas, following seas, and storm conditions where heel angles routinely exceed **20°–30°**. The metacentric curve is essential for **GZ-curve computation** at these large angles.
2. **Damage Stability (SOLAS Convention):** After compartment flooding, the metacentre shifts drastically. The metacentric curve for the **damaged waterline** is mandatory under IMO/SOLAS regulations.
3. **Warship Design:** Naval architects use the metacentric curve to ensure the ship can **survive combat damage** while still maintaining positive GM at extreme heel angles.
4. **Submarine Stability:** When a submarine submerges, the entire metacentric curve must be re-computed for the new waterplane geometry.
5. **Capsize Prediction:** The **shape and convexity** of the metacentric curve provides early warning of potential capsize modes.

> [!TIP]
> Always remember: the metacentric curve is plotted **M-φ** in the **transverse body plan**, while the **GZ curve** is plotted **GZ-φ** in a separate stability booklet. They are **companion diagrams**, not the same.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Transverse Metacentric Radius (BM₀)

### Statement
The transverse metacentric radius $BM_0$ of a floating body is given by the ratio of the transverse second moment of area of the waterplane ($I_T$) to the displaced volume ($\nabla$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider a ship floating upright, then heeled by a small angle $d\phi$. By the geometry of the shift of **B**:

$$dB = \frac{I_T \cdot d\phi}{\nabla}$$

This is the **standard naval architecture result** for the differential shift of the centre of buoyancy.

The metacentre **M** is defined as the point through which the line of action of buoyancy must pass for the centre of buoyancy to shift along the arc of radius $BM$:

$$BM = \frac{dB}{d\phi}$$

Substituting the expression for $dB$:

$$BM = \frac{I_T \cdot d\phi}{\nabla \cdot d\phi}$$

$$\boxed{\,BM_0 = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}\,}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The factor $d\phi$ cancels because, for an infinitesimal heel, the **arc length** and the **chord length** of the B-curve are equal to first order.

### Dimensional Check
- $[I_T] = m^4$
- $[\nabla] = m^3$
- $[BM_0] = m$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Position of Mφ (Large-Angle Construction)

### Statement
For a heel angle $\phi$ (not necessarily small), the metacentre $M_\phi$ is located at the intersection of the vertical through $B_\phi$ and the inclined line through $G$ parallel to the new waterline (or normal to the inclined centreline plane).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let us set up a coordinate system with origin at the original **B₀**, x-axis pointing to starboard, and z-axis pointing upward.

**Step (a):** Identify the new centre of buoyancy $B_\phi$. This is computed using the **wedge method** or the **wall-sided formula** depending on hull form.

**Step (b):** Draw a vertical line $VV'$ through $B_\phi$. This represents the line of action of the buoyancy force in the heeled condition.

**Step (c):** Through the centre of gravity **G**, draw a line $LL'$ at angle $\phi$ to the original vertical (or equivalently, perpendicular to the heeled centreline plane).

**Step (d):** The intersection of $VV'$ and $LL'$ defines the metacentre $M_\phi$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Coordinates of } M_\phi &= (x_{B_\phi},\ z_{B_\phi} + BM_\phi \cdot \text{projection}) \\
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The line through G is drawn at angle $\phi$ because the buoyancy force (acting vertically through $B_\phi$) and the weight (acting vertically through G) must be equal and opposite for equilibrium. The transverse separation between the two vertical lines creates the righting couple.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Wall-Sided Metacentric Curve

### Statement
For a **wall-sided ship** (constant rectangular cross-section from keel to a certain height), the metacentric curve is a **straight line parallel to the centreline**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

A wall-sided ship has **constant half-breadth** $y_0$ at all heel angles considered. The waterplane area is therefore rectangular, and the transverse second moment is:

$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot (2y_0)^3}{12} = \frac{2 L y_0^3}{3}$$

This is **independent of the heel angle** $\phi$. Since $\nabla$ is also constant (constant displacement):

$$BM_\phi = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{2Ly_0^3}{3\nabla} = \text{constant}$$

The metacentre $M_\phi$ therefore moves only **horizontally** (laterally) by an amount equal to the lateral shift of B, and **not vertically**.

Hence, the metacentric curve reduces to a **horizontal straight line** at height:

$$\boxed{\,z_{M_\phi} = z_{B_0} + BM_0 = \text{constant}\,}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Since both $I_T$ and $\nabla$ are constants in a wall-sided ship, the vertical position of M is fixed. The lateral motion of M mirrors the lateral motion of B.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Relation Between Metacentric Curve and GZ Curve

### Statement
The **GZ curve** (righting lever vs. heel angle) is the *vertical distance* from the metacentric curve (projected) to the centre of gravity **G**, multiplied by $\sin\phi$.

### Analytical Relation

For any heel angle $\phi$, the righting lever is:

$$GZ = GM_\phi \cdot \sin\phi$$

where $GM_\phi$ is the distance from G to $M_\phi$ measured along the line at angle $\phi$ to the vertical.

Equivalently, in terms of the metacentric curve height $z_{M_\phi}$ and the G height $z_G$:

$$GZ = (z_{M_\phi} - z_G) \cdot \sin\phi$$

> **Conversion logic:** The factor $\sin\phi$ appears because $GM_\phi$ is measured along the inclined line, while GZ is the *vertical* (or transverse, depending on convention) projection of that lever onto the stability axis.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Construction Methodology Summary

| Step | Action | Tool / Reference | Output |
|:----:|:-------|:-----------------|:-------|
| 1 | Plot ship hull lines in body plan | Lines plan / offsets | Transverse section profile |
| 2 | For each φ, find Bφ using wedge formula | $\overline{BB_1} = \dfrac{v \cdot \ell}{3\nabla}$ | B-B curve points |
| 3 | Draw vertical through Bφ | Set square / plotting board | Vertical line of buoyancy |
| 4 | Draw line through G at angle φ | Protractor | Inclined line of weight |
| 5 | Mark intersection → Mφ | Ruler | Metacentre point |
| 6 | Repeat for φ = 0°, 5°, 10°, ... 90° | — | Set of Mφ points |
| 7 | Draw smooth curve through Mφ points | French curve | **Metacentric curve** |
| 8 | Verify convexity toward centreline | Visual inspection | Validity check |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Construction Flow of Metacentric Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Ship Upright at phi = 0] --> B[Identify B0 and M0]
    B --> C[Heel Ship by angle phi]
    C --> D[Compute new centre of buoyancy Bphi]
    D --> E{Wall sided ship?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Use Bphi lateral shift formula]
    E -- No --> G[Use wedge-by-wedge integration]
    F --> H[Draw vertical line through Bphi]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Draw inclined line through G at angle phi]
    I --> J[Mark intersection point Mphi]
    J --> K[Plot Mphi on body plan]
    K --> L{More angles to plot?}
    L -- Yes --> C
    L -- No --> M[Draw smooth curve through Mphi points]
    M --> N[Verify convexity toward centreline]
    N --> O[End: Metacentric Curve Complete]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Stability Relationships

