<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a framing system and list its three main classifications.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

> A *framing system* is the structural skeleton of a ship, consisting of transverse and longitudinal stiffening members (frames, floors, girders, brackets) along with the shell, deck, and bottom plating, working together to resist hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, cargo, and inertial loads.

**The three main classifications are:**

1. **Transverse Framing System** — frames run athwartships.
2. **Longitudinal Framing System** — frames run fore-aft.
3. **Mixed Framing System** — combination of both, typically with longitudinal bottom and transverse deck.

---

### Question 2: Why is longitudinal framing preferred for long ships?
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Long ships (length-to-depth ratio $L/D > 12$) experience large wave-induced bending moments along their length. Because longitudinal framing places the primary stiffeners parallel to the direction of maximum bending stress, the effective section modulus of the hull girder is significantly higher for the same steel weight. This makes longitudinal framing structurally more efficient, lighter, and more economical for long vessels such as container ships and naval combatants.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with internal choice. Both alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) below are **fully independent**. Students answer *one* complete alternative.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of neat sketches, describe the transverse framing system of a merchant ship. List its merits and demerits.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A transverse framing system consists of closely spaced transverse frames running athwartships at regular intervals, supported by a few large longitudinal girders and stringers.

**2. Sketch description (3 marks):**

A typical cross-section of a transversely framed hull contains:
- **Bottom plating** — flat horizontal plate forming the outer bottom.
- **Side shell plating** — vertical plate on each side.
- **Floors** — vertical transverse plates at every frame station extending from bilge to bilge at the bottom, connected to the keel.
- **Frames** — vertical T-bars or L-angles on each side, fitted between floors and reaching up to the deck.
- **Reverse frames / Deck beams** — transverse members across the deck.
- **Side stringers** — horizontal girders in the side shell, breaking the frame height into panels.
- **Tank top plating** (in double-bottom ships) — inner horizontal plate above the floors.
- **Centerline keelson** — large longitudinal girder running fore-aft along the centerline.
- **Side girders** — longitudinal girders running fore-aft at the bottom.
- **Brackets** — triangular knee plates at the frame-floor-girder intersections.

**3. Merits (1.5 marks):**
- Easier fabrication — frames are short and identical.
- Excellent access for maintenance and tank inspection.
- Better transverse strength for racking loads.
- Simpler design calculations.
- Preferred for vessels with concentrated cargo loads (ore, oil).

**4. Demerits (1.5 marks):**
- Less efficient in resisting longitudinal bending.
- Heavier hull for long ships.
- Larger plate thickness required to span between frames.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare transverse and longitudinal framing systems in a tabular form. State three typical vessels for each system.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Transverse | Longitudinal |
|---|---|---|
| Frame orientation | Athwartships | Fore-aft |
| Section modulus for same weight | Lower | Higher (~20–30% more) |
| Hull weight | Higher | Lower |
| Plate thickness required | Greater | Smaller |
| Maintenance access | Excellent | Poor |
| Construction complexity | Simple | More complex |
| Best suited L/D | $< 10$ | $> 12$ |

**Typical vessels — Transverse Framing (1.5 marks):**
1. Bulk carriers
2. Oil tankers (small to medium)
3. General cargo ships

**Typical vessels — Longitudinal Framing (1.5 marks):**
1. Container ships
2. Naval combatants (destroyers, frigates)
3. Large crude oil tankers (VLCCs)

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the longitudinal framing system with a neat sketch. What are its advantages over transverse framing?**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
In longitudinal framing, the primary stiffeners run **fore-aft** (along the ship's length). Transverse members are reduced to heavy web frames at intervals of 2–3 m and bulkheads. The bottom, side shell, and deck plating are all stiffened by closely spaced longitudinals.

**2. Sketch description (3 marks):**
- **Longitudinals** — T-sections at 300–600 mm spacing on bottom, side, and deck plating.
- **Web frames** — deep transverse frames every 2–3 m, providing support to the longitudinals.
- **Transverse bulkheads** — watertight vertical plates at intervals.
- **Deck plating + longitudinals** form the top flange of the hull girder.
- **Bottom plating + longitudinals** form the bottom flange.
- **Floors** are typically replaced by a smooth tank top with stiffened plate.

**3. Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Higher longitudinal strength** — efficient in resisting wave-induced bending moment.
2. **Lighter hull** — less steel required for equivalent strength.
3. **Thinner plate required** — longitudinals reduce unsupported plate span.
4. **Reduced stress concentration** — better fatigue life.
5. **Preferred for high-speed vessels** — better dynamic response.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A side shell plate is 12 mm thick. The water pressure is 35 kN/m². The allowable bending stress is 165 MPa. Assuming the plate is clamped on all four edges with coefficient $k = 0.038$, calculate (i) the maximum theoretical frame spacing and (ii) the practical frame spacing using a factor of safety of 2.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Plate thickness $t = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m
- Pressure $p = 35$ kN/m² $= 35 \times 10^3$ N/m²
- Allowable stress $\sigma_{perm} = 165$ MPa $= 165 \times 10^6$ N/m²
- Edge-fixity coefficient $k = 0.038$
- Factor of safety $FS = 2$

**Formula:**
$$
s_{max} = \dfrac{t}{\sqrt{6k}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{perm}}{p}}
$$

**Step 1: Compute denominator inside first square root**

$$
\sqrt{6k} = \sqrt{6 \times 0.038} = \sqrt{0.228} = 0.4775
$$

**Step 2: Compute the stress-to-pressure ratio**

$$
\dfrac{\sigma_{perm}}{p} = \dfrac{165 \times 10^6}{35 \times 10^3} = 4714.29
$$

**Step 3: Take its square root**

$$
\sqrt{4714.29} = 68.66
$$

**Step 4: Compute $s_{max}$ (theoretical) (3 marks)**

$$
s_{max} = \dfrac{0.012}{0.4775} \times 68.66
$$

$$
s_{max} = 0.02513 \times 68.66 = 1.725 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $s_{practical}$ using FS = 2 (2 marks)**

$$
s_{practical} = \dfrac{s_{max}}{FS} = \dfrac{1.725}{2} = 0.8625 \text{ m} \approx 862 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion (2 marks)**

> The practical frame spacing works out to be approximately **862 mm**, which is at the upper end of the standard transverse frame spacing range (500–800 mm). For a typical merchant ship, this would be rounded down to **800 mm** to comply with classification society rules and provide additional margin against corrosion and fatigue.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students lose marks in framing-system questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in formulas — KTU examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** whenever units are missing in a numerical.
> 2. **Confusing "transverse" with "longitudinal"** — always state the orientation first when describing a framing system.
> 3. **Skipping the sketch label** — when the question says "with neat sketch," every member (frame, floor, girder, keel, bracket) must be **labeled**. A diagram without labels earns only 50% credit.
> 4. **Mixing up merits and demerits** — keep them in separate bullets, not interleaved.
> 5. **Wrong frame spacing** — students often quote 800–1000 mm without justification. Always derive it or reference a classification rule.
> 6. **Numerical errors in $s_{max}$ formula** — forgetting the $\sqrt{6k}$ factor or using $6$ instead of $12$ in the section modulus ($t^2/6$) is a frequent mistake.
> 7. **No mention of factor of safety** in numericals — KTU expects explicit mention of FS when converting theoretical to practical values.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Framing Systems (Module 4)**

- ☐ A framing system is the **structural skeleton** of a ship, comprising frames, floors, girders, brackets, and plating.
- ☐ Three classifications: **Transverse, Longitudinal, Mixed** — know the orientation, application, and at least 3 vessel types for each.
- ☐ **Transverse framing** → frames athwartships, 500–800 mm spacing, used in short bulk carriers and tankers.
- ☐ **Longitudinal framing** → stiffeners fore-aft, 300–600 mm spacing, used in long container ships and naval vessels.
- ☐ **Mixed framing** → bottom longitudinal + deck transverse, used in passenger ships and ferries.
- ☐ **Longitudinal framing is structurally more efficient** because ship bending moment is primarily longitudinal.
- ☐ Standard metrics: frame spacing = **500–800 mm**, web frame every 3–5 frame spaces, longitudinal spacing = 3× to 5× frame spacing.
- ☐ Key formula: $s_{max} = \dfrac{t}{\sqrt{6k}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{perm}}{p}}$ — memorize with units.
- ☐ Hull-girder bending moment (approx.): $M_{hog} = \dfrac{W \cdot L \cdot C_b}{20}$.
- ☐ Section modulus: $SM = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{perm}}$ — used to size longitudinals and plating.
- ☐ Always include **factor of safety ≈ 2** when converting theoretical to practical frame spacing.
- ☐ In answer scripts, **draw labeled diagrams** — KTU awards 30–40% marks for diagrams alone in Part B.
- ☐ Mention at least **two merits and two demerits** when comparing framing systems.
- ☐ For numericals, show **all steps** — KTU examiners check intermediate work, not just final answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primary and Secondary Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Primary and Secondary Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Ship Structural Engineering** as prescribed under the KTU 2024 Scheme, the structural skeleton of a ship's hull is broadly classified into two functional hierarchies based on their **load-carrying contribution** and **structural significance**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Members** are the main longitudinal and transverse structural elements of the ship's hull girder that collectively participate in resisting the **overall longitudinal bending stresses** (hogging and sagging) and **transverse racking forces** experienced by the vessel at sea. They form the principal load path from one extremity of the ship to the other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Secondary Members** are the smaller, locally-distributed structural components that provide **local strength, stiffness, and shape retention** to the plating panels and primary members. They do **not** contribute significantly to the global hull girder strength but are essential for preventing local plate buckling, vibration, and panel failure.

The classification is fundamentally based on **stress contribution hierarchy**:

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{longitudinal\,global} + \sigma_{transverse\,local} + \sigma_{secondary\,plate\,buckling}
$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{longitudinal\,global}$ → Handled by **Primary Members**
- $\sigma_{transverse\,local}$ + $\sigma_{secondary\,plate\,buckling}$ → Handled by **Secondary Members**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Human Skeleton vs. Skin**
>
> Imagine a human body:
> - The **spine, ribs, skull, and long bones (femur, tibia)** are the **Primary Members**. They carry the body's weight, enable standing upright, and transfer all major loads to the ground. Without them, the body collapses.
> - The **muscle fibers, tendons, and small connective tissues** are the **Secondary Members**. They give local shape, allow fine movement, and prevent the skin from collapsing inward, but they cannot support the body's weight on their own.
>
> Similarly, in a ship:
> - The **keel, shell plating, decks, bulkheads, girders, and floors** act like the skeleton — they hold the ship together against the massive forces of waves and cargo.
> - The **frames, beams, stiffeners, and brackets** act like the muscles — they keep the plating flat, prevent buckling, and distribute localized loads (like point loads from machinery) safely into the primary skeleton.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> - **Yield Stress of Mild Steel (Shipbuilding Grade)** $E$: typically **235 MPa** (Grade A) to **355 MPa** (Grade AH36).
> - **Young's Modulus for Steel**: $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ **MPa** (≈ 210 GPa).
> - **Standard Frame Spacing** in ships: typically **500 mm to 700 mm** (longitudinal frames) or **2 m to 3 m** (transverse frames).
> - **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)**: standard longitudinal reference for hull girder calculations.
> - **Section Modulus Requirement**: Class societies mandate minimum **Z ≥ f(L, B, D)** relationships, where $Z$ is the hull girder section modulus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Hierarchy — Detailed Breakdown

The structural members are arranged in a strict functional hierarchy. Each member has a clearly defined **role, load path, and failure consequence**.

### A. Primary Members (Main Structural Elements)

These are mandatory, continuous, and load-bearing. Their failure can lead to catastrophic hull loss.

#### A.1 Longitudinal Primary Members
These run along the **length** of the ship and resist longitudinal bending:

| # | Member | Function | Location |
|---|--------|----------|----------|
| 1 | **Keel** | Longitudinal backbone, bottom strength | Bottom centerline |
| 2 | **Shell Plating (Bottom, Side)** | Outer watertight skin + bending flange | Outer hull |
| 3 | **Deck Plating** | Top tensile/compressive flange of hull girder | Top of hull |
| 4 | **Sheer Strake** | Top strake of side shell, often thickened | Upper turn of bilge |
| 5 | **Stringer Plate** | Transition strake between side and deck | Deck edge |
| 6 | **Deck Longitudinals / Girders** | Strengthen deck against longitudinal bending | Within deck plating |
| 7 | **Inner Bottom Plating** | Double bottom upper plate (tankers) | Above keel |
| 8 | **Hatch Coamings** | Strengthen deck openings (cargo ships) | Around cargo hatches |

#### A.2 Transverse Primary Members
These run **athwartships** and resist transverse racking, shearing, and local loads:

| # | Member | Function |
|---|--------|----------|
| 1 | **Floors (Solid/Bracket)** | Transverse plate connecting port & starboard bottom |
| 2 | **Web Frames** | Deep transverse frames in machinery/engine spaces |
| 3 | **Transverse Bulkheads** | Watertight divisions, prevent flooding spread |
| 4 | **Side Girders** | Longitudinal members within double bottom |
| 5 | **Deck Girders** | Deep transverse beams supporting deck |
| 6 | **Pillars / Stanchions** | Vertical supports between decks/tanks |
| 7 | **Stem and Sternpost** | Fore and aft vertical strength members |

### B. Secondary Members (Local Strength Elements)

These are repetitive, smaller, and primarily resist **local bending and buckling** of plating.

| # | Member | Function |
|---|--------|----------|
| 1 | **Ordinary Frames** | Maintain shape of shell plating between web frames |
| 2 | **Reverse Frames** | Frames built in opposite orientation (some designs) |
| 3 | **Beams** | Support deck plating locally |
| 4 | **Stiffeners** | Small T/L sections welded to plating to prevent buckling |
| 5 | **Brackets** | Triangular connecting plates at frame-beam junctions |
| 6 | **Lug Plates** | Small connection plates for joints |
| 7 | **Deck Longitudinals (sometimes secondary)** | In longitudinally framed ships, these are sometimes classified as secondary if their contribution to global strength is small |
| 8 | **Bilge Keel Support Members** | Local reinforcement at bilge |

## 2.2 The Underlying Engineering Logic — Why Two Tiers?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Logic Behind the Classification:**
>
> **Step 1 — The Hull Girder Concept:**
> A ship floating in waves acts like a hollow box-girder (length = LBP, depth = ship's depth). The hull girder bends longitudinally — **hogging** (bow & stern down, midship up) and **sagging** (bow & stern up, midship down).
>
> **Step 2 — Identifying Global Load Carriers:**
> The components that lie at the **maximum distance from the neutral axis** (deck top and keel bottom) experience the highest bending stress $\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$. These must be **thick, continuous, and uninterrupted** — hence **Primary**.
>
> **Step 3 — Identifying Local Load Carriers:**
> The remaining parts of the structure (plating between floors, side shell between frames) must resist **hydrostatic pressure, wave slap, cargo pressure**, and **plate buckling** — but only locally. These are **Secondary**.
>
> **Step 4 — Stiffness Hierarchy:**
> Primary members are designed first, then secondary members are added to "stiffen" the plating panels so they don't buckle under in-plane compression from the primary bending.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** under KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress in hull girder | MPa |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | Section modulus of hull girder | m³ |
| 3 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Hull girder bending stress (simplified) | MPa |
| 4 | $I = \sum A_i \cdot y_i^2$ | Moment of inertia by parallel axis theorem | m⁴ |
| 5 | $Z_{min} = f(L, B, CB)$ | Minimum required section modulus per Class rules | m³ |
| 6 | $\sigma_{critical} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 E}{12 (1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | Plate buckling stress (for secondary members) | MPa |
| 7 | $P_{hydro} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure on shell | N/m² or Pa |
| 8 | $M_{wave} = k \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot CB$ | Wave bending moment (Rule-based) | N·m |
| 9 | $f_b = \dfrac{t}{b} \leq \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{\sigma_y}}$ | Slenderness ratio check for plate panels | dimensionless |

> **Where:**
> - $M$ = Bending moment (N·m)
> - $y$ = Distance from neutral axis (m)
> - $I$ = Second moment of area (m⁴)
> - $E$ = Young's Modulus = **2.1 × 10⁵ MPa**
> - $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio for steel = **0.3**
> - $k$ = Buckling coefficient (depends on boundary condition, typically 4 to 6.97)
> - $t$ = Plate thickness (mm or m)
> - $b$ = Plate breadth between stiffeners (mm or m)
> - $L, B$ = Length, Breadth of ship (m)
> - $CB$ = Block coefficient (typically 0.6 to 0.85)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> This classification is the **foundation of all class society rules** (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV). Naval architects first design primary members to satisfy hull girder strength, then distribute secondary members to satisfy **local strength and fatigue requirements**. A failure to distinguish between the two leads to either **over-design** (wasted steel, costly ship) or **under-design** (catastrophic failure like the *MV Mol Comfort*断裂事故 or the *Titanic*'s hull failure).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1: Hull Girder Section Modulus from Primary Members

The hull girder's moment of inertia is **not** calculated by treating the hull as a single beam — it is **assembled from all the primary longitudinal members**, each treated as a discrete flange contributing to the global $I$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — List all primary longitudinal members (continuous from bow to stern).**

$$
\text{Members} = \{A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots, A_n\}
$$

Where $A_i$ is the cross-sectional area of the $i^{th}$ primary member in m².

**Step 2 — Locate each member's centroidal distance from the baseline (keel).**

$$
y_i = \text{distance of } A_i \text{ from baseline (m)}
$$

**Step 3 — Locate the Neutral Axis (NA) of the entire hull girder.**

For a ship in **still water + symmetrical loading**, the neutral axis is approximately at the **midship level of the loaded waterline**. For Rule-based calculations, the NA is often taken at the **mid-depth** of the hull (deck + keel)/2. Using the **first moment of area balance**:

$$
y_{NA} = \dfrac{\sum A_i \cdot y_i}{\sum A_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute moment of inertia of each member about the NA using the Parallel Axis Theorem.**

For each primary member $A_i$ with its own moment of inertia $I_{i,local}$:

$$
I_i = I_{i,local} + A_i \cdot (y_i - y_{NA})^2
$$

**Step 5 — Sum over all primary members.**

$$
I_{NA} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ I_{i,local} + A_i \cdot (y_i - y_{NA})^2 \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Section Modulus.**

The deck experiences maximum tensile/compressive stress (furthest from NA), so:

$$
Z_{deck} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{D - y_{NA}}
$$

And the keel experiences:

$$
Z_{keel} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{NA}}
$$

The **governing section modulus** for Rule compliance is the **smaller** of the two:

$$
Z_{min} = \min(Z_{deck}, Z_{keel})
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Deck plating: $A_1 = 0.04$ m² at $y_1 = 10$ m
- Bottom shell: $A_2 = 0.05$ m² at $y_2 = 1$ m
- Side shell (avg): $A_3 = 0.06$ m² at $y_3 = 5.5$ m
- Sheer strake: $A_4 = 0.02$ m² at $y_4 = 9$ m

(Assume local $I_{i,local}$ is small and ignored for simplicity in this example.)

**Step 1 — Compute NA:**

$$
y_{NA} = \dfrac{(0.04)(10) + (0.05)(1) + (0.06)(5.5) + (0.02)(9)}{0.04 + 0.05 + 0.06 + 0.02}
$$

$$
y_{NA} = \dfrac{0.4 + 0.05 + 0.33 + 0.18}{0.17}
$$

$$
y_{NA} = \dfrac{0.96}{0.17} = 5.647 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_{NA}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{NA} &= (0.04)(10 - 5.647)^2 \\
&\quad + (0.05)(1 - 5.647)^2 \\
&\quad + (0.06)(5.5 - 5.647)^2 \\
&\quad + (0.02)(9 - 5.647)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.04)(4.353)^2 &= (0.04)(18.95) = 0.7580 \\
(0.05)(4.647)^2 &= (0.05)(21.59) = 1.0797 \\
(0.06)(0.147)^2 &= (0.06)(0.0216) = 0.0013 \\
(0.02)(3.353)^2 &= (0.02)(11.24) = 0.2249
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
I_{NA} = 0.7580 + 1.0797 + 0.0013 + 0.2249 = 2.0639 \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus (taking depth D = 10 m):**

$$
Z_{deck} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{D - y_{NA}} = \dfrac{2.0639}{10 - 5.647} = \dfrac{2.0639}{4.353} = 0.4742 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
Z_{keel} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{NA}} = \dfrac{2.0639}{5.647} = 0.3655 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Governing: $Z_{min} = 0.3655$ m³**

> [!NOTE]
> This $Z_{min}$ is now compared against the **Rule-required** minimum section modulus. If actual < required, the ship **cannot** be classed. The designer must increase plate thickness or add more primary longitudinals.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2: Plate Buckling Stress for Secondary Members

Secondary members (stiffeners) prevent the plating from buckling under in-plane compression. The **critical buckling stress** is derived from classical plate theory.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Consider a flat rectangular plate under uniform in-plane compression.**

The plate is simply supported on all four edges (typical assumption for plating between stiffeners), with dimensions:
- Length: $a$ (m)
- Breadth: $b$ (m, the **stiffener spacing**)
- Thickness: $t$ (m)

**Step 2 — Apply the classical plate buckling formula (Bryan/Timoshenko):**

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2
$$

Where:
- $k$ = Buckling coefficient, depends on aspect ratio $(a/b)$ and edge conditions.
  - For long plates $(a/b \to \infty)$ simply supported: $k = 4.0$
  - For square plates $(a/b = 1)$ simply supported: $k \approx 4.0$ (minimum)
  - For clamped edges: $k$ can be up to $6.97$ or higher
- $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa
- $\nu = 0.3$ for steel

**Step 3 — Plug in the numerical values for steel:**

$$
1 - \nu^2 = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91
$$

$$
\dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \dfrac{(9.8696)(2.1 \times 10^5)}{12 \times 0.91} = \dfrac{2.0726 \times 10^6}{10.92} = 1.898 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplified steel plate buckling formula:**

$$
\sigma_{cr} \approx 1.9 \times 10^5 \cdot k \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example:**

Given a side shell plate with:
- $t = 12$ mm
- $b = 600$ mm (frame spacing)
- Simply supported (k = 4.0)

Compute:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = 1.9 \times 10^5 \cdot 4.0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{12}{600}\right)^2
$$

$$
\sigma_{cr} = 7.6 \times 10^5 \cdot (0.02)^2 = 7.6 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.0004
$$

$$
\sigma_{cr} = 304 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since the yield stress $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa, the buckling stress is **higher than yield**, meaning the plate will yield before buckling — **safe design**.

**If frame spacing increases to $b = 1000$ mm:**

$$
\sigma_{cr} = 1.9 \times 10^5 \cdot 4.0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{12}{1000}\right)^2 = 7.6 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.000144 = 109.44 \text{ MPa}
$$

This is **less than yield stress (235 MPa)** → **Buckling occurs before yielding** → **Plate fails**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion from this derivation:**
> Secondary members (stiffeners, frames) are **essential** to reduce the unsupported plate breadth $b$. Closer spacing = higher buckling resistance = safer panel.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Is a Member Primary or Secondary?

| Criterion | Primary Member | Secondary Member |
|---|---|---|
| Contribution to hull girder $I$ | Large | Negligible |
| Failure consequence | Catastrophic, total loss | Local, repairable |
| Stress type resisted | Global bending ($M/I$) | Local bending + buckling |
| Size | Heavy, thick, deep | Light, thin, small |
| Continuity | Continuous bow to stern | Localized |
| Spacing | Wide (web frames every 2-3 m) | Close (every 500-700 mm) |
| Driving load | Wave bending, still-water shear | Hydrostatic pressure, local loads |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The primary members form the **outer skeleton** that defines the hull girder shape and carries global loads. The secondary members are the **internal mesh** that subdivide the plating into manageable panels and prevent local buckling. Both tiers are essential — they work as an integrated system, not in isolation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper, Part A]**
*Define the terms **Primary Members** and **Secondary Members** as applied to a ship's hull structure. Give two examples of each.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Primary Members:** These are the main structural components of the hull girder that participate in resisting the overall longitudinal and transverse loads (bending, shear, racking) experienced by the ship. They are thick, continuous, and critical for global structural integrity.

**Examples (any two):** (1) Keel, (2) Shell plating, (3) Deck plating, (4) Transverse bulkheads, (5) Floors, (6) Web frames, (7) Pillars.

**Secondary Members:** These are smaller, locally distributed structural elements that provide local strength, shape retention, and prevent buckling of the plating panels. They do not contribute significantly to the global hull girder strength.

**Examples (any two):** (1) Ordinary frames, (2) Stiffeners, (3) Beams, (4) Bracket plates, (5) Lug plates.

*(1 Mark for each correct definition + 0.5 Mark each for two examples = 3 Marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper, Part A]**
*Why are stiffeners classified as secondary members even though they carry some load? Justify with reference to hull girder strength.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

Stiffeners are classified as **secondary members** because:

1. **Negligible contribution to hull girder section modulus** — although stiffeners resist local plate buckling, their cross-sectional area is so small compared to the main plating that they contribute very little to the overall moment of inertia $I$ and section modulus $Z$ of the hull girder. (1 Mark)

2. **Local load function only** — they primarily prevent in-plane buckling of plating panels under compressive stresses induced by global bending. Their failure causes local plate dimpling but not catastrophic hull failure. (1 Mark)

3. **Class society convention** — per IACS and IRS rules, members whose removal or failure does not compromise the longitudinal strength of the hull girder are classified as secondary. Stiffeners fall in this category by definition. (1 Mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper, Part B]**

**(a)** Classify the structural members of a ship's hull into primary and secondary categories. Explain with a neat sketch the role of primary members in resisting longitudinal bending of the hull girder. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship has the following primary longitudinal members at midship:

| Member | Area (m²) | Distance from Baseline (m) |
|--------|-----------|----------------------------|
| Deck plating | 0.045 | 11.0 |
| Sheer strake | 0.025 | 9.5 |
| Side shell (avg) | 0.070 | 5.5 |
| Bottom shell | 0.060 | 1.0 |
| Keel plate | 0.030 | 0.5 |

Determine:
(i) The position of the neutral axis from the baseline.
(ii) The moment of inertia of the hull girder about the neutral axis.
(iii) The minimum section modulus of the hull girder.

Take depth of ship $D = 11$ m. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The structural members of a ship's hull are classified as follows:

**Primary Members (continuous, global load carriers):**
1. Keel (bar/flat plate/duct keel)
2. Shell plating (bottom, side, sheer strake)
3. Deck plating and stringer plate
4. Longitudinal girders and deck longitudinals
5. Transverse bulkheads
6. Floors (solid/plate)
7. Web frames
8. Side girders (in double bottom)
9. Pillars/stanchions
10. Hatch coamings
11. Stem and sternpost

**Secondary Members (local, shape-retaining):**
1. Ordinary frames
2. Reverse frames
3. Deck beams
4. Stiffeners
5. Bracket plates
6. Lug plates
7. Doubler plates

**Role in Longitudinal Bending:**

The hull girder, when floating in waves, experiences longitudinal bending — **hogging** (midship up) and **sagging** (midship down). The bending stress is given by:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

The primary members located farthest from the neutral axis (deck plating and keel) experience the maximum stress and act as the **flanges** of the hull girder (analogous to the flanges of an I-beam). The side shell and bulkheads act as the **webs** carrying shear. Thus, primary members ensure the hull girder has sufficient **section modulus $Z = I/y$** to resist the wave bending moment $M_{wave}$ within allowable stress limits.

*(Classification listing = 4 Marks; Role in bending explanation = 3 Marks)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $A_1 = 0.045$ m² at $y_1 = 11.0$ m
- $A_2 = 0.025$ m² at $y_2 = 9.5$ m
- $A_3 = 0.070$ m² at $y_3 = 5.5$ m
- $A_4 = 0.060$ m² at $y_4 = 1.0$ m
- $A_5 = 0.030$ m² at $y_5 = 0.5$ m
- $D = 11$ m

**(i) Position of Neutral Axis:**

$$
y_{NA} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute numerator:

$$
\sum A_i y_i = (0.045)(11.0) + (0.025)(9.5) + (0.070)(5.5) + (0.060)(1.0) + (0.030)(0.5)
$$

$$
= 0.4950 + 0.2375 + 0.3850 + 0.0600 + 0.0150 = 1.1925 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Compute denominator:

$$
\sum A_i = 0.045 + 0.025 + 0.070 + 0.060 + 0.030 = 0.230 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3:**

$$
y_{NA} = \dfrac{1.1925}{0.230} = 5.1848 \text{ m from baseline}
$$

**(2 Marks)**

---

**(ii) Moment of Inertia about NA:**

Using parallel axis theorem: $I = \sum A_i (y_i - y_{NA})^2$

| Member | $A_i$ | $y_i$ | $y_i - y_{NA}$ | $(y_i - y_{NA})^2$ | $A_i(y_i - y_{NA})^2$ |
|--------|-------|-------|----------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Deck | 0.045 | 11.0 | +5.8152 | 33.817 | 1.5218 |
| Sheer | 0.025 | 9.5 | +4.3152 | 18.621 | 0.4655 |
| Side | 0.070 | 5.5 | +0.3152 | 0.0994 | 0.0070 |
| Bottom | 0.060 | 1.0 | -4.1848 | 17.512 | 1.0507 |
| Keel | 0.030 | 0.5 | -4.6848 | 21.947 | 0.6584 |

Summing:

$$
I_{NA} = 1.5218 + 0.4655 + 0.0070 + 1.0507 + 0.6584 = 3.7034 \text{ m}^4
$$

**(3 Marks)**

---

**(iii) Minimum Section Modulus:**

$$
Z_{deck} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{D - y_{NA}} = \dfrac{3.7034}{11 - 5.1848} = \dfrac{3.7034}{5.8152} = 0.6369 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
Z_{keel} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{NA}} = \dfrac{3.7034}{5.1848} = 0.7143 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
Z_{min} = \min(0.6369, 0.7143) = 0.6369 \text{ m}^3
$$

**(2 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model Paper, Part B]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of hull girder bending stress. Derive the formula for bending stress and explain how primary members contribute to the section modulus. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A side shell plate between two stiffeners has dimensions: thickness $t = 14$ mm, breadth $b = 700$ mm. The plate is simply supported on all four edges. Determine whether the plate will yield or buckle first under an applied compressive stress. Take $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa, and buckling coefficient $k = 4.0$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The hull girder is conceptually a **box beam** with the keel as the bottom flange, the deck as the top flange, and the side shell + bulkheads as the webs. When the ship sails in waves, the vertical wave bending moment causes longitudinal stresses distributed linearly across the depth of the hull girder.

**Derivation of bending stress:**

Consider a small element of the hull girder at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis. The bending strain at this depth is:

$$
\epsilon = \dfrac{y}{\rho}
$$

Where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature. By Hooke's law:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon = \dfrac{E \cdot y}{\rho}
$$

The bending moment is the integral of stress times area times distance:

$$
M = \int \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int \dfrac{E \cdot y^2}{\rho} dA = \dfrac{E}{\rho} \int y^2 \, dA
$$

Define moment of inertia:

$$
I = \int y^2 \, dA
$$

So:

$$
M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{\rho}
$$

From the strain equation: $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{\epsilon}{y} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E \cdot y}$

Substituting back:

$$
M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{\rho} = \sigma \cdot \dfrac{I}{y}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

**Section modulus:**

$$
Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}} \Rightarrow \sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}
$$

**Primary members' contribution:**
Primary members located at maximum $y$ (deck and keel) contribute the most to $I$ because their area is multiplied by a large $(y - y_{NA})^2$ term in the parallel axis theorem. Hence, **increasing deck or keel thickness** dramatically increases $I$ and hence $Z$, reducing the maximum stress for a given bending moment.

*(Derivation = 4 Marks; Section modulus explanation = 3 Marks)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $t = 14$ mm
- $b = 700$ mm
- $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa
- $\nu = 0.3$
- $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa
- $k = 4.0$

**Step 1:** Compute the slenderness ratio:

$$
\dfrac{t}{b} = \dfrac{14}{700} = 0.02
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2 = (0.02)^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the plate buckling stress:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2
$$

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \cdot (9.8696) \cdot (2.1 \times 10^5)}{12 \cdot (1 - 0.09)} \cdot (4 \times 10^{-4})
$$

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 2.1 \times 10^5}{12 \cdot 0.91} \cdot 4 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Compute the numerator of the constant term:**

$$
4.0 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 2.1 \times 10^5 = 4.0 \cdot 2.0726 \times 10^6 = 8.2904 \times 10^6
$$

**Compute the denominator of the constant term:**

$$
12 \cdot 0.91 = 10.92
$$

**Constant factor:**

$$
\dfrac{8.2904 \times 10^6}{10.92} = 7.5927 \times 10^5
$$

**Multiply by slenderness squared:**

$$
\sigma_{cr} = 7.5927 \times 10^5 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-4} = 303.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Compare:**

$$
\sigma_{cr} = 303.7 \text{ MPa} > \sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion:**

Since the buckling stress (303.7 MPa) is **greater than** the yield stress (235 MPa), the plate will **yield before it buckles**. This means the plate is **safe** under compressive loading — it will deform plastically but not buckle elastically.

*(Critical buckling stress calculation = 5 Marks; Comparison and conclusion = 2 Marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Sheer Strake with Side Shell:** The sheer strake is a thickened upper strake of side shell, classified as PRIMARY. Students often incorrectly classify it as secondary. (−1 to −2 Marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting to take $y_i - y_{NA}$ in the parallel axis theorem** (using $y_i$ instead): This gives an inflated $I$ and wrong section modulus. Full marks deducted if the formula is misapplied. (−2 to −3 Marks)
>
> 3. **Not identifying the minimum of $Z_{deck}$ and $Z_{keel}$:** The rule-compliant section modulus is the smaller one. Students often pick only one and miss the comparison step. (−1 Mark)
>
> 4. **Plate buckling formula unit errors:** Mixing mm and m in $t/b$ ratio. Always keep $t$ and $b$ in the **same units**. (−1 to −2 Marks)
>
> 5. **Ignoring the secondary members' role:** Students sometimes list secondary members but fail to explain their **engineering purpose** (preventing local buckling). Examiners expect at least 1–2 lines on the function of each tier. (−1 Mark)

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Primary and Secondary Members**

- [x] **Primary members** carry global loads (longitudinal bending, transverse racking, shear) and contribute to hull girder $I$ and $Z$.
- [x] **Secondary members** carry local loads (plate buckling, local bending, vibration) and have negligible effect on hull girder $Z$.
- [x] **Keel, shell plating, deck plating, sheer strake, hatch coamings** → PRIMARY (longitudinal).
- [x] **Floors, web frames, bulkheads, side girders, pillars, stem, sternpost** → PRIMARY (transverse).
- [x] **Ordinary frames, stiffeners, beams, brackets, lug plates, doubler plates** → SECONDARY.
- [x] **Bending stress formula:** $\sigma = My/I = M/Z$.
- [x] **Section modulus formula:** $Z = I/y_{max}$.
- [x] **NA position:** $y_{NA} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$.
- [x] **Parallel axis theorem:** $I = \sum [I_{local} + A(y - y_{NA})^2]$.
- [x] **Plate buckling stress:** $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} (t/b)^2$.
- [x] For steel: $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y$ typically 235 to 355 MPa.
- [x] **Governing section modulus:** $Z_{min} = \min(Z_{deck}, Z_{keel})$.
- [x] Primary members must be **continuous bow-to-stern** for full hull girder action.
- [x] Secondary members are **closely spaced** (typically 500–700 mm) to prevent buckling.
- [x] **Failure of primary member = catastrophic**; failure of secondary member = local/repairable.
- [x] Class society rules (IRS, LR, DNV) mandate **minimum $Z$** based on $L$, $B$, $D$, $CB$.
- [x] Hull girder acts as a **box beam** with deck/keel as flanges and side shell as web.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shipbuilding Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Shipbuilding Materials

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Core Definition**
> **Shipbuilding Materials** are defined as the carefully selected engineering materials (ferrous, non-ferrous, alloys, composites, and hybrid systems) used in the construction of the hull, superstructure, machinery foundations, piping, and outfitting of marine vessels, evaluated against strict marine service requirements of **yield strength (Re) ≥ 235 N/mm²**, **ultimate tensile strength (Rm)**, **impact toughness (KV at 0°C ≥ 27 J)**, **corrosion resistance in saline environment**, **weldability per IACS / Classification Society rules**, and **fatigue endurance in wave-induced cyclic loading**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, shipbuilding materials are categorized by their **functional zone** within the vessel:
- **Primary hull structural material** (shell plating, frames, stiffeners)
- **Secondary structural material** (bulkheads, decks, superstructure)
- **Outfitting and machinery material** (propellers, shafts, valves, piping)
- **Specialized material** (corrosion-resistant alloys, non-magnetic, cryogenic for LNG carriers)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: "The Skeleton & Skin of a Ship"**
> Think of a ship as a giant floating building that must survive three brutal enemies simultaneously: **saltwater corrosion**, **cyclic wave loading**, and **impact collisions**.
> - The **hull steel** is like the **skeleton + skin of a human body** rolled into one — it must be strong, slightly flexible (to absorb wave shocks), and resistant to rust.
> - **Aluminum alloys** are like the **lightweight aluminum frame of an aircraft** — used where weight savings mean fuel savings (superstructures, fast ferries).
> - **GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic)** is like the **fiberglass body of a speedboat** — rust-proof, light, but needs careful engineering to handle high loads.
> - **Bronze propellers** are like the **brass plumbing fittings in your house** — copper + tin alloys that resist seawater cavitation damage.
> - **Wood (traditional teak/irola)** is the **original shipbuilding material** — like the ancient wooden longboats of the Vikings, still used in small craft and fishing boats.

The selection of a shipbuilding material is therefore an **optimization problem** between **strength, weight, cost, corrosion resistance, weldability, and availability**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Standard Marine Engineering Metrics (Bolded)**
> - **Density of mild steel (MS) = 7,850 kg/m³** (the baseline reference material)
> - **Density of seawater = 1,025 kg/m³** (used for buoyancy calculations)
> - **Yield strength of ordinary mild steel (Grade A) = 235 N/mm²**
> - **Yield strength of high tensile steel (Grade AH32) = 315 N/mm²**
> - **Yield strength of high tensile steel (Grade AH36) = 355 N/mm²**
> - **Yield strength of aluminum alloy (5083-H116) ≈ 215 N/mm²** (but only 1/3 the density of steel)
> - **Charpy V-notch impact energy requirement for hull steel at 0°C = 27 J minimum** (IACS UR S6)
> - **Young's Modulus of steel (E) = 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (= 210 GPa)
> - **Young's Modulus of aluminum (E) = 0.7 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (= 70 GPa)
> - **Young's Modulus of GRP (typical) = 0.15–0.30 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (= 15–30 GPa — anisotropic)

## 1.4 Material Classification Pyramid in Shipbuilding

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize the **hierarchical classification** of marine materials:

| Tier | Material Class | Typical Application |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Mild Steel (MS) — Grade A** | Conventional merchant ship hulls (>90% of world fleet) |
| 2 | **High Tensile Steel (HTS) — AH/DH/EH 32, 36, 40** | Warships, large container ships (saves weight) |
| 3 | **Aluminum Alloys (5083, 5086, 5456, 6082)** | Superstructures, fast ferries, naval craft |
| 4 | **Copper Alloys (Brass, Bronze, Cupro-Nickel)** | Propellers, seawater piping, valves |
| 5 | **Stainless Steel (304, 316, Duplex)** | Chemical carriers, LNG cargo tanks, fittings |
| 6 | **GRP / FRP Composites** | Small craft, patrol boats, minesweepers |
| 7 | **Wood (Teak, Iroko, Marine Plywood)** | Traditional boats, fishing vessels, decking |
| 8 | **Concrete / Ferrocement** | Barges, pontoons, specialized unmanned craft |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Property Requirements — The "Seven Pillars" of Marine Material Selection

> [!NOTE] **Why these seven properties?**
> A ship sails for 25–30 years in one of the most aggressive environments on Earth. Every material choice must satisfy ALL of these criteria simultaneously, and the compromise between them defines naval architecture.

### Pillar 1: Mechanical Strength
- **Yield Strength (Re)** — the stress at which permanent (plastic) deformation begins
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (Rm)** — maximum stress before fracture
- **Elongation at break (A%)** — ductility indicator; **minimum 22%** for hull steel per IACS

### Pillar 2: Toughness (Impact Resistance)
- **Charpy V-notch test (KV)** at the **design temperature** of the vessel
- Polar / North Sea vessels require testing at **-40°C** (Grade E steel)
- LNG carriers and Arctic ships need cryogenic toughness verification

### Pillar 3: Corrosion Resistance
- **Uniform corrosion rate in seawater ≤ 0.1 mm/year** for unprotected mild steel
- **Galvanic corrosion** prevention via proper alloy pairing
- **Cathodic protection** (sacrificial zinc/aluminum anodes) is mandatory for steel hulls

### Pillar 4: Weldability
- **Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) ≤ 0.45%** per IACS UR W11 for hull steel
- **Weldability formula:**

$$CEV = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15} \quad (\%)$$

- Lower CEV → easier to weld, less risk of Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC)

### Pillar 5: Formability
- Ability to be **rolled, bent, and shaped** into compound-curved hull plates
- Cold forming radius ≥ 2t (where t = plate thickness) for mild steel

### Pillar 6: Weight (Density Optimization)
- **Specific Strength = Yield Strength / Density** — the key figure-of-merit for naval architects

$$\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{R_e}{\rho} \quad \text{(N·m/kg)}$$

- High specific strength → lighter ship → higher payload or speed

### Pillar 7: Cost and Availability
- Mild steel is the **cheapest structural material** at roughly **USD 600–800/tonne**
- Aluminum costs **3–4× more** than mild steel
- GRP costs **5–10× more** than mild steel per kg of structure

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Major Shipbuilding Materials

### 2.2.1 Mild Steel (MS) — Grade A to Grade E (per IACS UR S6)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mild Steel: The Workhorse of World Shipping**
> Approximately **90–95% of all commercial ships** are built from mild steel. It is the default material because of its excellent balance of strength, weldability, and cost.

**Chemical Composition Range (typical):**
- **Carbon (C) ≤ 0.21%** (low carbon ensures weldability)
- **Manganese (Mn): 0.6–1.4%** (improves strength)
- **Silicon (Si) ≤ 0.35%**
- **Sulfur (S) ≤ 0.035%**, **Phosphorus (P) ≤ 0.035%** (kept low to prevent brittleness)

**Grade Classification (per IACS):**
| Grade | Yield Strength (Re) | Tensile Strength (Rm) | Min. Elongation (A%) | Impact Test Temp |
|-------|--------------------|-----------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| A | 235 N/mm² | 400–520 N/mm² | 22% | +20°C |
| B | 235 N/mm² | 400–520 N/mm² | 22% | 0°C |
| D | 235 N/mm² | 400–520 N/mm² | 22% | -20°C |
| E | 235 N/mm² | 400–520 N/mm² | 22% | -40°C |
| AH32 | 315 N/mm² | 440–590 N/mm² | 22% | 0°C |
| DH32 | 315 N/mm² | 440–590 N/mm² | 22% | -20°C |
| AH36 | 355 N/mm² | 490–620 N/mm² | 21% | 0°C |
| DH36 | 355 N/mm² | 490–620 N/mm² | 21% | -20°C |
| EH36 | 355 N/mm² | 490–620 N/mm² | 21% | -40°C |

> [!NOTE] **Naming Convention**
> The letters carry meaning:
> - **No prefix** = Normal Strength Steel (NSS), Re = 235 N/mm²
> - **A** prefix = Higher grade, **D** prefix = Grade D toughness, **E** prefix = Arctic grade
> - **32/36/40** = Yield strength in ksi or in ×10 N/mm²
> - The **tougher the grade (A→E)**, the more expensive it is, so naval architects use it only where needed (e.g., strength deck, sheer strake, bilge strake).

### 2.2.2 High Tensile Steel (HTS)

HTS allows **thinner plates** for the same strength, reducing hull weight by 15–25%.

**Hull Weight Saving Calculation:**

$$t_{HTS} = t_{MS} \times \frac{R_{e,MS}}{R_{e,HTS}}$$

For Grade AH36 vs Grade A:

$$t_{AH36} = t_A \times \frac{235}{355} = 0.662 \times t_A$$

This means a **33.8% reduction in plate thickness** for the same structural strength, directly reducing displacement and increasing cargo capacity.

### 2.2.3 Aluminum Alloys in Marine Use

> [!IMPORTANT] **Aluminum: The Lightweight Champion**
> Aluminum is **2.7 times lighter than steel** but with comparable (or slightly lower) yield strength. It is used for **superstructures, helicopter decks, fast ferries, and naval patrol craft**.

**Common Marine Aluminum Alloys:**

| Alloy | Temper | Yield Strength | Application |
|-------|--------|----------------|-------------|
| 5083 | H116 / H321 | 215 N/mm² | Hull plating of fast ferries |
| 5086 | H116 | 195 N/mm² | Hulls of small craft |
| 5456 | H116 | 230 N/mm² | Naval patrol boats |
| 6082 | T6 | 260 N/mm² | Superstructure, mast, deck fittings |
| 6061 | T6 | 240 N/mm² | Deck equipment, accommodation ladders |

**Key property — corrosion resistance:**
- Aluminum forms a **self-healing oxide layer (Al₂O₃)** that protects it in seawater
- **Galvanic corrosion** is the MAJOR risk — aluminum must NEVER be in direct electrical contact with steel or copper (always use **insulating gaskets** or **zinc anodes**)

### 2.2.4 Copper Alloys — Bronze Propellers and Cu-Ni Piping

**Manganese Bronze (High-Tensile Brass):**
- Composition: ~60% Cu, 38% Zn, 1.5% Mn, 0.5% Fe, 0.5% Al, 0.5% Sn
- Used for **propellers up to 25 knots**
- Tensile strength ≈ 470 N/mm²
- **Cavitation resistance** is excellent

**Nickel-Aluminum Bronze (NAB):**
- Composition: ~78% Cu, 9–11% Al, 4–6% Ni, 4–6% Fe
- Used for **high-performance naval propellers** (corvettes, frigates)
- **Superior cavitation-erosion resistance**
- Tensile strength ≈ 700 N/mm²
- Used in seawater for 30+ years without significant corrosion

**Cupro-Nickel (Cu-Ni 90/10 and 70/30):**
- Used for **seawater piping, heat exchangers, condensers**
- 90/10 = 90% Cu + 10% Ni + 1% Fe + 1% Mn
- 70/30 = 70% Cu + 30% Ni + 0.5% Fe + 0.5% Mn
- Excellent resistance to **biofouling and corrosion** in flowing seawater

### 2.2.5 Stainless Steel

**Austenitic Stainless Steel (304, 316):**
- **304 SS**: 18% Cr, 8% Ni — used in freshwater systems
- **316 SS**: 16% Cr, 10% Ni, 2% Mo — used in marine/chemical environments (better Cl⁻ resistance)
- Used in **LNG cargo tanks (membrane type)**, chemical tankers, food-grade piping

**Duplex Stainless Steel (2205, 2507):**
- Mixed austenitic-ferritic microstructure
- Yield strength ≈ 460 N/mm² (twice that of 316L)
- Excellent stress-corrosion cracking resistance
- Used in **submarine pressure hulls, deep-sea equipment**

### 2.2.6 GRP / FRP Composites

**Glass Reinforced Plastic (GRP):**
- Made of **glass fiber + polyester/vinylester/epoxy resin**
- Density ≈ 1,500–1,800 kg/m³ (about 1/4 the weight of steel)
- Used in: **small craft (<50 m), minesweepers, patrol boats, yachts**
- **Magnetic signature is minimal** → critical for **MCM (Mine Counter-Measure) vessels** to avoid triggering magnetic mines
- Can be built by **single-shot moulding, filament winding, or hand lay-up**

### 2.2.7 Wood (Traditional Marine Use)

- **Teak** (Tectona grandis) — gold standard, naturally oily, highly weather-resistant
- **Iroko** (African teak substitute) — cheaper
- **Marine plywood** — used for decking, bulkheads, furniture
- **Caulking with cotton + oakum** historically sealed plank seams
- Still used for **traditional dhows, fishing boats, classic yachts**

### 2.2.8 Concrete and Ferrocement

- **Concrete ships** were built in WWI, WWII (e.g., the SS *Faith*) and the oil crisis of 1973
- **Ferrocement** (cement mortar + wire mesh + steel bars) used for **barges, pontoons, fishing boats**
- Advantages: cheap, no corrosion, fireproof
- Disadvantages: heavy, brittle, hard to repair at sea

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV)** | $CEV = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | % | Weldability check; must be ≤ 0.45% |
| 2 | **Specific Strength** | $\sigma_{sp} = \frac{R_e}{\rho}$ | N·m/kg | Material selection for light structures |
| 3 | **Specific Stiffness** | $E_{sp} = \frac{E}{\rho}$ | N·m/kg | Resistance to elastic deflection |
| 4 | **Plate thickness reduction (HTS vs MS)** | $t_{HTS} = t_{MS} \cdot \frac{R_{e,MS}}{R_{e,HTS}}$ | mm | Weight savings calculation |
| 5 | **Corrosion rate (linear)** | $CR = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}$ | mm/year | Service life estimation |
| 6 | **Safety factor on yield** | $SF = \frac{R_e}{\sigma_{applied}}$ | – | Should be ≥ 1.5 for marine structures |
| 7 | **Bending stress in plate** | $\sigma = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{6M}{b \cdot t^2}$ | N/mm² | Hull girder stress check |
| 8 | **Hull girder section modulus** | $Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$ | cm³ | Required for bending moment |
| 9 | **Young's Modulus of Steel** | $E_s = 2.1 \times 10^5$ | N/mm² | Deflection, buckling calculations |
| 10 | **Young's Modulus of Aluminum** | $E_{Al} = 0.7 \times 10^5$ | N/mm² | Aluminum structure deflection |
| 11 | **Weight saving percentage** | $\%W = \left(1 - \frac{t_{HTS}}{t_{MS}}\right) \times 100$ | % | Compare material efficiency |
| 12 | **Galvanic series potential difference** | $\Delta E = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | V | Predict galvanic corrosion risk |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval architects** use the **specific strength** concept to select materials for fast vessels — for example, the **Independence-class Littoral Combat Ship** uses an **aluminum hull** to achieve 44+ knots.
- **Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV, IRS)** mandate material selection rules in their **Rules and Regulations** — these are legally binding for commercial ships.
- **Warship designers** must satisfy **NAVSEA standards** (US Navy) or **Defence Standard 02-101 (UK MoD)** for material selection in naval combatants.
- **LNG carrier designers** use **membrane-type stainless steel tanks** (e.g., **GTT Mark III** uses 304L SS corrugated sheets) — this is a direct application of materials selection.
- **Modern hybrid material ships** combine **steel hull + aluminum superstructure** to optimize weight (the famous **"frangible deck"** concept used in cruise ships to prevent progressive flooding).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hull Weight Reduction Using High Tensile Steel

### Problem Statement
A bulk carrier has a side shell plate of thickness **22 mm** manufactured from Grade A mild steel. The naval architect proposes replacing it with **Grade AH36** high tensile steel without compromising structural safety. Calculate the new plate thickness and the percentage weight reduction.

### Given Data
- Original thickness: $t_{MS} = 22 \text{ mm}$
- Yield strength of Grade A: $R_{e,MS} = 235 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Yield strength of Grade AH36: $R_{e,HTS} = 355 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Density of steel (both grades): $\rho = 7,850 \text{ kg/m}^3$

### Step 1: State the Design Equivalence Principle
The plate must carry the **same design load** with the **same factor of safety** against yielding. Therefore, the **allowable stress is identical**, and we can equate the bending resistance:

$$\sigma_{allow,MS} = \sigma_{allow,HTS}$$

### Step 2: Equate the Bending Stress in the Plate
For a simply supported plate under lateral pressure, the bending stress in a plate of width $b$ and thickness $t$ is:

$$\sigma = \frac{6M}{b \cdot t^2}$$

For equal design moment $M$ and equal unit width $b$:

$$\frac{6M}{b \cdot t_{MS}^2} = \frac{6M}{b \cdot t_{HTS}^2}$$

This simplifies to:

$$t_{HTS}^2 = t_{MS}^2 \cdot \frac{R_{e,MS}}{R_{e,HTS}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Yield Strengths

$$t_{HTS}^2 = (22)^2 \cdot \frac{235}{355}$$

$$t_{HTS}^2 = 484 \times 0.6620 = 320.4 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 4: Solve for $t_{HTS}$

$$t_{HTS} = \sqrt{320.4} = 17.90 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5: Round Up to Standard Plate Thickness
Per IACS rules, plate thickness is rounded to the next higher standard size. The next standard thickness above 17.90 mm is **18 mm**.

$$\boxed{t_{HTS} = 18 \text{ mm}}$$

### Step 6: Calculate the Weight Reduction

$$\%W_{saved} = \left(1 - \frac{t_{HTS}}{t_{MS}}\right) \times 100$$

$$\%W_{saved} = \left(1 - \frac{17.90}{22}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.8136) \times 100 = 18.64\%$$

### Step 7: Real-World Mass Saving for the Full Hull
For a 50,000 DWT bulk carrier with **hull steel weight of approximately 8,000 tonnes**:

$$\Delta W = 8,000 \times 0.1864 = 1,491 \text{ tonnes}$$

This translates to **~1,500 additional tonnes of cargo payload** — directly improving the ship's economic performance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Galvanic Corrosion Rate Estimation

### Problem Statement
A steel hull plate (cathode) is fastened with **aluminum rivets** in a seawater environment. Estimate the **galvanic corrosion current density** and the **time to failure** of the rivets.

### Given Data
- Area of aluminum rivet: $A_{Al} = 5 \text{ cm}^2 = 500 \text{ mm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
- Galvanic potential difference: $\Delta E = E_{steel} - E_{Al} = (-0.61) - (-1.05) = 0.44 \text{ V}$
- Polarization resistance of aluminum in seawater: $R_p = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Ω·m}^2$
- Density of aluminum: $\rho_{Al} = 2,700 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Faraday's constant: $F = 96,485 \text{ C/mol}$
- Molar mass of Al: $M = 26.98 \text{ g/mol}$
- Aluminum dissolves as $Al^{3+}$ → **n = 3 electrons**

### Step 1: Galvanic Current Density from Ohm's Law

$$i = \frac{\Delta E}{R_p} = \frac{0.44}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 110 \text{ A/m}^2$$

### Step 2: Mass Loss Rate (Faraday's Law)

$$\dot{m} = \frac{i \cdot A \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{110 \times (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 26.98}{3 \times 96,485}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{1.4839}{289,455} = 5.127 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g/s}$$

### Step 3: Convert to Penetration Rate (Corrosion Rate in mm/year)

$$CR = \frac{i \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_{Al}} \times (3600 \times 24 \times 365)$$

$$CR = \frac{110 \times 26.98}{3 \times 96,485 \times 2,700} \times 3.1536 \times 10^7$$

$$CR = \frac{2,967.8}{7.815 \times 10^8} \times 3.1536 \times 10^7$$

$$CR = 3.798 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.1536 \times 10^7 = 119.7 \text{ mm/year}$$

### Step 4: Time to Failure of an 8 mm Diameter Rivet

$$t_{fail} = \frac{8 \text{ mm}}{119.7 \text{ mm/year}} = 0.0668 \text{ year} = 24.4 \text{ days}$$

### Engineering Conclusion
> [!WARNING] **Critical Design Lesson**
> The aluminum rivet will fail in **less than 1 month** in seawater contact with steel! This is why **insulating gaskets** (PVC, neoprene) MUST be used between dissimilar metals in marine applications.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection for Different Ship Types

| Ship Type | Hull Material | Superstructure Material | Propeller Material | Piping Material | Why? |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------|--------------------|-----------------|------|
| **VLCC / Oil Tanker** | Mild steel Grade A (220,000 DWT, double hull) | Mild steel | Manganese bronze | Carbon steel + Cu-Ni 90/10 | Cost, cargo capacity, oil-corrosion resistance |
| **Bulk Carrier** | Grade A + AH36 (strength deck) | Mild steel | Manganese bronze | Carbon steel | Optimization of cost & weight |
| **Container Ship (ULCV)** | AH36, DH36, EH36 (high strength throughout) | Mild steel | Manganese bronze | Cu-Ni 90/10 | Weight savings for higher TEU capacity |
| **Naval Frigate** | DH36 / EH36 (HY-80 equivalent for some parts) | Aluminum 5083 | Nickel-aluminum bronze | Cu-Ni 70/30 | Shock resistance, signature reduction |
| **Fast Ferry** | Aluminum 5083-H116 | Aluminum 5083 | Nickel-aluminum bronze | Cu-Ni 90/10 | Lightweight, high speed (40+ knots) |
| **Minesweeper (MCM)** | GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic) | GRP / Aluminum | Bronze (non-magnetic preferred) | Cu-Ni 90/10 | Low magnetic signature |
| **LNG Carrier** | Mild steel + Invar/9%Ni steel for membrane | Mild steel | Manganese bronze | Stainless steel 304L | Cryogenic cargo containment |
| **Submarine Pressure Hull** | HY-80 / HY-100 steel (or titanium for deep diving) | Steel | Bronze | Cu-Ni | High yield strength to resist collapse at depth |
| **Yacht / Small Craft** | GRP / Aluminum 5083 / Wood | GRP / Wood | Bronze | Cu-Ni 90/10 | Aesthetics, weight, corrosion |
| **Patrol Boat** | Aluminum 5083 or 5456 | Aluminum | NAB | Cu-Ni | Speed, weight, durability |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Comparing Specific Strengths

### Question
Compare the **specific strength** of three candidate hull materials and determine which is most efficient for a 50 m fast patrol boat.

| Material | Yield Strength (Re) | Density (ρ) |
|----------|--------------------|------------|
| Mild Steel (Grade A) | 235 N/mm² | 7,850 kg/m³ |
| Aluminum 5083-H116 | 215 N/mm² | 2,700 kg/m³ |
| GRP (typical) | 200 N/mm² | 1,600 kg/m³ |

### Calculation

$$\sigma_{sp,MS} = \frac{235 \times 10^6}{7,850} = 29,936 \text{ N·m/kg} = 29.94 \text{ kN·m/kg}$$

$$\sigma_{sp,Al} = \frac{215 \times 10^6}{2,700} = 79,630 \text{ N·m/kg} = 79.63 \text{ kN·m/kg}$$

$$\sigma_{sp,GRP} = \frac{200 \times 10^6}{1,600} = 125,000 \text{ N·m/kg} = 125.0 \text{ kN·m/kg}$$

### Ranking
$$\boxed{\text{GRP} > \text{Aluminum 5083} > \text{Mild Steel}}$$

### Real-World Selection Logic
- **GRP wins on specific strength** but loses on **stiffness (E)**, **fire resistance**, and **cost**.
- For a **50 m fast patrol boat** with speed > 35 knots, **aluminum 5083** is typically selected (the right balance of weight, stiffness, and cost).
- GRP is used for **smaller craft (<30 m)** where its lower stiffness is manageable.
