# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define a "bulbous bow". Explain its working principle with reference to wave interference.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **bulbous bow** is a streamlined, forward-protruding bulge located at or just below the design waterline of a ship's bow, immediately aft of the stem. *(1 mark)*

**Working Principle:** When the ship moves forward, both the main hull and the bulb generate **bow waves**. By carefully sizing the bulb, its bow wave is generated **180° out of phase** (opposite crest to trough) with the hull's bow wave. The two waves **interfere destructively**, partially cancelling each other and thereby reducing **wave-making resistance**. *(2 marks)*

> **Key Condition:** The bulb is **only effective at or near the design Froude number**. At other speeds, the benefit is lost or may even become a penalty. Typical resistance reduction is **5%–15%**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State the function of the collision bulkhead and its location in a ship.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Function:** The **collision bulkhead** is a transverse, watertight bulkhead that prevents flooding of the entire ship in the event of a **forward collision damage**. *(1 mark)*

**Location:** It is placed at a distance of **0.05L to 0.10L** from the forward perpendicular (FP). *(1 mark)*

**Key Requirements:**
- Watertight up to the **freeboard deck**.
- **No openings** (doors, manholes, ventilation ducts) below the freeboard deck.
- Stepped/recessed by **not more than 25% of the rule length**. *(1 mark)*

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **main structural members at the aft end** of a merchant ship. Explain the functions of the stern frame, propeller post, and rudder post. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** List and briefly explain the **various types of main propulsion machinery** used in modern ships. Compare them based on efficiency, RPM, and typical application. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**Structural Members at the Aft End:**

1. **Stern Frame** *(1 mark)*: A heavy **cast or welded steel forging** forming the structural termination of the hull at the stern. It comprises:
   - **Propeller Post** (lower vertical member) — supports the lower end of the propeller shaft via the **A-bracket or boss**.
   - **Rudder Post** (upper vertical member) — supports the rudder hinges.
   - **Sole Piece** — horizontal member connecting propeller post to the keel.

2. **Transom** *(0.5 marks)*: The flat or curved aft shell plating forming the rear face.

3. **After Peak Bulkhead** *(0.5 marks)*: Transverse watertight bulkhead forming the forward boundary of the after peak tank.

4. **Stern Tube** *(0.5 marks)*: A watertight cylindrical housing carrying the tail shaft, supported by **white-metal bearings** with **sealing glands** to prevent sea water ingress.

5. **A-Bracket** *(0.5 marks)*: Modern substitute for stern frame on single-screw ships — a streamlined strut supporting the propeller shaft from above.

6. **Rudder Assembly** *(0.5 marks)*: Includes rudder stock, blade, tiller, and carrier bearing.

7. **Propeller** *(0.5 marks)*: 4 or 5 blades of high-strength manganese bronze or nickel-aluminum bronze.

**Functions:**
- **Stern Frame:** Resists **propeller-induced vibration**, supports the **propeller shaft weight**, and transmits **thrust reaction** to the hull girder.
- **Propeller Post:** Houses the **lower stern tube bearing** and supports the lower rudder hinge.
- **Rudder Post:** Provides the **pivot axis** for the rudder and supports the steering gear loads. *(2 marks for functional explanation)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Main Propulsion Machinery Types:** *(4 marks for listing + brief explanation)*

| Type | RPM | η_thermal | SFC (g/kWh) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow-speed 2-stroke Diesel** | 60–120 | ~50% | 165 | Tankers, bulk carriers |
| **Medium-speed 4-stroke Diesel** | 300–1000 | ~45% | 190 | Cargo, multi-purpose |
| **Steam Turbine** | 3000–8000 | ~30% | 280 | LNG, naval carriers |
| **Gas Turbine** | 15000+ | ~35% | 320 | Naval frigates, fast ferries |
| **Nuclear Steam** | 3000+ | ~28% | n/a | Submarines, CVN |

**Brief Explanations:** *(3 marks)*

1. **2-Stroke Diesel:** Directly coupled to propeller (no gearbox). Best fuel economy. Most common in merchant fleet (~85%).
2. **4-Stroke Diesel:** Requires reduction gearbox. Used in smaller vessels, gensets.
3. **Steam Turbine:** High power, low vibration. Steam from oil-fired or nuclear boilers. High maintenance.
4. **Gas Turbine:** Lightest weight, highest power density. Used in warships requiring speed.
5. **Nuclear:** Pressurized water reactor generates steam. Used where range is critical.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **various components of the fore end structure** of a ship with a suitable sketch. Mention the role of the forecastle, hawse pipes, and stem. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **auxiliary machinery systems** found in a ship's engine room. Explain the role of purifiers, pumps, and air compressors. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Components of Fore End Structure:**

1. **Stem** *(1 mark)*: Forged steel section forming the forward tip of the hull, joining port and starboard shell plates. Raked forward at **15°–30°**.

2. **Bulbous Bow** *(0.5 marks)*: Forward protrusion below waterline for wave interference and resistance reduction.

3. **Collision Bulkhead** *(0.5 marks)*: Watertight transverse bulkhead at **0.05L–0.10L from FP**; prevents progressive flooding after bow collision.

4. **Fore Peak Tank** *(0.5 marks)*: Compartment forward of collision bulkhead; used for trimming and as storage for fresh water or chain wash.

5. **Hawse Pipes** *(0.5 marks)*: Heavy steel tubes through which the anchor chain passes from the shell into the chain locker.

6. **Windlass** *(0.5 marks)*: Self-sustaining mechanism for heaving up the anchor and chain.

7. **Chain Locker** *(0.5 marks)*: Stores anchor chain; fitted with **chain stripper** and **bitter end** release.

8. **Forecastle Deck** *(0.5 marks)*: Raised deck forward; shelters mooring gear and bow crew in heavy weather.

**Roles:**
- **Forecastle:** Provides **sheltered working area**, houses windlass and chain lockers.
- **Hawse Pipes:** Allow **smooth chain passage** from external anchor to internal locker; lined with **chocks** to prevent chain abrasion.
- **Stem:** Provides **structural continuity**, **wave-piercing geometry**, and **anchorage for shell plates**. *(1.5 marks for role explanations)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Auxiliary Machinery Systems:**

1. **Steam Generation System** *(1 mark)*
   - **Auxiliary Boilers:** Oil-fired water-tube boilers producing steam at 7–10 bar.
   - **Exhaust Gas Boilers (EGB):** Waste-heat recovery from main engine exhaust.
   - Uses: fuel oil heating, accommodation, galley, laundry.

2. **Pumping Systems** *(1 mark)*
   - **Bilge Pumps:** Remove water from bilge wells.
   - **Ballast Pumps:** Fill/empty ballast tanks.
   - **Fire Pumps:** High-pressure seawater for firefighting.
   - **Cooling Pumps:** Circulate seawater for heat exchangers.
   - **Lube Oil Pumps:** Circulate lube oil.

3. **Air Systems** *(1 mark)*
   - **Starting Air Compressors:** Charge starting air bottles at 30 bar.
   - **Service Air Compressors:** General pneumatic tools at 7 bar.
   - **Control Air:** Pneumatic actuators for valves.

4. **Purifiers / Separators** *(1 mark)*
   - Use **centrifugal force** to remove water and solids from fuel/lube oil.
   - Alfa Laval / Westfalia disc-stack types.
   - Two-stage separation: purifier → clarifier.

5. **Electrical Generation** *(1 mark)*
   - Diesel generators (440V, 60Hz, 3-phase AC).
   - Emergency generator (above freeboard deck).
   - Main switchboard distribution.

6. **Heat Exchangers** *(1 mark)*
   - Lube oil cooler, jacket cooler, charge air cooler, freshwater generator.

7. **Distilling Plant / FW Generator** *(1 mark)*
   - Produces fresh water from seawater using waste heat.

> **Specific Roles:**
> - **Purifiers** ensure **clean fuel delivery** to main engine, preventing injector damage. They remove **sludge, water, and asphaltenes**.
> - **Pumps** are the **circulatory system** of the ship — failure of bilge pump can lead to flooding casualty.
> - **Air Compressors** provide **stored kinetic energy** for main engine starting; without starting air, the main engine cannot be started. *(1 mark for specific role emphasis)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "stern frame" with "transom."** The stern frame is the *cast structure carrying the propeller shaft*, while the transom is *the aft shell plate panel*.
> 2. **Do NOT write "bulbous bow reduces skin friction."** It reduces **wave-making resistance**, not skin friction. (Common error: loses 2 marks.)
> 3. **Do NOT state that steam turbines are "more efficient" than diesels.** Modern 2-stroke diesels are **far more fuel-efficient**.
> 4. **Always quote numerical values** (e.g., 30 bar starting air, 7 bar boiler pressure, 0.05L collision bulkhead position) — KTU awards marks for **standard numeric recall**.
> 5. **In sketches**, label all components — the **sketch carries 2–3 marks** even in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 6. **For auxiliary machinery**, mention at least **ONE specific brand/type** (e.g., Alfa Laval purifier) to demonstrate depth.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Fore and Aft Structures**

- ☐ **Stem** = forward tip forged steel; raked 15°–30° in modern ships.
- ☐ **Bulbous Bow** reduces wave-making resistance via **wave interference** at design $F_n \approx 0.25$ (tankers).
- ☐ **Collision Bulkhead** = at **0.05L to 0.10L from FP**, watertight, no openings below freeboard deck.
- ☐ **Hawse Pipes** = chain passage tubes; lined with chocks.
- ☐ **Chain Locker** = below forecastle; with bitter end release.
- ☐ **Stern Frame** = cast steel, has **propeller post + rudder post + sole piece**.
- ☐ **Modern stern** = open-water with **A-bracket**, no stern frame.
- ☐ **Rudder types**: simple, balanced, semi-balanced, spade, twin.
- ☐ **After Peak Tank** = trim tank + cofferdam.
- ☐ **Shaft Tunnel** = watertight trunk from ER to stern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Main and Auxiliary Machinery**

- ☐ **Main Engine:** 2-stroke slow-speed diesel is the **merchant fleet standard** (MAN B&W / Wärtsilä).
- ☐ **SHP** = EHP / η_S; **BHP** = SHP / η_G; **η_D** = EHP / BHP.
- ☐ **η_D** typical = **0.55–0.65** for single-screw merchant ships.
- ☐ **Steam Turbine** = high RPM, needs gearbox, used in LNG/naval.
- ☐ **Gas Turbine** = very high RPM, light, used in naval combatants.
- ☐ **Auxiliary Boiler** = 7–10 bar, oil-fired, water-tube.
- ☐ **Diesel Generators** = 440V, 60Hz, 3 sets normally.
- ☐ **Emergency Generator** = above freeboard deck, starts within 45 seconds.
- ☐ **Starting Air** = 30 bar; **Control/Service Air** = 7 bar.
- ☐ **Purifier** = centrifugal separator for fuel/lube oil (Alfa Laval).
- ☐ **Critical Pumps**: bilge, ballast, fire, cooling, lube, fuel transfer, boiler feed.
- ☐ **SOLAS redundancy**: essential systems have **N+1 redundancy**.
- ☐ **Engine room** is located **aft** in ~90% of merchant ships.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Fore and Aft Structures; Main and Auxiliary Machinery**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned | Outcome-Based Education (OBE)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Propulsion Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Propulsion Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Marine Propulsion Machinery** refers to the complete mechanical, thermal, and electrical system installed on a ship that converts the chemical energy of fuel (or nuclear/electrical energy) into useful mechanical work at the propeller shaft, ultimately producing the **thrust** required to overcome hull resistance and propel the vessel through water at a designed service speed.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** nomenclature for Naval Architecture and Shipping Engineering, propulsion machinery is classified on three orthogonal axes:

1. **By Prime Mover (Energy Conversion):** Steam Turbines, Diesel Engines, Gas Turbines, Nuclear Reactors, Electric Motors.
2. **By Transmission Type:** Direct Drive, Geared Drive, Diesel-Electric, Gas Turbine-Electric, CODAG, CODOG, COGAG, COGAS, COSAG.
3. **By Application Domain:** Main Propulsion (thrusters), Auxiliary Propulsion, Emergency Propulsion.

The complete propulsion plant consists of the **prime mover**, **transmission system (reduction gearing, shafting, couplings)**, **propeller**, and the **supporting auxiliaries** (lubrication, cooling, fuel, exhaust).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology:** The term **"Propulsion Plant"** is non-negotiable in exams. It includes the prime mover + transmission + propeller as ONE integrated system. Do NOT treat the engine and propeller as isolated components.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a ship's propulsion machinery exactly like the **powertrain of a car**, but scaled to a colossal, oceangoing level:

- The **Prime Mover** (Diesel/Turbine) is the **engine** — the heart that pumps energy.
- The **Reduction Gearbox** is the **transmission** — it steps down high RPM to a propeller-friendly speed (typically **80–120 RPM** for large ships).
- The **Shaft + Propeller** is the **driveshaft and wheels** — the part that finally pushes the vehicle forward.
- The **Fuel System, Lubrication, Cooling** are the **blood, oil, and radiator** — keeping the heart alive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Anchor:** A ship's propeller is most efficient at low RPM (about **100 RPM**), but engines spin at **3,000–20,000 RPM**. Therefore, **gearing is essential**. This is the single most important reason transmission systems exist in marine engineering.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Marine Metrics

The following **standard naval architecture constants** must be memorized for the KTU exam:

- **1 Metric Horsepower (HP) = 75 kgf·m/s = 0.7355 kW**
- **1 Shaft Horsepower (SHP) = 1 HP delivered at the propeller shaft**
- **1 Brake Horsepower (BHP) = 1 HP measured at the engine coupling** (before transmission losses)
- **Service Speed** for merchant ships: **15–25 knots**
- **Power-to-Weight Ratio** target: **0.05–0.15 kW/tonne** displacement
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC):** measured in **g/kWh**
- **Propulsive Coefficient (ηD)** typical range: **0.55–0.70**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Marine Propulsion Machinery

Marine propulsion systems are categorized systematically as follows:

### 2.1.1 Classification by Prime Mover Type

**(A) Steam Turbine Propulsion**
- Uses high-pressure superheated steam expanding through turbine stages.
- Typical operating pressure: **60–140 bar**; temperature: **480–565 °C**.
- RPM range: **3,000–6,000 RPM** (high speed, requires massive reduction gearing).
- Applications: Aircraft carriers, large LNG carriers, nuclear ships.
- Advantages: Smooth operation, high reliability, ability to burn low-grade fuel oil (HFO).
- Disadvantages: Low thermal efficiency (~30%), huge plant volume, slow response.

**(B) Diesel Engine Propulsion (Most Common in Merchant Fleet)**
- Two-stroke or four-stroke, slow-speed or medium-speed.
- Slow-speed 2-stroke diesels: **60–120 RPM** — can drive propeller **directly (direct drive)**.
- Medium-speed 4-stroke: **350–1,000 RPM** — requires reduction gearing.
- Thermal efficiency: **45–55%** (highest among all thermal prime movers).
- Applications: Bulk carriers, tankers, container ships, naval auxiliaries.
- Specific Fuel Consumption: **160–195 g/kWh** for modern 2-stroke marine diesels.
- Advantages: Highest thermal efficiency, lowest fuel cost, mature technology.
- Disadvantages: Heavy weight, large physical dimensions, requires quality fuel.

**(C) Gas Turbine Propulsion**
- Open cycle (Brayton cycle) — compressor → combustion chamber → turbine.
- RPM range: **8,000–20,000 RPM**.
- Power-to-weight ratio: **up to 5 kW/kg** (best among thermal systems).
- Applications: Naval frigates, destroyers, high-speed vessels, hydrofoils.
- Advantages: Lightweight, compact, rapid start, high power density.
- Disadvantages: High SFC (~300 g/kWh), high exhaust temperatures, poor part-load efficiency.

**(D) Nuclear Propulsion**
- Uses a pressurized water reactor (PWR) heating steam that drives turbines.
- Fuel: Enriched Uranium-235 (¹⁵⁵U); refueling cycle: **15–25 years**.
- Power output: Up to **550 MW thermal** per reactor.
- Applications: Aircraft carriers, submarines, icebreakers.
- Advantages: Unlimited range, no air dependency, high sustained power.
- Disadvantages: Extreme capital cost, radiation shielding complexity, political/regulatory hurdles.

**(E) Electric (Motor-Driven) Propulsion**
- Prime mover generates electricity → electric motor drives propeller.
- Sub-types: **Diesel-Electric, Turbo-Electric, Nuclear-Electric**.
- Motor types: AC induction, synchronous, permanent magnet.
- Applications: Cruise ships, submarines, dynamic positioning vessels, icebreakers.
- Advantages: Flexible layout, low vibration, high redundancy, silent running.
- Disadvantages: Conversion losses (~5–8%), high capital cost of motors and switchboards.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Combined/COGAG Systems (Hybrid)

These are **Combined (or Combi) Marine Propulsion** systems used in modern naval combatants:

| Acronym | Full Form | Configuration |
|---|---|---|
| **CODAG** | Combined Diesel and Gas Turbine | Diesel (cruise) + Gas Turbine (boost) on same shaft |
| **CODOG** | Combined Diesel or Gas Turbine | Either Diesel OR Gas Turbine engaged at a time |
| **COGAG** | Combined Gas Turbine and Gas Turbine | Two gas turbines for cruise + boost |
| **COGAS** | Combined Gas Turbine and Steam | Gas turbine (topping) + steam turbine (bottoming) |
| **COSAG** | Combined Steam and Gas Turbine | Steam turbine (cruise) + Gas turbine (boost) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Topic:** Expect direct questions asking you to expand these acronyms and explain which is used in **Indian Navy frigates** (typically **CODAG** or **COGAG**) and **submarines** (typically **Diesel-Electric** for conventional, **Nuclear** for nuclear-powered).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Table 2.1: Power, Thrust, and Efficiency Equations

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brake Power (BP) | $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | kW | Power developed at engine coupling |
| 2 | Shaft Power (SP) | $SP = BP \times \eta_{transmission}$ | kW | Power at propeller shaft |
| 3 | Thrust Power (TP) | $TP = \dfrac{T \cdot V_{S}}{75}$ | Metric HP | Power converted to thrust |
| 4 | Hull Efficiency ($\eta_H$) | $\eta_H = \dfrac{T \cdot V_{S}}{2\pi \rho n^{3} D^{5} K_{T}^{3}}$ | — | Fraction of TP reaching hull |
| 5 | Propulsive Efficiency ($\eta_D$) | $\eta_D = \eta_H \times \eta_O \times \eta_R$ | — | Overall propulsive coefficient |
| 6 | Quasi-Propulsive Coeff. ($\eta_P$) | $\eta_P = \eta_D \times \eta_{mech}$ | — | Includes mechanical losses |
| 7 | Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) | $SFC = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{P \cdot t}$ | g/kWh | Mass of fuel per unit energy |
| 8 | Power-to-Weight Ratio | $P/W = \dfrac{Power}{Mass}$ | kW/tonne | Compactness metric |
| 9 | Heat Rate | $HR = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{thermal}}$ | kJ/kWh | Energy input per unit work |
| 10 | Fuel Mass for Voyage | $m_{fuel} = SFC \times P \times t_{voyage}$ | tonnes | Total fuel required |

### Table 2.2: Comparative Characteristics of Prime Movers

| Property | Slow Diesel | Steam Turbine | Gas Turbine | Nuclear |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RPM at Output** | 60–120 | 3,000–6,000 | 8,000–20,000 | 3,000–6,000 |
| **Thermal Eff. (%)** | 45–55 | 28–35 | 30–38 | 30–35 |
| **SFC (g/kWh)** | 160–195 | 250–320 | 290–360 | N/A (fuel mass negligible) |
| **P/W Ratio (kW/tonne)** | 30–50 | 50–80 | 200–500 | 100–150 |
| **Starting Time** | Slow (5–10 min) | Very Slow (1–4 hr) | Very Fast (1–3 min) | Hours-Days |
| **Maintenance** | Medium | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Typical Ship Use** | Tankers, Bulk Carriers | Large LNG, Carriers | Frigates, Fast Craft | Carriers, Submarines |
| **Drive Type** | Direct | Geared | Geared | Geared |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exams:** Slow-speed 2-stroke diesels are the **dominant** prime mover in the world merchant fleet because they can directly connect to the propeller shaft, **eliminating the reduction gearbox** entirely, thereby saving weight, cost, and maintenance overhead.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of propulsion machinery is not arbitrary — it is governed by:

- **Mission Profile:** A bulk carrier (constant speed, long voyages) favors **diesel**. A naval frigate (silent cruise + high-speed dash) favors **CODAG/COGAG**.
- **Endurance:** Submarines and aircraft carriers favor **nuclear** for unlimited range.
- **Acoustic Signature:** Submarines favor **diesel-electric** for silent running on batteries.
- **Cargo Sensitivity:** LNG carriers favor **steam turbines** because the cargo boil-off gas (BOG) can be used as fuel.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** IMO 2020 sulphur cap favors LNG, dual-fuel, and scrubber-fitted systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Propulsive Efficiency and the Power Flow Chain

The KTU board expects students to demonstrate the **complete energy cascade** from chemical energy in fuel to useful thrust at the propeller disc. Below is the exhaustive derivation:

**Step 1: Define the calorific value of fuel.**

The energy released per unit mass of fuel is the **Lower Calorific Value (LCV)**, denoted $LCV$ in kJ/kg.

**Step 2: Heat input rate to the engine.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = m_{fuel} \times LCV \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

where $m_{fuel}$ is the fuel mass flow rate in kg/s.

**Step 3: Brake Power output (engine shaft power).**

Thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$ of the prime mover relates heat input to mechanical output:

$$
BP = \eta_{th} \times \dot{Q}_{in} = \eta_{th} \times m_{fuel} \times LCV
$$

**Step 4: Account for transmission losses.**

Power transmitted from engine to propeller shaft involves gearbox and shaft losses:

$$
SP = BP \times \eta_{G} \times \eta_{S}
$$

where $\eta_{G}$ is the gearbox efficiency (typically **0.96–0.98**) and $\eta_{S}$ is the shaft efficiency (typically **0.98–0.99**).

**Step 5: Propeller converts shaft power to thrust power.**

The propeller absorbs shaft power and produces thrust $T$ at ship speed $V_S$:

$$
TP = T \cdot V_{S}
$$

**Step 6: Account for hull-propeller interaction efficiency.**

The **Hull Efficiency** $\eta_H$ accounts for the difference between the wake fraction $w$ and the thrust deduction factor $t$:

$$
\eta_H = \dfrac{1 - t}{1 - w}
$$

**Step 7: Account for rotational losses in the propeller.**

The **Rotative Efficiency** $\eta_R$ accounts for the difference between torque absorbed and torque delivered at the propeller:

$$
\eta_R = \dfrac{\text{Thrust Power}}{\text{Torque Power}} = \dfrac{T \cdot V_{A}}{2\pi n Q}
$$

where $V_A = V_S(1-w)$ is the speed of advance and $Q$ is the torque.

**Step 8: Open-water propeller efficiency.**

$$
\eta_O = \dfrac{T \cdot V_{A}}{2\pi n Q} = \dfrac{\text{Thrust Power Delivered to Water}}{\text{Shaft Power}}
$$

**Step 9: Final overall propulsive efficiency.**

$$
\eta_D = \eta_H \times \eta_O \times \eta_R
$$

**Step 10: The complete energy flow chain (final consolidation).**

$$
\eta_D = \dfrac{T \cdot V_{S}}{SP} = \dfrac{\text{Useful Thrust Power}}{\text{Shaft Power}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner Tip:** If asked "Derive the propulsive coefficient", you must show all **intermediate efficiencies** ($\eta_H$, $\eta_O$, $\eta_R$) and clearly define them. Marks are lost when students jump directly to $\eta_D$ without justification.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Fuel Consumption of a Tanker

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) has a service speed of $V_S = 15$ knots and requires Effective Horsepower $EHP = 25,000$ HP. The ship uses a slow-speed 2-stroke diesel with $SFC = 175$ g/kWh and a propulsive efficiency $\eta_D = 0.65$. Calculate:

1. The required Shaft Horsepower (SHP).
2. The daily fuel consumption in tonnes.
3. The fuel required for a 30-day voyage.

**Step 1: Calculate Shaft Horsepower (SHP).**

The Effective Horsepower (EHP) equals the Thrust Power (TP). The propulsive efficiency is:

$$
\eta_D = \dfrac{EHP}{SHP}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
SHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \dfrac{25,000}{0.65}
$$

$$
SHP = 38,461.5 \text{ HP}
$$

**Step 2: Convert SHP to kW.**

$$
SHP_{kW} = 38,461.5 \times 0.7355
$$

$$
SHP_{kW} = 28,288 \text{ kW} \approx 28.29 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate daily fuel mass flow.**

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = SFC \times SHP_{kW} = 175 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/kWh} \times 28,288 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = 4,950.4 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to daily consumption.**

$$
m_{daily} = 4,950.4 \times 24 = 118,809.6 \text{ kg/day} \approx 118.81 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 5: Total fuel for 30-day voyage.**

$$
m_{voyage} = 118.81 \times 30 = 3,564.3 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Final Answer Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| SHP (Metric HP) | 38,461.5 HP |
| SHP (kW) | 28,288 kW |
| Hourly Fuel | 4,950.4 kg/h |
| Daily Fuel | 118.81 tonnes/day |
| 30-Day Voyage Fuel | 3,564.3 tonnes |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection Criteria for Prime Mover

| Selection Criterion | Best Choice | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Highest Thermal Efficiency | Slow-Speed Diesel | 45–55% thermal efficiency |
| Lowest Specific Fuel Consumption | Slow-Speed Diesel | 160–195 g/kWh |
| Highest Power-to-Weight Ratio | Gas Turbine | 200–500 kW/tonne |
| Longest Endurance / Range | Nuclear | Years of operation without refueling |
| Fastest Start-Up Time | Gas Turbine | 1–3 minutes from cold |
| Silentest Operation | Diesel-Electric on Batteries | 0 dB radiated noise |
| Lowest Capital Cost | Slow-Speed Diesel | Mature mass-produced technology |
| Best for LNG Carriers | Steam Turbine | Uses cargo boil-off gas as fuel |
| Best for Aircraft Carriers | Nuclear | Unlimited range, no refueling stops |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Marine Propulsion Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FuelSys["FUEL SUPPLY SYSTEM"]
    PrimeMover["PRIME MOVER"]
    RedGear["REDUCTION GEARBOX"]
    Shaft["PROPELLER SHAFT"]
    Propeller["PROPELLER"]
    Thrust["THRUST TO HULL"]

    FuelSys --> PrimeMover
    PrimeMover --> RedGear
    RedGear --> Shaft
    Shaft --> Propeller
    Propeller --> Thrust

    LubSys["LUB OIL SYSTEM"] --> PrimeMover
    CoolSys["COOLING SYSTEM"] --> PrimeMover
    ExhSys["EXHAUST SYSTEM"] --> PrimeMover
    CtrlSys["CONTROL SYSTEM"] --> PrimeMover
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification of Marine Prime Movers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PM["MARINE PRIME MOVERS"]
    PM --> A1["STEAM TURBINE"]
    PM --> A2["DIESEL ENGINE"]
    PM --> A3["GAS TURBINE"]
    PM --> A4["NUCLEAR REACTOR"]
    PM --> A5["ELECTRIC MOTOR"]

    A1 --> A1a["High Pressure Turbine"]
    A1 --> A1b["Low Pressure Turbine"]

    A2 --> A2a["2 Stroke Slow Speed"]
    A2 --> A2b["4 Stroke Medium Speed"]
    A2 --> A2c["4 Stroke High Speed"]

    A3 --> A3a["Open Cycle Brayton"]
    A3 --> A3b["Closed Cycle"]
    A3 --> A3c["CODLAG Combined"]

    A4 --> A4a["PWR Pressurized Water"]
    A4 --> A4b["Boiling Water Reactor"]

    A5 --> A5a["Diesel Electric"]
    A5 --> A5b["Turbo Electric"]
    A5 --> A5c["Nuclear Electric"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: COGAG / CODAG Hybrid System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CRUISE["CRUISE MODE"]
        D1["DIESEL ENGINE 1"] --> CR["CLUTCH COUPLING"]
        D2["DIESEL ENGINE 2"] --> CR
    end

    subgraph BOOST["BOOST MODE"]
        GT1["GAS TURBINE 1"] --> CL["CLUTCH COUPLING"]
        GT2["GAS TURBINE 2"] --> CL
    end

    CR --> RG["REDUCTION GEARBOX"]
    CL --> RG
    RG --> SH["PROPELLER SHAFT"]
    SH --> PR["PROPELLER"]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture: Direct Drive vs Geared Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DD["DIRECT DRIVE SYSTEM"]
        DD1["SLOW SPEED DIESEL"] --> DD2["FLEXIBLE COUPLING"]
        DD2 --> DD3["PROPELLER SHAFT"]
        DD3 --> DD4["PROPELLER"]
    end

    subgraph GD["GEARED DRIVE SYSTEM"]
        GD1["MEDIUM SPEED DIESEL"] --> GD2["REDUCTION GEARBOX"]
        GD2 --> GD3["PROPELLER SHAFT"]
        GD3 --> GD4["PROPELLER"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Students:** The **Direct Drive** is exclusive to slow-speed 2-stroke diesels (RPM matches propeller). All other prime movers (steam, gas, electric, nuclear) **MUST** use the geared path.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between slow-speed and medium-speed marine diesel engines. Give one example of ship where each is used.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Slow-Speed 2-Stroke | Medium-Speed 4-Stroke |
|---|---|---|
| RPM Range | 60–120 RPM | 350–1,000 RPM |
| Drive Type | Direct drive to propeller | Requires reduction gearbox |
| Cylinder Bore | Large (500–980 mm) | Smaller (200–400 mm) |
| Application | Large tankers, bulk carriers | Naval frigates, cruise ships |
| Specific Output | Low (kW per cylinder) | Higher (compact design) |
| Example Ship | VLCC Tanker | INS Kolkata (Frigate) |

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is meant by "Combined Diesel and Gas Turbine (CODAG)" propulsion? Name one warship class using it.

**Model Answer:**

**CODAG** is a hybrid marine propulsion arrangement where a **diesel engine** is used for economical cruising speeds and a **gas turbine** is engaged (alone or simultaneously) for high-speed boost operations. Both drive the same propeller shaft through a common reduction gearbox and clutch assembly.

**Example:** Indian Navy's **INS Kolkata-class stealth guided-missile destroyers** (or comparable Western frigates like the British Type 23 Duke-class).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat block diagram the working principle of a **Steam Turbine Propulsion Plant** used in LNG carriers. Explain why it is preferred over diesel for such vessels.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A naval frigate uses a **CODAG** system. The cruise diesel produces 8,000 kW at 1,000 RPM and the boost gas turbine produces 25,000 kW at 8,000 RPM. The reduction gearbox steps the combined output to propeller speed of 120 RPM. Calculate:
- (i) The total shaft power at propeller.
- (ii) The overall gear reduction ratio.
- (iii) The fuel consumption per hour if SFC = 240 g/kWh.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle of Steam Turbine Propulsion:**

A steam turbine plant in a marine vessel works as follows:
1. **Fuel oil** is burned in an **oil-fired boiler** (or boiler fed by cargo boil-off gas in LNG carriers).
2. **High-pressure superheated steam** (60–140 bar, 480–565 °C) is generated.
3. Steam expands through the **High-Pressure (HP) turbine**, then re-heated and passed to the **Low-Pressure (LP) turbine**.
4. Both turbines are coupled to a **reduction gearbox** that steps down the 3,000–6,000 RPM to propeller speed of 80–120 RPM.
5. Condenser, feed pumps, and de-aerator complete the Rankine cycle.

**Why preferred for LNG carriers:**
- The cargo (liquefied natural gas) boils off continuously. This **boil-off gas (BOG)** is fed directly into the boilers as fuel, eliminating the need to re-liquefy it.
- Steam plants tolerate **low-grade fuel** better than diesel.
- High reliability and long mean time between overhauls (MTBO > 30,000 hours).

**(b) CODAG Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total Shaft Power at Propeller:**

$$
P_{total} = P_{diesel} + P_{gas\,turbine} = 8,000 + 25,000 = 33,000 \text{ kW}
$$

Assuming gearbox efficiency $\eta_G = 0.97$:

$$
P_{shaft} = 33,000 \times 0.97 = 32,010 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Overall Gear Reduction Ratio:**

$$
R = \dfrac{N_{input}}{N_{output}} = \dfrac{8,000}{120} = 66.67 \text{ : 1}
$$

(Reduction ratio is calculated using the highest input RPM, i.e., the gas turbine.)

**(iii) Hourly Fuel Consumption:**

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = SFC \times P_{shaft} = 240 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/kWh} \times 32,010 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = 7,682.4 \text{ kg/h} \approx 7.68 \text{ tonnes/hour}
$$

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the constructional and operational differences between **Diesel-Electric** and **Nuclear-Electric** propulsion systems. State two advantages of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bulk carrier requires an **Effective Power (EHP)** of 12,000 HP at a service speed of 14 knots. The overall propulsive efficiency is 0.62. The main engine is a slow-speed 2-stroke diesel with an SFC of 185 g/kWh. Calculate:
- (i) The Brake Power (BHP) required.
- (ii) The fuel consumed in a 25-day voyage in tonnes.
- (iii) The propulsive coefficient $\eta_D$ in percent.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Diesel-Electric vs Nuclear-Electric Propulsion:**

| Aspect | Diesel-Electric | Nuclear-Electric |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Marine diesel oil (MDO/HFO) | Enriched Uranium-235 in PWR |
| Generator Output | 5–25 MW per set | 100–550 MW thermal |
| Plant Volume | Compact | Extremely large (shielding) |
| Application | Cruise ships, submarines | Aircraft carriers, submarines |
| Refueling Interval | Days to weeks | 15–25 years |
| Acoustic Signature | Low (battery mode) | Moderate (reactor coolant pumps) |
| Capital Cost | Low | Very high |
| Crew Radiation Training | Not required | Mandatory |

**Advantages of Diesel-Electric:**
1. Flexible machinery layout (no rigid shaft line through the ship).
2. Quiet operation; multiple generator sets can be shut down for silent running.

**Advantages of Nuclear-Electric:**
1. Virtually unlimited range and endurance.
2. Independent of atmospheric oxygen — can operate submerged indefinitely.

**(b) Bulk Carrier Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Brake Power (BHP):**

$$
BHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \dfrac{12,000}{0.62} = 19,354.8 \text{ HP}
$$

In kW:

$$
BHP_{kW} = 19,354.8 \times 0.7355 = 14,235 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Fuel for 25-Day Voyage:**

Daily fuel consumption:

$$
m_{day} = 185 \times 10^{-3} \times 14,235 \times 24
$$

$$
m_{day} = 185 \times 0.001 \times 341,640 = 63,203.4 \text{ kg/day} \approx 63.2 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

Total for 25 days:

$$
m_{voyage} = 63.2 \times 25 = 1,580.0 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**(iii) Propulsive Coefficient in percent:**

$$
\eta_D (\%) = 0.62 \times 100 = 62\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Students often forget to convert HP to kW before applying SFC. SFC is **always** in g/kWh. This error alone costs 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Acronym Confusion:** Mixing up CODAG with CODOG, or COSAG with COGAS. Memorize the **G**=Gas, **S**=Steam, **D**=Diesel pattern carefully.
> 3. **Direct vs Geared Drive:** A common misconception is that **all** diesels are direct-drive. Only **slow-speed 2-stroke** diesels can be direct-driven.
> 4. **Forgetting Gearbox Efficiency:** In numericals, assuming $\eta_G = 1.0$ is a common trap. Always assume **0.97** unless stated otherwise.
> 5. **Slow-Speed vs Medium-Speed RPM:** Slow-speed is **below 300 RPM**; medium-speed is **300–1,000 RPM**; high-speed is **above 1,000 RPM**. Many students confuse these.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Marine Propulsion Plant** = Prime Mover + Transmission + Propeller (integrated system).
- **Slow-Speed 2-Stroke Diesel** is the **workhorse** of the merchant fleet due to direct-drive capability and highest thermal efficiency (**45–55%**).
- **Gas Turbines** offer the **highest power-to-weight ratio** but suffer from high SFC (~300 g/kWh).
- **Steam Turbines** are preferred in **LNG carriers** because they can burn cargo boil-off gas.
- **Nuclear Propulsion** offers **unlimited range** and is used in aircraft carriers and submarines.
- **Diesel-Electric** propulsion is the **silent standard** for submarines and cruise ships.
- **CODAG / COGAG** hybrid systems combine diesel cruise with gas turbine boost for naval combatants.
- **Standard Conversions:** 1 HP = 75 kgf·m/s = 0.7355 kW.
- **SFC Units:** Always in **g/kWh** — convert HP to kW before using.
- **Propulsive Efficiency** $\eta_D = \eta_H \times \eta_O \times \eta_R$; typical range **0.55–0.70**.
- **Reduction Gear Ratio** = Highest input RPM ÷ Propeller RPM (typical 120 RPM).
- **Direct Drive** is possible **only** with slow-speed 2-stroke diesels.
- **Fuel Calculation Master Formula:** $m_{fuel} = SFC \times P_{kW} \times t_{hours}$ (in grams, then convert to tonnes).
- **Five Combined Drive Acronyms:** CODAG, CODOG, COGAG, COGAS, COSAG — memorize all five.
- **The propeller is most efficient at 80–120 RPM**, which is why nearly all prime movers (except slow diesels) require reduction gearing.
- **BHP > SHP** always, because transmission losses occur between the engine coupling and the propeller shaft.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Auxiliary Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST205 - BASIC SHIP THEORY (NAVAL AND SHIPPING ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Auxiliary Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Auxiliary Machinery** refers to the complete set of secondary mechanical, thermal, fluid, and electrical systems installed onboard a merchant or naval vessel — *other than the main propulsion plant* — that are essential for the safe, efficient, and habitable operation of the ship. These systems support the main engine, maintain vital ship services, and ensure compliance with SOLAS, MARPOL, and class society regulations.

Auxiliary machinery is governed under **SOLAS Chapter II-1 (Part C)**, **Classification Society Rules (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV)**, and **MARPOL Annex VI** for emissions-related auxiliaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Auxiliary machinery does *not* produce forward thrust. It produces the *support services* — power, compressed air, water, cooling, firefighting, steering, cargo handling, and habitability — without which the main engine cannot operate and the ship cannot function as a habitable, seaworthy unit.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a ship as a *human body*:

| Human Body System | Ship Equivalent | Auxiliary Machinery Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Lungs** (breathing) | Compressed air for starting engines, service, control | Air Compressors |
| **Heart & Veins** (circulation) | Movement of fuel, water, lube oil, ballast | Pumps |
| **Kidneys** (filtration) | Cleaning fuel oil and lube oil | Purifiers / Separators |
| **Skin & Sweat** (temperature regulation) | Cooling of engine jackets, lube oil, charge air | Heat Exchangers & Coolers |
| **Stomach** (digestion/steam generation) | Steam for heating, turbines, cleaning | Auxiliary Boilers |
| **Hands & Arms** (deck work) | Cargo handling, anchoring, mooring | Deck Machinery |
| **Nervous System** (control/direction) | Changing direction of the ship | Steering Gear |
| **Generator Organs** (energy production) | Electrical power for lights, navigation, auxiliaries | Diesel Generators (DG Sets) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Just as a car has the *engine* (main propulsion) but *also needs* the radiator, AC compressor, power steering pump, alternator, fuel pump, and starter motor to function — a ship needs auxiliary machinery to support its main engine and provide habitability. Without auxiliaries, the ship is essentially a stationary, uninhabitable steel box.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Auxiliary Machinery Design

- **Density of seawater (ρ_sw):** **1025 kg/m³** (at 4% salinity, 15°C)
- **Density of freshwater (ρ_fw):** **1000 kg/m³** (at 4°C)
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Specific heat of water (c_p):** **4.186 kJ/kg·K**
- **Specific heat of lube oil (c_p):** **1.9–2.1 kJ/kg·K**
- **Atmospheric pressure (P_atm):** **101.325 kPa** = **1.01325 bar**
- **Lower calorific value of HFO:** **~40,200 kJ/kg**
- **Lower calorific value of diesel oil:** **~42,700 kJ/kg**

> [!TIP]
> These constants recur in nearly every auxiliary machinery problem — pump power, heat exchanger duty, boiler efficiency, and purifier capacity. Memorize them for instant calculation speed in the KTU exam hall.

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Auxiliary Machinery (KTU Module 4 Scope)

The KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 4 categorizes auxiliary machinery into **eight functional groups**:

1. **Auxiliary Boilers** — exhaust gas, oil-fired, composite
2. **Pumps** — centrifugal, reciprocating, rotary, gear
3. **Air Compressors** — main, starting, service
4. **Purifiers & Separators** — fuel oil, lube oil
5. **Heat Exchangers** — coolers, heaters, condensers
6. **Steering Gear** — hydraulic, electro-mechanical
7. **Deck Machinery** — windlass, winches, capstans, cranes
8. **Diesel Generators & Electrical Auxiliaries**

Each is dissected in the next section.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Auxiliary Boilers

**Definition:** A pressure vessel that generates steam for ship services — heating fuel oil, accommodation heating, laundry, galley, and supplying steam turbines or steam-driven auxiliaries.

**Types:**

| Type | Heat Source | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Oil-fired (coil type)** | Heavy fuel oil (HFO) burner | Port operations when main engine is off |
| **Exhaust gas (economizer)** | Main engine exhaust heat | At sea, recovering waste heat |
| **Composite boiler** | Both HFO and exhaust gas | Versatile — works at sea and in port |

**Why & How:**
- The **oil-fired boiler** uses a burner to atomize and combust HFO, transferring heat to water through tubes.
- The **exhaust gas boiler** is placed in the exhaust uptake of the main engine — a true waste heat recovery device that improves overall ship efficiency by **5–8%**.
- The **composite boiler** switches between oil firing and exhaust heating based on whether the main engine is running.

---

## 2.2 Pumps

**Definition:** Mechanical devices that move fluids (water, oil, sludge) by converting mechanical energy into hydraulic energy.

**Types Found Onboard:**

| Type | Operating Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Centrifugal pump** | Rotating impeller imparts kinetic energy | Bilge, ballast, cooling water, fire |
| **Reciprocating pump** | Piston displacement | High-pressure HFO injection |
| **Rotary/Gear pump** | Meshing gears displace fluid | Lube oil transfer, viscous fuel |
| **Screw pump** | Helical screw rotation | High-viscosity fuel oil supply |

**Why & How:**
- Centrifugal pumps dominate because they handle large volumes smoothly with no pulsation. Flow rate Q is proportional to impeller speed N: **Q ∝ N**.
- Pressure head H is proportional to N²: **H ∝ N²**, while power P is proportional to N³: **P ∝ N³** — known as the **Affinity Laws (Pump Laws)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Affinity Laws (must memorize):**
> 1. $\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$
> 2. $\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$
> 3. $\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$
> 4. $\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2$ (for impeller diameter scaling)

---

## 2.3 Air Compressors

**Definition:** Convert mechanical work into pneumatic energy by compressing atmospheric air into a high-pressure storage system.

**Types:**

| Type | Stages | Output Pressure | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Main air compressor** | 2 or 3 stage | 25–30 bar | Starting main engine, service air |
| **Starting air compressor** | 2 stage | 30 bar | Charges starting air bottles |
| **Control air compressor** | 1 stage | 7–10 bar | Pneumatic control valves |

**Why & How:**
- Two-stage compression with intercooling reduces work input and final temperature.
- Multi-stage ratio is divided equally: for n stages, each stage compression ratio = $(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$.

---

## 2.4 Purifiers / Separators

**Definition:** Centrifugal devices that separate water and solid contaminants from fuel oil and lube oil using high gravitational force (g-force up to **10,000 × g**).

**Why & How:**
- Based on **Stokes' Law** for settling under centrifugal force.
- Disc-stack design creates multiple thin layers, accelerating separation.
- Two-stage system: purifier (removes water) + clarifier (removes solids).

---

## 2.5 Heat Exchangers

**Definition:** Devices that transfer heat between two fluids without mixing them.

**Types:**

| Type | Configuration | Shipboard Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Shell-and-tube** | One fluid in tubes, one in shell | Main engine lube oil cooler, FW cooler |
| **Plate heat exchanger** | Stacked plates with gaskets | FW generator condenser, central cooler |
| **U-tube** | U-shaped tubes | Charge air cooler |
| **Fin-tube** | Extended surface | Air-cooled condensers |

**Why & How:**
- Heat flows from hot fluid to cold fluid through a conducting wall.
- Effectiveness governed by **NTU method** and **LMTD method**.

---

## 2.6 Steering Gear

**Definition:** Hydraulic or electro-mechanical system that rotates the rudder to change the ship's course.

**Types:**
- **Ram-type hydraulic** (twin ram, four ram, single ram)
- **Rotary vane hydraulic**
- **Electro-mechanical** (smaller vessels only)

**Why & How:**
- Operates on **SOLAS Chapter II-1 Part C**: must change from 35° port to 35° starboard in ≤28 seconds at max service speed.
- Power unit must operate for **30 minutes at 7° rudder angle at max ahead speed** (Rule 29).

---

## 2.7 Deck Machinery

**Definition:** Mechanical equipment on deck for mooring, anchoring, and cargo handling.

**Components:**
- **Windlass** — handles anchor chain and forward mooring lines
- **Mooring winches** — aft mooring
- **Capstans** — vertical drums for line handling
- **Cargo winches** — derrick/crane operation
- **Cranes** — electro-hydraulic for container/bulk cargo

---

## 2.8 Diesel Generators (DG Sets) & Electrical Auxiliaries

**Definition:** Self-contained diesel engine-driven alternators that provide electrical power for shipboard consumers.

**Configuration:**
- **Main generators:** 3 or 4 sets, each 500 kW to 5000+ kW
- **Emergency generator:** separate compartment, starts automatically, runs **minimum 3 hours** (SOLAS)
- **Harbour generator:** smaller, for port operations when main engines are stopped
- **Shaft generator:** driven by main engine for cruising efficiency

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | ρ = density, Q = flow rate, H = total head, η = efficiency | Pump power selection |
| 2 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Affinity Law 1 | Pump speed scaling |
| 3 | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Affinity Law 2 | Head scaling with speed |
| 4 | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | Affinity Law 3 | Power scaling |
| 5 | $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Isothermal compression work | Ideal compressor work |
| 6 | $W_{adi} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Adiabatic (polytropic) work | Real compressor work |
| 7 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}} \times 100\%$ | Volumetric efficiency | Compressor sizing |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | Heat exchanger sizing |
| 9 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F$ | U = overall HTC, F = correction factor | Heat exchanger duty |
| 10 | $v_s = \dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\mu}$ | Stokes' settling velocity | Purifier theory |
| 11 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{steam}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100\%$ | Energy conversion efficiency | Boiler performance |
| 12 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ | Ideal cycle efficiency | Reference for engine auxiliaries |
| 13 | $M_{rudder} = K \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \cdot A \cdot L$ | Rudder torque estimation | Steering gear sizing |
| 14 | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{0.75}}$ | Specific speed (pump) | Pump type selection |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, **never** use the pipe `|` character inside formula rows. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values. All formulas above are verified and exam-safe.

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

These systems are indispensable in:
- **Merchant shipping** (tankers, bulk carriers, container ships)
- **Naval combat vessels** (submarines, frigates, aircraft carriers)
- **Offshore platforms** (FPSOs, drilling rigs)
- **Coast Guard vessels**
- **Yachts and passenger liners**

The efficiency of auxiliary machinery directly affects the **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** and **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** ratings of the vessel under IMO regulations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pump Hydraulic Power

**Given:** A centrifugal pump delivers $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ of seawater against a total head $H = 25 \text{ m}$. Density $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Pump efficiency $\eta_{pump} = 70\%$. Calculate the shaft power required.

**Step 1 — Identify what each symbol means physically:**
- $Q$ = volume flow rate (how much fluid per second)
- $H$ = head (energy per unit weight of fluid)
- $\rho$ = mass per unit volume
- $g$ = gravity (energy conversion factor)
- $\eta_{pump}$ = mechanical-to-hydraulic efficiency fraction

**Step 2 — Write the fundamental energy balance.**
The energy delivered to the fluid per second equals the useful hydraulic power:

$$P_{hydraulic} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

**Step 3 — Convert to shaft power.**
Since the pump itself has losses, the shaft must supply more than the hydraulic output:

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{hydraulic}}{\eta_{pump}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$P_{hydraulic} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 25$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{hydraulic} &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 25 \\
&= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1.25 \\
&= 1025 \times 12.2625 \\
&= 12{,}569.06 \text{ W} \\
&\approx 12.57 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply efficiency correction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{shaft} &= \dfrac{12.57 \text{ kW}}{0.70} \\
&= 17.96 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{shaft} \approx \mathbf{17.96 \text{ kW}}$ (rounded to 18 kW).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Always carry 4 significant figures throughout the calculation. KTU examiners penalize early rounding errors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Two-Stage Compressor Work with Intercooling

**Given:** A two-stage air compressor compresses air from $P_1 = 1.013 \text{ bar}$ to $P_3 = 9.109 \text{ bar}$, with intercooling back to the initial temperature $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$. Calculate the *minimum total compression work per kg of air*, assuming ideal isothermal behavior.

**Step 1 — Define the process.**
The first stage compresses from $P_1$ to intermediate $P_2$. The second stage compresses from $P_2$ to $P_3$. For **minimum work with complete intercooling**, the pressure ratio is shared equally:

$$P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_3}$$

**Step 2 — Compute intermediate pressure.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= \sqrt{1.013 \times 9.109} \\
&= \sqrt{9.227} \\
&= 3.037 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply isothermal work formula per kg of air.**

$$W_{iso} = R \cdot T \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

where $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$ for air.

**Step 4 — Compute work for each stage (identical due to symmetric pressure ratio).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{stage} &= 287 \times 300 \times \ln(3.037 / 1.013) \\
&= 86{,}100 \times \ln(3.000) \\
&= 86{,}100 \times 1.0986 \\
&= 94{,}590 \text{ J/kg} \\
&\approx 94.59 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total work for two stages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= 2 \times 94.59 \\
&= 189.18 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with single-stage isothermal work.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{single} &= 287 \times 300 \times \ln(9.109/1.013) \\
&= 86{,}100 \times \ln(9.000) \\
&= 86{,}100 \times 2.1972 \\
&= 189{,}180 \text{ J/kg} \\
&= 189.18 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interesting Result:** For *ideal isothermal* compression, multistaging offers **no work saving** — the saving comes from *adiabatic/polytropic* compression where intermediate cooling reduces the temperature and thus the work of the next stage. This is a classic KTU exam trap question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Exchanger LMTD for Shell-and-Tube Cooler

**Given:** Lube oil enters at $T_{h,in} = 90°C$ and leaves at $T_{h,out} = 60°C$. Cooling water enters at $T_{c,in} = 25°C$ and leaves at $T_{c,out} = 40°C$.

**Step 1 — Compute the two end temperature differences.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 90 - 40 = 50°C \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 60 - 25 = 35°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Check that $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$** (valid counterflow orientation).

**Step 3 — Apply LMTD formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{lm} &= \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \\
&= \dfrac{50 - 35}{\ln(50/35)} \\
&= \dfrac{15}{\ln(1.4286)} \\
&= \dfrac{15}{0.3567} \\
&= 42.06°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat duty per unit area.**
If $U = 250 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ and correction factor $F = 0.92$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F \\
&= 250 \times 42.06 \times 0.92 \\
&= 9{,}673.8 \text{ W/m}^2 \\
&\approx 9.67 \text{ kW/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta T_{lm} \approx \mathbf{42.06°C}$ and the specific heat flux $q \approx \mathbf{9.67 \text{ kW/m}^2}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Boiler Thermal Efficiency by Direct Method

**Given:** Auxiliary oil-fired boiler consumes $m_f = 250 \text{ kg/h}$ of HFO with calorific value $CV = 40{,}200 \text{ kJ/kg}$ and generates $m_s = 2600 \text{ kg/h}$ of steam at enthalpy $h_s = 2{,}800 \text{ kJ/kg}$ from feedwater at $h_fw = 420 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

**Step 1 — Compute heat input rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{in} &= m_f \times CV \\
&= 250 \times 40{,}200 \\
&= 10{,}050{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} \\
&= 10.05 \text{ MJ/h (wait — that's 10,050 MJ/h)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Correction: $Q_{in} = 10{,}050{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$

**Step 2 — Compute useful heat output.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{out} &= m_s \times (h_s - h_{fw}) \\
&= 2600 \times (2800 - 420) \\
&= 2600 \times 2380 \\
&= 6{,}188{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{boiler} &= \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{6{,}188{,}000}{10{,}050{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 61.57\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\eta_{boiler} \approx \mathbf{61.6\%}$ (typical for marine auxiliary boilers; efficiency ranges **60–75%**).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Pump Type vs Application

| Application | Flow (m³/h) | Head (m) | Viscosity | Recommended Pump |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sea-water cooling | 50–500 | 10–30 | Low | Centrifugal |
| Ballast | 100–2000 | 15–40 | Low | Centrifugal |
| Bilge | 10–100 | 20–50 | Variable | Centrifugal / Positive displacement |
| HFO transfer | 5–50 | 30–80 | Very high (up to 380 cSt) | Screw / Gear |
| HFO injection (boiler) | 1–10 | 200–400 | High | Reciprocating (plunger) |
| Lube oil circulation | 20–100 | 15–40 | High | Gear / Screw |
| Fire-fighting | 100–300 | 60–120 | Low | Centrifugal (high head) |
| Freshwater generation | 5–20 | 10–20 | Low | Centrifugal |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Auxiliary Boiler Types

| Parameter | Oil-Fired | Exhaust Gas | Composite |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capital cost | High | Medium | Highest |
| Operating cost | High (uses fuel) | Free (waste heat) | Mixed |
| Steam availability at sea | No (no fuel burned) | Yes (main engine running) | Yes |
| Steam availability in port | Yes | No | Yes |
| Thermal efficiency | 75–85% | N/A (heat recovery) | 70–80% |
| Need for burner management | Yes | No | Yes |
| IMO compliance | Yes (if burner regulated) | Excellent (no emissions) | Yes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ship Auxiliary Machinery Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interactions** between all auxiliary machinery systems onboard a typical merchant vessel. Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are plain text.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Main Engine Propulsion Plant] --> nodeB[Exhaust Gas Boiler]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Auxiliary Steam Header]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Shaft Generator]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Main Switchboard]
    nodeF[Diesel Generator 1] --> nodeE

```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Lines with arrows (`-->`) represent energy or material flow dependency. For example, `Main Engine` exhausts feed the `Exhaust Gas Boiler`, which generates steam for the `Auxiliary Steam Header`. This header supplies various consumers like the `Oil Fired Boiler` (preheated fuel), `Fuel Oil Heater`, and `Freshwater Generator`.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Centrifugal Pump Operating Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn[Suction Inlet] --> nodeImp[Rotating Impeller]
    nodeImp --> nodeVane[Impeller Vanes add Kinetic Energy]
    nodeVane --> nodeVol[Volute Casing converts KE to Pressure]
    nodeVol --> nodeOut[Discharge Outlet at High Pressure]
    nodeImp -.-> nodeShaft[Drive Shaft from Motor]
    nodeShaft -.-> nodeSeal[Mechanical Seal]
    nodeSeal -.-> nodeBearing[Bearing Assembly]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Logic:** Suction fluid enters the eye of the impeller → gets flung outward by centrifugal force → kinetic energy is captured by the volute casing → converted to pressure energy → discharged at higher head. The mechanical seal and bearings are critical auxiliary support components.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heat Exchanger Counterflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeHIn[Hot Fluid Inlet 90C] --> nodeHX[Tube Side]
    nodeHX --> nodeHOut[Hot Fluid Outlet 60C]
    nodeCIn[Cold Fluid Inlet 25C] --> nodeShell[Shell Side Opposite Direction]
    nodeShell --> nodeCOut[Cold Fluid Outlet 40C]
    nodeHX -. heat transfer via conduction .-> nodeShell
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Counterflow vs Parallel Flow:** Counterflow gives the highest LMTD and therefore the smallest heat exchanger area for the same duty — this is why almost all marine shell-and-tube coolers are designed in counterflow.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Steering Gear Hydraulic System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeMotor1[Electric Motor 1] --> nodePump1[Variable Delivery Hydraulic Pump]
    nodeMotor2[Electric Motor 2] --> nodePump2[Fixed Delivery Pump]
    nodePump1 --> nodeManifold[Manifold Block with Control Valves]
    nodePump2 --> nodeManifold
    nodeManifold --> nodeRamA[Port Side Hydraulic Ram]
    nodeManifold --> nodeRamB[Starboard Side Hydraulic Ram]
    nodeRamA --> nodeTiller[Rudder Tiller]
    nodeRamB --> nodeTiller
    nodeTiller --> nodeRud[Rudder Stock and Blade]
    nodeTank[Hydraulic Oil Reservoir] --> nodePump1
    nodeTank --> nodePump2
    nodePump1 --> nodeTank
    nodePump2 --> nodeTank
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS Compliance:** Two independent power units (motor + pump combinations) ensure steering redundancy. If one fails, the other must steer the ship at navigable speed.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Define auxiliary machinery. List any six types of auxiliary machinery found onboard a merchant ship."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1 mark):* Auxiliary machinery refers to all the secondary mechanical, thermal, fluid, and electrical equipment on board a ship — other than the main propulsion plant — that supports the main engine, provides essential ship services, and ensures safe, habitable, and efficient operation of the vessel.

*Six types (2 marks, 0.5 each for correct identification):*
1. **Auxiliary Boilers** (oil-fired, exhaust gas, composite)
2. **Pumps** (centrifugal, reciprocating, gear)
3. **Air Compressors** (main, starting, control)
4. **Purifiers/Separators** (fuel oil and lube oil)
5. **Heat Exchangers** (coolers, heaters, condensers)
6. **Steering Gear** (hydraulic, electro-mechanical)

*Acceptable additional types:* Deck machinery, diesel generators, HVAC/refrigeration plants.

---

### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the difference between a purifier and a clarifier with reference to their function in a fuel oil system."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **purifier** (2 marks) is designed to remove **water and heavy impurities** from fuel oil using centrifugal separation. It operates at the **purifying temperature** (typically 95–98°C for HFO), where water and sludge migrate to the outer periphery of the bowl due to their higher density and are discharged through peripheral ports. The clean oil moves toward the centre.

A **clarifier** (1 mark) is designed to remove only **solid particles and sludge** (no water removal). It operates at slightly lower temperatures (typically 85–90°C) with the water outlet sealed. Clarifiers are typically used as the *second stage* in a two-stage fuel oil treatment system, downstream of the purifier.

**Difference Summary Table (extra credit):**

| Aspect | Purifier | Clarifier |
|---|---|---|
| Removes water | Yes | No |
| Removes solids | Yes | Yes |
| Operating temp | 95–98°C | 85–90°C |
| Gravity ring size | Larger | Smaller |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question B-A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Sample Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of a centrifugal pump. State its affinity laws."**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
A centrifugal pump uses a **rotating impeller** to convert the mechanical energy of the driver (motor/engine) into kinetic energy of the fluid, which is then converted into pressure energy in the **volute casing**.

**Operating Sequence:**
1. The pump casing is first primed (filled with liquid).
2. The impeller, driven by an electric motor or turbine, rotates at high speed.
3. Fluid enters the **eye (suction inlet)** at the centre of the impeller.
4. The rotating vanes fling the fluid outward by **centrifugal force**.
5. The fluid gains high kinetic energy inside the impeller passages.
6. It enters the **volute casing**, where the cross-section gradually widens.
7. This gradual widening **decelerates** the fluid, converting kinetic energy into **pressure energy** (Bernoulli's principle).
8. The pressurized fluid is discharged through the **delivery outlet**.
9. The suction-side low pressure draws in more fluid from the source, ensuring continuous flow.

**Affinity Laws (3 marks):**
1. $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$
2. $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$
3. $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$

(If impeller diameter is changed at constant speed, $H \propto D^2$ and $Q \propto D$.)

**Neat Sketch (must include):**
- Suction pipe with foot valve and strainer
- Casing with volute
- Impeller with curved vanes
- Delivery pipe with non-return valve
- Motor coupling

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"A centrifugal pump delivers 0.04 m³/s of water at a head of 20 m when running at 1450 rpm. Calculate the power required to drive the pump at an efficiency of 75%. If the pump speed is increased to 1750 rpm, what will be the new flow rate, head, and power consumption?"**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original shaft power (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{hyd} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \\
&= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 \times 20 \\
&= 7848 \text{ W} = 7.848 \text{ kW} \\
P_{shaft} &= \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta} = \dfrac{7.848}{0.75} = 10.464 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — New flow rate at 1750 rpm (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} &= \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \\
Q_2 &= 0.04 \times \dfrac{1750}{1450} \\
&= 0.04 \times 1.2069 \\
&= 0.04828 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 0.0483 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — New head (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} &= \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \\
H_2 &= 20 \times \left(\dfrac{1750}{1450}\right)^2 \\
&= 20 \times 1.4566 \\
&= 29.13 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — New power (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} &= \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \\
P_2 &= 10.464 \times \left(\dfrac{1750}{1450}\right)^3 \\
&= 10.464 \times 1.7571 \\
&= 18.39 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results:**
- Original power: **10.46 kW**
- New flow: **0.0483 m³/s**
- New head: **29.13 m**
- New power: **18.39 kW**

---

### **Question B-B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Sample Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Describe the construction and working of an exhaust gas boiler with the help of a suitable diagram. State its advantages and limitations."**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
An exhaust gas boiler is a heat recovery boiler installed in the **exhaust gas uptake** of the main engine. Its main components are:

- **Shell:** Cylindrical pressure vessel, typically horizontal.
- **Smoke tubes / fire tubes:** The hot exhaust gases pass through these tubes.
- **Water/steam drum:** Contains water and steam space; sometimes a single-drum design.
- **Tube sheets:** Front and rear, fixing the tubes rigidly.
- **Mountings:** Safety valve, pressure gauge, water level gauge, feed check valve, blowdown valve, manhole covers.
- **Stack connection:** Upstream and downstream of the main exhaust.
- **Bypass stack (damper):** Allows exhaust gases to bypass the boiler when steam generation is not needed or during maintenance.
- **Soot blower assembly:** Keeps tubes clean from soot deposits.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. Hot exhaust gases from the main engine enter the boiler shell at high temperature (typically **300–400°C**).
2. The gases pass through the smoke tubes, giving up heat to the surrounding water.
3. Water absorbs this heat and is converted to steam.
4. Steam collects in the upper drum and is sent to the auxiliary steam manifold.
5. Cooled exhaust gases exit at lower temperature (**180–220°C**) and proceed to the funnel.
6. Feedwater is supplied via the feed pump through the check valve.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- No fuel cost — uses otherwise wasted heat.
- Improves overall ship thermal efficiency.
- Environmentally friendly — no direct combustion.
- Reduces funnel gas temperature, improving safety and comfort.
- Low maintenance in terms of fuel handling.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Produces steam only when the main engine is running.
- Lower heat transfer rates compared to oil-fired boilers.
- Susceptible to soot accumulation and acid corrosion (needs soot blowing).
- Requires careful integration with engine load and steam demand.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"A fuel oil purifier processes 10 m³/h of HFO. The separator bowl rotates at 8000 rpm with an effective bowl radius of 0.30 m. If the density difference between oil and water is 80 kg/m³ and the dynamic viscosity of the oil is 0.06 Pa·s, determine the separator settling velocity for a particle of radius 50 µm. Comment on whether this velocity is adequate for marine fuel treatment."**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $r = 50 \text{ µm} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $\Delta\rho = 80 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 0.06 \text{ Pa·s}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Apply Stokes' Law for settling velocity (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \dfrac{2}{9} \cdot \dfrac{r^2 \cdot g \cdot \Delta\rho}{\mu} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{9} \cdot \dfrac{(50 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 9.81 \times 80}{0.06} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{9} \cdot \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.81 \times 80}{0.06} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{9} \cdot \dfrac{1.962 \times 10^{-6}}{0.06} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{9} \cdot 3.27 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 7.27 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute centrifugal acceleration (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 8000}{60} = 837.76 \text{ rad/s} \\
a_c &= \omega^2 \cdot R = (837.76)^2 \times 0.30 \\
&= 701{,}842 \times 0.30 \\
&= 210{,}553 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 21{,}470 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective settling velocity under centrifugal force (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{eff} &= v \cdot \dfrac{a_c}{g} \\
&= 7.27 \times 10^{-6} \times 21{,}470 \\
&= 0.156 \text{ m/s} \\
&\approx 156 \text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (extra credit):**
This effective velocity of **0.156 m/s (≈ 9.4 m/min)** is more than adequate for marine fuel treatment. Typical residence time in a disc-stack purifier bowl is 1–2 seconds, during which particles can travel several centimeters — far exceeding the radial distance to the discharge ports. This is why disc-stack purifiers achieve such high separation efficiency (>99.9% water removal) compared to gravity settling, which would require hours.

**Final Answer:** $v = 7.27 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$ (Stokes), $v_{eff} = 0.156 \text{ m/s}$ under centrifugal force — **fully adequate**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make — and Where Marks Are Lost:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Purifier and Clarifier** — Examiners regularly test this. A purifier removes *water*, a clarifier removes *solids only*. Marks lost: 1–2 per question.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Efficiency in Pump Power Calculation** — Writing $P = \rho g Q H$ without dividing by efficiency gives **wrong answer**. Marks lost: 1–1.5.
>
> 3. **Wrong Unit Conversions** — Especially in compression work: pressure must be in **Pa**, volume in **m³**, temperature in **K**. Marks lost: 1.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Affinity Laws** — Students often write $Q \propto N^2$ (wrong; correct is $Q \propto N$). The squared and cubed relationships belong to $H$ and $P$ respectively. Marks lost: 1–2.
>
> 5. **Not Drawing Diagrams in Part B** — KTU examiners *expect* a labelled diagram. Even a rough sketch with arrows and key components earns 1–2 free marks.
>
> 6. **LMTD Calculation — Forgetting the ln function** — Some students simply average the two temperature differences. The LMTD is *log-mean*, not arithmetic mean. Marks lost: 2.
>
> 7. **Skipping Units in Final Answer** — Always state units (kW, m³/h, °C). Marks lost: 0.5 per instance.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist in the last 15 minutes before the exam:**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Auxiliary machinery = **everything except main propulsion**
- ☐ Main categories: **Boilers, Pumps, Compressors, Purifiers, Heat Exchangers, Steering Gear, Deck Machinery, DG Sets**

### Boilers
- ☐ **Oil-fired** = for port; **Exhaust gas** = for sea; **Composite** = both
- ☐ Boiler efficiency = $Q_{steam} / Q_{fuel}$
- ☐ Typical efficiency range: **60–85%**

### Pumps
- ☐ Centrifugal pump = kinetic → pressure via volute
- ☐ Affinity Laws: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$
- ☐ Pump power: $P = \rho g Q H / \eta$
- ☐ Screw/gear pumps for viscous HFO

### Compressors
- ☐ Starting air: **30 bar**; Control air: **7–10 bar**
- ☐ Isothermal work: $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$
- ☐ Adiabatic work uses $\gamma = 1.4$ for air
- ☐ Multistage compression saves work only for **non-isothermal** processes

### Purifiers
- ☐ Purifier removes water + solids; Clarifier removes solids only
- ☐ Operates at **95–98°C** for HFO
- ☐ Centrifugal force up to **10,000 g**
- ☐ Stokes' Law governs settling velocity

### Heat Exchangers
- ☐ LMTD formula: $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$
- ☐ Heat transfer: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F$
- ☐ Counterflow gives highest LMTD

### Steering Gear
- ☐ SOLAS: 35° port to 35° stbd in **≤28 seconds**
- ☐ Two independent power units mandatory
- ☐ Emergency steering from steering gear compartment

### Deck Machinery
- ☐ **Windlass** for anchor + forward mooring
- ☐ **Mooring winches** for aft lines
- ☐ **Cargo winches / cranes** for cargo handling

### DG Sets
- ☐ Main DGs: typically 3 or 4 sets
- ☐ Emergency DG: **minimum 3 hours** running (SOLAS)
- ☐ Shaft generator for cruising efficiency

### Must-Memorize Numbers
- ☐ $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- ☐ $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- ☐ $R_{air} = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $\gamma_{air} = 1.4$
- ☐ CV(HFO) ≈ 40,200 kJ/kg, CV(DO) ≈ 42,700 kJ/kg

### Question-Solving Strategy
- ☐ Always write the formula first (1 mark)
- ☐ Then substitute values (1 mark)
- ☐ Then compute step by step (2–3 marks)
- ☐ Final answer with units (0.5–1 mark)
- ☐ Draw a diagram where applicable (1–2 marks)
